# Preface {-}

"Is coffee a necessary condition for you?" Someone asked me this question after hearing that I had taken time away in Italy to focus on writing my book, and that I like Italy's coffee culture. "No, it is not; it only helps." Focus, time, perseverance, and several other things are true necessary conditions. Without any of them, the book would not exist.

And here it is! In the book I present the principles of Necessary Condition Analysis (NCA) and their application in empirical studies in research and practice. NCA is an approach that uses necessity causal logic to identify necessary conditions from data. The core idea is that a necessary condition must be present to make the outcome possible, and that its absence guarantees the absence of the outcome. Compensation is not possible.

My intention with this book is to help researchers, data analysts, practitioners, and others develop a deeper understanding of the NCA approach and apply it effectively and with high-quality standards. The book brings together the fundamentals of NCA and integrates the most recent methodological advancements and tools. It synthesizes ideas from my earlier publications that describe NCA at introductory levels [e.g., @dul2016orm; @dul2020book] as well as more advanced treatments [@dul2020orm; @dul2021online; @dul2024different], while also introducing more background, new topics and recent developments. From 2021 to 2026, a regularly updated online predecessor of this book was available [@dul2021online], which is now archived and integrated into the present book.

The first chapter of this book provides a general introduction to the possibilities and current use of NCA. Next, Part I contains five chapters on the *principles* of NCA, covering NCA's causality, theory, mathematics, statistics, and its credibility for identifying necessity from data. Part II has six chapters about the *application* of these principles in empirical studies discussing NCA's hypothesis formulation, data collection, data analysis, reporting, and how to apply NCA in multimethod research and in practice. I finish the book with a summary and a personal reflection.

The book's content expresses my current understanding and interpretation of NCA. NCA develops over time, just like other methodological approaches do. As NCA is applied more widely, new questions, topics, insights, and ideas will emerge. An online version of the book with updates and additional materials is available via the book-page: https://jandul.github.io/NCA/. For the physical version of the book see @dul2025book.

I hope this book serves those who wish to gain a comprehensive understanding of the principles of NCA and their application, both in academic research and practical contexts.

Enjoy discovering and applying NCA! \
  \  
  \  
  \
Leiden, March 2026

# Acknowledgements {-}

I created NCA during a long process that spans more than a decade. NCA and the content of this book are not only the result of my own thinking and reading but also of many discussions with fellow researchers during in person and online meetings, at conferences, workshops, seminars, webinars, and in email exchanges. These interactions have played an important role in shaping NCA.

I wish to express my gratitude to the universities and other research organizations that invited me to deliver NCA seminars and workshops, both in its early years and more recently. These engagements enabled valuable face-to-face discussions that contributed to the development of NCA. I would like to mention the following institutions in alphabetical order: AGH University of Krakow (Poland), Bocconi University (Italy), Chalmers University (Sweden), Deakin University (Australia), Eindhoven University of Technology (Netherlands), Erasmus University (Netherlands), Federal University of Pernambuco (Brazil), George Washington University (USA), Helmut Schmidt University (Germany), INSEAD Business School (France), Jagiellonian University (Poland), LUISS (Italy), Mines Paris Tech (France), Nottingham University (UK), Oslo University (Norway), Oxford University (UK), Penn State University (USA), Radboud University of Nijmegen (Netherlands), RIVM (Netherlands), Tilburg University (Netherlands), University of Amsterdam (Netherlands), TNO (Netherlands), University of Bayreuth (Germany), University of Beira Interior (Portugal), University of Bologna (Italy), University of Coimbra (Portugal), University of Côte d'Azur (France), University of Durham (UK), University of Groningen (Netherlands), University of Kassel (Germany), University of Lincoln (UK), University of Maastricht (Netherlands), University of Paris (France), University of Porto (Portugal), University of Sevilla (Spain), University of Trento (Italy), University of Valencia (Spain), and Wroclaw University of Economics and Business (Poland).

I would also like to express my special appreciation to the Rotterdam School of Management at Erasmus University (Netherlands). Since the incubation of NCA, the school has supported me in developing and disseminating NCA. In particular, I wish to thank Erik van Raaij and Finn Wynstra for their enduring support.

I extend my acknowledgements to the dedicated team of NCA ambassadors and supporters who have contributed in different roles to the dissemination and advancement of this approach, ranging from student assistants to co-authors and co-developers: Jorick Alberga, Florence Allard-Poesie, Tatiana Andreeva, Joost Baart, Gijs van Biezen, Ruben Blom, Stefan Breet, Jon Bokrantz, Govert Buijs, Silvia Dello Russo, Monique van Donzel, Ger Haan, Tony Hak$^\dagger$, Sven Hauff, Patrycja Klimas, Wilfred Knol, Roelof Kuik, Erwin van der Laan$^\dagger$, Wangoo Lee, Igor Marchetti, Magdalena Marczewska, Justine Massu, Indy Oostrom, Erik van Raaij, Henk van Rhee, Nicole Richter, Krista Schellevis, Chloé Schwitzgebel, Daniele Spinelli, Zsófia Tóth, and Caroline Witte. Their support greatly accelerated the adoption of NCA in research.

Throughout the development of NCA, I engaged in extensive and ongoing discussions with numerous scholars and practitioners regarding the positioning of NCA in relation to mainstream approaches. In particular, I learned a lot from my conversations with Gary Goertz, regarding the positioning of NCA vis-à-vis regression analysis and statistics; Barbara Vis and Claude Rubinson on using NCA within the context of QCA; Oskar Kosch on the relationship between NCA and machine learning; Roelof Kuik on the detailed mathematical and statistical foundations of NCA, and Ger Haan on strategies for making NCA appealing to practitioners.

Finally, I would like to thank Bob Bastian, Gijs van Biezen, Jon Bokrantz, Patrycja Klimas, Roelof Kuik, Igor Marchetti, and José Luis Roldán for commenting on a draft version of this book.

Thank you all. Without your help, NCA would not be what it is today.

\mainmatter

# Introduction {#introduction}

NCA is a methodological approach that uses a *necessity* causal perspective for identifying necessary conditions from data. Necessity causal logic in NCA implies that if a certain level of the condition is not present, a certain level of the outcome will not occur: If not $X$, then not $Y$. No other factor can compensate for the absence of the necessary condition (or a required level of it). The necessary condition *allows* the outcome to exist, but does not produce it.^[This book uses specific terminology, such as condition and outcome, that is explained in the glossary of Appendix \@ref(glossary).] A necessary condition operates independently of the rest of the causal structure and serves as a 'critical success factor' or 'must-have'. When absent, it creates a 'bottleneck' or 'constraint' that prevents the target outcome, which cannot be compensated by other factors. For example, a student must have a high school diploma for admission to a university. Without such a diploma, the student will not be admitted. Other factors like a student's high motivation cannot compensate for the lack of a diploma.

Necessity causal logic and NCA differ from *sufficiency* causal logic and related methods. In sufficiency causal logic the condition *produces* the outcome: if $X$, then $Y$. A single factor may be sufficient for the outcome, for example when students graduate, they receive a diploma. Often, not a single factor but a combination of factors (a 'configuration') may be sufficient for the outcome: if [combination of $X$'s], then $Y$. A university diploma alone is not sufficient for admission to a PhD program; however, combining it with strong motivation, good grades, and a recommendation letter will secure it. Such a configurational sufficiency logic is used in methodologies and frameworks like Rothman’s Sufficient-Component Cause model in medicine [SCC, @rothman1976causes], Wright’s Necessary Element of a Sufficient Set in law [NESS, @wright1985causation], and Ragin’s Qualitative Comparative Analysis in the social sciences [QCA, @ragin1987comparative].

Necessity causal logic also differs from *probabilistic* causal sufficiency logic where the single factor is a 'contributing cause' that helps to produce the outcome and contributes to the outcome 'on average': 'if $X$, then probably $Y$'. Highly motivated people are more likely to be admitted to a PhD program than those with lower motivation, although there are highly motivated people who are not admitted and less motivated people who are admitted. Such a probabilistic causal sufficiency logic is applied in statistical methodologies for causal inference, such as in Pearl's Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs) and do-operator approach [@pearl2009causality] and Angrist et al.'s Instrumental Variable approach [IV, @angrist1996identification].

The different causal lenses (single necessity causes, configurational sufficiency causes, and single probabilistic sufficiency causes) offer fundamentally distinct perspectives on causality. These lenses reflect different theoretical expectations (hypotheses) about how outcomes arise. This means that phenomena and data can be approached with different causal lenses (Chapter \@ref(causality)). The choice of lens may be guided by the specific nature of the phenomenon, the study question, or the analyst’s theoretical priorities^[In this book the general term for a user of NCA or other methodological approaches is *analyst*. The analyst can be an academic scholar or a practitioner. The general term for an academic research activity or a project in practice is *study*.]. For instance, if the question is whether a certain resource (e.g., access to capital) is required for a firm to enter a market, a necessity lens is appropriate. This lens helps identify bottleneck factors: conditions that must be in place for success. In contrast, if the aim is to identify combinations of national conditions that are sufficient for strong climate policy commitment (e.g., public support + high income, or climate risk + high fossil fuel dependence), a configurational sufficiency perspective, as used in QCA, may be appropriate. This approach assumes that different pathways can each lead to the same outcome. If the goal is to understand whether self-efficacy increases the likelihood of engaging in a behavior, a probabilistic sufficiency lens is fitting. This approach helps estimate how much more likely the outcome is when a cause is present.

No lens is inherently superior; each has unique strengths and limitations. Thus, there are no better or worse lenses; there are no more or less relevant lenses. These lenses are different and shed different light on the same issues. What matters is that the chosen lens aligns with the goals of the study, the nature of the causal claim being made, and indeed the preference of the analyst. Once a causal lens is selected, it is essential to apply a methodological approach that matches the logic of that lens, ensuring *theory–method fit*, which is essential for the credibility and usefulness of the findings (Chapter \@ref(multimethodstudies)).

This book's emphasis on causality has both theoretical and practical reasons. Theoretically, scientific theories aim to explain why outcomes occur, not merely to describe patterns of co-occurrence (Chapters \@ref(theory) and \@ref(hypothesisformulation)). Practically, changing or influencing an outcome requires intervention on causes of the outcome, not on co-occurring factors (Chapter \@ref(practice)). The assumption that $X$ is a necessary cause of $Y$ implies the assumption that $X$ temporally precedes $Y$ (Chapter \@ref(causality)).

<!-- Single necessity causes, single and configurational sufficiency causes, and single probabilistic sufficiency causes are fundamentally different approaches to causality. They represent different theoretical 'lenses' to describe and analyze the phenomena of interest. One lens is not better than another, and it is up to -->
<!-- <!-- some phenomena may be better understood and described from one specific causal perspective, while others might benefit from using another perspective or multiple perspectives. -->
<!-- the analyst to choose the lens most suitable to the context. However, once the lens is selected, a proper method should be used to ensure theory-method fit. -->

<!-- it is assumed that single causes are probabilistic sufficient for the outcome, and contribute to the outcome on average ('average effect logic') and that the absence of such cause can be compensated by  another cause ('additive logic'). For example, regression analysis describes the complexity of different contributing factors to the outcome with an additive model (the regression equation). In such model a low value of one factor can be compensated by a high value of another factor: a less motivated person may be admitted because a higher grade compensates the lack of motivation. NCA is a quantitative method. Whereas conventional quantitative research commonly focuses on probabilistic sufficiency causes, NCA's focus is on necessity causes. NCA ensures theory-method fit when the theory assumes a necessity causal relationship between factors^[In this book we use the general term 'factor' for a variable, independently of its role as cause or effect. When referring to a necessity relationship between variables, NCA uses the term 'condition' for what is commonly called independent variable, explanatory variable, input variable, predictor variable, antecedent variable, or determinant, and the term 'outcome' for what is commonly called dependent variable, response variable, criterion variable, output variable, effect variable, etc.] -->

<!-- , and this relationship must be evaluated (tested) with empirical data. -->

NCA is an approach that is specifically designed to analyze necessity. It has two intertwined parts: a 'soft' part and a 'hard' part. The soft part is NCA's soul: the causal perspective of necessity (NCA methodology). The hard part is NCA's data analysis method (NCA method) that fits this perspective. Therefore, the term 'NCA' refers to a combination of the NCA methodology with its causal necessity perspective, and the corresponding NCA method for analyzing data.^[In previous publications I have inconsistently used the terms 'approach', 'methodology' and 'method' in the context of NCA. In this book, I follow the common distinction between *methodology*, referring to the philosophical and theoretical framework, and *method* referring to the specific techniques, tools, or procedures used to collect and analyze data. The combination of the two parts is called *approach* or *methodological approach*.] NCA's necessity causal lens extends beyond common binary conditional necessity logic. Specifically, NCA adds causal reasoning to conditional logic, incorporates a continuous view on necessity, and permits exceptions (the 'typicality' perspective, see Chapter \@ref(causality)).

The book is organized as follows. Part I discusses the methodological principles of NCA, explaining its causality (e.g., compared to other causal perspectives), theory (e.g., main elements of a necessity theory, different types of necessity theories), mathematics (e.g., mathematical estimation of ceiling line, NCA parameters such as effect size, and measures of fit) and statistics (e.g., NCA's null hypothesis tests, Monte Carlo simulations with NCA), and NCA's credibility to identify necessity (e.g., using statistical and empirical criteria).

Part II of the book outlines how to conduct NCA. Specifically, this part discusses NCA's hypothesis formulation, data collection, data analysis, reporting, how to apply NCA in a multimethod study, and how NCA can be used in practice. The book concludes with a summary and personal reflection on 10 years of NCA, and Part III provides additional materials.

<!-- It can provide new theoretical insights: since a necessary condition blocks the outcome if absent, the condition must be part of any theory that aims to predict the outcome. NCA is also relevant for practice: all identified necessary conditions must be established to enable a target outcome. If a necessary condition is not met it is a bottleneck for the outcome. Any change process that fails to address this bottleneck will be ineffective, leading to wasted effort. -->

With its innovative approach to causality and data analysis, NCA has rapidly entered the social, medical, and technical sciences.^[The NCA methodology and method can be used in any type of investigation that aims to describe causal relationships. Although usually quantitative data (numbers) are used in NCA, NCA can also be used with qualitative data (words, symbols, see Chapter \@ref(datacollection)) as long as scores (values, levels) for the condition and the outcome are available.] The publication in 2016 of NCA's core paper [@dul2016orm] marks the start of NCA, although proto-versions of NCA were introduced earlier [@dul2008case; @dul2010necessary].^[The dissemination of NCA's core paper [@dul2016orm] has been welcomed by @bergh2022constitutes: "The significant expansion of an unknown method along with its usability (including an illustration, user-friendly recommendations, and a software tool) makes the article a gold standard for a contribution via transfer" (p. 1840).] Subsequently, several extensions were developed, including a statistical significance test [@dul2020orm], and specialized software (Appendix \@ref(appendixb)). Various publications, such as a textbook [@dul2020book] and an online book [@dul2021online] with practical guidelines on applying the method were developed to facilitate the understanding and dissemination of NCA. Furthermore, publications introducing and summarizing NCA have emerged across various fields of business and management, including Human Resource Management [@hauff2021hrm], Marketing and Sales [@dul2021marketing; @conde2025uncovering], Tourism Management [@toth2019tourism], Entrepreneurship [@linder2022necessary], Supply Chain Management [@bokrantz2022building], and International Business [@richter2022necessary]. Also outside the business and management field, the method was introduced in fields such as Public Health [@gregoproposing], Clinical Psychology [@marchetti2025necessity], Education [@tynan2020education], and Creativity [@dul2020creativity].
<!-- \textcolor{red}{Update} -->

Editors of academic journals have recognized the value of NCA by publishing editorial comments on the approach [e.g., @dul2024identifying; @fainshmidt2020contributions; @hernaus2022from; @robinson2022; @sarstedt2023advanced]. Review articles [e.g., @aguinis2020methodological; @boon2019systematic; @dabic202140; @frazier2017psychological; @rozenkowska2023theory;@delsordo2025role] and a plethora of journal articles suggest using NCA in future studies, for example, in the fields of education [e.g., @crede2021should], human resource management [e.g., @hauff2021analytical; @parker2024smart], information systems [e.g., @sharma2024framework], international business/management [e.g., @richter2022use; @zahoor2023international], marketing [e.g., @guenther2023improving; @jovanovic2022under], operations and supply chain management [e.g., @acquah2023invest], strategy [e.g., @bouncken2020value; @deist2023digital; @klimas2023attributes; @ogundipe2022interfirm], and tourism and hospitality [e.g., @becker2023pls]. At the time of writing this book, hundreds of articles that apply NCA have been published already. The number of publications in 'Web of Science' ranked journals^[Journals ranked in Clarivate's Journal Citation Reports.] that apply NCA has increased steadily. Appendix \@ref(appendixa) shows these articles that have appeared through the end of 2025.

Published articles use NCA as the sole or primary method (Appendix \@ref(appendixa); Table \@ref(tab:pubstablealone)), or combine NCA in a multimethod study (Chapter \@ref(multimethodstudies)) with Qualitative Comparative Analysis (QCA, Table \@ref(tab:pubstableqca)) or with regression-based methods. Examples of regression-based methods include multiple linear regression (MLR, Table \@ref(tab:pubstablemlr)) and structural equation modeling (SEM, Tables \@ref(tab:pubstablesem) and \@ref(tab:pubstableplssem)). NCA is also combined with a variety of other methods (Tables \@ref(tab:pubstableother) and \@ref(tab:pubstablecombiother)). Although the number of articles with NCA has grown rapidly, NCA is not always applied according to minimum standards [Chapter \@ref(reporting), @dul2023necessary], and several misconceptions have been published.^[Most misconceptions relate to a lack of awareness about the principles of NCA. Example misconceptions are: "NCA should specify the full data generation process (DGP)" (given its purpose, NCA specifies only a part of the DGP), "NCA is just a statistical method" (NCA is a broad methodological approach that combines theory, mathematics (geometry) and uses statistical tools), "NCA's statistical test is a test of H1" (NCA's statistical tests are null hypothesis tests, testing H0), "NCA is the necessity analysis of QCA" (the necessity analysis of NCA and that of fuzzy-set QCA are fundamentally different), "Necessary conditions according to NCA are the same as INUS conditions" (INUS conditions are local necessary parts of a sufficient configuration that produces the outcome; NCA considers overall necessary conditions for the outcome), "NCA cannot test sufficiency" (NCA can test the absence of $X$ being necessary for absence of $Y$, which is equivalent to $X$ is sufficient for $Y$), etc. Several misconceptions are addressed in specific publications [@dul2019necessary; @dul2021necessary; @dul2022problematic].] Therefore, **the main goal of this book is to promote proper understanding and high-quality application of NCA**.

Many articles that apply NCA conclude that necessity was identified, although the evidence is not always convincing. Other articles that apply NCA concluded that necessity was not found [e.g., @batey2021designing; @luo2022pathway; @gu2022configurations; @peng2022pathway; @golini2016exploiting]. Not finding a necessary condition might be a valuable result, because such a result shows that a supposed essential factor for an outcome does not need to be present and its absence can be compensated [e.g., @arenius2017no]. On the other hand, finding a large number of potential necessary conditions, for example in an exploration study [e.g., @gantert2022moral; @klimas2021strategy; @stek2021train] does not mean that all are informative because several conditions may not have a theoretical meaning or may be trivial, which could have been easily identified before the analysis was done (Chapter \@ref(hypothesisformulation)).^[A positive or negative conclusion about necessity depends on the analyst's judgement of many elements, summarized in NCA's SCoRe checklist for conducting and reporting an NCA study (Chapter \@ref(reporting)).]

<!-- Whereas most quantitative research (implicitly) adopts the probabilistic sufficiency perspective when causal claims are made, NCA offers an alternative by focusing on necessity causality. NCA can also be used in qualitative research to identify conditions that must be present for an outcome to occur. A component of this approach is a specific method for selecting and analyzing a small number of cases [@dul2024how]. In both quantitative and qualitative research it is important to know whether causes are necessary or not, because if any necessary cause is absent, the effect cannot be achieved. For theory development aimed at predicting an outcome, the necessary condition must be included as part of the theory.  -->


<!-- For practice, this means that necessary conditions must be met, and addressing other causes while leaving the unsatisfied necessary condition (the bottleneck) unresolved will have no effect, resulting in waste of effort.  -->
<!-- With conventional regression-based research methods (e.g, multiple linear regression, structural equation modeling), research questions about cause-effect relationships are often formulated like 'what is the effect of $X$ on $Y$?' and related hypotheses are similar to '$X$ has a positive (or negative) effect on $Y$'. The regression equation consisting of terms that are added to produce the average effect including an 'error term', implicitly assumes additive causal sufficiency logic and cause $X$ is assumed  to contribute to producing effect $Y$ *on average*, and the absence of $X$ can be compensated by the presence of other causes. In other words: $X$ is an average contributor to $Y$: a probabilistic cause. -->
<!-- Such causal relationship that is studies with regression analysis implies an *average effect* of $X$ on $Y$. -->
<!-- However, the type of causality is usually not specified.  -->
<!-- ,  which can be graphically shown as an arrow between $X$ and $Y$ indicating a temporal direction: first $X$, then $Y$, with a + or - sign shown above the arrow indicating that $X$ increases or decreases $Y$ on average.  -->
<!-- In NCA, cause $X$ is assumed to be a *necessary* cause of $Y$. The research question is formulated as 'is $X$ necessary for $Y$?'. The corresponding hypothesis is: '$X$ is necessary for $Y$'. If $X$ is necessary for $Y$, single cause $X$ can stop the outcome when it is absent or has a low value. It can be a *bottleneck*, a *critical success factor* or a *must have* factor.  -->

<!-- The NCA method consists of three parts:  -->

<!-- * Using  *necessity logic* for developing theoretical causal statements (theory, hypotheses).  -->

<!-- * *Data analysis* for calculating necessity parameters. -->

<!-- * *Statistical testing* for evaluating necessity parameters.  -->
<!-- NCA can identify necessary conditions from data.  -->
Conducting NCA consists of four stages:

1. Formulate the formal necessity hypothesis.
2. Collect data.
3. Analyze data.
4. Report results.

In the first stage (Chapter \@ref(hypothesisformulation)), necessity causal logic is employed to formulate a theoretical expectation about conditions that are necessary for the outcome^[In this book, it is assumed that hypothesis formulation is done before data collection. The reasons are discussed in Chapter \@ref(hypothesisformulation).].  \
<!-- has three reasons. First, it usually relatively simple to formulate a necessary condition hypothesis (see Chapter \@ref(hypothesisformulation). Second, it is often more efficient to use empirical data for testing a developed hypothesis than for developing a hypothesis with data. In the latter situation the developed hypothesis should be tested with new data. Third, for identifying a necessary condition, NCA requires theoretical support, which means that also in a theory/testing or exploratory study a hypothesis must be formulated after data analysis. This implies that in this book the focus is on using data to *test* necessity, and not on using data for building or *exploring* necessity, e.g., like in data mining].  -->
<!-- Backgrounds of necessary condition hypotheses are discussed in chapter  \@ref(theory), and chapter  \@ref(hypothesisformulation) explains how such hypothesis ($X$ is necessary for $Y$) can be developed and formulated.  -->
The second stage (Chapter \@ref(datacollection)) consists of collecting new or existing data for testing the hypothesis, which includes the consideration of study design, sampling or case selection, and measurement. NCA does not have a 'measurement model', as SEM and QCA have (in QCA it is called 'calibration'). This means that any type of data can be used as input to NCA as long as the scores of $X$ and $Y$ are meaningful, valid, and reliable. NCA poses no new requirements on the data, although some aspects of data collection (case selection in qualitative studies; power analysis in quantitative studies, see for example, @dul2024how may differ. NCA can also be used with archival data to give a new perspective on previous findings [@dul2024advancing]. \    
<!-- . Chapter  \@ref(data) explains this and gives examples of data that can be used in NCA.  -->
In the third stage (Chapters \@ref(dataanalysis), \@ref(multimethodstudies)), NCA's data analysis is conducted, which differs entirely from common types of data analysis. \    
In the fourth stage (Chapter \@ref(reporting)), the results of NCA are reported. 

<!-- The mathematical and statistical backgrounds of the data analysis are shown in chapters  \@ref(mathematical) and  \@ref(statistical). Details of the application of the data analysis are in chapter (\@ref(dataanalysis)), while chapter  \@ref(multimethod) discusses how NCA can be used in multimethod research with regression-based methods and QCA.  -->
<!-- Chapter \@ref(reporting) gives guidelines for this. -->

<!-- While the first part of this book introduces the underlying principles of NCA (with chapters on causality, theory, mathematics, statistics, and the credibility of NCA), the second part focuses on its application, detailing the four above stages (with the chapters Hypothesis formulation, Study design, Sampling, Measurement, Data types, Data analysis, Multimethod studies, Reporting, and Practice). -->

Currently, most applications of NCA are in academic research, where researchers use the NCA approach to develop and test theories. NCA is also entering practical settings where NCA is applied by practitioners such as consultants and data analysts as an innovative approach for addressing challenges and exploring new opportunities. This is discussed in Chapter \@ref(practice). 

After discussing the fundamental backgrounds of the NCA methodology in Part I, the four stages of the NCA method  are discussed in Part II. 

<!-- NCA ensures theory-method fit when the theory assumes a necessity causal relationship between factors^[In this book we use the general term 'factor' for a variable, independently of its role as cause or effect. When referring to a necessity relationship between variables, NCA uses the term 'condition' for what is commonly called independent variable, explanatory variable, input variable, predictor variable, antecedent variable, or determinant, and the term 'outcome' for what is commonly called dependent variable, response variable, criterion variable, output variable, effect variable, etc.] -->
<!-- NCA can be used as a stand-alone method or be used together with  conventional methods such as multiple linear regression, structural equation modeling, or Qualitative Comparative Analysis (QCA). The decision to apply NCA as a stand-alone method or a complementary method depends on the goal of the research.  -->
<!-- ## NCA as stand-alone method {#standalone} -->
<!-- A researcher may have two reasons to use *NCA as a stand-alone method* in a particular study. First, the researcher may want to employ just a necessity view on the phenomenon of interest.  He may have formulated a parsimonious 'pure necessity theory' (see Chapter \@ref(theory)) consisting of one or more necessity relations [e.g., @karwowski2016intellligence; @knol2018implementing]. To ensure theory-method fit, NCA is used for testing pure necessity theories. A regression-based method is not appropriate for this purpose. -->
<!-- [Similarly, when a researcher wants to test a theory consisting only of generic causal relations (most current theories), a conventional probability-based method such as regression analysis should be selected and NCA is not appropriate]. A second reason for using NCA as the stand-alone method is that the researcher may want to add a necessity view to an existing generic causal theory from the literature. Without (re)testing the generic causal relationships, she may want to test whether some concepts of the theory are (also) necessary conditions.  -->
<!-- The advantage of using NCA as a stand-alone method is that the study and its theoretical reasoning can focus on the single necessary concepts. There is no need to include other concepts (e.g., contributing factors, control variables) into the reasoning and analysis. This allows a clear story line and efficient data collection and analysis. -->
<!-- ## NCA as a complementary method {#complementary} -->
<!-- A researcher may also have reasons to use *NCA in combination with other methods* in a single study. The researcher may want to add a necessity view to a generic causal theory and to analyze necessity and generic relationships in combination. This can be done in two ways. First, the *generic causal theory is leading* and some concepts from this theory are tested for necessity. Second  a *combined necessity-generial causal theory* is developed with both necessity and generic causal relationships between concepts.  -->

<!-- In the first option, an existing or new *generic causal theory* has concepts that are considered being contributing factors that can change the probability of the outcome (including control variables). From these concepts, potential necessary conditions are selected. The necessity and generic causal relationships of these concepts with the outcome are tested with the appropriate methods and the methods are conducted successively. The integration occurs when the results are discussed. Several NCA multimethod studies that start with a generic causal theory have been reported in the literature. For example, when NCA is used in combination with (multiple) *regression* [e.g., @jain2022drives1; @klimas2021strategy; @stek2021train] or *structural equation modeling* [e.g., @della2021relevance; @lee2021distinctive; @renner2022understanding;  @richter2020predictors] "important" factors are identified with a regression-based method, and the necessity of these and other factors is analyzed with NCA for identifying whether the factors are necessary or not (see section \@ref(ncaregression)). When NCA is used in combination with *Qualitative Comparative Analysis- QCA* [e.g., @torres2021levels; @kopplin2021equifinal] the results of NCA are compared to the sufficient configurations that are identified by QCA (see section \@ref(ncaqca)).  -->
<!-- In the second option, the researcher starts the study with a combined *necessity and generic causal theory*. This is a complex 'embedded necessity theory' (see Chapter \@ref(theory)) that consists of both necessity and generic causal relations from the start. Some concepts may only have a generic causal but not a necessity relationship with the outcome; other concepts may be a necessary cause but not a generic cause, and yet others may be both a necessary cause and a generic cause. Embedded necessity theories that combine necessity and generic causal theorizing for describing relations are still rare. In QCA, most studies theorize from the perspective of sufficiency and test the potential necessity of the single factors, but do not include necessity theorizing from the start. Also, most studies that combine NCA with multiple regression or structural equation modeling do not theorize about necessity and generic causation in combination [for an exception see @dul2019physical]. Testing embedded necessity theories requires a multimethod approach with both regression-based method and a necessity-based method. -->
<!-- When combining NCA with regression analysis or QCA, the order of conducting NCA and the other method is not relevant. When NCA is used in combination with structural equation modeling (SEM) the order matters. First SEM is applied followed by NCA. The reason is that the outcome of the SEM measurement model is used to define the constructs to be tested for necessity with NCA. -->
<!-- ## Formulate the necessary condition hypothesis {#formulate} -->
<!-- NCA starts with a theoretical notion that a necessity relation may exist between $X$ (the potential condition) and $Y$ (the outcome). This is usually done by formulating and justifying a hypothesis that is part of a theory (see Chapter \@ref(theory)). -->
<!-- NCA is mainly used in theory-testing research, which starts with formulating the theory and proceeds with empirical testing the theory with data. Hence, theory formulation comes before data collection and analysis. This is also the focus in this book. However, NCA can also be used in theory-building and exploratory research. Then formulating the theory is based on the results of the data analysis, and thus comes after it [e.g., @stek2021train]. -->
<!-- ## Collect the data {#collect} -->
<!-- Collecting data in NCA is not different from collecting data in general. The goal of data collection is to have scores (values, levels) for the condition $X$ and the outcome $Y$ for each case. The selected research design (e.g., 'experiment', 'survey', 'case study') must meet common quality standards. Also the selection of cases for measurement and data analysis must fit the goal of the research (e.g., random sampling, purposive sampling for specific reasons). The data must be 'good'. This means that the data must be valid (the measurement scores reflect what they are intended to reflect) and reliable (when measurement is repeated, the results are the same).  -->
<!-- NCA has no new requirements on collecting the data. There are a few exceptions. First, the setup of a 'necessity experiment' is different than the setup of a 'sufficiency experiment' or a common 'average treatment effect experiment' (see section \@ref(experiment)). Second, in certain situations it is possible to sample just a single case to perform NCA (see section \@ref(samplesize)). Third, the way that NCA is conducted may differ depending on the types of data that are used:  quantitative data (section \@ref(quantitative)), qualitative data (section \@ref(qualitative)), longitudinal data (section \@ref(longitudinal)), and set membership scores (section \@ref(setmembership)). Furthermore, the identification of potential outliers is partly different from the common way of identifying outliers (see section \@ref(outliers)).  -->


<!-- ## \textcolor{gray}{analyze the data}  -->
<!-- ## <span style="color: #D3D3D3">analyze the data</span> {#analyze} -->
<!-- under construction -->

<!-- ## analyze the data {#dataanalysissummary} -->
<!-- Data analysis is at the core of NCA. NCA's data analysis assumes that it makes theoretically sense to analyze the data with necessity logic (see section \@ref(formulate)) and that the data are meaningful (see section \@ref(collect)). For a quantitative necessary condition analysis the 'scatter plot' approach can be used. The scatter plot maps cases in the XY plane and NCA conducts a bivariate analysis on the scatter plot. -->


<!--chapter:end:010-introductionJO.Rmd-->

# Part I. Principles {-}

Part I of the book about Principles presents the theoretical background and assumptions of NCA. Understanding the principles of NCA is important for recognizing the method's possibilities and limitations. It is also essential for avoiding unrealistic expectations and misinterpretations.

The first chapter (Chapter \@ref(causality)) discusses NCA's causality perspective and how it is different from conventional perspectives. The next chapter (Chapter \@ref(theory)) discusses what a necessity theory is. The subsequent two chapters present the mathematical (Chapter \@ref(mathematics)) and statistical (Chapter \@ref(statistics)) backgrounds of NCA. The final chapter (Chapter \@ref(sensitivityspecificity)) evaluates NCA's credibility for identifying necessary conditions from data.

# Causality {#causality}

## Summary of this chapter {#summarycausality}

NCA uses a causal perspective that is (partly) different from common views on causality. This chapter presents NCA's causal perspective by comparing it to other causal perspectives. The chapter begins by discussing what a cause is (Section \@ref(whatisacause)), followed by a discussion of the commonly used sufficiency causality (Section \@ref(sufficiencycausality)). Next, necessity causality is discussed (Section \@ref(necessitycausality)). For both types of causality, three perspectives are distinguished: deterministic, probabilistic, and typicality (deterministic with exceptions). NCA uses the deterministic (or typicality) necessity causal perspective. In the next section (Section \@ref(causalversusconditionallogic)), NCA's use of conditional logic (assuming a causal direction) is compared with the conventional view on conditional logic (in which no causal direction is assumed). Subsequently, another unique feature of NCA is discussed: the concept of necessary condition in degree (NiD), which extends the conventional binary approach (absence or presence of $X$ is necessary for absence or presence of $Y$) to a continuous approach: level $x$ of $X$ is necessary for level $y$ of $Y$ (Section \@ref(binarytocontinuous)). Finally, Section \@ref(underdetermination) discusses that, due to causal underdetermination, no causal perspective is superior to another.

## What is a cause? {#whatisacause}
Causality is often loosely defined as the *influence* of a cause $X$ on an effect $Y$. General expressions like $X$ has an effect on $Y$, $X$ affects $Y$, or $X$ contributes to $Y$ without specifying the type of causality are commonplace. Causality is a complex subject that is extensively debated in the philosophy of science literature and elsewhere. The Scottish philosopher David Hume (1711-1776) laid the foundation for modern discussions about causation. Hume defined a cause as follows:

>“… we may define a cause to be an object, follow’d by another, and where all the objects, similar to the first, are follow’d by objects, similar to the second: Or in other words, where, if the first object had not been, the second never had existed.” [@hume1756essays, p. 121].

The first part of this definition refers to *sufficiency* causality (if $X$, then $Y$).^[If $X$, then $Y$ means: if we see $X$, we also see $Y$, and if we add $X$, we get $Y$.] It includes a specification of the temporal order: $Y$ follows $X$, and a generalization: the statement applies also to "similar objects". The second part of the definition refers to *necessity* causality (if not $X$, then not $Y$)^[If not $X$, then not $Y$ means: if we do not see $X$, we also do not see $Y$, and if we remove $X$, we do not see $Y$ anymore.] although without the generalization [for a further discussion see @dul2024different]. The two types of causality are illustrated in Figure \@ref(fig:cornerssufficiencynecessity), showing observed $XY$ patterns where $X$ is the condition and $Y$ is the outcome that can have two values (absent/present).^[Causality itself cannot be observed, see Section \@ref(underdetermination) and Chapter \@ref(hypothesisformulation).] When $X$ is a sufficient cause for $Y$ (Figure \@ref(fig:cornerssufficiencynecessity)-left), observations (points, cases) can exist in all corners except corner 4: If $X$ is present, then $Y$ is present. When $X$ is a *necessary* cause for $Y$ (Figure \@ref(fig:cornerssufficiencynecessity)-right), observations can exist in all corners except in corner 1: If $X$ is absent, then $Y$ is absent. The corner where observations are not possible is called the *empty space* and the corner where observations are possible the *feasible area*.

<!-- ^[When $X$ is *necessary and sufficient* for $Y$ cases are possible in corners 2 and 3 but not in corners 1 and 4.]   -->

```{r cornerssufficiencynecessity, echo=FALSE, fig.show="hold", out.width='45%', fig.cap="$XY$-tables illustrating  sufficiency causality (Left) and necessity causality (Right) with binary concepts. Gray = observations (points, cases) are possible. White = no observations are possible."}

 #par(mar = c(4, 4, .1, .1))
 par(mfrow = c(1,2))
 knitr::include_graphics("sufficiency.png")
 knitr::include_graphics("necessity.png")
```


## Sufficiency causality {#sufficiencycausality}

Most phenomena in the social, biomedical and other sciences are studied from the perspective of sufficiency causality (if $X$, then $Y$). When people talk about a cause or causality they (almost always) implicitly mean sufficiency causality.
<!-- For example, when regression-based statistical methods are used to infer causality, sufficiency causality is implicit and the type of causality is probabilistic: if $X$, then probably $Y$.  Then a cause is a 'contributory cause' that has an 'average effect' on the outcome. Next the common use of probabilistic sufficiency in regression-based statistical methods for causal inference,  -->
<!-- A third type of sufficiency, introduced in physics and not yet explicitly used in the social sciences, is the typicality perspective on causality. This perspective is non-probabilistic but allows exceptions.     -->

Three fundamentally different perspectives on sufficiency causality can be distinguished: deterministic, probabilistic, and typicality [@dul2024different]. Table \@ref(tab:causalitysuf) summarizes these perspectives and lists some main methods that (implicitly) use this perspective when inferring causality.

```{r causalitysuf, echo = FALSE, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE, include=TRUE, cache=TRUE}

library("kableExtra")
caussuf <- read.csv("causalitySUF.csv")

words <- c("always", "probably", "typically")

caussuf[] <- lapply(caussuf, function(x) {
  if (!is.character(x)) return(x)
  for (w in words) {
    x <- gsub(w, paste0("\\emph{", w, "}"), x, fixed = TRUE)
  }
  x
})

csuf <- kbl(caussuf, 
    col.names = gsub("[.]", " ", names(caussuf)),
    caption = "Perspectives on sufficiency causality: if $X$, then ... $Y$.",
    booktabs = TRUE,
    longtable = FALSE,
    align = c("l"),
    escape = FALSE
    ) %>%
    kableExtra::kable_styling(latex_options = "HOLD_position")
footnote(csuf, c("QCA = Qualitative Comparative Analysis", "SCC = Sufficient Component Cause model", "NESS = Necessary Element of a Sufficient Set", "LR = Logistic (Logit) Regression" ,"MLR = Multiple Linear Regression ", "SEM = Structural Equation Modeling"),fixed_small_size = TRUE)
```

### Deterministic sufficiency {#deterministicsufficiency}
Hume's sufficiency definition has been interpreted as being about deterministic causality: if $X$, then *always* $Y$ [e.g., @baumgartner2009inferring]. Many phenomena in the physical world may be described by such a *deterministic sufficiency causal perspective*. For example, reaching the speed of sound produces a sonic boom. In the social sciences, single deterministic sufficiency causes are rare, as normally several factors together produce an outcome. Such a sufficiency perspective is used in 'parts-whole' methods and frameworks [@varzi2019mereology; @machamer2000thinking], for example, in the Sufficient-Component Cause model in medicine [SCC; @rothman1976causes], the Necessary Element of a Sufficient Set approach in law [NESS, @wright1985causation], and Qualitative Comparative Analysis in social sciences [QCA; @ragin1987comparative; @campbell2026tackling], use such a sufficiency perspective. The combination of causal factors (called configurations in QCA) is considered deterministically sufficient for the outcome: If [combination of $X$'s], then $Y$. Several combinations may be sufficient ('equifinality'), such that none of these configurations is necessary for the outcome. The causal factors that are parts of the configurations are called INUS conditions: Insufficient but Necessary parts of Unnecessary but Sufficient configurations [@mackie1965causes]. INUS conditions should not be confused with necessary conditions; INUS conditions are 'locally' necessary for the configuration to produce the outcome, and usually not overall necessary for the outcome itself [@dul2021necessary]. NCA considers only overall necessary conditions for the outcome. Although the theoretical foundations of SCC, NESS and QCA are based on a deterministic ontology, in practice the determinism may be imperfect.^[See for example QCA's use of the consistency score < 1 to allow imperfect sufficiency.] Deterministic sufficiency means that corner 4 of Figure \@ref(fig:cornerssufficiencynecessity)-left remains empty.

### Probabilistic sufficiency {#probabilisticsufficiency}
As single deterministic sufficiency causes are rare in the social sciences, a non-deterministic causal approach is often adopted: probabilistic sufficiency: 'if $X$, then *probably* $Y$', or 'if $X$, then *likely* $Y$'. Pearl states that:
```{=tex}
\vspace{3\baselineskip}
```

 
> “According to one of the basic tenets of probabilistic causality, a cause should raise the probability of the effect.” [@pearl2009causality, p. 254]

The dominant framework of the *probabilistic sufficiency* causal perspective focuses on the average effects of 'parts' of the whole. The lower-left corner is not empty but is less populated than the other cells. It identifies the Average Causal Effect (ACE) or Average Treatment Effect (ATE) of a causal factor. The approach is currently mainstream in quantitative studies using statistical methods. Statistical models are based on the assumption of the probability distributions of the cause and the effect. The effect is produced by an unknown 'data generation process' that describes the underlying relationships between concepts and the stochastic processes involved in generating the observed data. When statistical models with regression-based methods are used to infer causality, the underlying causal perspective is probabilistic sufficiency. Examples of regression-based methods that are commonly used for inferring causality are multiple linear regression (MLR), logistic (logit) regression, and structural equation modeling (SEM). Probabilistic sufficiency may be inferred if the lower-right corner of $XY$-tables or $XY$-plots is less densely populated with observations than the other corners (Figures \@ref(fig:cornerssufficiencynecessity) and \@ref(fig:dichdisconxyplottotfig)).
<!-- ^[See for example the definition of the odds ratio in a probabilistic sufficiency experiment, Section \@ref(traditionalexperiment).] -->

### Typicality sufficiency {#typicalitysufficiency}
A recently developed non-deterministic and non-probabilistic perspective on causality is the 'typicality' perspective. The *typicality sufficiency causal perspective* was introduced in physics, including quantum mechanics and thermodynamics [e.g., @goldstein2012typicality; @wilhelm2022typical]. It challenges the deterministic ontology but does not adopt a probabilistic ontology. The typicality interpretation also gets attention in other scientific disciplines, including the social sciences [@wagner2020typicality]. This perspective can be described as deterministic with exceptions. It accepts the perspective if $X$, then $Y$ but fundamentally allows exceptions: if $X$, then *typically* $Y$, or if $X$, then *almost always* $Y$. The typicality perspective does not describe exceptions in terms of likelihood of occurrence, but in terms of cardinality [@wilhelm2022typical]: the number of elements that deviates from the majority of the elements in the set. Typicality sufficiency means that corner 4 of Figure \@ref(fig:cornerssufficiencynecessity)-left remains empty, but a few observations may be exceptions. 

<!-- The three different causal sufficiency perspectives and some contemporary methods that rely on them when inferring causality are summarized in Table \@ref(tab:causalitysuf). Although in practice methods like QCA may also accept exceptions (see for example the use of QCA's 'consistency' scores < 1), in discussions about QCA's ontology e.g., usually a deterministic and not a typicality underlying causal perspective is assumed. -->

<!-- Hume’s original definition of a cause is often cited as the foundation in current (sufficiency) thinking about causality. Hume’s 1748 version of this definition reads as follows: -->

<!-- >“… we may define a Cause to be an Object, follow’d by another, and where all the Objects, similar to the first, are follow’d by Objects, similar to the second.” [@hume1748enquiry, p. 124]. -->

<!-- In this definition Objects are characteristics of a phenomenon of interest (an event, a process). The characteristic can be a cause ($X$) or an effect ($Y$). The first part of the sentence states that the cause $X$ is followed by the effect $Y$ and the second part states that this is regularly observed in different cases, which is a generalization. This ‘regularity theory’ of causation [@andreas2021regularity] assumes that $X$ ‘produces’, ‘ensures’, or ‘guarantees’ $Y$. In other words: $X$ is a sufficient cause of Y. Hume’s definition can therefore be summarized as *if $X$, then $Y$*, see equation \@ref(eq:nec0), assuming a causal direction^[Hume requires the temporal order that the cause $X$ precedes the effect $Y$. Originally, Hume also required a spatiotemporal connection and a constant conjunction between $X$ and $Y$, but these requirements were dropped in later versions of his work.]. -->

<!-- Hume focused on single causal factors. Because often multiple factors produce an effect, particularly in the social sciences, Hume’s regularity theory was further developed to accommodate combinations of factors that are sufficient for the effect [e.g., @mill1843system; @mackie1965causes]. In such regularity interpretation, ‘if $X$, then $Y$’ means ‘if combination of factors ($X_1$, $X_2$, …) then $Y$’. The aforementioned non-statistical approaches and methods [@rothman1976causes; @wright1985causation; @ragin1987comparative; @baumgartner2009inferring] are based on regularity theory of causation and study combinations of causal factors that are sufficient for the effect. -->

<!-- A regularity theory of causation is often interpreted as being deterministic: *if $X$, then **always** $Y$*. -->

<!-- The deterministic interpretation of Hume’s regularity theory of causation is often criticized. For example, in the deterministic sufficiency perspective, smoking is **not** a cause of long cancer because not all smokers develop long cancer [@hitchcock2021probabilistic]. As a reaction to this deterministic view of causality, indeterministic causal theories have been developed. -->
<!-- The most common indeterministic theory of causality is the probabilistic theory of causation. In the *probabilistic sufficiency perspective* the sufficiency relationship is formulated as a probability: *if $X$, then **probably** $Y$*. In this perspective, smoking is a sufficient cause of long cancer because smokers have a high probability of developing long cancer. In the probabilistic sufficiency perspective phenomena encompass randomness and are driven by a ‘data generation process’ (DGP) with a probability distribution. The use of regression-based methods or average treatment effect approaches for inferring causality [@pearl2009causality; @angrist1996identification] imply the probabilistic view on causality. This view is now so pervasive and implicit that sufficiency propositions and hypotheses formulated deterministically (if $X$, then $Y$) are often interpreted probabilistically (if $X$, then probably $Y$) and subsequently tested using methods such as regression analysis. The utilization of these methods to infer causality implies the use of a probabilistic causal lens on sufficiency. -->

<!-- Another indeterministic perspective pertinent to a `typicality’ view of causation was recently presented in physics including in quantum mechanics and in thermodynamics [e.g., @goldstein2012typicality; @wilhelm2022typical]. This *typicality sufficiency perspective* is gaining traction in other scientific disciplines, among which the social sciences [@wagner2020typicality]. The typicality theory of causation accepts the regularity ‘if $X$, then $Y$’ but allows for *exceptions* expressed as: $X$ typically produces $Y$: *if $X$, then **typically** $Y$*. It describes that $X$ almost always produces $Y$. The typicality perspective on causality differs from the probabilistic perspective since it does not count on phenomena being described in terms of DGP's and probability distributions. Typicality defines exceptions in terms of cardinality [@wilhelm2022typical], which refers to the number of elements in a set, whereas probability defines exceptions is terms of likelihood of occurrence. The typicality perspective can be interpreted as ‘deterministic with exceptions’. For example, smoking typically causes long cancer but there are exceptions.Table \@ref(tab:causalitysuf) summarizes the different sufficiency perspectives. The table also includes a list of methods that employ a particular sufficiency perspective. Although it is not always clear how the aforementioned non-statistical approaches can be inked to theoryies of causation, Table \@ref(tab:causalitysuf) positionens them as being linked to the regularity theory of causation. For example, QCA is often explicitly referred to as having a regularity perspective on causality [e.g., @rohlfing2021check; @mahoney2022regularity], with a deterministic ontology [@pula2021logico; @rutten2021uncertainty], although "the current state of the scientific debate does not warrant a conclusive attribution of QCA to one of the existing theories of causation [@mello2021qualitative, p. 74]^[When these methods are applied in practice they often accommodate exceptions, without relying on DGP's and probability distributions. Therefore these methods could also be positioned as having a typicality perspective on causality. Nevertheless, Table \@ref(tab:causalitysuf) does not list the regularity-based approaches as typicality methods because proponents have not positioned them that way.]. The regression-based statistical methods like Multiple Linear Regression (MLR), Covariance-based Structural Equation Modeling (SEM) and Partial Least Squares Structural Equation Modeling (PLS-SEM) are positioned as methods for inferring probabilistic sufficiency causality, as they are commonly used for that purpose (e.g., for testing probabilistic sufficiency hypotheses). -->


<!-- This clarification should help to ensure theory-method fit: using a proper method for investigating a theory with a certain causality perspective. For example, a theory that assumes probabilistic sufficiency causality (most theories in quantitative research) could be tested with a regression-based method but not with NCA. Similarly, a theory that is based on necessity causality could be tested with NCA but not with a regression-based method. Theory-method fit is an essential element of research quality. -->

## Necessity causality {#necessitycausality}
The three types of perspectives on causality can also be applied to necessity causality. This is shown in Table \@ref(tab:causalitynec). 

### Deterministic necessity {#deterministicnecessity}
The second sentence of Hume's definition of causality can be considered a deterministic view on necessity causality [@goertz2012tale]. If $X$ does not occur, $Y$ will not happen. Whereas single sufficient causes are seldom on their own sufficient for an effect, single necessity causes alone can stop an effect from occurring if absent. For example, oxygen is necessary for human life but not sufficient on its own. There are multiple necessity causes for human life, and each will stop the outcome from occurring if it is absent. Deterministic necessity means that corner 1 of Figure \@ref(fig:cornerssufficiencynecessity)-right remains empty.

### Probabilistic necessity {#probabilisticnecessity}
The probabilistic necessity perspective can be phrased as: If not $X$, then probably not $Y$. Probabilistic necessity means that corner 1 of Figure \@ref(fig:cornerssufficiencynecessity)-right may have observations but is less densely populated than the other corners.
<!-- \textcolor{red}{[CHECK]} -->

### Typicality necessity {#typicalitynecessity}
The idea of typicality was developed for sufficiency causality but can also be applied to necessity causality [@dul2024different]. It accepts a deterministic perspective with exceptions: if not $X$, then *typically* not $Y$, or if not $X$, then *almost always* not $Y$. Again, the typicality perspective does not describe exceptions in terms of probabilities but in terms of cardinality. Typicality necessity means that corner 1 of Figure \@ref(fig:cornerssufficiencynecessity)-right remains empty, but a few observations may be exceptions. Exceptions are rare without quantifying what is "rare". If an exception is not an error, it could be a unique counterexample (e.g., an innovative case), worth studying in detail. However, the phenomenon of interest is still described with deterministic necessity, excluding the exception.^[Note the difference between Exception and Noise as discussed in Section \@ref(purity).] Note that NCA has a deterministic view on causality and that the ceiling line is a sharp border between an area with cases and an area without cases (Chapter \@ref(causality)). The presence of outliers (in the "empty" space) indicates that necessity does not exist, rather than that necessity exists with high probability. 

With a deterministic view on necessity causality, a case with high $Y$ and low $X$ rejects necessity. The data point may be mistakenly in the empty area because of measurement error or sampling or case selection error. Such errors may be corrected, or the case may be removed from further consideration if the error cannot be repaired. However, if the case is not an error it is a special case: the rare exceptions may stay in the  otherwise empty space, representing another phenomenon not captured by necessity. Only the presence of *such* exception may be the reason for adopting the typicality perspective. NCA keeps its deterministic approach, but allows rare exceptions. Having many outlier cases in the "empty" space that cannot be explained by errors is a reason to reject necessity (or to redefine the formal hypothesis, see Chapter \@ref(hypothesisformulation) if these cases have a common characteristic), but is not a reason to accept necessity with a lenient view on (typicality) necessity.


<!-- Hume's original definition of a cause, as presented in his 1748 publication, remained unchanged through several revisions of his work in the eight years between 1748 and 1756. However, in 1756 Hume introduced a new definition [@hume1756essays] and preserved it in all later editions until the final 1777 edition of this work [@hume1777enquiry]. The final edition is a culmination of 35 years of editorial changes and Hume considered this work to be ‘my philosophy’ [@fieser2001introduction]. The new definition about a cause reads: -->

<!-- >“… we may define a cause to be an object, follow’d by another, and where all the objects, similar to the first, are follow’d by objects, similar to the second: Or in other words, where, if the first object had not been, the second never had existed.” [@hume1756essays, p. 121]. -->

<!-- Apart from capitalization, the first sentence is the same as the first sentence of the 1748 definition. However, Hume added another sentence: -->
<!-- “*… if the first object had not been, the second never had existed.*” -->
<!-- This sentence can be summarized as: "if not $X$, then not $Y$". -->
<!-- Hume did not extensively elaborate on the second sentence. It has been interpreted as a counterfactual view on causality [@lewis1974causation; @menzies2020counterfactual]: If $X$ had not occurred, $Y$ would not have occurred. The counterfactual phrase captures the notion of a necessity cause. This means that an effect cannot exist without the necessary condition: If not $X$, then not $Y$ [@mill1843system; @mackie1965causes; @goertz2002necessary; @goertz2012tale]. -->
<!-- Whereas single sufficient causes are seldom on their own sufficient for an effect, single necessity causes alone can stop an effect to occur if absent. For example, internet is certainly not sufficient on its own for having an online meeting. Also a computer device, software, a scheduled time, a motivation to attend, and many other factors must be present to have an online meeting. However, without internet no online meeting will occur. There may be multiple necessity causes, but each individual necessary condition will stop the outcome to occur if it is absent. -->

<!-- In the new definition, Hume connected the first sentence with the second, by using the phrase “*Or in other words, …*”.  This has puzzled scholars [e.g., @millican2007introduction; @nadathur2020causal] because the causal meaning of the first sentence (sufficiency: if $X$, then $Y$) differs from the causal meaning of the second sentence (necessity: If not $X$, then not $Y$).  -->


```{r causalitynec, echo = FALSE, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE, include=TRUE, cache=TRUE}

library("kableExtra")
causnec <- read.csv("causalityNEC.csv")

words <- c("always", "probably", "typically")

causnec[] <- lapply(causnec, function(x) {
  if (!is.character(x)) return(x)
  for (w in words) {
    x <- gsub(w, paste0("\\emph{", w, "}"), x, fixed = TRUE)
  }
  x
})

cnec <- kbl(causnec, 
            col.names = gsub("[.]", " ", names(causnec)),
            caption = "Perspectives on necessity causality: if not $X$, then ... not $Y$.",
            booktabs = TRUE,
            longtable = FALSE, #avoid splitting the table
            align = c("l"),
            escape = FALSE
            ) %>% 
            
    kableExtra::kable_styling(latex_options = "HOLD_position")
    footnote(cnec, c(
      "NCA = Necessary Condition Analysis",
      "QCA = Qualitative Comparative Analysis (only in kind; typicality implicit)",
      "PN = Probability of Necessity (only binary variables; no generic necessity)" 
      ),fixed_small_size = TRUE)

```

Table \@ref(tab:causalitynec) maps different methods on the three causal perspectives. It shows that NCA can be used for the deterministic and typicality perspectives. The table includes also QCA for identifying necessity using a deterministic or typicality perspective. Although QCA's main focus is on sufficiency, and QCA studies often ignore necessity, QCA can also identify necessity. A necessity analysis of QCA is less detailed than that of NCA, as it only identifies *necessity-in-kind* (NiK)^[Necessity-in-kind (NiK) refers to the qualitative statement that $X$ is necessary for $Y$ without specifying the level of $X$ and $Y$ other than presence/absence or low/high. This contrasts NCA's necessity-in-degree (NiD) where specific levels of $X$ and $Y$ are specified.] and often misses necessary conditions [@torres2021levels]. The differences between necessity approaches of NCA and QCA are explained in @dul2016jbr and @vis2018analyzing and summarized in Section \@ref(ncaqca). The table also mentions Pearl's Probability of Necessity (PN) approach for identifying probabilistic necessity using a counterfactual analysis. @pearl2009causality introduced the Probability of Necessity (PN) concept (where $X$ and $Y$ are binary) to address the question: “Was event $X$ necessary for event $Y$ to occur?” PN is the probability that $Y$ would not have occurred if $X$ had not occurred, given that both $X$ and $Y$ actually occurred.^[Formally, PN is defined as: $PN = P(Y_{X=0} = 0 \mid X = 1, Y = 1)$.] This is a counterfactual probability conditioned on the actual world where $X$ and $Y$ happened. PN evaluates a hypothetical world where $X$ did not happen. PN is used for identifying necessity causes at individual level, for example in legal reasoning, where the question is not whether in general $X$ is a probabilistic cause of $Y$, but rather whether $X$ was the necessity cause of $Y$ in a specific situation that has happened. PN assumes that $X$ and $Y$ are binary.
<!-- Although the main focus of QCA is on sufficiency, the method can also identify necessary conditions. QCA's necessity analysis is listed under the regularity perspective because proponents of QCA position QCA as a regularity-based method^[QCA's necessity analysis is limited to necessity 'in kind', see Section \@ref(ncaqca). For details about the differences between NCA and the necessity analysis of QCA see @dul2016jbr and @vis2018analyzing.]. -->

<!-- the main focus of QCA is on sufficients may also accept exceptions (see for example the use of QCA's 'consistency' scores < 1), in discussions about QCA's ontology e.g., usually a deterministic and not a typicality underlying causal perspective is assumed. -->

<!-- The different causal perspectives for sufficiency causality (regularity, probabilistic and typicality) can also be applied to necessity causality as is done in Table \@ref(tab:causalitynec).  -->

<!-- Although Hume's second sentence is not followed by a regularity statement, and the 'regularity theory of causation' usually refers to sufficiency,  this sentence has been interpreted as a necessity *regularity* theory of causation [@goertz2012tale]. In the deterministic interpretation of regularity, the necessity causality can be described as: *if not $X$, then **always** not $Y$*.   -->
<!-- In the *probabilistic* perspective on necessity causality, the necessity relationship is formulated as a probability: *if not $X$, then **probably** not $Y$* The idea of probabilistic necessity has been developed [e.g., @pearl2009causality], but it is not often applied. -->
<!-- Also the *typicality* theory of causation can be applied to necessity causality: *if not $X$, then **typically** not $Y$*. It describes that without $X$ almost always $Y$ will not occur, but there may be exceptions that $Y$ will occur without $X$.  -->

<!-- NCA can be used with a deterministic *regularity* perspective and an indeterministic *typicality* perspective. With a deterministic regularity perspective, the absence of a single necessity cause will always stop the outcome to exist. Some phenomena may be described with a deterministic regularity perspective. For example, oxygen is always necessary for humans being alive. However, other phenomena may be described with a indeterministic typicality perspective allowing exceptions to the rule. For example, an internet connection is necessary for an online meeting, but online meetings (exceptionally) can also take place by using technologies that do not rely on an internet connection. For instance, utilizing a Local Area Network (LAN) network can enable individuals to engage in online meetings without an internet connection. These exceptions challenge the deterministic understanding of necessary conditions by demonstrating that the absence of the condition does not always lead to the absence of the outcome. Since, in particular in the social science exceptions to the rule are possible, NCA is positioned as having the typicality perspective. NCA's typically view does not *require* that exceptions exist, but only allows them to exist when they do exist. NCA does not have a probabilistic causal perspective on necessity as described above as it does not rely on assumptions about a Data Generation Process and probability distributions^[In earlier publications when I was not yet aware of the typicality perspective on causality, I named NCA's indeterministic perspective a 'probabilistic view' on necessity. However, in this book I abandon this term for NCA because  I reserve it for the causality perspective that assumes a description of a phenomenon by a Data Generation Process and probability functions.], although NCA uses certain statistical techniques to assist in identifying necessary conditions from data (see Chapter \@ref(statistical)). Table \@ref(tab:causalitynec) lists  NCA under the typicality perspective. The table also lists QCA. Although the main focus of QCA is on sufficiency, the method can also identify necessary conditions. QCA's necessity analysis is listed under the regularity perspective because proponents of QCA position QCA as a regularity-based method^[QCA's necessity analysis is limited to necessity 'in kind', see Section \@ref(ncaqca). For details about the differences between NCA and the necessity analysis of QCA see @dul2016jbr and @vis2018analyzing.]. -->



<!-- This chapter explains necessity causality as used in NCA. It is based on conventional conditional logic and extends this logic with a causal interpretation, while allowing an indeterministic view on causality.  -->

## Causal versus conditional logic {#causalversusconditionallogic}
NCA extends conditional logic with causality. In conditional logic, as used in philosophy and mathematics, relationships are described in terms of if-then statements. In the statement 'if $A$, then $B$', $A$ is the antecedent and $B$ is the consequent, and $A$ and $B$ usually have only two values: true or false. The truth or falsity of $B$ depends on the truth or falsity of $A$. In conditional logic, 'if $A$, then $B$' means that $A$ implies $B$, or if $A$ occurs, then $B$ occurs. This can be interpreted as $A$ being a *sufficient condition* for $B$. For example, being born in Italy ($A$) implies having Italian citizenship ($B$). This can be written as:

\begin{equation} 
(\#eq:nec0)
A_s \Rightarrow B 
\end{equation}

Here the subscript '$s$' emphasizes the interpretation of $A$ as a sufficient condition, and the double arrow $\Rightarrow$ means 'implies'.

In conditional logic, the statement that $A$ is a *necessary condition* for $B$ implies that $B$ can only be true if $A$ is true: if $B$, then $A$. For example, oxygen is necessary for human life because being alive implies that the person has access to oxygen. Therefore, truth of $B$ (being alive) implies the truth of $A$ (oxygen). This can be written as:

\begin{equation} 
(\#eq:nec2)
B \Rightarrow A_n 
\end{equation}

where the subscript '$n$' emphasizes the necessary condition interpretation of $A$. This can be rewritten as:

\begin{equation} 
(\#eq:nec1)
A_n \Leftarrow B 
\end{equation}

where the double arrow $\Leftarrow$ means "is implied by". 

Because $B$ can only be true when $A$ is true, the falsity of $A$ implies the falsity of $B$. This can be written as:

\begin{equation} 
(\#eq:nec3)
\neg A_n \Rightarrow \neg B
\end{equation}

or

\begin{equation} 
(\#eq:nec4)
\neg B \Leftarrow \neg A_n
\end{equation}

where the symbol $\neg$ indicates negation, or 'no' or 'not'. 

Equations \@ref(eq:nec2) - \@ref(eq:nec4) are four equivalent expressions for $A_n$ being necessary for $B$ from the perspective of conditional logic. 

The phrase $B$ implies $A_n$ \@ref(eq:nec2) can also be understood as $B$ is *sufficient* for $A_n$. Therefore, the expression $A_n$ is necessary for $B$ can be written in four equivalent ways:

1. $A_n$ is necessary for $B$ (oxygen is necessary for being alive). 

2. $B$ is sufficient for $A_n$ (being alive is sufficient for oxygen).

3. \neg $A_n$ is sufficient for \neg $B$ (no oxygen is sufficient for not being alive).

4. \neg $B$ is necessary for \neg $A_n$ (not being alive is necessary for no oxygen).

A major difference between necessity in *causal logic* as used in NCA, and necessity logic as used in *conditional logic* is that NCA assumes a causal direction between $A_n$ and $B$ whereas conditional logic does *not* assume such a causal direction. This means that NCA assumes a temporal order: first $A_n$, followed by $B$. Conditional logic is ignorant about whether there is first oxygen and then life or first life and then oxygen. Therefore, NCA's causal logic can be seen as an extension of conditional logic where a causal direction is assumed between $A_n$ and $B$. This temporal order excludes the possibility of 'first $B$, then $A_n$'. The assumption of a temporal order may be justified when first the occurrence of $A_n$ is observed and afterwards the occurrence of $B$, or that it is theoretically implausible that $A_n$ occurs after $B$ (see Section \@ref(causalexplanation) for a further discussion on temporality). It means that when NCA formulates that $A_n$ is a *necessary condition* for $B$, it is assumed that $A_n$ is a *causal* necessary condition for $B$. Assuming also no reverse or reciprocal causality (Section \@ref(reversecausality)), it implies that only two of the four conditional expressions are causal statements:

<!-- for HTML -->
<!-- <ol> -->
<!--  <li value="1">$A_n$ is a necessity cause of $B$ (oxygen is necessary for life).</li> -->
<!--  <li value="3">$\neg A_n$ is a sufficient cause of $\neg B$ (no oxygen is sufficient for no life).</li> -->
<!-- </ol> -->

\begin{enumerate}
\item[1.] $A_n$ is a necessity cause of $B$ (oxygen is necessary for life).
\item[3.] \neg $A_n$ is a sufficient cause of \neg $B$ (no oxygen is sufficient for no life).
\end{enumerate}

<!-- 1. $A_n$ is a necessity cause of $B$ (oxygen is necessary for life). -->

<!-- 3. \neg $A_n$ is a sufficient cause of \neg $B$ (no oxygen is sufficient for no life). -->

Therefore, in NCA the antecedent $A_n$ is considered the *necessity cause* of the consequent $B$ (the effect). Statements 2 and 4 still apply as logical statements, but not as causal statements, assuming no reverse or reciprocal causality.

To stress that NCA makes a causal assumption about the relationship between antecedent and consequent, the symbols $A$ and $B$ or $P$ and $Q$ that are common in conventional conditional logic, are replaced by the symbols $X$ and $Y$ to express the cause-effect relationship, where $X$ is the cause and $Y$ is the effect. Then the two equivalent causal expressions for a necessity cause are:

\begin{equation} 
(\#eq:necnec) 
X \text{ is necessary for } Y 
\end{equation}

\begin{equation} 
(\#eq:sufnec)
\neg X \text{ is sufficient for } \neg Y 
\end{equation} 

<!-- Since NCA considers necessity as (quasi-)deterministic [@dul2016orm], depending on the analyst's perspective, NCA can be applied with a deterministic or typicality perspective on necessity.  -->
In the remainder of this book, the expression '$X$ is a necessary condition for $Y$' implies that $X$ is a necessity cause of $Y$ and both expressions \@ref(eq:necnec) and \@ref(eq:sufnec) apply. @dul2016orm refers to expression \@ref(eq:necnec) as the 'necessity formulation' of the necessary condition and expression \@ref(eq:sufnec) as the 'sufficiency formulation' of the necessary condition. In this book the statement $X$ is necessary for $Y$ is understood in a deterministic or typicality causal perspective: '$X$ is always necessary for $Y$' or '$X$ is almost always/typically necessary for $Y$.'
