Consequently, all or nearly all observations with the outcome $Y$ do have the necessity cause $X$, and that all or nearly all observations that do not have the necessity cause $X$ do not have the outcome $Y$.^[Until recently, I was not aware of the existence of the typicality perspective on causality. What I previously called a 'probabilistic view' on necessity causality [@dul2020book] refers to what I now [@dul2024different] call the 'typicality perspective' of necessity causality (based on cardinality). The current description 'probabilistic perspective' of causality refers to the use of probabilities to describe causality, but this is not part of NCA.]

## From binary necessity to continuous necessity {#binarytocontinuous}
Another extension that NCA introduces to necessity logic involves the levels of condition $X$ and outcome $Y$. Until now, the book considered the levels of $X$ and $Y$ as binary. Only two values are possible: absent or present (like in Figure \@ref(fig:cornerssufficiencynecessity)-right), 0 or 1, or false or true, etc. This binary approach is common in conditional logic as well as in the SCC, NESS, and QCA methods and frameworks.^[Also in fuzzy set QCA (fsQCA) the necessary condition in the sufficiency solution is binary, see Section \@ref(ncaqca).]

NCA extends the binary levels of condition and outcome to multiple possible levels by allowing $X$ and $Y$ to be discrete concepts (with a number of finite levels of more than 2) or continuous concepts (with an infinite number of levels) within minimum and maximum *bounds* of $X$ and $Y$. This has several consequences. 

First, the $XY$-table is extended to an $XY$-plot as shown in Figure \@ref(fig:dichdisconxyplotfig). 

```{r dichdisconxyplotfig, echo=FALSE, fig.show="hold", out.width='30%', fig.align = "center", fig.cap="$XY$-plot with dichotomous (Left), discrete (Middle) and continuous (Right) values of condition $X$ and outcome $Y$."}

 par(mfrow = c(1,2))
 knitr::include_graphics("dichotomousXYplot.png")
 knitr::include_graphics("discreteXYplot.png")
 knitr::include_graphics("continuousXYplot.png")
```

Instead of mapping observations on the cells of the $XY$-table, observations are now mapped on a 2D Euclidean coordinate system. The bounds of $X$ and $Y$ are again 0 and 1, but could be any other minimum and maximum values. The bounds on $X$ and $Y$ create a *bounding box* (Section \@ref(formalexpression)). In a *dichotomous necessity relationship* where both the condition and the outcome are binary, the observations are mapped at corners of the $XY$-plot (Figure \@ref(fig:dichdisconxyplotfig)-left). When necessity applies, no observations are possible in the upper-left corner [0,1]. In a *discrete necessity relationship* observations can be on the intersections of a grid representing the possible discrete values of $X$ and $Y$ (Figure \@ref(fig:dichdisconxyplotfig)-middle), and in a *continuous necessity relationship* observations can exist anywhere in the unit square (Figure \@ref(fig:dichdisconxyplotfig)-right), but not in the upper-left corner. Combinations of dichotomous, discrete and continuous values are also possible in NCA. This is shown in Figure \@ref(fig:dichdisconxyplottotfig).


```{r dichdisconxyplottotfig, echo=FALSE, fig.show="hold", out.width='90%', fig.align = "center", fig.cap="$XY$-plot with combinations of dichotomous, discrete, and continuous values of condition $X$ and outcome $Y$. The upper-left corner is empty if the presence/high value of $X$ is necessary for the presence/high value of $Y$."}
 knitr::include_graphics("nineplots.png")
```



```{r dichotomousxyplotallcode, echo = FALSE, eval = FALSE, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE, include=FALSE, cache=TRUE}
#adapted 18 march 2025
#Figure 2.4; Dul, J. (2020). Conducting necessary condition analysis. London: Sage Publications.
par(mar=c(5,5,4,1))
# 9 figures arranged in 3 rows and 3 columns
par(mfrow=c(3,3))

#Figure 2.4A
#delete '#' in the next line to make a pdf
#pdf("Figure 2.4A.pdf", 6.5,5)  
X<- c(0.25, 0.75, 0.75) #2-2
Y<- c(0.25, 0.25, 0.75) #2-2
plot (X,Y, xlab="X", ylab="Y", xlim = c(0,1), ylim=c(0,1), pch=19, cex=2, axes = F, yaxs="i", xaxs="i", type="n") #3-3
axis(1, at = c(0,0.25,0.75,1.25), pos=0, tck=0, labels=rep(c("","0", "1", ""))) #X2
axis(2, at = c(0,0.25,0.75,1.25), pos=0, tck=0, labels=rep(c("","0", "1", ""))) #y2
abline(v=c(0.5,1), h=c(0.5,1)) #2-2
rect(0,0,0.5,0.5,  col = "grey") # 1-1
rect(0.5,0,1,0.5,  col = "grey") # 2-1
rect(0.5,0.5,1,1,  col = "grey") # 2-2
points(X,Y, pch=19, cex=2)
segments(0,0.5,0.5,0.5, lty=1, col="black", lwd = 4) #horizontal part 1 ceiling line
segments(0.5,0.5,0.5,1, lty=1, col="black", lwd = 4) #vertical part 1 ceiling line
#delete '#' in the next line to make a pdf
#dev.off()

#Figure 2.4B
#delete '#' in the next line to make a pdf
#pdf("Figure 2.4B.pdf", 6.5,5)
X<- c(0.25, 0.75, 1.25, 1.25) #3-2
Y<- c(0.25, 0.25, 0.25, 0.75) #3-2
plot (X,Y, xlab="X", ylab="Y", xlim = c(0,1.5), ylim=c(0,1), pch=19, cex=2, axes = F, yaxs="i", xaxs="i", type="n") #3-2
axis(1, at = c(0,0.25,0.75,1.25, 1.5), pos=0, tck=0, labels=rep(c("","0", "0.5", "1",""))) #X3
axis(2, at = c(0,0.25,0.75,1.25), pos=0, tck=0, labels=rep(c("","0", "1", ""))) #Y2
abline(v=c(0.5,1,1.5), h=c(0.5,1)) #3-2
rect(0,0,0.5,0.5,  col = "grey") # 1-1
rect(0.5,0,1,0.5,  col = "grey") # 2-1
rect(1,0, 1.5,0.5,  col = "grey") # 3-1
rect(1,0.5,1.5,1,  col = "grey") # 3-2
points(X,Y, pch=19, cex=2)
segments(0,0.5,1,0.5, lty=1, col="black", lwd = 4) #horizontal part 1 ceiling line
segments(1,0.5,1,1.5, lty=1, col="black", lwd = 4) #vertical part 1 ceiling line
#delete '#' in the next line to make a pdf
#dev.off()

#Figure 2.4C
#delete '#' in the next line to make a pdf
#pdf("Figure 2.4C.pdf", 6.5, 5) 
plot (X,Y, xlab="X", ylab="Y", xlim = c(0,1.5), ylim=c(0,1.), pch=19, cex=2, axes = F, yaxs="i", xaxs="i", type="n") #3-3
axis(1, at = c(0,0.6,1.5), pos=0, tck=0, labels=rep(c("0","Xc","1"))) #X=c
axis(2, at = c(0,0.25,0.75,1.25), pos=0, tck=0, labels=rep(c("","0", "1", ""))) #X=2
abline(h=c(0,0.5,1, 1.5), v=c(0, 1.5))
rect(0,0,1.5,0.5,  col = "grey") # 1-1
rect(0.6,0.5,1.5,1,  col = "grey") # 1-2
segments(0,0.5,0.6,0.5, lty=1, col="black", lwd = 4) #horizontal part 1 ceiling line
segments(0.6,0.5,0.6,1.5, lty=1, col="black", lwd = 4) #vertical part 1 ceiling line
segments(0.6,0, 0.6, 0.5, lty=3, lwd=2, col="dark grey") #dashed line for Yc2
#delete '#' in the next line to make a pdf
#dev.off()

#Figure 2.4
#delete '#' in the next line to make a pdf
#pdf("Figure 2.4D.pdf", 5,7.5) 
X<- c(0.25, 0.75, 0.75, 0.75) #2-3
Y<- c(0.25, 0.25, 0.75, 1.25) #2-3
plot (X,Y, xlab="X", ylab="Y", xlim = c(0,1), ylim=c(0,1.5), pch=19, cex=2, axes = F, yaxs="i", xaxs="i", type="n") #2-3
axis(1, at = c(0,0.25,0.75,1.25), pos=0, tck=0, labels=rep(c("","0", "1", ""))) #X2
axis(2, at = c(0,0.25,0.75,1.25, 1.5), pos=0, tck=0, labels=rep(c("","0", "0.5", "1", ""))) #Y3
abline(v=c(0.5,1), h=c(0.5,1, 1.5)) #2-3
rect(0,0,0.5,0.5,  col = "grey") # 1-1
rect(0.5,0,1,0.5,  col = "grey") # 2-1
rect(0.5,0.5,1,1,  col = "grey") # 2-2
rect(0.5,1,1,1.5,  col = "grey") # 2-3
points(X,Y, pch=19, cex=2)
segments(0,0.5,0.5,0.5, lty=1, col="black", lwd = 4) #horizontal part 1 ceiling line
segments(0.5,0.5,0.5,1.5, lty=1, col="black", lwd = 4) #vertical part 1 ceiling line
#delete '#' in the next line to make a pdf
#dev.off()

#Figure 2.4E
#delete '#' in the next line to make a pdf
#pdf("Figure 2.4E.pdf", 5,5)
X<- c(0.25, 0.75, 0.75, 1.25, 1.25, 1.25) #3-3
Y<- c(0.25, 0.25, 0.75, 0.25, 0.75, 1.25) #3-3
plot (X,Y, xlab="X", ylab="Y", xlim = c(0,1.5), ylim=c(0,1.5), pch=19, cex=2, axes = F, yaxs="i", xaxs="i", type="n") #3-3
axis(1, at = c(0,0.25,0.75,1.25, 1.5), pos=0, tck=0, labels=rep(c("","0", "0.5", "1",""))) #X3
axis(2, at = c(0,0.25,0.75,1.25, 1.5), pos=0, tck=0, labels=rep(c("","0", "0.5", "1", ""))) #Y3
abline(v=c(0.5,1,1.5), h=c(0.5,1, 1.5)) #3-3
rect(0,0,0.5,0.5,  col = "grey") # 1-1
rect(0.5,0,1,0.5,  col = "grey") # 2-1
rect(0.5,0.5,1,1,  col = "grey") # 2-2
rect(1,0, 1.5,0.5,  col = "grey") # 3-1
rect(1,0.5,1.5,1,  col = "grey") # 3-2
rect(1,1,1.5,1.5,  col = "grey") # 3-3
points(X,Y, pch=19, cex=2)
segments(0,0.5,0.5,0.5, lty=1, col="black", lwd = 4) #horizontal part 1 ceiling line
segments(0.5,0.5,0.5,1, lty=1, col="black", lwd = 4) #vertical part 1 ceiling line
segments(0.5,1,1.0,1, lty=1, col="black", lwd = 4) #horizontal part 2ceiling line
segments(1,1,1,1.5, lty=1, col="black", lwd = 4) #vertical part 1 ceiling line
#delete '#' in the next line to make a pdf
#dev.off()

#Figure 2.4F
#delete '#' in the next line to make a pdf
#pdf("Figure 2.4F.pdf", 6.5, 5) 
plot (X,Y, xlab="X", ylab="Y", xlim = c(0,1.5), ylim=c(0,1.5), pch=19, cex=2, axes = F, yaxs="i", xaxs="i", type="n") #3-3
axis(1, at = c(0,0.6,0.9,1.5), pos=0, tck=0, labels=rep(c("0","Xc1","Xc2","1"))) #X=c
axis(2, at = c(0,0.25,0.75,1.25, 1.5), pos=0, tck=0, labels=rep(c("","0", "0.5", "1", ""))) #Y=3
abline(h=c(0,0.5,1, 1.5), v=c(0, 1.5))
rect(0,0,1.5,0.5,  col = "grey") # 1-1
rect(0.6,0.5,1.5,1,  col = "grey") # 1-2
rect(0.9,1,1.5,1.5,  col = "grey") # 1-2
segments(0.6,0,0.6,0.5, lty=3, lwd=2, col="dark grey") #dashed line for Xc1
segments(0.9,0,0.9,1, lty=3, lwd=2, col="dark grey") #dashed line for Xc2
segments(0,0.5,0.6,0.5, lty=1, col="black", lwd = 4) #horizontal part 1 ceiling line
segments(0.6,0.5,0.6,1, lty=1, col="black", lwd = 4) #vertical part 1 ceiling line
segments(0.6,1,0.9,1, lty=1, col="black", lwd = 4) #horizontal part 2ceiling line
segments(0.9,1,0.9,1.5, lty=1, col="black", lwd = 4) #vertical part 1 ceiling line
#delete '#' in the next line to make a pdf
#dev.off()

#Figure 2.4G
#delete '#' in the next line to make a pdf
#pdf("Figure 2.4G.pdf", 5, 6.5) # make the vertical axis larger 
plot (X,Y, xlab="X", ylab="Y", xlim = c(0,1), ylim=c(0,1.5), pch=19, cex=2, axes = F, yaxs="i", xaxs="i", type="n") #3-3
axis(1, at = c(0,0.25,0.75,1.25), pos=0, tck=0, labels=rep(c("","0", "1", ""))) #X=2
axis(2, at = c(0,0.6,1.5), pos=0, tck=0, labels=rep(c("0","Yc","1"))) #Y=c
abline(v=c(0,0.5,1, 1.5), h=c(0, 1.5))
rect(0,0,0.5,0.6,  col = "grey") # 1-1
rect(0.5,0,1,1.5,  col = "grey") # 1-2
segments(0,0.6,0.5,0.6, lty=1, col="black", lwd = 4) #horizontal part 1 ceiling line
segments(0.5,0.6,0.5,1.5, lty=1, col="black", lwd = 4) #vertical part 1 ceiling line
#delete '#' in the next line to make a pdf
#dev.off()

#Figure 2.4H
#delete '#' in the next line to make a pdf
#pdf("Figure 2.4H.pdf", 5, 5) 
plot (X,Y, xlab="X", ylab="Y", xlim = c(0,1.5), ylim=c(0,1.5), pch=19, cex=2, axes = F, yaxs="i", xaxs="i", type="n") #3-3
axis(1, at = c(0,0.25,0.75,1.25, 1.5), pos=0, tck=0, labels=rep(c("","0", "0.5", "1", ""))) #X=3
axis(2, at = c(0,0.6,0.9,1.5), pos=0, tck=0, labels=rep(c("0","Yc1","Yc2","1"))) #Y=c
abline(v=c(0,0.5,1, 1.5), h=c(0, 1.5))
rect(0,0,0.5,0.6,  col = "grey") # 1-1
rect(0.5,0,1,0.9,  col = "grey") # 1-2
rect(1,0,1.5,1.5,  col = "grey") # 1-2
segments(0,0.9,0.5,0.9, lty=3, lwd=2, col="dark grey") #dashed line for Yc2
segments(0,0.6,0.5,0.6, lty=1, col="black", lwd = 4) #horizontal part 1 ceiling line
segments(0.5,0.6,0.5,0.9, lty=1, col="black", lwd = 4) #vertical part 1 ceiling line
segments(0.5,0.9,1.0,0.9, lty=1, col="black", lwd = 4) #horizontal part 2ceiling line
segments(1,0.9,1,1.5, lty=1, col="black", lwd = 4) #vertical part 1 ceiling line
#delete '#' in the next line to make a pdf
#dev.off()

#Figure 2.4I
#delete '#' in the next line to make a pdf
#pdf("Figure 2.4I.pdf", 5, 5)  
plot (X,Y, xlab="X", ylab="Y", xlim = c(0,1.5), ylim=c(0,1.5), pch=19, cex=2, axes = F, yaxs="i", xaxs="i", type="n") #3-3
# add grey area
x<-c(0,1.2,1.5)
y.low<-c(0,0,0)
y.high<-c(0.6,1.5,1.5)        
polygon(c(x, rev(x)), c(y.high, rev(y.low)),col = "grey", border = NA)
axis(1, at = c(0,0.53,1.5), pos=0, tck=0, labels=rep(c("0", "Xc","1"))) #X=c
axis(2, at = c(0,1,1.5), pos=0, tck=0, labels=rep(c("0","Yc","1"))) #Y=c
abline(v=c(0,1.5), h=c(0, 1.5))
segments(0,0.6,1.2,1.5, lty=1, col="black", lwd = 3) #ceiling line
segments(0,1,0.53,1, lty=3, lwd=2,col="dark grey") #horizontal dashed line for Yc
segments(0.52,0,0.53,1, lty=3, lwd=2, col="dark grey") #vertical dashed line for Xc
#delete '#' in the next line to make a pdf
#dev.off()
```

Second, whereas in the dichotomous situation the bounding box can be either full or empty, in the discrete and continuous situation it can be partly empty. The size of the empty space may vary, depending on the location of the observations. A border line (*ceiling line*) separates the empty space (*ceiling zone*) from the area where observations are possible (*feasible area*). Referring to the $XY$-plot in Figure \@ref(fig:dichdisconxyplotfig) where both the condition and the outcome are dichotomous, the ceiling line between empty and feasible area is predefined: the step line connecting the observations [0,0], [0,1], and [1,1] is always the same. The full bounding box (unit square) is empty, such that points can be only on the ceiling line. No observations can be above or to the left of the ceiling line.  In the discrete and continuous situations, the location of the ceiling line depends on the location of the observations in the unit square. This means that the location of the ceiling line and the size of the empty space vary. In any case, there can be no points above or to the left of the ceiling line. If necessity applies, points can only be on, below, or to the right of the ceiling: the feasible area. The ceiling line can be assumed (predefined) or estimated from data. In all situations, the assumption is that the ceiling line represents the sharp border between the empty space and the feasible area to represent deterministic necessity (see model fit measure *Sharpness* discussed in Section \@ref(sharpness)). When a relevant empty space exists that is caused by necessity, the necessary condition is formulated qualitatively as necessity-in-kind (NiK): $X$ is necessary for $Y$. 

Third, the discrete and continuous situation adds the possibility of a quantitative formulation of a necessary condition statement: *level of* $X$ is necessary for *level of* $Y$. This is called *necessity-in-degree* (NiD) and is shown for the continuous situation in Figure \@ref(fig:ncainshort). Any observation $C$ on the ceiling line defines a specific necessity-in-degree: level $X = x_c$ is necessary for level $Y = y_c$. Assuming an increasing ceiling line, the condition must have at least level $x_c$ for outcome level $y_c$. If the condition is less than $x_c$, the outcome will be less than $y_c$. If $X = x_c$, it cannot be concluded that $Y = y_c$, but only that $Y \leq y_c$. This situation can be described by level $x_c$ of $X$ is *necessary but not sufficient* for level $y_c$ of $Y$ (Section \@ref(necessarybutnotsufficient) for a further discussion on the meaning of necessary but not sufficient). 


```{r ncainshort, echo=FALSE, fig.show="hold", out.width='45%', fig.align="center", fig.cap="$XY$-plot illustrating necessity causality when $X$ and $Y$ are continuous (necessity-in-degree, NiD)."}

 knitr::include_graphics("NCAinShort.png") 
```

<!-- ## Causal underdetermination {#underdetermination} -->
<!-- A causal relation cannot be directly observed. What can be observed are only sequences and patterns. For example, when a glass falls from the table, we see the motion of the hand, the contact with the glass, the glass's acceleration, and its eventual impact on the ground. Nowhere in this stream of sensory data is there an observable entity called "causation". The causal link must instead be *inferred*. Was the push responsible for the fall, was the table moved at the same moment, or did the glass fall by  coincidence?  -->
<!-- Because causality cannot be observed, the same observational data can support multiple causal explanations. This is the phenomenon known as *causal underdetermination* [e.g, @stanford2023underdetermination]. An observed relation between $X$ and $Y$ may reflect that $X$ causes $Y$, $Y$ causes $X$, an unobserved cause or mere chance. Underdetermination implies that a single dataset can be explained by different causal models whether framed in terms of necessity, sufficiency, probabilistic tendencies, or typicality. -->

<!-- Given the inherent underdetermination of causal claims by data, theory becomes indispensable. Theory provides the assumptions and background knowledge needed to distinguish among causal models that are all consistent with the same observations. Without such guiding principles, data remain compatible with infinitely many interpretations. Theory specifies, for example, which mechanisms are plausible and which temporal order is even possible. -->

<!-- Some causal theories may be more plausible than others. For example, if we observe that plants exposed to more sunlight grow more, the explanation that sunlight causes plant growth is far more credible than the idea that plant growth causes sunlight. An observed relation is considered *spurious* when an alternative explanation is clearly more credible because the theory is more plausible or because of better model fit.^[Model fit refers to how well a model captures the pattern in the data. Section \@ref(modelfit) discusses model fit in the context of NCA.] -->

<!-- However, several causal theories may also be equally credible. This is the situation known as *causal pluralism*: different causal perspectives may each illuminate different aspects of the same phenomenon. (see Section \@ref(methodtriangulationversuscausalpluralism)). Causal pluralism can help to better understand the phenomenon of interest.  If several causal theories are equally credible it is also possible to select one theory for pragmatic reasons. Such reasons could be simplicity (see Section \@ref(parsimonious)), usefulness for prediction, computational elegance, compatibility with other accepted theories, interpretability, or practical actionability.  -->

<!-- This book is about the use of the necessity causal perspective when interpreting phenomena. It therefore follows that a necessity-based theoretical framework is essential for describing such phenomena coherently. What constitutes such a necessity theory is discussed in the next chapter. -->


## Causal underdetermination {#underdetermination}

A causal relationship cannot be directly observed. What can be observed are only sequences and patterns. For example, when a glass falls from the table, we see the motion of the hand, the contact with the glass, the glass’s acceleration, and its eventual impact on the ground. Nowhere in this stream of sensory data is there an observable entity called “causation”. The causal link must instead be *inferred*. Was the push responsible for the fall, was the table moved at the same moment, or did the glass fall by coincidence? 

Because causality cannot be observed, a human interpretation of observed data must be made. The same observational data can support multiple causal explanations. This is the phenomenon known as *causal underdetermination* [e.g., @stanford2023underdetermination]. An observed relationship between $X$ and $Y$ may reflect that $X$ causes $Y$, $Y$ causes $X$, an unobserved cause, or mere chance. Underdetermination implies that a single dataset can be explained by different causal models, whether framed in terms of necessity, sufficiency, probabilistic tendencies, or typicality.


The idea that the same evidence can be “seen” differently is illustrated by the classic ambiguous picture below. Some viewers immediately see a young woman, while others see an old woman, and it can be hard to switch between the two. Likewise, the same data can support different causal interpretations, and once someone becomes familiar with one perspective, it may be difficult to adopt another.


```{r youngold, echo=FALSE, fig.show="hold", out.width='50%', fig.align='center', fig.cap ="The same image can be seen in different ways. Likewise, the same data can be viewed through different causal lenses. From: My wife and my mother-in-law. They are both in this picture – find them. Prints \\& Photographs Online Catalog, Library of Congress.  https://www.loc.gov/pictures/item/2010652001/. Retrieved 1 October 2025."}
#knitr::include_graphics("youngold2.jpg")
#knitr::include_graphics("master-pnp-ds-00100-00175u.png")
knitr::include_graphics("master-pnp-ds-00100-00175uFLATGREYSCALE.png")
```


A related implication is the problem of non-identifiability (or observational equivalence): distinct models can generate the same observable data patterns and therefore fit the data equally well, even though they imply different underlying structures [e.g., @rothenberg1971identification]. When this happens, the evidence cannot distinguish between these competing explanations. For example, when a correlation between $X$ and $Y$ is observed, this could be interpreted as a result of a probabilistic sufficiency relationship, or as a result of a necessity relationship (Appendix \@ref(appendixc)). In principle, both interpretations are possible. 

Given the inherent underdetermination of causal claims by data, theory becomes indispensable. Theory provides the assumptions and background knowledge needed to justify a causal model that is consistent with the observations. Without such guiding principles, data remain compatible with infinitely many interpretations. Theory specifies, for example, which mechanisms are plausible or which temporal order is even possible. The central role of theory in data analysis for causal inference is illustrated by Nancy Cartwright's slogan:

 
> “No causes in, no causes out” [@cartwrightnature, pp. 39-90]. 

Some causal theories may be more plausible than others. For example, if we observe that plants exposed to more sunlight grow more, the explanation that sunlight causes plant growth is far more credible than the idea that plant growth causes sunlight. An observed relationship is considered spurious when an alternative explanation is clearly more credible because the theory is more plausible or because of better model fit.^[Section \@ref(modelfit1) discusses model fit in the context of NCA.]

However, several causal theories may also be equally credible. This is the situation known as causal pluralism: different causal perspectives may each illuminate different aspects of the same phenomenon (Section \@ref(methodtriangulationversuscausalpluralism)). Causal pluralism can help to better understand the underlying causal mechanisms of the phenomenon of interest. If several causal theories are equally credible, it is also possible to select one theory for pragmatic reasons. Such reasons could be simplicity (Section \@ref(parsimonious)), usefulness for prediction, computational elegance, compatibility with other accepted theories, interpretability, or practical actionability. A necessity theory could be a candidate.

Underdetermination and pluralism imply that no single causal perspective is inherently the “proper” one for every study question. In particular, commonly used probabilistic sufficiency perspectives and their associated regression tools are not inherently preferred. It is the analyst’s freedom to select an appropriate perspective and tools, and the analyst’s responsibility to justify the corresponding hypothesis and interpretation (Chapter \@ref(hypothesisformulation)).

This book is about the use of the necessity causal perspective and tools when studying phenomena. It therefore follows that a necessity-based theoretical framework is essential for describing such phenomena coherently. What constitutes such a necessity theory is discussed in the next chapter.

<!-- #####################HERE IS THE CODE OF THE FIGURES IN THIS CHAPTER####################### -->

```{r suffciencynecessitycode,echo=FALSE, eval=FALSE}

#make empty 2x2 contingency table
X<- c(0.25, 0.75) #X-values center of cell
Y<- c(0.25, 0.25) #Y-values center of cell

th = 3 #thickness of lines lwd
th1 = 5 #horizontal vertical axes
th2 = 10 #thickness of points

#give names to axes
xname = "X"
yname = "Y"

#give labels to axis values
xlabel = rep(c("","Absent", "Present", ""))
ylabel = rep(c("","Absent", "Present", ""))

#Fig1
#pdf("sufficiency.pdf")
#par after calling pdf
#set positions of labels and axes names
par(mar=c(5,5,4,2)+0.1) # order: bottom, left, top, right: increase left margin from 4 to 5, rest defaults 
par(mgp = c(3, 1, 0)) # default distance between label (axis name) and axis: decrease first to get label closer to axis 
#cex.lab is size of the label = axis name
plot (X,Y, xlab=xname, ylab=yname, xlim = c(0,1), ylim=c(0,1), pch=19, cex.lab=2, axes = F, yaxs="i", xaxs="i", type="n")
#cex.axis is size of the values at the axes
axis(1, at = c(0, 0.25, 0.75, 1.25), pos=0, tck=0, lwd = th, cex.axis = 2, labels=xlabel) #X has two values
axis(2, at = c(0, 0.25, 0.75, 1.25), pos=0, tck=0,lwd = th,  cex.axis = 2, labels=ylabel) #Y has two values
abline(v=0.5, h=0.5, lwd = th)
abline(v=1, h = 1, lwd = th1)
#fill cells
rect(0, 0.5, 0.5, 1,  col = "grey", lwd = th) #corner 1
rect(0.5, 0.5, 1, 1,  col = "grey", lwd = th) #corner 2
rect(0, 0, 0.5, 0.5,  col = "grey", lwd = th) #corner 3
#rect(0.5, 0, 1, 0.5,  col = "grey", lwd = th) #corern 4


#add points in cells
# points(0.25, 0.75, pch = 19, col = "black", lwd = th2) #corner 1
# points(0.75, 0.75, pch = 19, col = "black", lwd = th2) #corner 2
# points(0.25, 0.25, pch = 19, col = "black", lwd = th2) #corner 3
# points(0.75, 0.25, pch = 19, col = "black", lwd = th2) #corner 4

#add text in cells
fontsize <- 2
text(0.25,0.75,"corner 1", cex=fontsize) #corner 1 
text(0.75,0.75,"corner 2", cex=fontsize) #corner 2
text(0.25,0.25,"corner 3", cex=fontsize) #corner 3
text(0.75,0.25,"corner 4", cex=fontsize) #corner 4

#dev.off()

#Fig2
#pdf("necessity.pdf")
par(mar=c(5,5,4,2)+0.1) 

# Create the plot with square aspect ratio
plot (X,Y, xlab=xname, ylab=yname, xlim = c(0,1), ylim=c(0,1), pch=19, cex.lab=2, axes = F, yaxs="i", xaxs="i", type="n")

axis(1, at = c(0, 0.25, 0.75, 1.25), pos=0, tck=0, lwd = th, cex.axis = 2, labels=xlabel) #X has two values
axis(2, at = c(0, 0.25, 0.75, 1.25), pos=0, tck=0, lwd = th, cex.axis = 2, labels=ylabel) #Y has two values
abline(v=0.5, h=0.5, lwd = th)
abline(v=1, h = 1, lwd = th1)
#fill cells
#rect(0, 0.5, 0.5, 1,  col = "grey", lwd = th) #corner 1
rect(0.5, 0.5, 1, 1,  col = "grey", lwd = th) #corner 2
rect(0, 0, 0.5, 0.5,  col = "grey", lwd = th) #corner 3
rect(0.5, 0, 1, 0.5,  col = "grey", lwd = th) #corner 4

#add points
# points(0.25, 0.75, pch = 19, col = "black", lwd = th2) #corner 1
# points(0.75, 0.75, pch = 19, col = "black", lwd = th2) #corner 2
# points(0.25, 0.25, pch = 19, col = "black", lwd = th2) #corner 3
# points(0.75, 0.25, pch = 19, col = "black", lwd = th2) #corner 4

#add text in cells
fontsize <- 2
text(0.25,0.75,"corner 1", cex=fontsize) #corner 1 
text(0.75,0.75,"corner 2", cex=fontsize) #corner 2
text(0.25,0.25,"corner 3", cex=fontsize) #corner 3
text(0.75,0.25,"corner 4", cex=fontsize) #corner 4

#dev.off()


# #Fig3
# pdf("sufficiencynecessity.pdf")
# par(mar=c(5,5,4,2)+0.1)

```{r dichotomousxyplotcode, echo = FALSE, eval = FALSE, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE, include=FALSE, cache=TRUE}

library(ggplot2)

# Create a grid of points at intersections
x <- c(0, 1, 1)
y <- c(0, 0, 1)
random_points <- data.frame(x, y)

# Create the ggplot
gg0 <- ggplot() +
  geom_segment(aes(x = 0, y = 0, xend = 0, yend = 1), color = "black", linetype = 'solid') +
  geom_segment(aes(x = 0, y = 1, xend = 1, yend = 1), color = "black", linetype = 'solid') +
  geom_segment(aes(x = 0, y = 0, xend = 1, yend = 0), color = "black") +
  geom_segment(aes(x = 1, y = 0, xend = 1, yend = 1), color = "black") +
  
  # Plot the valid random points
  geom_point(data = random_points, aes(x = x, y = y), color = "black", size = 3) +
  
  # Change labels of axes
  labs(x = "X", y = "Y") +  # Axis labels

  # Set the x and y limits to [0, 1] and show only ticks at 0 and 1
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = c(0, 1), limits = c(0, 1)) +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = c(0, 1), limits = c(0, 1)) +
  
  # Customize theme
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major = element_blank(),  # No major grid lines
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),  # No minor grid lines
    panel.background = element_blank(),   # Ensure no background is drawn
    panel.border = element_blank(),       # Remove the outer box
    axis.line = element_blank()           # Remove axis lines
  )

# Save the plot as a PDF
#ggsave("dichotomousXYplot.pdf", plot = gg0, width = 5, height = 4, dpi = 600)

# Display the ggplot
print(gg0)
```



```{r discretexyplotcode, echo = FALSE, eval = FALSE, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE, include=FALSE, cache=TRUE}

library(ggplot2)

# Create a data frame with the ceiling line intercept = 0.3 and slope 1
ceiling_data <- data.frame(x = c(0, 1), y = c(0.3, 1.3))

# Calculate coordinates of point S2
S2_x <- (1 - 0.3) / 1.0
S2_y <- 1

# Calculate coordinates of point S1
S1_x <- 0
S1_y <- 0.3

# Define the coordinates of point C and Xc, Yc values
C_x <- 0.5
C_y <- 0.8
Xc <- C_x
Yc <- C_y

# Create a grid of points at intersections
grid_points <- expand.grid(x = seq(0, 1, by = 0.1), y = seq(0, 1, by = 0.1))

# Calculate ceiling function for filtering points below the ceiling line
ceiling_func <- function(x) { 1 - x + 0.3 }

# Filter points that are inside the polygon and below the ceiling line
valid_points <- grid_points[grid_points$y < ceiling_func(grid_points$x) & grid_points$y < 1 - grid_points$x + 0.3, ]

# Randomly select 20 points from the valid grid points
set.seed(42)  # Setting seed for reproducibility
random_points <- valid_points[sample(nrow(valid_points), 20), ]

# Create a data frame of additional random points
x <- c(0.5, 0.4, 0.2, 0.8, 0.6, 0.9, 0.3, 0.1, 0.9, 0.3, 0.8, 1.0, 0.2, 0.7, 0.4, 0.2, 0.4, 0.0, 0.5, 0.5, Xc)
y <- c(0.4, 0.6, 0.2, 0.8, 0.1, 1.0, 0.4, 0.2, 0.7, 0.3, 0.1, 0.2, 0.0, 0.3, 0.2, 0.3, 0.0, 0.3, 0.7, 0.5, Yc)
random_points <- data.frame(x, y)

# Create the ggplot
gg1 <- ggplot() +
  geom_polygon(data = data.frame(x = c(0, 1, 1, 0), y = c(0, 0, 0.3, 0.3)),
               aes(x, y), fill = "grey", alpha = 1) +  # Grey rectangle below the steps
  geom_polygon(data = data.frame(x = c(0.3, 1, 1, 0.3), y = c(0.3, 0.3, 0.4, 0.4)),
               aes(x, y), fill = "grey", alpha = 1) +  # Grey rectangle below the steps
  geom_polygon(data = data.frame(x = c(0.4, 1, 1, 0.4), y = c(0.4, 0.4, 0.6, 0.6)),
               aes(x, y), fill = "grey", alpha = 1) +  # Grey rectangle below the steps
  
  geom_polygon(data = data.frame(x = c(0.5, 1, 1, 0.5), y = c(0.6, 0.6, 0.8, 0.8)),
               aes(x, y), fill = "grey", alpha = 1) +  # Grey rectangle below the steps
  geom_polygon(data = data.frame(x = c(0.8, 1, 1, 0.8), y = c(0.8, 0.8, 1.0, 1.0)),
               aes(x, y), fill = "grey", alpha = 1) +  # Grey rectangle below the steps
  
  
  geom_segment(aes(x = 0, y = 0.3, xend = 0.3, yend = 0.3), color = "black", linetype = 'solid', lwd = 0.8) +
  geom_segment(aes(x = 0.3, y = 0.3, xend = 0.3, yend = 0.4), color = "black", linetype = 'solid', lwd = 0.8) +
  geom_segment(aes(x = 0.3, y = 0.4, xend = 0.4, yend = 0.4), color = "black", linetype = 'solid', lwd = 0.8) +
  geom_segment(aes(x = 0.4, y = 0.4, xend = 0.4, yend = 0.6), color = "black", linetype = 'solid', lwd = 0.8) +
  geom_segment(aes(x = 0.4, y = 0.6, xend = 0.5, yend = 0.6), color = "black", linetype = 'solid', lwd = 0.8) +
  geom_segment(aes(x = 0.5, y = 0.6, xend = 0.5, yend = 0.8), color = "black", linetype = 'solid', lwd = 0.8) +
  geom_segment(aes(x = 0.5, y = 0.8, xend = 0.8, yend = 0.8), color = "black", linetype = 'solid', lwd = 0.8) +
  geom_segment(aes(x = 0.8, y = 0.8, xend = 0.8, yend = 1.0), color = "black", linetype = 'solid', lwd = 0.8) +
  #geom_segment(aes(x = 0.5, y = 0.8, xend = 0.8, yend = 0.8), color = "black", linetype = 'solid', lwd = 0.8) +
  
  
  
  geom_segment(aes(x = 0, y = 0, xend = 0, yend = 1), color = "black", linetype = 'solid') +
  geom_segment(aes(x = 0, y = 1, xend = 1, yend = 1), color = "black", linetype = 'solid') +
  geom_segment(aes(x = 0, y = 0, xend = 1, yend = 0), color = "black") +
  geom_segment(aes(x = 1, y = 0, xend = 1, yend = 1), color = "black") +
  #geom_segment(aes(x = S1_x, y = S1_y, xend = S2_x, yend = S2_y), color = "black", lwd = 0.8) +
  
  # Plot the valid random points
  geom_point(data = random_points, aes(x = x, y = y), color = "black", size = 3) +
  
  # Change labels of axes
  labs(x = "X", y = "Y") +  # Axis labels
  
  # Set the x and y limits to [0, 1] and show only ticks at 0.1 etc
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 1, by = 0.1), limits = c(0, 1)) +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 1, by = 0.1), limits = c(0, 1)) +
  
  # # Add consistent grid lines
  # geom_hline(yintercept = seq(0, 1, by = 0.1), color = "black", size = 0.2) +
  # geom_vline(xintercept = seq(0, 1, by = 0.1), color = "black", size = 0.2) +
  
  # Add consistent grid lines (avoid crossing axes)
  annotate("segment", x = 0, xend = 1, y = seq(0.1, 0.9, by = 0.1), yend = seq(0.1, 0.9, by = 0.1), color = "black", size = 0.2) +
  annotate("segment", x = seq(0.1, 0.9, by = 0.1), xend = seq(0.1, 0.9, by = 0.1), y = 0, yend = 1, color = "black", size = 0.2) +
  
  
  # Customize theme
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),                # No minor grid lines
    panel.background = element_blank(),                # Ensure no background is drawn
    panel.border = element_blank(),                    # Remove the outer box
    axis.line = element_blank()                        # Remove axis lines
  ) 

# Save the plot as a PDF
#ggsave("discreteXYplot.pdf", plot = gg1, width = 5, height = 4, dpi = 600)

# Display the ggplot
print(gg1)

```


```{r continuousxyplotcode, echo = FALSE, eval = FALSE, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE, include=FALSE, cache=TRUE}

library(ggplot2)

# Create a data frame with the ceiling line intercept = 0.3 and slope 1
ceiling_data <- data.frame(x = c(0, 1), y = c(0.3, 1.3))

# Calculate coordinates of point S2
S2_x <- (1 - 0.3) / 1.0
S2_y <- 1

# Calculate coordinates of point S1
S1_x <- 0
S1_y <- 0.3

# Define the coordinates of point C and Xc, Yc values
C_x <- 0.5
C_y <- 0.8
Xc <- C_x
Yc <- C_y

x <- c(0.52, 0.43, 0.24, 0.85, 0.6, 0.97, 0.3, 0.19, 0.89, 0.28, 0.77, 1.0, 0.16, 0.65, 0.4, 0.14, 0.33, 0.0, 0.42, 0.41, Xc)
y <- c(0.39, 0.58, 0.17, 0.76, 0.1, 1.0, 0.35, 0.14, 0.63, 0.22, 0.01, 0.21, 0.0, 0.32, 0.23, 0.34, 0.0, 0.30, 0.7, 0.57, Yc)
random_points <- data.frame(x,y)

# Create the ggplot
gg2 <- ggplot() +
  geom_polygon(data = data.frame(x = c(0, S1_x, S2_x, 1, 1), y = c(0, S1_y, S2_y, 1, 0)),
               aes(x, y), fill = "grey") +
  geom_segment(aes(x = 0, y = 0, xend = 0, yend = 1), color = "black", linetype = 'solid') +
  geom_segment(aes(x = 0, y = 1, xend = 1, yend = 1), color = "black", linetype = 'solid') +
  geom_segment(aes(x = 0, y = 0, xend = 1, yend = 0), color = "black") +
  geom_segment(aes(x = 1, y = 0, xend = 1, yend = 1), color = "black") +
  geom_segment(aes(x = S1_x, y = S1_y, xend = S2_x, yend = S2_y), color = "black", lwd = 0.8) +

  # Plot the valid random points
  geom_point(data = random_points, aes(x = x, y = y), color = "black", size = 3) +
  
  # Change labels of axes
  labs(x = "X", y = "Y") +  # Axis labels
  
  # Set the x and y limits to [0, 1] and show only ticks at 0 and 1
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = c(0, 1), limits = c(0, 1)) +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = c(0, 1), limits = c(0, 1)) +
  
  # Customize theme
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major = element_blank(),  # No major grid lines
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),  # No minor grid lines
    panel.background = element_blank(),   # Ensure no background is drawn
    panel.border = element_blank(),       # Remove the outer box
    axis.line = element_blank()           # Remove axis lines
  )

# Save the plot as a PDF
#ggsave("continuousXYplot.pdf", plot = gg2, width = 5, height = 4, dpi = 600)

# Display the ggplot
print(gg2)
```


```{r ncainshortcode, echo = FALSE, eval = FALSE, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE, include=FALSE, cache=TRUE}

library("ggplot2")

# Create a data frame with the ceiling line intercept = 0.3 and slope 1
ceiling_data <- data.frame(x = c(0, 1), y = c(0.3, 1.3))

# Calculate coordinates of point S2
S2_x <- (1 - 0.3) / 1.0
S2_y <- 1

# Calculate coordinates of point S1
S1_x <- 0
S1_y <- 0.3

# Define the coordinates of point C and Xc, Yc values
C_x <- 0.5
C_y <- 0.8
Xc <- C_x
Yc <- C_y

# Create the ggplot
#pdf("NCAinShort.pdf", height = 3.5, width = 3.5)
gg <- ggplot() +
  # geom_segment(aes(x = 0, y = 0, xend = 0, yend = 1), color = "black", linetype = 'dashed') +
  # geom_segment(aes(x = 0, y = 1, xend = 1, yend = 1), color = "black", linetype = 'dashed') +
  geom_polygon(data = data.frame(x = c(0, S1_x, S2_x, 1, 1), y = c(0, S1_y, S2_y, 1, 0)),
               aes(x, y), fill = "grey") +
  geom_segment(aes(x = 0, y = 0, xend = 0, yend = 1), color = "black", linetype = 'solid') +
  geom_segment(aes(x = 0, y = 1, xend = 1, yend = 1), color = "black", linetype = 'solid') +
  # geom_segment(aes(x = 0, y = 0, xend = 1, yend = 0), color = "black", lwd = 0.8) +
  # geom_segment(aes(x = 1, y = 0, xend = 1, yend = 1), color = "black", lwd = 0.8) +
  geom_segment(aes(x = 0, y = 0, xend = 1, yend = 0), color = "black") +
  geom_segment(aes(x = 1, y = 0, xend = 1, yend = 1), color = "black") +
  # geom_segment(aes(x = S2_x, y = S2_y, xend = 1, yend = 1), color = "black", lwd = 0.8) +
  # geom_segment(aes(x = S1_x, y = S1_y, xend = 0, yend = 0), color = "black", lwd = 0.8) +
  geom_segment(aes(x = S1_x, y = S1_y, xend = S2_x, yend = S2_y), color = "black", lwd = 0.8) +
  
  geom_point(aes(x = C_x, y = C_y), color = "black", size = 3) +  # Add point C
  geom_segment(aes(x = C_x, y = 0, xend = Xc, yend = Yc), color = "black", linetype = "dotted") +  # Connect C to Xc
  geom_segment(aes(x = C_x, y = C_y, xend = Xc, yend = Yc), color = "black", linetype = "dotted") +  # Connect C to Xc
  geom_segment(aes(x = 0, y = Yc, xend = Xc, yend = Yc), color = "black", linetype = "dotted") +  # Horizontal dotted line at Yc
  
  annotate("text", x = C_x, y = C_y + 0.05, label = "C", color = "black", size = 4) +  # Label "C" above the line
  annotate("text", x = Xc, y = -0.05, label = expression(x[c]), color = "black", size = 4) +  # Label for Xc
  annotate("text", x = -0.05, y = Yc, label = expression(y[c]), color = "black", size = 4) +  # Label for Yc
  
  
  xlim(0, 1) +
  ylim(0, 1) +
  xlab("X = Condition") +
  ylab("Y = Outcome") +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = c(0, 1)) +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = c(0, 1)) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(panel.grid = element_blank(),
        #panel.background = element_rect(fill = "transparent"))
        panel.background = element_blank(),      # Ensure no background is drawn
        panel.border = element_blank(),          # Remove the outer box
        axis.line = element_blank())             # Remove axis lines

# Display the ggplot
print(gg)
#dev.off()
```

<!-- ## Causality -->

<!-- Causality cannot be directly observed. It is a human interpretation of something (the cause) affecting something else (the effect). There is an ongoing debate in philosophy and elsewhere about what is causality and what is a cause. In this section NCA's interpretation of causality is explained. -->

<!-- ### Determinstic causality  -->
<!-- The discussion about causality often starts with David Hume's definition of a cause^[The quote is from the final edition of "Enquiry of Human Understanding" @hume1777enquiry. The second sentence was missing in the first edition [@hume1748enquiry] and was added in the third edition of 1756 [@Falkenstein2013].] represents a deterministic view on causality:  -->

<!-- > "We may define a cause to be an object, followed by another, and where all the objects, similar to the first, are followed by objects, similar to the second. Or in other words, where, if the first had not been, the second never had existed." -->

<!-- ### Probabilistic causality -->


<!-- ### Necessity "in kind" and "in degree" -->
<!-- Rather than the two values 'true' or 'false', in NCA $X$ and $Y$ usually have the values 'presence' or 'absence' (e.g., the presence/absence of $X$ is necessary for the presence/absence of $Y$). Such qualitative statement is called necessity 'in kind'. NCA also allows that $X$ and $Y$ have certain specific levels (e.g., 'level $X=X_c$ is a necessary for level $Y = Y_c$'). Such quantitative necessity statement is called necessity-in-degree.  -->

<!-- ### Exceptions:  "almost always necessary" -->


<!-- ```{r} -->
<!-- chunk_names -->
<!-- ``` -->


<!--chapter:end:020-causalityJO.Rmd-->

# Theory {#theory}

## Summary of this chapter {#summarytheory}

This chapter discusses causal theory in general and necessity theory in particular. A causal theory helps us understand phenomena, predict effects, and explain empirical findings. The chapter begins (Section \@ref(definitionnecessitytheory)) by discussing the four fundamental elements of a theory: (1) focal unit (e.g., person, organization, country), (2) concepts (varying characteristics of the focal unit), (3) propositions (causal relationships between the concepts), and (4) theoretical domain (generalization to where the theory holds). This is followed by a discussion of parsimonious necessity theories (Section \@ref(parsimonious)) and types of necessity theories (Section \@ref(theorytypes)), for example a pure necessity theory where all relationships between the concepts are described in terms of necessity, and an embedded necessity theory, where part of the theory has one or more necessity relationships. Next, the direction of a necessity proposition is discussed (Section \@ref(direction)), which depends on whether $X$ and $Y$ are present/have high value or are absent/have low value. The most common direction is that the presence or high value of $X$ is necessary for the presence or high value of $Y$ (high-high direction), which is the default when the direction is not specified. Other possible directions are high-low, low-high, and low-low. The following section (Section \@ref(expectedemptycorner)) discusses the expected data pattern when necessity holds. A consequence of a high-high necessary condition is that the upper-left corner in an $XY$-plot is expected to be empty. The next section (Section \@ref(morecorners)) discusses the possibility of having double necessity when multiple corners are expected to be empty. The chapter finishes with a discussion of the theoretical meaning of 'necessary but not sufficient' (Section \@ref(necessarybutnotsufficient)).

## Main elements of a theory {#definitionnecessitytheory}
Because there are multiple ways to conceptualize theory, there is no consensus on what a theory is. This book adopts the 'four elements' approach to theory [@dul2008case], which enables straightforward empirical testing. It uses a broad and inclusive definition of what constitutes a theory, ranging from established theories that are broadly accepted to theoretical assertions by an analyst made in a specific study. Not only academic theories but also 'theories-in-use' qualify as a theory. At a minimum, a theory must have four fundamental elements: focal unit, concepts, propositions, and theoretical domain. The *focal unit* is the object to which the theory applies: country, person, firm, project, etc. The *concepts* are the variant characteristics of the focal unit representing properties of a phenomenon that can increase or decrease. In NCA, a concept that is the cause is called the 'condition' and a concept that is the effect is called the 'outcome'. The *proposition* describes the relationship between cause and effect, including the causal explanation. In NCA, the causal relationship is a necessity relationship: $X$ is necessary for $Y$, meaning that $X$ is (almost always) necessary for $Y$ [@dul2024different]. The words 'almost always' may be omitted from the necessity proposition, allowing both a deterministic and a typicality perspective. As theories in the social sciences are usually semantic (expressed in words), specific levels of the condition (cause) and the outcome (effect) are not specified. The *theoretical domain* is the set of cases of the focal unit where the theory is supposed to hold, and is defined by the boundary conditions of the theory. A *case* is one particular example of the focal unit: a specific country, a specific person, etc. For example, a theory that proposes a relationship between physical exercise and stress might be applicable to working adults, but not to children. In that case, the theoretical domain includes only working adults.

For example, the established Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB) explains how an individual's intention for a specific non-habitual behavior relates to the performance of that behavior [@ajzen1991theory]. The *focal unit* is 'individual' or 'person', two of its *concepts* are 'Intention for the behavior' (the cause) and 'Performing the behavior' (the effect), the *proposition* describes the causal relationship between these concepts, and one possible *theoretical domain* is 'all people in the world'.

<!-- ######################## -->
<!-- Bagozzi, R. P. (1992). The self-regulation of attitudes, intentions, and behavior. Social -->

<!-- review, 86(5), 452. -->

<!-- entrepreneurial self-efficacy, and entrepreneurial intention: A study based on China -->
<!-- college students’ social entrepreneurship project. Frontiers in psychology, 11, 547627. -->

<!-- <!-- ########################## --> 

The relationship between the two TPB concepts is usually specified as a *probabilistic sufficiency relationship*, for example 'Intention to perform a behavior likely to result in Performing the behavior', or, as the founder of TPB  puts it: "As a general rule, the stronger the intention to engage in a behavior, the more likely should be its performance" [@ajzen1991theory, p. 181]. Recent interpretations of TPB suggest formulating the relationship between the two concepts in terms of *necessity* [e.g., @frommeyer2022utility; @rozenkowska2023theory; @eccarius2024examining]: for example 'Intention to perform a behavior is necessary for Performing the behavior'. The key difference between a conventional (probabilistic sufficiency) theory and a necessity theory is the difference in specification of the causal relationship, in other words the *causal perspective* [@dul2024different] that is used to describe the relationship between the concepts (i.e., the proposition).

A theory is only complete if it is accompanied by a plausible substantive explanation of why the cause affects the outcome. For a necessity proposition, the theory should explain (1) why all cases from the theoretical domain with the outcome have the condition, (2) why without the condition, the outcome does not exist, (3) why the absence of the condition is not compensable by other factors (no possibility of substitution), and (4) that the condition precedes the outcome (fulfilling the temporality requirement of causality). Details about how to formulate a formal necessity hypothesis embedded in a necessity theory are explained in Chapter \@ref(hypothesisformulation). When applying NCA, a necessity theory that causally explains the necessity relationship is essential.

## Parsimonious necessity theories {#parsimonious}
Necessity theories are inherently parsimonious: single concepts make a strong, actionable prediction: the absence of a necessary condition perfectly predicts the absence of the outcome. Starting with William of Ockham's parsimony principle^[An important element of parsimony is that theories should be simple: "*... frustra fit per plura, quod potest fieri per pauciora*." (it is pointless to do with more what can be done with fewer) [@ockham1323summa].] (also known as Occam's Razor), many famous scholars including Newton^[ "*Causas rerum naturalium non plures admitti debere, quam quae & vera sint & earum phaenomenis explicandis sufficiunt*." (The causes of natural things should not be multiplied beyond what is true and sufficient to explain their appearances) [@newton1687principia].], Einstein^[ "*It can scarcely be denied that the supreme goal of all theory is to make the irreducible basic elements as simple and as few as possible without having to surrender the adequate representation of a single datum of experience*." [@einstein1934method].], Popper^[ "*We do not seek highly probable theories, but explanations; and the best explanations are often simple explanations.*" [@popper1959logic].], and Simon^[ "*Simplicity is not an optional accessory in human thought and action; it is a necessity*." [@simon1957models].] emphasized the importance of keeping theories simple, such that they are understandable, practically useful, and falsifiable.

Because necessary conditions operate in isolation from the rest of the causal structure (see Section \@ref(multipleconditions)), even the simplest necessity theory with only one or a few necessary conditions can be actionable. Figure \@ref(fig:fig1model) shows a necessity theory with two concepts: one condition $X$ and one outcome $Y$. To emphasize the necessity perspective used to describe the relationship, nc (necessary cause or necessary condition) is placed above the arrow.



<!-- It can range be a formal, generally accepted theory in the literature [such as the Theory of Planned Behavior @ajzen1991theory], or an emerging theory that has been proposed in the literature but is not (yet) broadly accepted, for example as part of a specific theory testing empirical study.  -->

<!-- As a minimum, a theory must have four fundamental components.  -->

<!-- In the context of a necessity theory, an additional requirement is that the theory should not be refuted or invalidated by a thought experiment (see section \@ref(thoughtexperiment)). -->

<!--  necessity causal relationship is -->

<!-- The four fundamental components of a theory --including a necessity theory-- are: -->


<!-- ^[As another illustration, the four characteristics of the necessity theory in `nca.example` of the `NCA` software are: Focal unit = country; Independent concepts (conditions) = Individualism and Risk taking; Dependent concept (outcome) = Innovation performance; Propositions = Individualism is necessary for Innovation performance, Risk taking is necessary for Innovation performance; Theoretical domain: Western countries.] ^[In theory testing research the four elements of a theory (concepts, focal unit, propositions and theoretical domain) are operationalized: concepts are operationalized into *variables* that can be measured, propositions are operationalized into *hypotheses* with relations between variables, focal unit becomes the *unit of analysis*, and the theoretical domain becomes the *study domain* from which the cases for the study are selected. For case selection usually a *population* of cases is identified in the theoretical domain and a *sample* is taken from this population, but purposive selection of cases is possible as well [@dul2024how]. From the sample a *dataset* is obtained with variables scores of the cases. See also section \@ref(datacollection).]  -->

```{r fig1model, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE, out.width = "45%", fig.align = 'center', fig.cap= "Conceptual model of a necessity theory with one condition and one outcome."}
knitr::include_graphics('Fig1model.png')
```

It is also possible that multiple concepts (e.g., $X_1$, $X_2$, $X_3$) are necessary for the same outcome ($Y$) as shown in Figure \@ref(fig:fig2model)-left, or that one or more conditions are necessary for several outcomes (e.g., $Y_1$, $Y_2$, $Y_3$), which is shown in Figure \@ref(fig:fig2model)-right. Note that conditions $X_1$, $X_2$, and $X_3$ are all individual necessary conditions. If any one condition is missing (below its required level) the outcome cannot occur (at its target level), while the presence of all conditions is normally not sufficient for the outcome because other contributing factors must be in place as well.


```{r fig2model, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE, fig.show="hold", fig.align = 'center', out.width = "45%", fig.cap= "Conceptual models of a necessity theory with three conditions and one outcome (Left), and of a theory with one condition and three outcomes (Right)."}
#par(mfrow = c(1,2))
knitr::include_graphics('Fig2model.png')
knitr::include_graphics('Fig3model.png')
```

<!-- ```{r fig3model, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE, out.width = "50%", fig.align = 'center', fig.cap= "Conceptual model of a necessity theory with one condition and three outcomes."} -->
<!-- knitr::include_graphics('Fig3model.png') -->
<!-- ```  -->

Furthermore, several necessary conditions can combine into a necessity causal chain where $X_1$ is necessary for $X_2$, and $X_2$ is necessary for $Y$. This situation is shown in Figure \@ref(fig:fig4model) and is called 'chain necessity' of $X_1$ for $Y$. Chain necessity differs from 'direct necessity' as shown in Figure \@ref(fig:fig1model). In the dichotomous necessity-in-kind (NiK) situation, the necessity of $X_1$ for $X_2$ and of $X_2$ for $Y$ implies the necessity of $X_1$ for $Y$. However, in the necessity-in-degree (NiD) situation it is possible that the chain necessity of $X_1$ for $Y$ is absent despite the necessity between the elements of the chain ($X_1$ for $X_2$ and $X_2$ for $Y$), as discussed in Section \@ref(chain).

```{r fig4model, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE, out.width = "90%", fig.align = 'center', fig.cap= "Conceptual model of a chain of necessary conditions. $X_1$ is necessary for $X_2$ and $X_2$ is necessary for $Y$."}
knitr::include_graphics('Fig4model.png')
```

<!-- An essential part of a necessity theory is the specification of the theoretical domain where the necessity claim is expected to hold. For example if the claim holds for people, it may or may not hold for females, or if it does not hold for people it may hold for female. . , it also applies to a specific part of that theoretical domain. For example when the theoretical domain is defined as all people from the world, all cases are in the feasible space and no cases are in the empty space of Figure \@ref(fig:cornerssufficiencynecessity) Right for binary concepts and Figure \@ref(fig: ncainshort) for continuous concepts. Removing a selection of cases from the feasible space, will not change the empty space in the binary situation, and will not decrease the empty space in the continuous situation. However, in the continuous situation is it possible the the empty space increases when a specific selection of cases based on a certain characteristic (e.g. female) is removed and all cases near the ceiling have that characteristic, which results in lowering the ceiling and increasing the empty space. .  are all theses cases turn out to be all near the ceiling: removing them  , namely (cases near the ceiling) are removed, which may increase necessity. This indicates that the removed  In the binary case  However, it may increase the empty space, for example when alHowever, by taking  a *new* theory may de defined with where the theoretical domain is smaller than the origical domain and for that subdomain there may be more necessity.   (This means that the idea of 'necessity moderation' does not exist in necessity theories.      -->

Necessity models are inherently parsimonious. A single necessary condition can stop the outcome, independently of other conditions and other contributing factors. However, in sufficiency-based perspectives, a single factor is seldom enough to produce the outcome. For a probabilistic or configurational sufficiency model, a series of factors are included to model causal complexity. This applies to both probabilistic sufficiency (e.g., structural causal models) and configurational sufficiency (e.g., as in QCA). Parts of the relationships of sufficiency models could (also) be necessity relationships. For the necessity analysis these parts can be isolated (as in Figure \@ref(fig:fig1model)) from the rest of the causal structure and independently be analyzed for necessity (Chapter \@ref(multimethodstudies)).


## Types of necessity theories {#theorytypes}
In addition to the established/emerging distinction, necessity theories can also be classified according to the extent to which necessity propositions are part of the theory. @bokrantz2022building distinguish between *pure necessity theories*, which are theories that consist only of necessity relationships between the concepts and *embedded necessity theories*, which are theories that consist of a mix of necessity relationships and other relationships (e.g., probabilistic sufficiency or configurational sufficiency). In embedded necessity theories, some of the relationships may be necessity relationships (e.g., $X_1 \xrightarrow{nc\ } Y$), whereas others may be probabilistically sufficient relationships ($X_2 \xrightarrow{+\ } Y$). It is also possible that one condition has both causal roles in a model such that the presence of $X$ is necessary for the presence of $Y$, but also that $X$ likely increases $Y$ ($X \xrightarrow{nc\ ,\ +} Y$).^[See also Section \@ref(direction) for various possible directions of necessity and probabilistic sufficiency relationships and their notation. It is possible that $X$ is necessary for $Y$ with or without being also probabilistically sufficient for $Y$. NCA only considers the necessity role of the concept. Assuming a double role of a concept is commonplace in theories that are analyzed both by NCA and a regression-based model (see Section \@ref(ncawithregressionbasedmethods)).] 


<!-- (ref:caption1) Typology of necessity theories with examples. Established Pure necessity theories: Intelligence-Creativity theory [@guilford1967nature]; AMO = Ability-Motivation-Opportunity framework [@van2017limiting; @hauff2021hrm; @tuuli2021ability]. Established Embedded necessity theories: TPB = Theory of Planned Behavior [e.g., @frommeyer2022utility; @rozenkowskatheory; @eccarius2024examining]; TAM = Technology Acceptance Model [e.g., @richter2020predictors; @low2023isn; @kopplin2023chatbots; @su2023identifying]; SDT = Self Determination Theory [e.g.,@ding2023using; @cassia2024value]; Emerging Pure necessity theories: theories in theory testing/building NCA studies; Emerging Embedded necessity theories: theories in theory testing/building combines NCA-regression studies or combined NCA-QCA studies.  -->


<!-- ```{r typestheory, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE, out.width = "75%", fig.align = 'center', fig.cap="Typology of necessity theories with examples. Established–Pure necessity theories: Intelligence–Creativity theory; AMO = Ability–Motivation–Opportunity framework. Established–Embedded necessity theories: TPB = Theory of Planned Behavior, TAM = Technology Acceptance Model, SDT = Self-Determination Theory. Emerging–Pure necessity theories: NCA alone studies (see Appendix~\\ref{appendixncaalone}). Emerging–Embedded necessity theories: NCA–regression studies (see Appendix~\\ref{appendixncamlr}, Appendix~\\ref{appendixncasem}, Appendix~\\ref{appendixncaplssem}) or NCA–QCA studies (see Appendix~\\ref{appendixncaqca})."} -->
<!-- knitr::include_graphics('types_necessity_theoriesa.png') -->
<!-- ``` -->

```{r typestheory, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE, out.width = "75%", fig.align = 'center', fig.cap="Typology of necessity theories with examples. AMO = Ability-Motivation-Opportunity. TPB = Theory of Planned Behavior. TAM = Technology Acceptance Model. SDT = Self-Determination Theory."}
knitr::include_graphics('types_necessity_theoriesa.png')
```

<!-- for HTML -->

<!-- ```{r typestheory, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE, out.width = "75%", fig.align = 'center', fig.cap="Typology of necessity theories with examples. Established-Pure necessity theories: Intelligence-Creativity theory; AMO = Ability-Motivation-Opportunity framework. Established-Embedded necessity theories: TPB = Theory of Planned Behavior TAM = Technology Acceptance Model; SDT = Self Determiniation Theory; Emerging-Pure necessity theories: NCA alone studies (see Appendix\\@ref(appendixncaalone)); Emerging-Embedded necessity theories: NCA-regression studies (see Appendix \\@ref(appendixncamlr), Appendix \\@ref(appendixncasem), Appendix \\@ref(appendixncaplssem)) or NCA-QCA studies (see Appendix \\@ref(appendixncaqca))."} -->
<!-- knitr::include_graphics('types_necessity_theoriesa.png') -->
<!-- ``` -->

Figure \@ref(fig:typestheory) shows the four possible types of necessity theories by also considering if the necessity theory is established or new.  \
*Established pure necessity theories*, where all relationships in the theory are necessity relationships as in Figures \@ref(fig:fig1model), \@ref(fig:fig2model), and \@ref(fig:fig4model) in Section \@ref(definitionnecessitytheory) are relatively scarce. One example is the early theory of @guilford1967nature that intelligence is necessary for creativity. Shortly after NCA was introduced [@dul2016orm], this theory was empirically tested with NCA and supported [@karwowski2016intellligence]. Another established pure necessity theory is the original Ability, Motivation, Opportunity model (AMO) or Motivation, Opportunity, Ability (MOA) model of human behavior. Necessity lies at the core of this theory assuming that A, M, and O are single necessary conditions for behavior.^[In this framework it is also assumed that the three necessary conditions are jointly sufficient. Referring to the origins of the AMO model, it has recently been analyzed as a pure necessity theory [@van2017limiting; @hauff2021hrm; @tuuli2021ability] although AMO studies until then (re)formulated the original necessity relationships as probabilistic sufficiency relationships and used regression analyses for testing them [e.g., @siemsen2008motivation].] 

*Established embedded necessity theories* are broadly accepted theories consisting of both necessity and probabilistic sufficiency relationships. Often, these theories are originally formulated as probabilistic sufficiency theories for all their relationships, and revisited to suggest a necessity perspective for one or more relationships. An example is @frommeyer2022utility's interpretation of one relationship of the Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB):  Intention of the behavior is necessary for Performance of the behavior, while keeping the other relationships as probabilistic sufficiency.^[In another example, @eccarius2024examining added Trust to the TPB model as a necessary antecedent of the drivers of Intention.] Another example is the Technology Acceptance Model (TAM) that is usually interpreted and studied as a probabilistic sufficiency theory and that was recently revisited with a necessity causal perspective for all relationships while maintaining also the probabilistic sufficiency perspective  [e.g., @richter2020predictors; @low2023isn; @kopplin2023chatbots; @su2023identifying; @erdmann2025influence; @hassan2025decentralized].^[These are examples of 'causal pluralism' studies where both perspectives are studied at the same time, see Chapter \@ref(multimethodstudies).] Also the Self-Determination Theory (SDT) was recently revisited from the perspective of necessity only [@ding2023using; @ding2025exploring] or in combination with the probabilistic sufficiency perspective [@cassia2024value]. SDT suggests that humans have three basic psychological needs (motivation, competence, relatedness) that must be satisfied for optimal human development and well-being.

Many established theories exist (not shown in Figure \@ref(fig:typestheory)) that *could be* revisited from a necessity perspective to develop pure or embedded necessity theories because their founders clearly employed necessity causal reasoning to formulate these theories. Examples include Porter’s theory on competitive advantage of nations [@porter1985competitive], Wernerfelt’s resource-based view of the firm [@wernerfelt1984resource], Teece’s theory on dynamic capabilities [@teece1997dynamic; @teece2007explicating], Rogers’ theory on person-centered psychotherapy [@rogers1957necessary], Turner’s framework for project management success [@turner2009handbook], and Meehl's concept of 'specific etiology' in medicine [@meehl1962schizotaxia]. Until recently, such theories were mostly tested using regression-based methods that implicitly assume probabilistic sufficiency, suggesting theory-method misfit (Table \@ref(tab:theorymethodfit)). With NCA available, testing necessity with a dedicated methodology becomes possible.

<!-- Also several other established theories that have been extensively studied from the perspective of probabilistic sufficiency have been revisited from the necessity perspective. For example Van Rhee and colleagues [@van2017limiting; @hauff2021hrm; @tuuli2021ability] revisited the Ability Motivation, Opportunity model (AMO) or Motivation, Opportunity, Ability (MOA) model of human behavior. They redefined the relationship between A, M and O as being necessary for behavior, assuming that all three must be present to show a specific behavior. -->

<!-- @frommeyer2022utility revisited the Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB) and formulated this theory as a necessity theory by redefining the relationships between Intention and Behavior from a necessity causal perspective^[The theory was tested with NCA showing that Intention is a necessary condition for Behavior. @rozenkowskatheory suggests to use the necessity perspective and NCA in future TPB studies.].  -->

<!-- Another example is the Technology Acceptance Model (TAM) that is studies as a probabilistic theory and was recently revisited with a necessity causal perspective [e.g., @richter2020predictors; @low2023isn; @kopplin2023chatbots; @su2023identifying]. The proposed necessity theories state that Usefulness and Ease of use and similar concepts are necessary for Intention to use and Technology use^[The studies test the necessity theory with NCA and most show that Ease of use and Usefulness are necessary conditions.]. -->

Since the introduction of NCA, the number of *emerging pure necessity theories* has risen. Appendix \@ref(appendixncaalone) includes examples of NCA studies in which only necessity relationships are theoretically formulated and tested with NCA. After replication and further theorizing, these emerging necessity theories may become established necessity theories.  An example of an *empirical* study with an emerging pure necessity theory is a study by @yanmatters that proposes and finds four necessary conditions for a country's high level of early COVID-19 mortality rate: high levels of a delayed first response, political decentralization, elderly populations, and urbanization. An example of a *theoretical* study with an emerging pure necessity theory is the study by @andrevski2022forbearance about defining the concept 'strategic forbearance'. When firms are attacked by competitors, forbearance is defined by three necessary conditions: the presence of awareness of the attack, the presence of capabilities to react, and the absence of a motivation to react. 

Many studies formulate *emerging embedded necessity theories*. Such studies often start with a probabilistic sufficiency theory based on earlier empirical regression-based studies and then suggest that one or more of the relationships could (also) be formulated as necessity relationships. In an *empirical* study @lee2021distinctive propose an emerging embedded necessity theory in which two dimensions of tourist happiness experience (Hedonic enjoyment and Personal expressiveness) are related to Satisfaction (customer’s judgment about their fulfillment with a product or service) and Place attachment (bonding between individuals and places). This is shown in the conceptual model of Figure \@ref(fig:FigLeeb). By conducting both NCA  and structural equation modeling (SEM), they conclude that Hedonic enjoyment (pleasure, comfort) has both a necessity relationship and a probabilistic sufficiency relationship with Satisfaction, but only a necessity relationship with Place attachment. Furthermore, they conclude that Personal expressiveness (a subjective experience of self-realization that one is acting in such a way that one is truly being oneself) has no probabilistic sufficiency relationship with Satisfaction, and both a necessity relationship and a probabilistic sufficiency relationship with Place attachment. Other examples of emerging embedded necessity theories may be found in empirical studies that apply NCA in combination with regression-based methods (Tables \@ref(tab:pubstablemlr), \@ref(tab:pubstablesem), and \@ref(tab:pubstableplssem) in Appendix \@ref(appendixa)). @liehr2022must propose in a *theoretical* study an emerging embedded necessity theory about leadership competencies for employee innovative behavior. They review a large number of competencies that contribute to innovative behavior and conclude that three competencies (according to the literature) have a probabilistic sufficiency relationship with innovative behavior but are not necessary (rewards, sharing expertise, resource management) and four competencies are (also) necessary: providing support, communicating a vision, granting autonomy and discretion, and providing feedback.

(ref:caption2)  Example of an emerging embedded necessity theory for the effect of two dimensions of happiness experiences (Hedonic enjoyment and Personal expressiveness) on two outcomes (Satisfaction and Place attachment). The symbols + represents a positive probabilistic relationship and the symbol nc represents a necessity relationship with high-high direction [After @lee2021distinctive].

```{r FigLeeb, echo=FALSE, fig.show="hold", message=FALSE, out.width = "90%", fig.align = 'center', fig.cap= "(ref:caption2)"}
knitr::include_graphics('FigLeeb2.png')
```

<!-- In addition, researchers often propose a pure necessity theory as part of a specific study in which NCA is used to test the theory. Examples of such necessity theories are a theory for lean manufacturing [@knol2018implementing], a theory for workplace spirituality [@garg2017workplace], and a theory for innovation in buyer-supply relationships [@van2016contracts].  -->

<!-- Other examples can be found in Table \@ref(tab:pubstablealone) in the Appendix.   -->

<!-- Second, _embedded necessity theories_ are theories that consist of a mix of necessity relations and other (e.g., probabilistic sufficiency) relations. For example, a theory may not only assume that certain concepts are probabilistic sufficiency causes that contribute on average to the outcome, but also that some concepts (additionally) are necessity causes. These theories are usually complex as their main goal is to describe how the outcome is produced (probabilistic sufficiency).  -->

<!-- @richter2020predictors's illustration of combining Partial Least Squares Structural Equation Modeling (PLS-SEM) with NCA could be seen as an example of an embedded necessity theory. Their extended  Technology Acceptance Model (TAM) was first formulated as a conventional structural model with probabilistic sufficiency relations that was tested with PLS-SEM and next they explored each relationship of the structural model from the perspective of necessity using NCA. The PLS-SEM results show that not all relationships of the TAM model were significant from the perspective of probabilistic sufficiency. Intention to use is related on average with Technology use, Usefulness is related on average with Intention to use, but not with Technology use, and Ease of use is neither related with Intention to use nor with Technological use. However, the NCA results show that all relationships of the TAM model were significant from the perspective of necessity. Based on these empirical results the embedded necessity TAM theory as shown in Figure \@ref(fig:fig5model) could be formulated. -->

<!-- ```{r fig5model, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE, out.width = "80%", fig.align = 'center', fig.cap= "Example of a conceptual model of an embedded necessity theory for the Technology Acceptance Model (TAM). A solid arrow with 'nc' represents a necessity relationship. A dashed arrows with '+' represents a probabilistic sufficiency relationship."} -->
<!-- knitr::include_graphics('Fig5model.png') -->
<!-- ```  -->

<!-- This theory states, for instance, that Intention to use not only helps to produce the Technology use on average (probabilistic sufficiency), but it is also necessary for it. -->

## Direction of a necessity relationship {#direction}
In Figure \@ref(fig:FigLeeb) the direction of the probabilistic sufficiency relationship is given by a + symbol above the arrow. This convention indicates a positive effect: $X$ increases $Y$ or $X$ has a positive effect on $Y$ or more precisely, that a higher value of $X$ increases the probability of a higher value of $Y$. Similarly, a – symbol indicates a negative effect: $X$ decreases or has a negative effect on $Y$: a higher value of $X$ decreases the probability of a higher value of $Y$.

Such single symbols do not convey meaning for a necessity relationship. In a necessity proposition the direction is not described by a verb as in $X$ *increases* $Y$ or $X$ *has* a positive effect on $Y$. For a necessity proposition a noun or adverb (or a combination) is used to describe relationships between levels of $X$ and $Y$. For example, in the dichotomous situation *absence* of $X$ is necessary for *presence* of $Y$ or in the continuous situation *low* level of $X$ is necessary for *high* level of $Y$. For a necessity relationship four possible directions exist (Figure \@ref(fig:direction1)). 

First, for a necessary condition with a *high-high* direction, the presence/high value of $X$ is necessary for presence/high value of $Y$. This is symbolized as \mbox{\text{+ nc +}} (see Figure \@ref(fig:direction1)- top-left). For example, high level of Urbanization is necessary for a high level of Early COVID-19 mortality [@yanmatters]. 

Second, for a necessary condition with a *low-high* direction, the absence/low value of $X$ is necessary for the presence/high value of $Y$ (\mbox{\text{- nc +}}). For example, low Intensity of production is necessary for high Environmental sustainability in agriculture [@lankoski2023environmental].

Third, for a necessary condition with a *high-low* direction, the presence/high value of $X$ is necessary for the absence/low value of $Y$ (\mbox{\text{+ nc -}}). For example, the presence/high value of social support is necessary for the absence/low value of stress. 

Fourth, for a necessary condition with a *low-low* direction, the absence/low value of $X$ is necessary for the absence/low value of $Y$ (\mbox{\text{- nc -}}). For example, the absence of rain is necessary for the absence of a wet surface. 

The third and fourth formulations of a necessary condition are less common than the first and second. Often the absence/low value of the outcome is redefined as the presence/high level of the opposite of the outcome. For example, the presence/high value of social support is necessary for the presence/high value of relaxation or the absence of rain is necessary for the presence of a dry surface. Thus, the direction of the necessary condition depends on the definition of the concepts that are part of the proposition.
 
In the absence of a specified direction, the implied direction is \mbox{\text{+ nc +}}, as shown in Figure \@ref(fig:FigLeeb). Throughout this book, the default assumption is the \mbox{\text{+ nc +}} direction, unless stated otherwise.

Figure \@ref(fig:direction1) shows the four possible conceptual models depending on the direction of the necessary condition.

```{r direction1, echo=FALSE, fig.show="hold", out.width='45%', fig.align="center", fig.cap="Conceptual models for necessary conditions. Top-Left: high-high = presence/high value of $X$ is necessary for presence/high value of $Y$. Top-Right: low-high = absence/low value of $X$ is necessary for presence/high value of $Y$. Bottom-Left: high-low = presence/high value of $X$ is necessary for absence/low value of $Y$. Bottom-Right: low-low = absence/low value of $X$ is necessary for absence/low value of $Y$."}
 par(mfrow = c(2,2))
 knitr::include_graphics("Fig1modelDIRECTION.png")
 knitr::include_graphics("Fig2modelDIRECTION.png")
 knitr::include_graphics("Fig3modelDIRECTION.png")
 knitr::include_graphics("Fig4modelDIRECTION.png")
```



```{r direction2, echo=FALSE, fig.show="hold", out.width='45%', fig.align='center', fig.cap="$XY$-tables showing empty corners without observations depending on the direction of the necessity relationship. Top-Left: high-high = presence of $X$ is necessary for presence of $Y$. Top-Right: low-high = absence of $X$ is necessary for presence of $Y$. Bottom-Left: high-low = presence of $X$ is necessary for absence of $Y$. Bottom-Right: low-low = absence of $X$ is necessary for absence of $Y$."}
 par(mfrow = c(2,2))
 knitr::include_graphics("Fig1emptydich.png")
 knitr::include_graphics("Fig2emptydich.png")
 knitr::include_graphics("Fig3emptydich.png")
 knitr::include_graphics("Fig4emptydich.png")
```


## Expected empty corner {#expectedemptycorner}
Depending on the direction of necessity, different corners in the $XY$-table or $XY$-plot are expected to be empty. For the *dichotomous situation*, the $XY$-table of Figure \@ref(fig:cornerssufficiencynecessity)-right illustrates necessity causality when the presence of $X$ is necessary for the presence of $Y$. Corner 1 cannot have observations (points, cases) whereas the other corners may have observations. This is also shown in Figure \@ref(fig:direction2)-top-left. In Figure \@ref(fig:direction2) rows represent the two values of the outcome $Y$ (Absent/Present), and columns the two values of the condition $X$. The condition $X$ increases to the right, and the outcome $Y$ increases upward. Each corner in the table represents a certain combination of $X$ and $Y$. For example, the upper-left corner represents absence of $X$ and presence of $Y$. A gray corner with a black dot indicates that the corner may contain observations. A white corner without dots indicates that no observations are possible.
<!-- While a Venn diagram is commonly employed to illustrate a necessary condition, it cannot visualize necessity "in degree" (see Section \@ref(necessityindegree)). On the other hand, a contingency table can be expanded to accommodate various discrete levels of variables $X$ and $Y$.  -->

When the presence of $X$ is necessary for the presence of $Y$, the upper-left corner is empty. This corresponds to the necessity statement 'if not $X$, then not $Y$' (see the necessity statement of expression \@ref(eq:necnec) in Chapter \@ref(causality)). The outcome is absent when the condition is absent. This means that the upper-left corner does not have observations, and that the lower-left corner has observations.
