Similarly, when the outcome is present, the condition must also be present: the upper-right corner has observations. The content of the lower-right corner is irrelevant for \mbox{\text{+ nc +}} necessity:  this corner may or may not have observations when the necessary condition applies. For \mbox{\text{+ nc +}} necessity, the emptiness of the upper-left corner is essential.

For other directions of the necessary condition, other corners in the $XY$-table remain empty when necessity applies. When \mbox{\text{- nc +}} necessity is valid, the upper-right corner remains empty. Similarly, for \mbox{\text{+ nc -}} necessity, the lower-left corner is empty, and for \mbox{\text{- nc -}} necessity, the lower-right corner stays unoccupied. Therefore, the empty corner depends on how the necessary condition is formulated in the theory (and how the direction of the axes and the concepts are defined). Each formulation of the necessity proposition results in a specific corner of the $XY$-table being empty, referred to as the *expected empty corner*.

For the continuous situation, Figure \@ref(fig:corners) shows the four directions and corresponding empty corners. Here an $XY$-plot instead of a $XY$-table is used as a visualization method for representing that a low/high value of the condition is necessary for a low/high value of the outcome. The condition $X$ increases to the right, and the outcome $Y$ increases upward. Each corner in the plot represents a certain combination of low/high $X$- and $Y$-values. For example, the upper-left corner represents low level of $X$ and high level of $Y$. A gray area indicates that the area may contain observations.


```{r corners, echo=FALSE, fig.show="hold", out.width='50%', fig.cap="$XY$-plots showing the direction of a necessity relationship. Top-Left: a high value of $X$ is necessary for a high value of $Y$ (+ nc +,  high-high). Top-Right: a low value of $X$ is necessary for a high value of $Y$ (- nc +, low-high). Bottom-Left: a high value of $X$ is necessary for a low value of $Y$ (+ nc -, high-low). Bottom-Right: a low value of $X$ is necessary for a low value of $Y$ (- nc -, low-low)."}
 #par(mar = c(4, 4, .1, .1))
 par(mfrow = c(2,2))
 knitr::include_graphics("corner1.png")
 knitr::include_graphics("corner2.png")
 knitr::include_graphics("corner3.png")
 knitr::include_graphics("corner4.png")
```


## Double necessity {#morecorners} 
It is possible that a single factor has two necessity relationships with the outcome at the same time, implying that more than one corner is expected to be empty. When the concepts are continuous, it is possible that two *adjacent* corners are empty. This allows theorizing that an *optimum* (not low, not high) level of $X$ is necessary for a *high* level of $Y$, implying that the upper-left *and* the upper-right corners are expected to be empty (Figure \@ref(fig:corners1234)-left). For example, in team performance, moderate levels of conflict ($X$) may be necessary for high team innovation performance ($Y$). Low conflict leads to group think, which may limit performance; high conflict leads to dysfunction, which also limits team innovation performance. Therefore, a moderate level of conflict enables teams to challenge each other productively, providing the possibility for high innovation performance.  
Similarly, it may be theorized that an *optimum* (not low, not high) level of $X$ is necessary for a *low* level of $Y$, implying that the lower-left and lower-right corners are expected to be empty (Figure \@ref(fig:corners1234)-right). For example, in stress management, moderate levels of workload ($X$) may be necessary for low employee burnout ($Y$). Low workload can result in boredom and lack of purpose, which may limit the possibility of low burnout; high workload can cause stress and exhaustion, which also limits the possibility of low burnout. Therefore, a moderate workload is necessary for low burnout because both too low and too high workload create psychological strain that prevents burnout from remaining low.


```{r corners1234, echo=FALSE, fig.show="hold", out.width='45%', fig.align='center', fig.cap="$XY$-plot showing an optimum level of the necessary condition. Left: An optimum level of $x_{c1} <x < x_{c2}$ is necessary for a high level of $y = y_c$. Right: An optimum level of $x_{c1} < x < x_{c2}$ is necessary for a low level of $y = y_c$."}
 par(mfrow = c(1,2))
 knitr::include_graphics("corners12.png")
 knitr::include_graphics("corners34.png")
```

It is also possible to theorize that a *high* level of $X$ is necessary for an *extreme* (low or high) level of $Y$. Then the upper-left and the lower-left corners are expected to be empty (Figure \@ref(fig:corners1324)-left). Similarly, it may be theorized that a *low* level of $X$ is necessary for an *extreme* (low or high) level of $Y$. Then the upper-right and the lower-right corners are expected to be empty (Figure \@ref(fig:corners1324)-right).


```{r corners1324, echo=FALSE, fig.show="hold", out.width='45%', fig.align='center', fig.cap="$XY$-plot showing necessity for an extreme level of the outcome. Left: A high level of $x > x_c$ is necessary for an extreme (low or high) level of $y = y_{c1}$ or $y = y_{c2}$. Right: A low level of $x < x_c$ is necessary for an extreme (low or high) level of $y = y_{c1}$ or $y = y_{c2}$."}
 par(mfrow = c(1,2))
 knitr::include_graphics("corners13.png")
 knitr::include_graphics("corners24.png")
```


The situations shown in Figures \@ref(fig:corners1234) and \@ref(fig:corners1324) do not have dichotomous equivalents, as this would imply that the outcome or the condition is always absent (Figure \@ref(fig:adjacenttwocorners)-top-left) or always present (Figure \@ref(fig:adjacenttwocorners)- top-right), or that the condition is always present (Figure \@ref(fig:adjacenttwocorners)-bottom-left), or always absent (Figure \@ref(fig:adjacenttwocorners)-bottom-right).

```{r adjacenttwocorners, echo=FALSE, fig.show="hold", out.width='40%', fig.align='center', fig.cap="$XY$-tables showing two adjacent empty corners without observations. Top-Left: presence of $Y$ is not possible. Top-Right: absence of $Y$ is not possible. Bottom-Left: absence of $X$ is not possible. Bottom-Right: presence of $X$ is not possible."}
 par(mfrow = c(2,2))
 knitr::include_graphics("Fig1two_emptydich.png")
 knitr::include_graphics("Fig2two_emptydich.png")
 knitr::include_graphics("Fig3two_emptydich.png")
 knitr::include_graphics("Fig4two_emptydich.png")
```


When two *opposite* corners are empty, as shown in Figure \@ref(fig:corners1423)-left, two claims apply: the *presence/high level* of $X$ is necessary for the *presence/high level* of $Y$ (empty upper-left corner) and that the *absence/low level* of $X$ is necessary for the *absence/low level* of $Y$ (empty lower-right corner). Similarly, it may be theorized that the *absence/low level* of $X$ is necessary for the *presence/high level* of $Y$ (empty upper-right corner) and that the *presence/high level* of $X$ is necessary for the *absence/low level* of $Y$ (empty lower-left corner). This is shown in Figure \@ref(fig:corners1423)-right. This situation has dichotomous equivalents, as shown in Figure \@ref(fig:oppositetwocorners).


```{r corners1423, echo=FALSE, fig.show="hold", out.width='45%', fig.align='center', fig.cap="Double necessity. Left: A high level of $x > x_{c1}$ is necessary for a high level of $y = y_{c1}$, and a low level of $x < x_{c2}$ is necessary for a low level of $y = y_{c2}$. Right: A low level of $x < x_{c1}$ is necessary for a high level of $y = y_{c1}$, and a high level of $x > x_{c2}$ is necessary for a low level of $y = y_{c2}$."}
 par(mfrow = c(1,2))
 knitr::include_graphics("corners14.png")
 knitr::include_graphics("corners23.png")
```


```{r oppositetwocorners, echo=FALSE, fig.show="hold", out.width='45%', fig.align='center', fig.cap="$XY$-tables showing two opposite empty corners without observations. Left: the presence of $X$ is necessary for the presence of $Y$ and the absence of $X$ is necessary for the absence of $Y$. Right: the absence of $X$ is necessary for the presence of $Y$ and the presence of $X$ is necessary for the absence of $Y$."}
 par(mfrow = c(1,2))
 knitr::include_graphics("Fig1two_opposite_emptydich.png")
 knitr::include_graphics("Fig2two_opposite_emptydich.png")

```


<!-- The final possible situation with two empty corners is that a researcher theorizes that a *high* level of $X$ is necessary for a *high* level of $Y$ and a *low* level of $X$ is necessary for a *low* level of $Y$ (Figure \@ref(fig:corners1423), left). Similarly, a researcher may theorize that a *low* level of $X$ is necessary for a *high* level of $Y$ and that a *high* level of $X$ is necessary for a *low* level of $Y$. Then two opposite corners are empty (Figure \@ref(fig:corners1423), right). -->

<!-- In the ```NCA``` software, it is possible to do a combined statistical test of the two necessity relations of a single factor by using the argument ```effect_aggregation``` in the ```nca_analysis``` function. The software estimates the aggregate effect size, which is the sum of the effect sizes of the separate empty spaces, and its $p$-value. This can be illustrated with the example that a country's high level of Risk taking is necessary for a high level and sufficient for a low level of a country's Innovation performance. -->


<!-- ```{r, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE,  eval=FALSE, echo=FALSE, include=FALSE} -->
<!-- library(NCA) -->
<!-- data(nca.example) -->
<!-- #High Risk taking is necessary for High Innovation performance -->
<!-- model1 <- nca_analysis(nca.example, -->
<!--                        2,3, -->
<!--                        corner = 1, -->
<!--                        ceilings = "ce_fdh", -->
<!--                        test.rep = 10000) -->
<!-- model1 -->
<!-- nca_output(model1, plots = T, summaries = F) -->

<!-- #High Risk taking is sufficient for low Innovation performance -->
<!-- model2 <- nca_analysis(nca.example -->
<!--                        ,2,3, -->
<!--                        corner = 4, -->
<!--                        ceilings = "ce_fdh", -->
<!--                        test.rep = 10000) -->
<!-- model2 -->
<!-- nca_output(model2, plots = T, summaries = F) -->

<!-- #High Risk taking is necessary for high Innovation performance and sufficient for low Innovation performance -->
<!-- model3 <- nca_analysis(nca.example, -->
<!--                        2,3, -->
<!--                        effect_aggregation = c(4), -->
<!--                        ceilings = "ce_fdh", -->
<!--                        test.rep = 10000) -->
<!-- model3 -->
<!-- ``` -->

<!-- Model 1 tests the emptiness of the upper-left corner; model 2 of the lower-right corner and model 3 is the aggregate test. The results of the latter test shows that the combined necessity/sufficiency relationship of Risk taking for Innovation performance is not rejected. -->



## The meaning of 'necessary but not sufficient' {#necessarybutnotsufficient}
To understand what is necessary *but not* sufficient, this section first explains what is meant by necessary *and* sufficient. In the dichotomous situation, 'necessary *and* sufficient' means that the upper-left corner and the lower-right corner of the $XY$-plot are both empty. This is shown in Figure \@ref(fig:oppositetwocorners), which is interpreted as a *double necessity* relationship of $X$ for $Y$: a necessity relationship where the presence of $X$ is necessary for the presence of $Y$ (upper-left corner empty) and a necessity relationship where the absence of $X$ is necessary for the absence of $Y$ (lower-right corner empty). By logic, in the dichotomous situation the second relationship can be reformulated as a *sufficiency* relationship where the presence of $X$ is sufficient for the presence of $Y$, as shown in Figure \@ref(fig:cornerssufficiencynecessity)-left. The first relationship (necessity) and the second relationship (sufficiency) now have the same $X$-value (presence) and the same $Y$-value (presence). This allows a single statement that the presence of $X$ is necessary *and* sufficient for the presence of $Y$. If the sufficiency relationship does not hold, the formulation of the combined relationship would be: the presence of $X$ is necessary *but not* sufficient for the presence of $Y$. This means that the lower-right corner is not empty.

In discrete and continuous situations, necessity *and* sufficiency are more complex. Like in the dichotomous situation, the upper-left corner and the lower-right corner are both empty. This is shown in Figure \@ref(fig:corners1423)-left, which is interpreted as a double necessity relationship of $X$ for $Y$: a necessity relationship where a high level of $X$ is necessary for a high level of $Y$ (upper-left corner empty) and a necessity relationship where a low level of $X$ is necessary for a low level of $Y$ (lower-right corner empty). By logic, the second relationship can again be reformulated as a sufficiency relationship: a not-low level of $X$ is sufficient for a not-low level of $Y$. However, a 'not-low' level is usually not the same as a 'high' level. For example, in Figure \@ref(fig:corners1423)-left, level $X = x_{c1}$ is necessary for level $Y = y_{c1}$, whereas level $X = x_{c2}$ is sufficient for level $Y = y_{c2}$. There are no common $X$-levels and common $Y$-levels that would allow a single statement that level $X = x_{c}$ is necessary *and* sufficient for level $Y = y_{c}$, unless the upper ceiling line and the lower ceiling line (floor line) overlap or intersect at the point (1,1). If the lines overlap there are only observations on the ceiling line and the necessity *and* sufficiency statement holds for all observations. If the lines intersect at point [1,1], only at that point the necessity *and* sufficiency statement holds. Both situations are, however, exceptional. 

In all other situations the statement that level $X = x_{c}$ is necessary *but not* sufficient for level $Y = y_{c}$ has a broader meaning than that the lower-right corner is not empty. If the lower-right corner can have observations, observations are normally possible in the entire area below the ceiling line, not just in the lower-right corner. This allows a single statement that $X = x_{c}$ is necessary *but not* sufficient for $Y = y_{c}$. When $X = x_{c}$, no observations are above point $C$ on the ceiling line, whereas observations below point $C_1$ are possible. In other words, for a given level $X = x_{c}$, it is not possible to have values of $Y > y_{c}$ (necessity applies) but it is possible to have values $Y \leq y_{c}$ ("non-sufficiency" applies). Consequently, where in the dichotomous situation the statement $X$ is necessary *but not* sufficient for $Y$ means that observations are possible in the lower-right corner, in the discrete and continuous situations it means that there are observations below the ceiling line. 
NCA focuses on the identification of necessity (rather than on the identification of non-sufficiency) in any corner of the $XY$-table or the $XY$-plot. If the focus is on the lower-right corner, the emptiness of this corner is interpreted as indicating that the absence or a low level of $X$ is necessary for the absence or a low level of $Y$.

<!-- such that it is not possible to formulate one statement that a given level of $X$is necessary and sufficient for a particular level of $Y$   -->

<!-- the present of $X$ is necessary for the presence of $Y$, as shown in Figure \@ref(fig:corners1423)-left.    -->




<!-- two meanings depening on wheter the relationship is approach 'in kind' or 'in degree' For necessity in kind degree,  -->

<!-- depending on that the upper-left and the lower-right corner of the XY plot are both empty. This is shown in Figure \@ref(fig:oppositetwocorners) and can be interpreted as a double necessity relationship of $X$ for $Y$: a necessity relationship where the presence of $X$ is necessary for the presence of $Y$ (upper-left corner empty) and a necessity relationship where the absence of $X$ is necessary for the absence of $Y$ (lower-right corner empty). The second relationship can be reformulated as a sufficiency relationship where the present of $X$ is necessary for the presence of $Y$, as shown in Figure \@ref(fig:corners1423)-Left.   -->



<!-- the condition (the presence of $X$) and the presence of $Y$ has both a necessity and a sufficiency relations      -->

<!-- means that there are two necessity relationships as s  Left this is formul  -->

<!-- It is the combination of the two XY tables that are shown in Figure \@ref(fig:cornerssufficiencynecessity),  -->

<!-- resulting in the XY plot shown in -->
<!-- . $X$ has two separate necessity relationships with $Y$: . The second relationship can be reformulated as a sufficiency relationship where the present of $X$ is necessary for the presence of $Y$ (lower-right corner empty). The second relationship  -->

<!-- oppositetwocorners -->
<!-- Figure \@ref(fig:corners1423) Left. , and that the other two corners have points.  -->

<!-- Often the phrase ‘necessary but not sufficient’ is used. To understand this expression in the context of NCA two situations should be distinguished: the qualitative approach of necessity ‘in kind’, and the quantitative approach of necessity ‘in degree’ (see section \@ref(binarytocontinuous)).  In the context of necessity in kind ($X$ is necessary for $Y$), the focus is on the location and size of the empty space. An empty space in the upper-left corner in the $XY$-table or $XY$-plot suggests that the presence/high value of $X$ is necessary for the presence/high value of $Y$. Similarly, an empty space in the lower-right corner in the $XY$-table or XY plot suggests that the absence/low value of $X$ is necessary for the absence/low value of $Y$. When $X$ and $Y$ are dichotomous, an empty space in the lower-right corner can also be interpreted as a sufficient condition: the presence of $X$ is sufficient for the presence of $Y$. When both the upper-left and the upper-right corners are empty, the statement could be that $X$ is necessary *and* sufficient for $Y$. When the upper-left corner is empty while the lower-right corners may have points, the statement could be that the presence of $X$ is necessary *but not* sufficient for the presence $Y$. -->

<!-- The context of necessity-in-degree (level $X$ is necessary for level $Y$ is somethat more complex. An empty space in both the upper-left and lower-right corners should be interpreted as $X$ having two distinct relationships with $Y$. As discussed in section \@ref(morecorners), particularly Figure \@ref(fig:r corners1423) Left, in necessity-in-degree an empty space in the upper-left corner means that  level $X > X_{c1}$ is necessary for level  $Y = Y_{c1}$ and an empty space in the lower-right corner of the $XY$-plot means that level $X < X_{c2}$ is necessary for level $Y = Y_{c2}$. Although the the latter expression can be reformulated as:  level $X > X_{c2}$ is sufficient for level $Y = Y_{c2}$, there is no level of $X$ taht is necessary *and* $sufficient$ for a given level of $Y$,  unless the ceiling line in the upper-left corner and the line in the lower-right corner ('floor line') overlap.  However, this is an exceptional situation that hardly exits in reality. Normally, the floor line is below the ceiling line, which means that level  $X > X_{c}$ is necessary *but not* sufficient for level $Y = Y_{c2}$. It is therefore not uncommon that a necessary condition in degree is almos 'automatically' necessary *but not* sufficient. -->




<!-- Figure \@ref(fig:corners) shows a single factor with a one necessity relationship, and Figures \@ref(fig:corners1234), \@ref(fig:corners1324), and \@ref(fig:corners1423) show a single factor with two necessity relationships. In all figures, consistent with necessity, there are no observations in the empty space, whereas observations are possible in the gray area. If the necessity relationship holds, and also no observations were in the gray area, all observations would be exactly on the border line. Then, the necessary condition is also a sufficient conditions. However, this is not realistic as single necessary conditions are seldom also sufficient. This implies that the words 'but not sufficient' can normally be added to all necessity statements. For example, Figure \@ref(fig:corners) (Top-Left), shows a single factor with one necessary condition: only the upper-left corner is empty. This corresponds to the statement "a high level of $X$ is necessary for a high level of $Y$". If all points were on the border line (ceiling line) the condition is also sufficient. This results in the statement: 'a high level of $X$ is necessary *and sufficient* for a high level of $Y$'. In practice, observations will appear below the ceiling line. This justifies the statement that 'a high level of $X$ is necessary *but not sufficient* for a high level of $Y$'. -->

<!-- Figure \@ref(fig:corners1234) (Left) is an example of a factor with two necessity relations, 'a high level of $X$ is necessary for a high level of $Y$' and 'a low level of $X$ is necessary for a high level of $Y$'. This can be reformuled as: 'an optimum level of $X$ is necessary for a high level of $Y$'. If all observations were on the two ceiling lines, the words 'and sufficient' can be added: 'an optimum level of $X$ is necessary and sufficient for $Y$'. Again, in practice observations show up below the border lines such that the words 'but not sufficient' can be added: 'an optimum level of $X$ is necessary but not sufficient for $Y$'. Similar formulations are possible for Figures \@ref(fig:corners1324): a high level of $X$ (Figure \@ref(fig:corners1324), Left) or a low level of $X$ (Figure \@ref(fig:corners1324), Right) is necessary but not sufficient for an extreme (low or high) level of $Y$. The comparable formulations related to Figure \@ref(fig:corners1423) are: '    -->


<!-- ######### -->
<!-- In the dichotomous case this means that it is expected that the presence of a single condition is both necessary and sufficient for the presence of the outcome. In the continuous case it means that it is expected a high level of a single condition is necessary for a high level of the outcome and sufficient for a "not so high" level of the outcome -->

<!-- In the dichotomous case this means that it is expected that the absence of a single condition is both necessary and sufficient for the presence of the outcome. In the continuous case it means that it is expected the low level of single condition is necessary for a *high* level of the outcome and sufficient for a "not so high" level of the outcome  (Figure \@ref(fig:corners1423), Right). -->
<!-- ############# -->



<!-- Comparable formulation   -->

<!-- The same holds for any other necessity relationship shown in Figures \@ref(fig:corners), \@ref(fig:corners1234), \@ref(fig:corners1324), and \@ref(fig:corners1423). -->

<!-- Any necessity statement can be rephrased as a sufficiency statement by formulating its logical complement. For example, the upper-left corner of Figure \@ref(fig:corners1234) (Left) (ignoring the upper-right corner) indicates that a high level of $X$ is necessary but not sufficient for a high value of $Y$. This can be reformulated as 'the absence of a high level of $X$ is sufficient but not necessary for the absence of a high value of $Y$'. This means that a level of $X < X_{c1}$ guarantees (is sufficient for) $Y < Y_c$. Similarly, the lower-right corner of Figure \@ref(fig:corners1234) (Right) (ignoring the lower-left corner) indicates that a low level of $X$ is necessary but not sufficient for a low value of $Y$. This can be reformulated as: 'the absence of a low level of $X$ is sufficient but not necessary for the absence of a high level of $Y$'. A value of $X > X_{c2}$ guarantees that $Y > Y_c$.  -->

<!-- When a single factor has two necessary relationships, it is possible to use the necessity formulation for one necessary condition, and the sufficiency formulation for the other. For example, in Figure \@ref(fig:corners1423), (Left) the double necessity formulation is: 'a high level of $X \geq\ X_{1c}$ is necessary but not sufficient for a high level of $Y = Y_{c1}$', and 'the absence or low level of $X \leq  X_{c2}$ is necessary but not sufficient for the absence or low level of $Y = Y_{2c}$'. When the necessity formulation is used for the upper-left necessity relation, and the sufficiency formulation for the lower-right necessity relation, the new equivalent statement is: 'a high level of $X \geq X_{1c}$ is necessary but not sufficient for a high level of $Y = Y_{c1}$', and 'a high level of $X \leq X_{c2}$ is sufficient but not necessary for a high level of $Y = Y_{2c}$'.  -->


<!-- Note that dual statements cannot be combined into a single statement, because the two border lines have a different location. This means that for a given $Y$ there are different levels of $X$ (see Figures \@ref(fig:corners1234), and \@ref(fig:corners1423)) and for a given $X$ there different levels of $Y$ (see Figures \@ref(fig:corners1324), and \@ref(fig:corners1423)). -->

<!-- Sometimes, like for the situation in Figure \@ref(fig:corners1423) (Left),  double necessity can be formulated 'in kind' as '$X$ is necessary and sufficient for $Y$', which implies that the upper-left necessity relationship is formulated in terms of necessity, and the lower-right in terms of sufficiency. However, this formulation is problematic when the actual necessity relationships are 'in degree'. The 'in degree' formulation of the situation is *not*: 'a high level of $X$ is necessary and sufficient for a high level of $Y$', but instead: '$X > X_{c1}$ is necessary for $Y = Y_{c1}$ and $X > X_{c2}$ is sufficient for $Y = Y_{c2}$'. There is no common $X$ that is necessary and sufficient for a common $Y$ and no common $Y$ for which a common $X$ is necessary and sufficient. -->

<!-- Only when the two borderlines overlap, the 'in kind' statement also holds in an 'in degree' situation: 'a certain level of $X$ is necessary and sufficient for a certain level of $Y$'. At best, the 'in kind' formulation could be re-formulated as 'a high level of $X$ is necessary for a high level of $Y$ and sufficient for a not so high level of $Y$', or similar. The theoretical meaning is that a high level of $X$ is necessary for (enables) a high level of $Y$ (border line = ceiling line) and is sufficient for (ensures) a lower level of $Y$ (border line = floor line).    -->

<!-- XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX -->

<!-- For example, a theory that assumes probabilistic sufficiency causal relationship between certain concepts, can additionally assume a typicality necessity relationship between these or other concepts of the theory. -->

<!-- on a for some of the relationship   perspective,  some of the relations when NCA is combined with a regression-based analysis, some relations are only combines with  dbetween concepts are also considered but also this way, wherease other are condiealso other types of relationships       -->


<!-- s between all or a selection of the concepts of a theory [@bokrantz2022building]. Necessity *theorizing* is formulating necessity theory using necessity logic when describing causal relationships between concepts.   -->


<!-- One  theory about the necessary conditions for success of start-up firms was developed based on literature and common beliefs, but this necessity theory was rejected after empirical testing with NCA [@arenius2017no].  -->

<!-- ^[A moderator in a probabilistic theory is a concept that influences the average *relationship* between other concepts. A moderator  does not exist in a necessity theory, as the necessity relationship can only exist between concepts] -->


<!-- XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX START OF THE CODE FOR THIS CHAPTER XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX -->
```{r figs123theorycode, echo=FALSE, include=FALSE, eval = FALSE}
library(DiagrammeR)
library(DiagrammeRsvg)
library(rsvg)

# Function to export a figure as a PDF
export_figure_to_pdf <- function(fig, filename) {
  svg_content <- fig %>% DiagrammeRsvg::export_svg()
  pdf_content <- charToRaw(svg_content) %>% rsvg::rsvg_pdf()
  writeBin(pdf_content, filename)
}

# Fig1
Fig1 <- grViz('
  digraph {
    # Set graph properties for horizontal layout
    graph [layout = dot, rankdir = LR]

    # Set node properties with font family "Helvetica"
    node [shape = box, style = filled, fillcolor = grey, fontname = "Helvetica", fontsize = 10]

    # Define nodes with font family "sans"
    X [label = "X"] # Box labeled "X"
    Y [label = "Y"]

    # Define edges and arrow properties
    X -> Y [dir = forward, arrowhead = normal, arrowsize = 0.5, penwidth = 0.5, label = "nc", fontname = "Helvetica", fontsize = 8] # Connect the new box above X2 to X2 with a transparent arrow
  }')
#plot Fig1
Fig1

# Uncomment and use the following lines to create a PDF and high-resolution PNG for Fig1
 export_figure_to_pdf(Fig1, "Fig1model.pdf")


# Fig2
Fig2 <- grViz('
  digraph {
    # Set graph properties for horizontal layout
    graph [layout = dot, rankdir = LR]

    # Set node properties with font family "Helvetica"
    node [shape = box, style = filled, fillcolor = grey, fontname = "Helvetica", fontsize = 10]

    # Define nodes with font family "sans"
    X1 [label = "X1"] # Box labeled "X1"
    Y [label = "Y"]

    # Add a new node
    X2 [label = "X2", shape = box, style = filled, fillcolor = grey, fontname = "Helvetica", fontsize = 10]

   # Add a new node
    X3 [label = "X3", shape = box, style = filled, fillcolor = grey, fontname = "Helvetica", fontsize = 10]

    # Define edges and arrow properties
    X1 -> Y [dir = forward, arrowhead = normal, arrowsize = 0.5, penwidth = 0.5, label = "nc", fontname = "Helvetica", fontsize = 8] # Connect the new box above X2 to X2 with a transparent arrow
    X2 -> Y [dir = forward, arrowhead = normal, arrowsize = 0.5, penwidth = 0.5, label = "nc", fontname = "Helvetica", fontsize = 8] # Connect X2 to Y with a straight arrow
    X3 -> Y [dir = forward, arrowhead = normal, arrowsize = 0.5, penwidth = 0.5, label = "nc", fontname = "Helvetica", fontsize = 8] # Connect X2 to Y with a straight arrow
  }')
#plot Fig2
Fig2

# Uncomment and use the following lines to create a PDF and high-resolution PNG for Fig2
 export_figure_to_pdf(Fig2, "Fig2model.pdf")

# Fig3
Fig3 <- grViz('
  digraph {
    # Set graph properties for horizontal layout
    graph [layout = dot, rankdir = LR]

    # Set node properties with font family "Helvetica"
    node [shape = box, style = filled, fillcolor = grey, fontname = "Helvetica", fontsize = 10]

    # Define nodes with font family "sans"
    X [label = "X"] # Box labeled "X"
    Y1 [label = "Y1"]

    # Add a new node
    Y2 [label = "Y2", shape = box, style = filled, fillcolor = grey, fontname = "Helvetica", fontsize = 10]

   # Add a new node
    Y3 [label = "Y3", shape = box, style = filled, fillcolor = grey, fontname = "Helvetica", fontsize = 10]

    # Define edges and arrow properties
    X -> Y1 [dir = forward, arrowhead = normal, arrowsize = 0.5, penwidth = 0.5, label = "nc", fontname = "Helvetica", fontsize = 8] # Connect the new box above X2 to X2 with a transparent arrow
    X -> Y2 [dir = forward, arrowhead = normal, arrowsize = 0.5, penwidth = 0.5, label = "nc", fontname = "Helvetica", fontsize = 8] # Connect X2 to Y with a straight arrow
    X -> Y3 [dir = forward, arrowhead = normal, arrowsize = 0.5, penwidth = 0.5, label = "nc", fontname = "Helvetica", fontsize = 8] # Connect X2 to Y with a straight arrow
  }')
#plot Fig3
Fig3

# Uncomment and use the following lines to create a PDF and high-resolution PNG for Fig3
 export_figure_to_pdf(Fig3, "Fig3model.pdf")

# Fig4
Fig4 <- grViz('
  digraph {
    # Set graph properties for horizontal layout
    graph [layout = dot, rankdir = LR]

    # Set node properties with font family "Helvetica"
    node [shape = box, style = filled, fillcolor = grey, fontname = "Helvetica", fontsize = 10]

    # Define nodes with font family "sans"
    X1 [label = "X1"] # Box labeled "X1"
    Y [label = "Y"]

    # Add a new node
    X2 [label = "X2", shape = box, style = filled, fillcolor = grey, fontname = "Helvetica", fontsize = 10]

    # Define edges and arrow properties
    X1 -> X2 [dir = forward, arrowhead = normal, arrowsize = 0.5, penwidth = 0.5, label = "nc", fontname = "Helvetica", fontsize = 8] # Connect the new box above X2 to X2 with a transparent arrow
    X2 -> Y [dir = forward, arrowhead = normal, arrowsize = 0.5, penwidth = 0.5, label = "nc", fontname = "Helvetica", fontsize = 8] # Connect X2 to Y with a straight arrow
  }')
#plot Fig4
Fig4

# Uncomment and use the following lines to create a PDF and high-resolution PNG for Fig4
 export_figure_to_pdf(Fig4, "Fig4model.pdf")
```
 
```{r figtamcode, echo=FALSE, include=FALSE, eval = FALSE}
library(DiagrammeR)
library(DiagrammeRsvg)
library(rsvg)

 # FigTAM
 FigTAM <- grViz('
  digraph {
    # Set graph properties for horizontal layout
    graph [layout = dot, rankdir = LR]

    # Set node properties with font family "Helvetica"
    node [shape = box, style = filled, fillcolor = grey, fontname = "Helvetica", fontsize = 10]

    # Define nodes with font family "sans"
    X1 [label = "Usefulness"] # 
    X2 [label = "Ease of use"] # 
    Y [label = "Technolology Use "]

    # Add a new node
    M [label = "Intention to use", shape = box, style = filled, fillcolor = grey, fontname = "Helvetica", fontsize = 10]

    # Define edges and arrow properties
    X1 -> M [dir = forward, arrowhead = normal, arrowsize = 0.5, penwidth = 0.5, label = "nc", fontname = "Helvetica", fontsize = 8] # Connect the new box above X2 to X2 with a transparent arrow
    X2 -> M [dir = forward, arrowhead = normal, arrowsize = 0.5, penwidth = 0.5, label = "nc", fontname = "Helvetica", fontsize = 8] # Connect the new box above X2 to X2 with a transparent arrow
    M -> Y [dir = forward, arrowhead = normal, arrowsize = 0.5, penwidth = 0.5, label = "nc", fontname = "Helvetica", fontsize = 8] # Connect X2 to Y with a straight arrow
    X1 -> Y [dir = forward, arrowhead = normal, arrowsize = 0.5, penwidth = 0.5, label = "nc", fontname = "Helvetica", fontsize = 8] # Connect the new box above X2 to X2 with a transparent arrow
    X2 -> Y [dir = forward, arrowhead = normal, arrowsize = 0.5, penwidth = 0.5, label = "nc", fontname = "Helvetica", fontsize = 8] # Connect the new box above X2 to X2 with a transparent arrow
    M -> Y [dir = forward, arrowhead = normal, arrowsize = 0.5, penwidth = 0.5, label = "+", fontname = "Helvetica", fontsize = 8, style = "dashed"] # Connect X2 to Y with a straight arrow
    X1 -> M [dir = forward, arrowhead = normal, arrowsize = 0.5, penwidth = 0.5, label = "+", fontname = "Helvetica", fontsize = 8, style = "dashed"] # 
  }')
 #plot FigTAM
 FigTAM
 
 # Uncomment and use the following lines to create a PDF and high-resolution PNG for Fig5
 export_figure_to_pdf(FigTAM, "FigTAM.pdf") 
```



```{r figleeb_code, echo=FALSE, include=FALSE, eval = FALSE}
library(ggplot2)
library(grid)

# Define node positions (center point of box)
x_offset_right <- 3

nodes <- data.frame(
  name = c("Hedonic enjoyment", "Personal expressiveness", "Satisfaction", "Place attachment"),
  x = c(1, 1, x_offset_right, x_offset_right),
  y = c(1.5, 1, 1.5, 1)
)

# Arrow data
n_arrows <- 5
arrows <- data.frame(
  from_x = c(1, 1, 1, 1, 1),
  from_y = c(1.5, 1.5, 1.5, 1, 1),
  to_x = rep(x_offset_right, n_arrows),
  to_y = c(1.55, 1.45, 1, 1.05, 0.95),
  label = c("+", "nc", "nc", "+", "nc"),
  linetype = c("dashed", "solid", "solid", "dashed", "solid"),
  color = c("black", "black", "black", "black", "black")
)

# Box size
box_width <- 1
box_height <- 0.2

# Arrow geometry
start_x <- arrows$from_x + box_width / 2
start_y <- arrows$from_y
end_x <- arrows$to_x - box_width / 2
end_y <- arrows$to_y

mid_x <- (start_x + end_x) / 2
mid_y <- (start_y + end_y) / 2
dx <- end_x - start_x
dy <- end_y - start_y
len <- sqrt(dx^2 + dy^2)
len[len == 0] <- 1

# Label positioning
offset <- 0.02
label_offset_x <- 0.03
perp_dx <- -dy / len * offset
perp_dy <- dx / len * offset
label_x <- mid_x + perp_dx + label_offset_x
label_y <- mid_y + perp_dy

# Update arrows data
arrows$start_x <- start_x
arrows$start_y <- start_y
arrows$end_x <- end_x
arrows$end_y <- end_y
arrows$label_x <- label_x
arrows$label_y <- label_y

# Split dashed vs solid arrows
arrows_dashed <- subset(arrows, linetype == "dashed")
arrows_solid  <- subset(arrows, linetype == "solid")

# Calculate short solid segment for arrowheads only
arrow_frac <- 0.95
arrows_dashed$arrow_xend <- arrows_dashed$start_x + (arrows_dashed$end_x - arrows_dashed$start_x) * arrow_frac
arrows_dashed$arrow_yend <- arrows_dashed$start_y + (arrows_dashed$end_y - arrows_dashed$start_y) * arrow_frac

# Build plot
p <- ggplot() +
  # Dashed lines (body only)
  geom_segment(data = arrows_dashed,
               aes(x = start_x, y = start_y, xend = end_x, yend = end_y, color = color),
               linetype = "dashed", size = 0.6) +
  
  # Arrowheads as solid overlays
  geom_segment(data = arrows_dashed,
               aes(x = arrow_xend, y = arrow_yend, xend = end_x, yend = end_y),
               arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.3, "cm"), type = "closed", angle = 15),
               size = 0.6, color = "black", linetype = "solid") +
  
  # Solid arrows
  geom_segment(data = arrows_solid,
               aes(x = start_x, y = start_y, xend = end_x, yend = end_y,
                   linetype = linetype, color = color),
               arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.3, "cm"), type = "closed", angle = 15),
               size = 0.6) +
  
  # Arrow labels
  geom_text(data = arrows,
            aes(x = label_x, y = label_y, label = label),
            size = 5, color = "black", fontface = "bold") +
  
  # Node boxes
  geom_rect(data = nodes,
            aes(xmin = x - box_width / 2, xmax = x + box_width / 2,
                ymin = y - box_height / 2, ymax = y + box_height / 2),
            fill = "grey", color = "black", size = 0.8) +
  
  # Node labels
  geom_text(data = nodes,
            aes(x = x, y = y, label = name),
            size = 6, fontface = "plain") +
  
  # Styling
  scale_color_manual(values = c("black" = "black")) +
  scale_linetype_manual(values = c("solid" = "solid", "dashed" = "dashed")) +
  theme_void() +
  theme(
    legend.position = "none",
    plot.margin = unit(c(0, 0, 0, 0), "cm"),  # Remove white margins
    plot.background = element_rect(fill = "white", color = NA),
    panel.background = element_rect(fill = "white", color = NA)
  ) #+
  # xlim(0, 5) +
  # ylim(0, 4)

# Display
print(p)

# Save as PDF
ggsave("FigLeeb.pdf", plot = p, width = 10, height = 4, dpi = 600, bg = "white", units = "in", limitsize = FALSE)

# Save as PNG without white margins
ggsave("FigLeeb.png", plot = p, width = 10, height = 4, dpi = 600, bg = "white", units = "in", limitsize = FALSE)

print("Saved as 'FigLeeb.pdf' and 'FigLeeb.png'")
###########################################################
library(ggplot2)
library(grid)

# Define node positions (center point of box)
x_offset_right <- 3

nodes <- data.frame(
  name = c("Hedonic enjoyment", "Personal expressiveness", "Satisfaction", "Place attachment"),
  x = c(1, 1, x_offset_right, x_offset_right),
  y = c(1.5, 1, 1.5, 1)
)

# Arrow data
n_arrows <- 3
arrows <- data.frame(
  from_x = c(1, 1, 1),
  from_y = c(1.5, 1.5, 1),
  to_x = rep(x_offset_right, n_arrows),
  to_y = c(1.5,
           1,
           1),
  label_reg = c("+","nc", "+"),
  
  label_nec = c("nc", "", "nc"), 
  linetype = c("solid", "solid", "solid"),
  color = c("black", "black", "black")
)

# Box size
box_width <- 1
box_height <- 0.2

# Arrow geometry
start_x <- arrows$from_x + box_width / 2
start_y <- arrows$from_y
end_x <- arrows$to_x - box_width / 2
end_y <- arrows$to_y

mid_x <- (start_x + end_x) / 2
mid_y <- (start_y + end_y) / 2
dx <- end_x - start_x
dy <- end_y - start_y
len <- sqrt(dx^2 + dy^2)
len[len == 0] <- 1

# Label positioning
offset <- 0.03
label_offset_x <- 0.03
perp_dx <- -dy / len * offset
perp_dy <- dx / len * offset
label_reg_x <- mid_x + perp_dx + label_offset_x
label_reg_y <- mid_y + perp_dy
label_nec_x <- mid_x - perp_dx + label_offset_x
label_nec_y <- mid_y - perp_dy


# Update arrows data
arrows$start_x <- start_x
arrows$start_y <- start_y
arrows$end_x <- end_x
arrows$end_y <- end_y
arrows$label_reg_x <- label_reg_x
arrows$label_reg_y <- label_reg_y
arrows$label_nec_x <- label_nec_x
arrows$label_nec_y <- label_nec_y

# Split dashed vs solid arrows
arrows_dashed <- subset(arrows, linetype == "dashed")
arrows_solid  <- subset(arrows, linetype == "solid")

# Calculate short solid segment for arrowheads only
arrow_frac <- 0.95
arrows_dashed$arrow_xend <- arrows_dashed$start_x + (arrows_dashed$end_x - arrows_dashed$start_x) * arrow_frac
arrows_dashed$arrow_yend <- arrows_dashed$start_y + (arrows_dashed$end_y - arrows_dashed$start_y) * arrow_frac

# Build plot
p <- ggplot() +
  # Dashed lines (body only)
  geom_segment(data = arrows_dashed,
               aes(x = start_x, y = start_y, xend = end_x, yend = end_y, color = color),
               linetype = "dashed", size = 0.6) +
  
  # Arrowheads as solid overlays
  geom_segment(data = arrows_dashed,
               aes(x = arrow_xend, y = arrow_yend, xend = end_x, yend = end_y),
               arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.3, "cm"), type = "closed", angle = 15),
               size = 0.6, color = "black", linetype = "solid") +
  
  # Solid arrows
  geom_segment(data = arrows_solid,
               aes(x = start_x, y = start_y, xend = end_x, yend = end_y,
                   linetype = linetype, color = color),
               arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.3, "cm"), type = "closed", angle = 15),
               size = 0.6) +
  
  # Arrow labels reg
  geom_text(data = arrows,
            aes(x = label_reg_x, y = label_reg_y, label = label_reg),
            size = 5, color = "black", fontface = "bold") +
  
  # Arrow labels nec
  geom_text(data = arrows,
            aes(x = label_nec_x, y = label_nec_y, label = label_nec),
            size = 5, color = "black", fontface = "bold") +
  
  # Node boxes
  geom_rect(data = nodes,
            aes(xmin = x - box_width / 2, xmax = x + box_width / 2,
                ymin = y - box_height / 2, ymax = y + box_height / 2),
            fill = "grey", color = "black", size = 0.8) +
  
  # Node labels
  geom_text(data = nodes,
            aes(x = x, y = y, label = name),
            size = 6, fontface = "plain") +
  
  # Styling
  scale_color_manual(values = c("black" = "black")) +
  scale_linetype_manual(values = c("solid" = "solid", "dashed" = "dashed")) +
  theme_void() +
  theme(
    legend.position = "none",
    plot.margin = unit(c(0, 0, 0, 0), "cm"),  # Remove white margins
    plot.background = element_rect(fill = "white", color = NA),
    panel.background = element_rect(fill = "white", color = NA)
  ) #+
# xlim(0, 5) +
# ylim(0, 4)

# Display
print(p)

# Save as PDF
ggsave("FigLeeb2.pdf", plot = p, width = 10, height = 4, dpi = 600, bg = "white", units = "in", limitsize = FALSE)

# Save as PNG without white margins
ggsave("FigLeeb2.png", plot = p, width = 10, height = 4, dpi = 600, bg = "white", units = "in", limitsize = FALSE)

print("Saved as 'FigLeeb2.pdf' and 'FigLeeb2.png'")


```



```{r figlee_code, echo=FALSE, include=FALSE, eval = FALSE}
library(DiagrammeR)
library(DiagrammeRsvg)
library(rsvg)

# Function to export a figure as a PDF
export_figure_to_pdf <- function(fig, filename) {
  svg_content <- fig %>% DiagrammeRsvg::export_svg()
  pdf_content <- charToRaw(svg_content) %>% rsvg::rsvg_pdf()
  writeBin(pdf_content, filename)
}

FigLee <- grViz('
  digraph {
    # Set graph properties for horizontal layout
    graph [layout = dot, rankdir = LR] 

    # Set node properties with font family "Helvetica" and fixed width and height for equal-sized boxes
    node [shape = box, style = filled, fillcolor = grey, fontname = "Helvetica", fontsize = 10, fixedsize = true]

    # Define nodes with equal width and height for X1 and X2
    X1 [label = "Hedonic enjoyment", width = 1.7, height = 0.6]  
    X2 [label = "Personal expressiveness", width = 1.7, height = 0.6] 

    # Define nodes with equal width and height for Y1 and Y2
    Y1 [label = "Satisfaction", width = 1.2, height = 0.6]
    Y2 [label = "Place attachment", width = 1.2, height = 0.6]

    # # # Align nodes horizontally #vertically
    #  {rank = same; X1; X2}
    #  {rank = same; Y1; Y2}

    # Invisible node to raise X1 and X2
    invisible_node [style=invis, width=0, height=0]

    {rank = same; invisible_node; X1; X2}
    {rank = same; Y1; Y2}


    # Define edges and arrow properties
    X1 -> Y1 [dir = forward, arrowhead = normal, arrowsize = 0.5, penwidth = 0.5, label = "nc", fontname = "Helvetica", fontsize = 8]  
    X2 -> Y2 [dir = forward, arrowhead = normal, arrowsize = 0.5, penwidth = 0.5, label = "nc", fontname = "Helvetica", fontsize = 8]  
    X1 -> Y2 [dir = forward, arrowhead = normal, arrowsize = 0.5, penwidth = 0.5, label = "nc", fontname = "Helvetica", fontsize = 8]  
    #X2 -> Y1 [dir = forward, arrowhead = normal, arrowsize = 0.5, penwidth = 0.5, label = "nc", fontname = "Helvetica", fontsize = 8]  
    X1 -> Y1 [dir = forward, arrowhead = normal, arrowsize = 0.5, penwidth = 0.5, label = "+", fontname = "Helvetica", fontsize = 8, style = "dashed", labeldistance = 1.5, labelangle = -90]  
    X2 -> Y2 [dir = forward, arrowhead = normal, arrowsize = 0.5, penwidth = 0.5, label = "+", fontname = "Helvetica", fontsize = 8, style = "dashed", labeldistance = 1.5, labelangle = -90]  
    #X1 -> Y2 [dir = forward, arrowhead = normal, arrowsize = 0.5, penwidth = 0.5, label = "+", fontname = "Helvetica", fontsize = 8, style = "dashed", labeldistance = 1.2, labelangle = -90]  
    #X2 -> Y1 [dir = forward, arrowhead = normal, arrowsize = 0.5, penwidth = 0.5, label = "+", fontname = "Helvetica", fontsize = 8, style = "dashed", labeldistance = 1.2, labelangle = 45]  

  }')

# Uncomment for plot FigLee in screen
#FigLee
 # Uncomment and use the following lines to create a PDF and high-resolution PNG for Fig5
 export_figure_to_pdf(FigLee, "FigLee.pdf") 
```

```{r typestheorycode, echo=FALSE, out.width="100%", out.height="100%",fig.align ='center', results='hide', message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, eval=FALSE}

#make empty 2x2 contingency table
X<- c(0.25, 0.75) #X-values center of cell
Y<- c(0.25, 0.25) #Y-values center of cell

# make plot window square and set size of the axes and labels
par(pty = "s", cex.axis = 0.8, cex.lab = 1.5)

#give names to axes
xname = "Necessity theory"
yname = "Status"

#give names to axis labels
# xlabel = rep(c("","0", "1", ""))
# ylabel = rep(c("","0", "1", ""))
xlabel = rep(c("","Pure", "Embedded", ""))
ylabel = rep(c("","Emerging", "Established", ""))

# Create the plot with square aspect ratio
#pdf("types_necessity_theoriesa.pdf")
plot (X,Y, xlab=xname, ylab=yname, xlim = c(0,1), ylim=c(0,1), pch=19, cex=2, axes = F, yaxs="i", xaxs="i", type="n", cex.lab=1.5) 
axis(1, at = c(0, 0.25, 0.75, 1.25), pos=0, tck=0, labels=xlabel, cex.axis = 1.2) #X has two values
axis(2, at = c(0, 0.25, 0.75, 1.25), pos=0, tck=0, labels=ylabel, cex.axis = 1.2) #Y has two values
abline(v=c(0.5,1), h=c(0.5,1))

#fill cells
# rect(0, 0.5, 0.5, 1,  col = "grey")
# rect(0, 0, 0.5, 0.5,  col = "grey")
# rect(0.5, 0, 1, 0.5,  col = "grey")
# rect(0.5, 0.5, 1, 1,  col = "grey")

#add points in cells
#points(X,Y, pch=19, cex=2)

fontsize <- 1.2

#add text in cells
text(0.25,0.30,"Emerging-Pure", cex=fontsize) #left low
text(0.25,0.25,"(NCA alone studies; see Appendix)", cex=0.75*fontsize) #left low

text(0.25,0.80,"Established-Pure", cex=fontsize) #left high 
text(0.25,0.75,"(Intelligence-Creativity; AMO)", cex=0.75*fontsize) #left high 

text(0.75,0.30,"Emerging-Embedded", cex=fontsize) #right low
text(0.75,0.25,"(NCA-regression studies; see Appendix)", cex=0.75*fontsize) #right low
#text(0.75,0.20,"(NCA-QCA studies; see Appendix)", cex=0.75*fontsize) #right low

text(0.75,0.80,"Established-Embedded", cex=fontsize) #right high 
text(0.75,0.75,"(TPB, TAM, SDT)", cex=0.75*fontsize) #right high


#add ceiling line
# segments(0,0.5,0.5,0.5, lty=1, col="black", lwd = 3) #horizontal part of ceiling line
# segments(0.5,0.5,0.5,1, lty=1, col="black", lwd = 3) #vertical part of ceiling line
#dev.off()
```

```{r directionconceptualcode, eval= FALSE, echo=FALSE, include=FALSE}
library(DiagrammeR)
library(DiagrammeRsvg)
library(rsvg)

# Function to export a figure as a PDF
export_figure_to_pdf <- function(fig, filename) {
  svg_content <- fig %>% DiagrammeRsvg::export_svg()
  pdf_content <- charToRaw(svg_content) %>% rsvg::rsvg_pdf()
  writeBin(pdf_content, filename)
}


# Fig1
Fig1 <- grViz('
  digraph {
    # Set graph properties for horizontal layout
    graph [layout = dot, rankdir = LR]

    # Set node properties with font family "Helvetica"
    node [shape = box, style = filled, fillcolor = grey, fontname = "Helvetica", fontsize = 10]

    # Define nodes with font family "sans"
    X [label = "X"] # Box labeled "X"
    Y [label = "Y"]

    # Define edges and arrow properties
    X -> Y [dir = forward, arrowhead = normal, arrowsize = 0.5, penwidth = 0.5, label = "+ nc +", fontname = "Helvetica", fontsize = 8]
  }')
#plot Fig1
Fig1

# Fig2
Fig2 <- grViz('
  digraph {
    # Set graph properties for horizontal layout
    graph [layout = dot, rankdir = LR]

    # Set node properties with font family "Helvetica"
    node [shape = box, style = filled, fillcolor = grey, fontname = "Helvetica", fontsize = 10]

    # Define nodes with font family "sans"
    X [label = "X"] # Box labeled "X"
    Y [label = "Y"]

    # Define edges and arrow properties
    X -> Y [dir = forward, arrowhead = normal, arrowsize = 0.5, penwidth = 0.5, label = "- nc +", fontname = "Helvetica", fontsize = 8]
  }')
#plot Fig2
Fig2

# Fig3
Fig3 <- grViz('
  digraph {
    # Set graph properties for horizontal layout
    graph [layout = dot, rankdir = LR]

    # Set node properties with font family "Helvetica"
    node [shape = box, style = filled, fillcolor = grey, fontname = "Helvetica", fontsize = 10]

    # Define nodes with font family "sans"
    X [label = "X"] # Box labeled "X"
    Y [label = "Y"]

    # Define edges and arrow properties
    X -> Y [dir = forward, arrowhead = normal, arrowsize = 0.5, penwidth = 0.5, label = "+ nc -", fontname = "Helvetica", fontsize = 8]
  }')
#plot Fig3
Fig3

# Fig4
Fig4 <- grViz('
  digraph {
    # Set graph properties for horizontal layout
    graph [layout = dot, rankdir = LR]

    # Set node properties with font family "Helvetica"
    node [shape = box, style = filled, fillcolor = grey, fontname = "Helvetica", fontsize = 10]

    # Define nodes with font family "sans"
    X [label = "X"] # Box labeled "X"
    Y [label = "Y"]

    # Define edges and arrow properties
    X -> Y [dir = forward, arrowhead = normal, arrowsize = 0.5, penwidth = 0.5, label = "- nc -", fontname = "Helvetica", fontsize = 8]
  }')
#plot Fig4
Fig4

# Uncomment and use the following lines to create a PDF and high-resolution PNG for Fig1
export_figure_to_pdf(Fig1, "Fig1modelDIRECTION.pdf")
export_figure_to_pdf(Fig2, "Fig2modelDIRECTION.pdf")
export_figure_to_pdf(Fig3, "Fig3modelDIRECTION.pdf")
export_figure_to_pdf(Fig4, "Fig4modelDIRECTION.pdf")

# save as high resolution (Settings) png in acrobat
# inmport in PPT, rearragen in grid and save as png picture
```

```{r oneemptycornercontingencycode,echo=FALSE, eval=FALSE}
# code for four empty corners contingency table

#make empty 2x2 contingency table
X<- c(0.25, 0.75) #X-values center of cell
Y<- c(0.25, 0.25) #Y-values center of cell

th = 3 #thickness of lines lwd
th1 = 5 #horizontal vertical axes
th2 = 10 #thickness of points

#give names to axes
xname = "X"
yname = "Y"

#give labels to axis values
xlabel = rep(c("","Absent", "Present", ""))
ylabel = rep(c("","Absent", "Present", ""))

#Fig1
##pdf("Fig1emptydich.pdf")
#par after calling pdf
#set positions of labels and axes names
par(mar=c(5,5,4,2)+0.1) # order: bottom, left, top, right: increase left margin from 4 to 5, rest defaults 
par(mgp = c(3, 1, 0)) # default distance between label (axis name) and axis: decrease first to get label closer to axis 
#cex.lab is size of the label = axis name
plot (X,Y, xlab=xname, ylab=yname, xlim = c(0,1), ylim=c(0,1), pch=19, cex.lab=2, axes = F, yaxs="i", xaxs="i", type="n")
#cex.axis is size of the values at the axes
axis(1, at = c(0, 0.25, 0.75, 1.25), pos=0, tck=0, lwd = th, cex.axis = 2, labels=xlabel) #X has two values
axis(2, at = c(0, 0.25, 0.75, 1.25), pos=0, tck=0,lwd = th,  cex.axis = 2, labels=ylabel) #Y has two values
abline(v=0.5, h=0.5, lwd = th)
#fill cells
#rect(0, 0.5, 0.5, 1,  col = "grey", lwd = th)
rect(0, 0, 0.5, 0.5,  col = "grey", lwd = th)
rect(0.5, 0, 1, 0.5,  col = "grey", lwd = th)
rect(0.5, 0.5, 1, 1,  col = "grey", lwd = th)
abline(v=1, h = 1, lwd = th1)
#add points
#points(0.25, 0.75, pch = 19, col = "black", lwd = th2)
points(0.25, 0.25, pch = 19, col = "black", lwd = th2)
points(0.75, 0.25, pch = 19, col = "black", lwd = th2)
points(0.75, 0.75, pch = 19, col = "black", lwd = th2)
##dev.off()

#Fig2
##pdf("Fig2emptydich.pdf")
par(mar=c(5,5,4,2)+0.1) 
# Create the plot with square aspect ratio
plot (X,Y, xlab=xname, ylab=yname, xlim = c(0,1), ylim=c(0,1), pch=19, cex.lab=2, axes = F, yaxs="i", xaxs="i", type="n")
axis(1, at = c(0, 0.25, 0.75, 1.25), pos=0, tck=0, lwd = th, cex.axis = 2, labels=xlabel) #X has two values
axis(2, at = c(0, 0.25, 0.75, 1.25), pos=0, tck=0, lwd = th, cex.axis = 2, labels=ylabel) #Y has two values
abline(v=0.5, h=0.5, lwd = th)
#fill cells
rect(0, 0.5, 0.5, 1,  col = "grey", lwd = th)
rect(0, 0, 0.5, 0.5,  col = "grey", lwd = th)
rect(0.5, 0, 1, 0.5,  col = "grey", lwd = th)
#rect(0.5, 0.5, 1, 1,  col = "grey", lwd = th)
abline(v=1, h = 1, lwd = th1)
#add points
points(0.25, 0.75, pch = 19, col = "black", lwd = th2)
points(0.25, 0.25, pch = 19, col = "black", lwd = th2)
points(0.75, 0.25, pch = 19, col = "black", lwd = th2)
#points(0.75, 0.75, pch = 19, col = "black", lwd = th2)
##dev.off()

#Fig3
##pdf("Fig3emptydich.pdf")
par(mar=c(5,5,4,2)+0.1) 
# Create the plot with square aspect ratio
plot (X,Y, xlab=xname, ylab=yname, xlim = c(0,1), ylim=c(0,1), pch=19, cex.lab=2, axes = F, yaxs="i", xaxs="i", type="n")
axis(1, at = c(0, 0.25, 0.75, 1.25), pos=0, tck=0, lwd = th, cex.axis = 2, labels=xlabel) #X has two values
axis(2, at = c(0, 0.25, 0.75, 1.25), pos=0, tck=0, lwd = th, cex.axis = 2, labels=ylabel) #Y has two values
abline(v=0.5, h=0.5, lwd = th)
#fill cells
rect(0, 0.5, 0.5, 1,  col = "grey", lwd = th)
#rect(0, 0, 0.5, 0.5,  col = "grey", lwd = th)
rect(0.5, 0, 1, 0.5,  col = "grey", lwd = th)
rect(0.5, 0.5, 1, 1,  col = "grey", lwd = th)
abline(v=1, h = 1, lwd = th1)
#add points
points(0.25, 0.75, pch = 19, col = "black", lwd = th2)
#points(0.25, 0.25, pch = 19, col = "black", lwd = th2)
points(0.75, 0.25, pch = 19, col = "black", lwd = th2)
points(0.75, 0.75, pch = 19, col = "black", lwd = th2)
##dev.off()

#Fig4
##pdf("Fig4emptydich.pdf")
par(mar=c(5,5,4,2)+0.1) 
# Create the plot with square aspect ratio
plot (X,Y, xlab=xname, ylab=yname, xlim = c(0,1), ylim=c(0,1), pch=19, cex.lab=2, axes = F, yaxs="i", xaxs="i", type="n")
axis(1, at = c(0, 0.25, 0.75, 1.25), pos=0, tck=0, lwd = th, cex.axis = 2, labels=xlabel) #X has two values
axis(2, at = c(0, 0.25, 0.75, 1.25), pos=0, tck=0, lwd = th, cex.axis = 2, labels=ylabel) #Y has two values
abline(v=0.5, h=0.5, lwd = th)
#fill cells
rect(0, 0.5, 0.5, 1,  col = "grey", lwd = th)
rect(0, 0, 0.5, 0.5,  col = "grey", lwd = th)
#rect(0.5, 0, 1, 0.5,  col = "grey", lwd = th)
rect(0.5, 0.5, 1, 1,  col = "grey", lwd = th)
abline(v=1, h = 1, lwd = th1)
#add points
points(0.25, 0.75, pch = 19, col = "black", lwd = th2)
points(0.25, 0.25, pch = 19, col = "black", lwd = th2)
#points(0.75, 0.25, pch = 19, col = "black", lwd = th2)
points(0.75, 0.75, pch = 19, col = "black", lwd = th2)
##dev.off()

#make in adobe png: file/export to/image/png settings 1200 dpi
```



```{r contcornercode,echo=FALSE, eval=FALSE}
library("ggplot2")
# Example
corner1 = 1
slope1 = 1
effect1 = 0.1
if (corner1 == 1) {intercept1 <- 1 - sqrt(2 * effect1 * slope1)}
if (corner1 == 2) {intercept1 <- 1 + abs(slope1) - sqrt(2 * effect1 * abs(slope1))}
if (corner1 == 3) {intercept1 <- sqrt(2 * effect1 * abs(slope1))}
if (corner1 == 4) {intercept1 <- -1 * (slope1 - sqrt(2 * effect1 * slope1))}
int <- intercept1

#ceiling directions #####
xbox <- c(0,1,0,1)
ybox <- c(0,0,1,1)
box <- data.frame (xbox, ybox)

#all corners based on corner1
true.effect <- c(0.05, 0.05, 0.05, 0.05)
true.slope <- c(1,-1,-1,1)
true.intercept <- c(int, 1 + int, 1 - int, -int )
coefs <- data.frame(true.intercept,true.slope)


lwdceil <- 1.5
lwdcorner <- 5

#Fig1 corner 1
fill_polygon <- data.frame(
  x = c(0, 1, 1, 1-int, 0),
  y = c(0, 0, 1, 1, int)
)
p1 <- ggplot(box, aes(x = xbox, y=ybox)) +
  geom_blank() + 
  geom_polygon(data = fill_polygon, aes(x = x, y = y), fill = "grey", alpha = 1) +  # Fill the area with grey
  geom_hline(aes(yintercept = 0.0), linetype = 'solid', size = 1.2) + 
  geom_hline(aes(yintercept = 1.0), linetype = 'solid', size = 1.2) +
  geom_vline(aes(xintercept = 0.0), linetype = 'solid', size = 1.2) +
  geom_vline(aes(xintercept = 1.0), linetype = 'solid', size = 1.2) +
  #geom_abline(data=coefs, aes(intercept=true.intercept, slope=true.slope), lwd = lwdceil) +
   geom_abline(aes(intercept=true.intercept[1], slope=true.slope[1]), lwd = lwdceil) + #corner 1
  #geom_abline(aes(intercept=true.intercept[2], slope=true.slope[2]), lwd = lwdceil) + #corner 2
  # geom_abline(aes(intercept=true.intercept[3], slope=true.slope[3]), lwd = lwdceil) + #corner 3
  # geom_abline(aes(intercept=true.intercept[4], slope=true.slope[4]), lwd = lwdceil) + #corner 4
  
  #ggtitle("ceiling direction = 'diagonal'") +
  annotate("text", x = 0, y = 0.85, label = "corner = 1", hjust = -0.1, vjust = 0, size = lwdcorner) +
  #annotate("text", x = 0.75, y = 0.85, label = "corner = 2", hjust = 0, vjust = 0, size = lwdcorner) +
  # annotate("text", x = 0, y = 0.07, label = "corner = 3", hjust = -0.1, vjust = 0, size = 6) +
  # annotate("text", x = 0.8, y = 0.07, label = "corner = 4", hjust = 0, vjust = 0, size = 6) +
  labs(x = 'X', y = 'Y') +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 1), expand = c(0, 0), breaks = c(0, 1)) +  # Set X-axis limits and breaks
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 1), expand = c(0, 0), breaks = c(0, 1)) +  # Set Y-axis limits and breaks
  coord_fixed(ratio = 1) +  # Ensures that axes are equal in length
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major = element_blank(),  # Remove major grid lines
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),  # Remove minor grid lines
    axis.text = element_text(size = 14, color = "black"),  # Increase size of axis labels
    axis.title = element_text(size = 16, color = "black"), # Increase size of axis titles
    #axis.line = element_line(size = 1.2,color = "black"),  # Make the axis lines thicker
    #plot.background = element_rect(fill = "white"),  # Set the background color
    plot.margin = margin(20, 20, 20, 20)  # Set the margin around the plot
  )

# Save the plot as a PDF
#ggsave("contcorner1.pdf", plot = p1, width = 5, height = 5, dpi = 600)
print(p1)

#Fig2 corner 2
fill_polygon <- data.frame(
  x = c(0, 1, 1, int, 0),
  y = c(0, 0, int, 1, 1)
)
p2 <- ggplot(box, aes(x = xbox, y=ybox)) +
  geom_blank() + 
  geom_polygon(data = fill_polygon, aes(x = x, y = y), fill = "grey", alpha = 1) +  # Fill the area with grey
  geom_hline(aes(yintercept = 0.0), linetype = 'solid', size = 1.2) + 
  geom_hline(aes(yintercept = 1.0), linetype = 'solid', size = 1.2) +
  geom_vline(aes(xintercept = 0.0), linetype = 'solid', size = 1.2) +
  geom_vline(aes(xintercept = 1.0), linetype = 'solid', size = 1.2) +
  #geom_abline(data=coefs, aes(intercept=true.intercept, slope=true.slope), lwd = lwdceil) +
  # geom_abline(aes(intercept=true.intercept[1], slope=true.slope[1]), lwd = lwdceil) + #corner 1
  geom_abline(aes(intercept=true.intercept[2], slope=true.slope[2]), lwd = lwdceil) + #corner 2
  # geom_abline(aes(intercept=true.intercept[3], slope=true.slope[3]), lwd = lwdceil) + #corner 3
  # geom_abline(aes(intercept=true.intercept[4], slope=true.slope[4]), lwd = lwdceil) + #corner 4

  #ggtitle("ceiling direction = 'diagonal'") +
  #annotate("text", x = 0, y = 0.85, label = "corner = 1", hjust = -0.1, vjust = 0, size = lwdcorner) +
  annotate("text", x = 0.75, y = 0.85, label = "corner = 2", hjust = 0, vjust = 0, size = lwdcorner) +
  # annotate("text", x = 0, y = 0.07, label = "corner = 3", hjust = -0.1, vjust = 0, size = 6) +
  # annotate("text", x = 0.8, y = 0.07, label = "corner = 4", hjust = 0, vjust = 0, size = 6) +
  labs(x = 'X', y = 'Y') +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 1), expand = c(0, 0), breaks = c(0, 1)) +  # Set X-axis limits and breaks
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 1), expand = c(0, 0), breaks = c(0, 1)) +  # Set Y-axis limits and breaks
  coord_fixed(ratio = 1) +  # Ensures that axes are equal in length
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major = element_blank(),  # Remove major grid lines
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),  # Remove minor grid lines
    axis.text = element_text(size = 14, color = "black"),  # Increase size of axis labels
    axis.title = element_text(size = 16, color = "black"), # Increase size of axis titles
    #axis.line = element_line(size = 1.2,color = "black"),  # Make the axis lines thicker
    #plot.background = element_rect(fill = "white"),  # Set the background color
    plot.margin = margin(20, 20, 20, 20)  # Set the margin around the plot
  )

# Save the plot as a PDF
#ggsave("contcorner2.pdf", plot = p2, width = 5, height = 5, dpi = 600)

print(p2)

#Fig3 corner 3
fill_polygon <- data.frame(
  x = c(1-int, 1, 1, 0, 0),
  y = c(0,     0, 1, 1, 1 -int)
)
p3 <- ggplot(box, aes(x = xbox, y=ybox)) +
  geom_blank() + 
  geom_polygon(data = fill_polygon, aes(x = x, y = y), fill = "grey", alpha = 1) +  # Fill the area with grey
  geom_hline(aes(yintercept = 0.0), linetype = 'solid', size = 1.2) + 
  geom_hline(aes(yintercept = 1.0), linetype = 'solid', size = 1.2) +
  geom_vline(aes(xintercept = 0.0), linetype = 'solid', size = 1.2) +
  geom_vline(aes(xintercept = 1.0), linetype = 'solid', size = 1.2) +
  #geom_abline(data=coefs, aes(intercept=true.intercept, slope=true.slope), lwd = lwdceil) +
  #geom_abline(data=coefs[1,], aes(intercept=true.intercept[1], slope=true.slope[1]), lwd = lwdceil) + #corner 1
  #geom_abline(aes(intercept=true.intercept[2], slope=true.slope[2]), lwd = lwdceil) + #corner 2
   geom_abline(aes(intercept=true.intercept[3], slope=true.slope[3]), lwd = lwdceil) + #corner 3
  # geom_abline(aes(intercept=true.intercept[4], slope=true.slope[4]), lwd = lwdceil) + #corner 4
  # geom_abline(data=coefs, aes(intercept=true.intercept, slope=true.slope), lwd = 2) +
  # geom_abline(data=coefs, aes(intercept=true.intercept, slope=true.slope), lwd = 2) +
  # geom_abline(data=coefs, aes(intercept=true.intercept, slope=true.slope), lwd = 2) +
  #ggtitle("ceiling direction = 'diagonal'") +
  #annotate("text", x = 0, y = 0.85, label = "corner = 1", hjust = -0.1, vjust = 0, size = lwdcorner) +
  #annotate("text", x = 0.75, y = 0.85, label = "corner = 2", hjust = 0, vjust = 0, size = lwdcorner) +
   annotate("text", x = 0, y = 0.12, label = "corner = 3", hjust = -0.1, vjust = 0, size = lwdcorner) +
  # annotate("text", x = 0.8, y = 0.07, label = "corner = 4", hjust = 0, vjust = 0, size = lwdcorner) +
  labs(x = 'X', y = 'Y') +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 1), expand = c(0, 0), breaks = c(0, 1)) +  # Set X-axis limits and breaks
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 1), expand = c(0, 0), breaks = c(0, 1)) +  # Set Y-axis limits and breaks
  coord_fixed(ratio = 1) +  # Ensures that axes are equal in length
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major = element_blank(),  # Remove major grid lines
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),  # Remove minor grid lines
    axis.text = element_text(size = 14, color = "black"),  # Increase size of axis labels
    axis.title = element_text(size = 16, color = "black"), # Increase size of axis titles
    #axis.line = element_line(size = 1.2,color = "black"),  # Make the axis lines thicker
    #plot.background = element_rect(fill = "white"),  # Set the background color
    plot.margin = margin(20, 20, 20, 20)  # Set the margin around the plot
  )

# Save the plot as a PDF
#ggsave("contcorner3.pdf", plot = p3, width = 5, height = 5, dpi = 600)

print(p3)

#Fig4 corner 4
fill_polygon <- data.frame(
  x = c(0, int, 1    , 1, 0),
  y = c(0, 0  , 1-int, 1, 1)
)
p4 <- ggplot(box, aes(x = xbox, y=ybox)) +
  geom_blank() + 
  geom_polygon(data = fill_polygon, aes(x = x, y = y), fill = "grey", alpha = 1) +  # Fill the area with grey
  geom_hline(aes(yintercept = 0.0), linetype = 'solid', size = 1.2) + 
  geom_hline(aes(yintercept = 1.0), linetype = 'solid', size = 1.2) +
  geom_vline(aes(xintercept = 0.0), linetype = 'solid', size = 1.2) +
  geom_vline(aes(xintercept = 1.0), linetype = 'solid', size = 1.2) +
  
  #geom_abline(data=coefs, aes(intercept=true.intercept, slope=true.slope), lwd = lwdceil) +
  #geom_abline(data=coefs[1,], aes(intercept=true.intercept[1], slope=true.slope[1]), lwd = lwdceil) + #corner 1
  #geom_abline(aes(intercept=true.intercept[2], slope=true.slope[2]), lwd = lwdceil) + #corner 2
  #geom_abline(aes(intercept=true.intercept[3], slope=true.slope[3]), lwd = lwdceil) + #corner 3
   geom_abline(aes(intercept=true.intercept[4], slope=true.slope[4]), lwd = lwdceil) + #corner 4
  
  #ggtitle("ceiling direction = 'diagonal'") +
  #annotate("text", x = 0, y = 0.85, label = "corner = 1", hjust = -0.1, vjust = 0, size = lwdcorner) +
  #annotate("text", x = 0.75, y = 0.85, label = "corner = 2", hjust = 0, vjust = 0, size = lwdcorner) +
  #annotate("text", x = 0, y = 0.12, label = "corner = 3", hjust = -0.1, vjust = 0, size = lwdcorner) +
   annotate("text", x = 0.75, y = 0.12, label = "corner = 4", hjust = 0, vjust = 0, size = lwdcorner) +
  labs(x = 'X', y = 'Y') +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 1), expand = c(0, 0), breaks = c(0, 1)) +  # Set X-axis limits and breaks
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 1), expand = c(0, 0), breaks = c(0, 1)) +  # Set Y-axis limits and breaks
  coord_fixed(ratio = 1) +  # Ensures that axes are equal in length
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major = element_blank(),  # Remove major grid lines
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),  # Remove minor grid lines
    axis.text = element_text(size = 14, color = "black"),  # Increase size of axis labels
    axis.title = element_text(size = 16, color = "black"), # Increase size of axis titles
    #axis.line = element_line(size = 1.2,color = "black"),  # Make the axis lines thicker
    #plot.background = element_rect(fill = "white"),  # Set the background color
    plot.margin = margin(20, 20, 20, 20)  # Set the margin around the plot
  )

# Save the plot as a PDF
#ggsave("contcorner4.pdf", plot = p4, width = 5, height = 5, dpi = 600)

print(p4)


#general figure with four empty corners
# Create the polygon to fill the center area (excluding corners)
fill_polygon <- data.frame(
  x = c(0, 1-int, int, 1, 1, int, 1-int, 0),
  y = c(1-int, 0, 0, 1-int, int, 1, 1, int)
)


p <- ggplot(box, aes(x = xbox, y=ybox)) +
  geom_blank() + 
  geom_polygon(data = fill_polygon, aes(x = x, y = y), fill = "grey", alpha = 1) +  # Fill the area with grey
  geom_hline(aes(yintercept = 0.0), linetype = 'solid') + 
  geom_hline(aes(yintercept = 1.0), linetype = 'solid') +
  geom_vline(aes(xintercept = 0.0), linetype = 'solid') +
  geom_vline(aes(xintercept = 1.0), linetype = 'solid') +
  geom_abline(data=coefs, aes(intercept=true.intercept, slope=true.slope), lwd = 2) +
  #ggtitle("ceiling direction = 'diagonal'") +
  annotate("text", x = 0, y = 0.9, label = "corner = 1", hjust = -0.1, vjust = 0, size = 6) +
  annotate("text", x = 0.8, y = 0.9, label = "corner = 2", hjust = 0, vjust = 0, size = 6) +
  annotate("text", x = 0, y = 0.07, label = "corner = 3", hjust = -0.1, vjust = 0, size = 6) +
  annotate("text", x = 0.8, y = 0.07, label = "corner = 4", hjust = 0, vjust = 0, size = 6) +
  labs(x = 'X', y = 'Y') +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 1), expand = c(0, 0)) +  # Set X-axis limits and expand
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 1), expand = c(0, 0)) +  # Set Y-axis limits and expand
  
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major = element_blank(),  # Remove major grid lines
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),  # Remove minor grid lines
    axis.text = element_text(size = 14),  # Increase size of axis labels
    axis.title = element_text(size = 16), # Increase size of axis titles
    axis.line = element_line(size = 1.2),  # Make the axis lines thicker
    #plot.background = element_rect(fill = "white"),  # Set the background color
    plot.margin = margin(20, 20, 20, 20)  # Set the margin around the plot
  )
print(p)
#ggsave("unitbox_with_corners_no_grid.png", plot = p, width = 5, height = 4, dpi =600)

# Save the plot as a PDF
#ggsave("unitbox_with_corners_no_grid.pdf", plot = p, width = 5, height = 4, dpi = 600)
```


``` {r doubleadjacenttopdowncode, echo=FALSE, eval=FALSE}
pdf("corners12.pdf")
X= NULL
Y= NULL
#set positions of labels and axes names
par(mar=c(5,5,4,2)+0.1) # order: bottom, left, top, right: increase left margin from 4 to 5, rest defaults 
par(mgp = c(3, 1, 0)) # default distance between label (axis name) and axis: decrease first to get label closer to axis 
plot (X,Y, xlab="X", ylab="Y", xlim = c(0,1), ylim=c(0,1), pch=19, cex.lab=2, axes = F, yaxs="i", xaxs="i", type="n")

polygon (x = c(0,0,0.4,0.6,1, 1), 
         y = c(0,0.6,1,1,0.6,0),
         col = "grey",
         border = NA)

axis(1, at = c(0,1), pos=0, tck=0, cex.axis = 2, labels=rep(c("0","1")))
axis(2, at = c(0,1), pos=0, tck=0, cex.axis = 2, labels=rep(c("0", "1")))

abline(v=c(0,1), h=c(0,1))
segments(0,0.6,0.4,1, lty=1, col="black", lwd = 4) #ceiling line
segments(0.6,1,1,0.6, lty=1, col="black", lwd = 4) #ceiling line
abline(h=0.8, col="black", lty =2)
abline(v=0.2, col="black", lty =2)
abline(v=0.8, col="black", lty =2)
# text(0.03,0.83, expression(y[c]))
# text(0.17,0.03, expression(x[c1]))
# text(0.77,0.03, expression(x[c2]))

text(0.05,0.83, expression(y[c]), cex = 2)
text(0.15,0.03, expression(x[c1]), cex = 2)
text(0.75,0.03, expression(x[c2]), cex = 2)

dev.off()

pdf("corners34.pdf")
#set positions of labels and axes names
par(mar=c(5,5,4,2)+0.1) # order: bottom, left, top, right: increase left margin from 4 to 5, rest defaults 
par(mgp = c(3, 1, 0)) # default distance between label (axis name) and axis: decrease first to get label closer to axis 
plot (X,Y, xlab="X", ylab="Y", xlim = c(0,1), ylim=c(0,1), pch=19, cex.lab=2, axes = F, yaxs="i", xaxs="i", type="n")

polygon (x = c(0,0,0.4,0.6,1, 1), 
         y = c(1,0.4,0,0,0.4,1),
         col = "grey",
         border = NA)

axis(1, at = c(0,1), pos=0, tck=0, cex.axis = 2, labels=rep(c("0","1")))
axis(2, at = c(0,1), pos=0, tck=0, cex.axis = 2, labels=rep(c("0", "1")))

abline(v=c(0,1), h=c(0,1))
segments(1,0.4,0.6,0, lty=1, col="black", lwd = 4) #ceiling line
segments(0.4,0,0,0.4, lty=1, col="black", lwd = 4) #ceiling line
abline(h=0.2, col="black", lty =2)
abline(v=0.2, col="black", lty =2)
abline(v=0.8, col="black", lty =2)
# text(0.03,0.23, expression(y[c]))
# text(0.17,0.03, expression(x[c1]))
# text(0.77,0.03, expression(x[c2]))

text(0.05,0.23, expression(y[c]), cex = 2)
text(0.15,0.03, expression(x[c1]), cex = 2)
text(0.75,0.03, expression(x[c2]), cex = 2)

dev.off()
```

pdf("corners13.pdf")

Y= NULL

#set positions of labels and axes names
par(mar=c(5,5,4,2)+0.1) # order: bottom, left, top, right: increase left margin from 4 to 5, rest defaults 
par(mgp = c(3, 1, 0)) # default distance between label (axis name) and axis: decrease first to get label closer to axis 
plot (X,Y, xlab="X", ylab="Y", xlim = c(0,1), ylim=c(0,1), pch=19, cex.lab=2, axes = F, yaxs="i", xaxs="i", type="n")

polygon (x = c(0,0,0.4,0.6,1, 1, 0.4), 
         y = c(0.4,0.6,1,1,1,0,0),
         col = "grey",
         border = NA)

axis(1, at = c(0,1), pos=0, tck=0, cex.axis = 2, labels=rep(c("0","1")))
axis(2, at = c(0,1), pos=0, tck=0, cex.axis = 2, labels=rep(c("0", "1")))

abline(v=c(0,1), h=c(0,1))

segments(0,0.6,0.4,1, lty=1, col="black", lwd = 4) #ceiling line
segments(0,0.4,0.4,0, lty=1, col="black", lwd = 4) #ceiling line

abline(h=0.2, col="black", lty =2)
# text(0.065,0.83, expression(y[c1]))

# text(0.06,0.23, expression(y[c2]))

text(0.16,0.03, expression(x[c]), cex = 2)
text(0.05,0.23, expression(y[c2]), cex = 2)

pdf("corners24.pdf")
#set positions of labels and axes names
par(mar=c(5,5,4,2)+0.1) # order: bottom, left, top, right: increase left margin from 4 to 5, rest defaults 
par(mgp = c(3, 1, 0)) # default distance between label (axis name) and axis: decrease first to get label closer to axis 

plot (X,Y, xlab="X", ylab="Y", xlim = c(0,1), ylim=c(0,1), pch=19, cex.lab=2, axes = F, yaxs="i", xaxs="i", type="n")

polygon (x = c(0,
                0.6,
                1,
                1,
                0.6,
                0), 
         y = c(0,
               0,
               0.4,
               0.6,
               1,
               1),
         col = "grey",
         border = NA)

axis(1, at = c(0,1), pos=0, tck=0, cex.axis = 2, labels=rep(c("0","1")))
axis(2, at = c(0,1), pos=0, tck=0, cex.axis = 2, labels=rep(c("0", "1")))

abline(v=c(0,1), h=c(0,1))

segments(0.6,0,1,0.4,lty=1, col="black", lwd = 4) #ceiling line
segments(1,0.6,0.6,1,lty=1, col="black", lwd = 4) #ceiling line
abline(h=0.8, col="black", lty =2)
abline(v=0.8, col="black", lty =2)
abline(h=0.2, col="black", lty =2)
# text(0.065,0.83, expression(y[c1]))
# text(0.77,0.03, expression(x[c]))
# text(0.06, 0.23, expression(y[c2]))

text(0.05,0.83, expression(y[c1]), cex = 2)
text(0.76,0.03, expression(x[c]), cex = 2)
text(0.05,0.23, expression(y[c2]), cex = 2)

dev.off()
