``` {r 3dnonlinearcodesampling, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE,  eval=FALSE, echo=FALSE, include=FALSE}
#Conduct NCA on the sampled data under the 3D surface
library(NCA)
model1 <- nca_analysis(sampled_data, c("X1" ,"X2"), "Y", corner = 1)
nca_output(model1)
model2 <- nca_analysis(sampled_data, c("X1" ,"X2"), "Y", corner = 2)
nca_output(model2)
```




<!-- NCA is not designed as a multivariate technique for analysing the multidimensional ceiling but as a method of analysing the bivariate ceiling lines. This has several related reasons.  -->




<!-- NCA is a an analysis of the two-dimensional plane of condition $X$ and outcome $Y$. In this book capital letters are used for (random) variables and lower case letters for specific values of the variables. When several condition are necessary for an outcome, NCA does a two-dimensional analysis for each necessary condition separately. The reason for this approach is explained in Section \@ref(multipleconditions).   -->
<!-- Figure \@ref(fig:formalmodel) shows the two-dimensional plane of condition $X$ and outcome $Y$.  -->




<!-- The basic mathematical expression of NCA is therefore: -->

<!-- \begin{equation}  -->
<!-- (\#eq:math0) -->
<!-- Y \leq f_c(x)  -->
<!-- \end{equation} -->
<!-- where $f_c(x)$ is the ceiling function.  -->


### Chain of necessity {#chain}

Normally, a necessary condition is hypothesized (Chapter \@ref(hypothesisformulation)) and analyzed (Chapter \@ref(dataanalysis)) as a necessity relationship between $X$ and $Y$. When multiple necessary conditions are proposed, several overall necessity relationships between $X_j$ and $Y$ may be hypothesized. In addition to such parallel necessary conditions, a serial system—a chain of necessity conditions—can also be hypothesized. In that case, an overall relationship $X_1 \rightarrow Y$ (if present) still holds, but it may be (partly) explained by an indirect chain $X_1 \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow Y$.

Section \@ref(parsimonious) discussed, in general terms, a necessity theory consisting of a chain of necessary conditions (Figure \@ref(fig:fig4model)). For example, a certain amount of study time may be necessary for a certain level of knowledge retention (quiz score), which in turn may be necessary for a high exam grade. This section analyzes such a chain algebraically. Numerical examples are provided for a chain with two links.

#### Analysis of chain necessity

A general necessity chain consists of a sequence of necessary conditions: the first condition $X_1$, intermediate conditions $X_2, X_3, \ldots, X_J$, and the outcome $Y$:

(\#eq:ch1)
\begin{equation}
X_1 \rightarrow X_2 \rightarrow X_3 \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow X_J \rightarrow Y
\end{equation}

Here, $J$ is the number of necessary conditions (and thus the chain has $J$ links ending in $Y$). For example, an additional intermediate necessary condition between study hours and quiz score could be active class participation (engagement, asking questions).

For each link, a ceiling function bounds the subsequent variable:

(\#eq:ch2)
\begin{equation}
X_{j+1} \le f_j(X_j), \quad j = 1,\ldots,J-1, \qquad
Y \le f_J(X_J).
\end{equation}

The chain ceiling $f_{\mathrm{chain}}(X_1)$ expresses the implied necessity of $X_1$ for $Y$ through the chain. It is defined as the composition of the link-specific ceiling functions:

(\#eq:ch3)
\begin{equation}
Y \le f_J\!\big(f_{J-1}(\cdots f_1(X_1)\cdots)\big)
= f_{\mathrm{chain}}(X_1).
\end{equation}

Here, $f_J(X_J)$ is the ceiling function of the last link.

The phrase “a chain is as strong as its weakest link” also applies to a necessity chain. If any link fails to represent a necessity relationship, the chain collapses, and $X_1$ is not necessary for $Y$ through the chain. This situation can occur, for example, when a link is sufficient but not necessary, or when a link is described only by an average effect or correlation without necessity.

Throughout, all variables are assumed to be min–max normalized, and all ceilings are defined within the same bounding box.

In the linear case with the unit square bounding box ($[0,1] \times [0,1]$), each link is described by a truncated linear ceiling:

(\#eq:effectsizechainceilinglinear1)
\begin{equation}
f_j(X_j) = \min(a_j + b_j X_j,\,1), \qquad j=1,\ldots,J.
\end{equation}

Here, $a_j$ is the intercept and $b_j$ is the slope. Assume that each link represents the standard case of necessity (a ceiling line on or above the diagonal in the unit square), i.e., $0<a_j<1$ and $1<a_j+b_j$.

The resulting chained ceiling is again linear:

(\#eq:effectsizechainceilinglinear2)
\begin{equation}
Y \le f_{\mathrm{chain}}(X_1) = a_{\mathrm{chain}} + b_{\mathrm{chain}}\,X_1.
\end{equation}

The chain intercept and slope are:

(\#eq:effectsizeachain)
\begin{equation}
a_{\mathrm{chain}} =
\sum_{j=1}^{J}
\left( a_j \prod_{k=j+1}^{J} b_k \right),
\end{equation}

(\#eq:effectsizebchain)
\begin{equation}
b_{\mathrm{chain}} = \prod_{j=1}^{J} b_j,
\end{equation}

with the convention that an empty product equals $1$.

For ceilings on or above the diagonal, $a_{\mathrm{chain}} \ge 1-b_{\mathrm{chain}}$, which implies $d_{\mathrm{chain}} \le \tfrac{1}{2}b_{\mathrm{chain}}$. In the unit square bounding box $[0,1]\times[0,1]$, the effect size of the (linear) chain ceiling $Y \le a_{\mathrm{chain}} + b_{\mathrm{chain}}X_1$ is:

(\#eq:effectsizechain)
\begin{equation}
d_{\mathrm{chain}} =
\begin{cases}
\dfrac{(1-a_{\mathrm{chain}})^2}{2\,b_{\mathrm{chain}}}, & \text{if } a_{\mathrm{chain}} < 1,\\[6pt]
0, & \text{otherwise.}
\end{cases}
\end{equation}

As shown in Equations \@ref(eq:effectsizeachain) and \@ref(eq:effectsizebchain), each intercept $a_j$ is weighted by the product of downstream slopes $b_{j+1},\ldots,b_J$, and the total intercept $a_{\mathrm{chain}}$ accumulates across the chain. As a result, composing multiple necessary conditions can shift the chain ceiling upward relative to a single link with a comparable slope. Consequently, the chain constraint can become less stringent and may even become non-binding. This explains why, even when each link has a positive effect size, the overall chain can yield a null effect of $X_1$ on $Y$ through the chain.

In other words, for ceilings on or above the diagonal within the bounding box, the “weakest link” principle extends to effect size: the chain effect size is less than or equal to the smallest link effect size.















<!-- ######################## -->
<!-- Normally, a necessary condition is hypothesized (Chapter \@ref(hypothesisformulation)) and analyzed (Chapter \@ref(dataanalysis)) as the necessity relationship between $X$ and $Y$. For multiple necessary conditions, multiple overall necessity relationships between $X_j$ and $Y$ are hypothesized. However, next to parallel necessary conditions, a serial system consisting of  a chain of necessity conditions can also be hypothesized. The overall $X_1 \rightarrow Y$ relationship (if present) always holds, but it may be (partly) explained by a chain of necessity $X_1 \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow Y$. -->

<!-- Section \@ref(parsimonious) has discussed, in general terms, a necessity theory consisting of a chain of necessary conditions (Figure \@ref(fig:fig4model)). For example, a certain amount of study time is necessary for a certain level of knowledge retention (good quiz score), which in turn is necessary for a high exam grade. This section analyzes the chain of necessity algebraically for a chain of necessary conditions. Numerical examples are shown for a chain with two links. -->

<!-- <!-- (a  of $X$ on $Y$ could be explained by an indirect effect via a chain of necessary conditions,  . effect of $X$ on $Y$ where the chain   can be used to evaluate if a observed direct necessity of of direct a direct effect of the first condition on the outcome is added to the indirect effect via the chain. -->

<!-- #### Analysis of chain necessity -->
<!-- The general necessity chain consists of a series of necessary conditions: the first necessary condition $X_1$, then intermediate necessary conditions $X_2, X_3, \ldots, X_J$, and finally the outcome $Y$, with the sequence: -->

<!-- \begin{equation} -->
<!-- (\#eq:ch1) -->
<!-- X_1 \rightarrow X_2 \rightarrow X_3 \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow X_J \rightarrow Y -->
<!-- \end{equation}  -->

<!-- where $J$ is the number of necessary conditions (and this the chain has $J$ links ending in $Y$). -->
<!-- For example, an additional intermediate necessary condition between study hours and quiz score could be active class participation (engagement, asking questions).  -->

<!-- For each link, a ceiling function bounds the subsequent variable: -->

<!-- <!-- \begin{equation} -->
<!-- <!-- (\#eq:ch2) -->
<!-- <!-- X_{j+1} \;\le\; f_j(X_j), \qquad j=1,2,\ldots,J-1, -->
<!-- <!-- \end{equation} -->

<!-- (\#eq:ch2) -->
<!-- \begin{equation} -->
<!-- X_{j+1} \le f_j(X_j), \quad j = 1,\ldots,J-1, \qquad -->
<!-- Y \le f_J(X_J). -->
<!-- \end{equation} -->



<!-- The chain ceiling $f_{\mathrm{chain}}(X_1)$ is the necessity of $X_1$ for $Y$ via the chain. It is the composition: -->

<!-- \begin{equation} -->
<!-- (\#eq:ch3) -->
<!-- Y \;\le\; f_J\!\big(f_{J-1}(\cdots f_1(X_1)\cdots)\big) -->
<!-- \;\;=\;\; f_{\mathrm{chain}}(X_1). -->
<!-- \end{equation} -->

<!-- where $f_J(X_J)$ is the ceiling function of the last link. -->

<!-- The general phrase "the chain is as strong as the weakest link" applies also to the necessity chain. If one of the links is not necessary, the necessity chain collapses and $X_1$ is not necessary for $Y$ via the chain. This applies, for example, when one link is sufficient but not necessary, or when a link is described as an average effect or a correlation without necessity.   -->
<!-- It is assumed that all variables are min–max normalized and all ceilings are in the same bounding box. -->

<!-- In the linear case with the unit square bounding box ($[0,1] \times [0,1]$): -->

<!-- <!-- \begin{equation} -->
<!-- <!-- (\#eq:effectsizechainceilinglinear1) -->
<!-- <!-- f_j(X_j) \;=\; \min(a_j + b_j X_j,1),  -->
<!-- <!-- \qquad Y\le \min(a_j + b_j X_j,1),  -->
<!-- <!-- \qquad j=1,\ldots,J, -->
<!-- <!-- \end{equation} -->

<!-- (\#eq:effectsizechainceilinglinear1) -->
<!-- \begin{equation} -->
<!-- f_j(X_j) \;=\; \min(a_j + b_j X_j,\,1), \qquad j=1,\ldots,J, -->
<!-- \end{equation} -->



<!-- where $a_j$ is the intercept and $b_j$ is the slope.  -->

<!-- Assuming that  each link is the standard case of necessity (ceiling line above the diagonal in the unit square), i.e., $0<a_j<1$ and $1 <a_j+b_j$ -->

<!-- The chained ceiling is again linear: -->

<!-- \begin{equation} -->
<!-- (\#eq:effectsizechainceilinglinear2) -->
<!-- Y \;\le\; f_{\mathrm{chain}}(X_1) \;=\; a_{\mathrm{chain}} + b_{\mathrm{chain}}\,X_1, -->
<!-- \end{equation} -->

<!-- where -->
<!-- \begin{equation} -->
<!-- (\#eq:effectsizeachain) -->
<!-- a_{\mathrm{chain}} \;=\;  -->
<!-- \sum_{j=1}^{J}  -->
<!-- \Bigg( a_j \prod_{k=j+1}^{J} b_k \Bigg), -->
<!-- \end{equation} -->
<!-- \begin{equation} -->
<!-- (\#eq:effectsizebchain) -->
<!-- b_{\mathrm{chain}} \;=\; \prod_{j=1}^{J} b_j, -->
<!-- \end{equation} -->

<!-- with the convention that an empty product equals $1$. -->


<!-- For ceilings on or above the diagonal, $a_{\mathrm{chain}} \ge 1-b_{\mathrm{chain}}$, which implies -->
<!-- $d_{\mathrm{chain}} \le \tfrac{1}{2}b_{\mathrm{chain}}$ and with a unit square $[0,1]\times[0,1]$, bounding box, the effect size of the (linear) chain ceiling -->
<!-- $Y \le a_{\mathrm{chain}} + b_{\mathrm{chain}}X_1$ is: -->

<!-- (\#eq:effectsizechain) -->
<!-- \begin{equation} -->
<!-- d_{\mathrm{chain}} = -->
<!-- \begin{cases} -->
<!-- \dfrac{(1-a_{\mathrm{chain}})^2}{2\,b_{\mathrm{chain}}}, & \text{if } a_{\mathrm{chain}} < 1,\\[6pt] -->
<!-- 0, & \text{otherwise.} -->
<!-- \end{cases} -->
<!-- \end{equation} -->














<!-- In the common situation that each ceiling line is within the bounding box and on or above the diagonal (starting on the left bound and ending on the right bound), with the bounding box min-max normalized to the unit square $[0,1] \times [0,1]$ such that $0 \le b_j \le 1$ and $a_j \ge 1-b_j$, the chain ceiling is also on or above the diagonal: -->

<!-- \begin{equation} -->
<!-- (\#eq:effectsizeachainx) -->
<!-- a_{\mathrm{chain}} \;\ge\; 1 - b_{\mathrm{chain}}. -->
<!-- \end{equation} -->


<!-- In this situation the chain effect size is:  -->

<!-- \begin{equation} -->
<!-- (\#eq:effectsizeachainxx) -->
<!-- d_{\mathrm{chain}} \;\le\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,b_{\mathrm{chain}} \;\le\; \min_j \tfrac{1}{2}\,b_j -->
<!-- \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; -->
<!-- d_{\mathrm{chain}} \;\le\; \min(d_1,\ldots,d_J). -->
<!-- \end{equation} -->

<!-- where \( d_j \) is the effect size of the \( j \)-th link in the chain.  -->

<!-- As shown in Equations \@ref(eq:effectsizeachain) and \@ref(eq:effectsizebchain), each intercept \( a_j \) is amplified by the product of the downstream slopes \( b_{j+1} \), and the total intercept \( a_{\text{chain}} \) accumulates across the entire chain. This composition can push the chain ceiling function upward relative to what would be expected from a single link with comparable slope. In effect, the composition of multiple necessary conditions in a chain creates a less stringent, and possibly non-binding, constraint. This explains why even when each link has an effect size, the overall chain can result in a null effect of $X_1$ on $Y$ via the chain.  -->

<!-- This means that in the situation of ceiling lines on or above the diagonal within the bounding box, the general phrase "the chain is as strong as the weakest link" is extended to the chain effect size: The chain effect size is less than or equal to the smallest effect size in the chain.  -->

<!-- A simulation without restriction on the location of the ceiling line within the bounding box, assuming two links with ceiling line intercepts $0 \leq a_j \leq 0.6$ and slopes of $0 \leq b_j \leq 1.2$, shows that 38% of chain effect sizes satisfy $d_{chain} < \min(d_1,d_2)$ (including 2% with $d_{chain}=0$), 54% lie between the $\min(d_1,d_2)$ and $\max(d_1,d_2)$, and 8% exceed both ($d_{chain} > \max(d_1,d_2)$). -->


<!-- xxxxxh -->

<!-- ing between 0 and 1 on the vertical axis and ending between 0 and 1 on the right edge, without clipping the top or bottom. (``Case A'', where $0 \le a_{\mathrm{chain}} \le 1$ and  -->
<!-- $0 \le a_{\mathrm{chain}} + b_{\mathrm{chain}} \le 1$), the effect size is -->
<!-- \begin{equation} -->
<!-- \label{eq:effectsizechain} -->
<!-- d_{\mathrm{chain}} \;=\; 1 - a_{\mathrm{chain}} - \tfrac{1}{2}\,b_{\mathrm{chain}}. -->
<!-- \end{equation} -->

<!-- % Above-diagonal property -->
<!-- If each link is above the diagonal (that is, $0 \le b_i \le 1$ and $a_i \ge 1-b_i$ for all $i$), -->
<!-- then the chain ceiling is also above the diagonal: -->
<!-- \begin{equation} -->
<!-- a_{\mathrm{chain}} \;\ge\; 1 - b_{\mathrm{chain}}. -->
<!-- \end{equation} -->

<!-- % Bound on effect size -->
<!-- In this regime the chain effect size satisfies -->
<!-- \begin{equation} -->
<!-- d_{\mathrm{chain}} \;\le\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,b_{\mathrm{chain}} \;\le\; \min_i \tfrac{1}{2}\,b_i -->
<!-- \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; -->
<!-- d_{\mathrm{chain}} \;\le\; \min(d_1,\ldots,d_k). -->
<!-- \end{equation} -->

<!-- xxxxx -->
<!-- It is assumed that the ceiling line is within the same bounding box and above the diagonal, which means that the ceiling line crosses the left and upper bounds of the bounding box: -->
<!-- \begin{equation}  -->
<!-- (\#eq:ch3x) -->
<!-- a_c \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{m} a_i \prod_{k=i+1}^{m} b_k  -->
<!-- \;\;\ge\;\; \sum_{i=1}^{m} (1-b_i)\prod_{k=i+1}^{m} b_k -->
<!-- \;=\; 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{m} b_i  -->
<!-- \;=\; 1 - b_c. -->
<!-- \end{equation}  -->

<!-- To express the *chain necessity* of \( X_1 \) for \( Y \) (the necessity of $X_1$ for $Y$ via the chain), the ceiling functions are composed recursively as follows: -->

<!-- \begin{equation}  -->
<!-- (\#eq:ch3) -->
<!-- Y \leq f_k(f_{k-1}(\cdots f_1(X_1))) -->
<!-- \end{equation}  -->

<!-- where $f_k$ is the ceiling function of the last link. -->

<!-- Suppose that each ceiling function is linear within the same bounding box: -->

<!-- \begin{equation} -->
<!-- (\#eq:effectsizechainceilinglinear1) -->
<!-- X_{i+1} \leq f_i(X_i) = a_i + b_i X_i, \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, \ldots, k -->
<!-- \end{equation} -->

<!-- where $a_i$ is the ceiling intercept, $b_i$ is the ceiling slope, and $X_{k+1} = Y$. -->

<!-- Then the ceiling function of the chain necessity \( X_1 \) for \( Y \) is: -->

<!-- \begin{equation}  -->
<!-- (\#eq:effectsizechainceilinglinear2) -->
<!-- Y \leq f_{\text{chain}}(X_1) = a_{\text{chain}} + b_{\text{chain}} X_1 -->
<!-- \end{equation} -->

<!-- where: -->

<!-- \begin{equation}  -->
<!-- (\#eq:effectsizeachain) -->
<!-- a_{\text{chain}} = \sum_{i=1}^k \left( a_i \cdot \prod_{j=i+1}^k b_j \right) -->
<!-- \end{equation} -->

<!-- \begin{equation}  -->
<!-- (\#eq:effectsizebchain) -->
<!-- b_{\text{chain}} = \prod_{i=1}^k b_i -->
<!-- \end{equation} -->

<!-- (with the convention that an empty product like \( \prod_{j=k+1}^k b_j \) equals 1) -->


<!-- The phrase "the  chain is as strong as the weakest link: applies to the necessity chain as well. If one of the links is not necessary ($a_i \geq  1$) the necessity chain collapses and $X_1$ is not necessary for $Y$ via the chain. This applies for example when one link is sufficient but not necessary, or that the links describes an average effect  without necessity    -->



Since 'a chain is as strong as its weakest link', the effect size $d_{chain}$ is less than or equal to the effect sizes of all links:

\begin{equation}
(\#eq:ch4)
d_{\text{chain}} \leq \min(d_1, d_2, \ldots, d_k)
\end{equation}

where \( d_i \) is the effect size of the \( i \)-th link in the chain.

<!-- This phenomenon follows directly from the mathematical structure of the chain ceiling function in Equation \@ref(eq:ch3), which recursively composes the individual ceiling functions of each link.  -->


```{r chainsimulatin, echo=FALSE, eval = FALSE}
# --- NCA chain effect size simulation in normalized space --------------------
set.seed(42)

# Settings
N        <- 200000           # number of simulated cases
eps      <- 1e-9             # tolerance for equality
grid_n   <- 801              # resolution for numerical integration on [0,1]

# Sample intercepts and slopes (normalized space)
# Intercepts a1,a2 ~ Uniform(0, 0.6)
# Slopes     b1,b2 ~ Uniform(0, 1.2)  (allows >1 as well)
a1 <- runif(N, 0, 0.6)
b1 <- runif(N, 0, 1.2)
a2 <- runif(N, 0, 0.6)
b2 <- runif(N, 0, 1.2)

# Chain parameters in normalized space (valid with global normalization)
ac <- a2 + b2 * a1
bc <- b1 * b2

# Effect size function on the unit square:
# d = area above the line y' = a + b x', clipped to [0,1]
effect_size <- function(a, b, grid_n = 801) {
  x  <- seq(0, 1, length.out = grid_n)
  y  <- a + b * x
  y  <- pmin(1, pmax(0, y))          # clip to [0,1]
  h  <- pmax(0, 1 - y)               # height of ceiling zone
  # Trapezoidal integration over x
  dx <- (1 - 0) / (grid_n - 1)
  area <- sum((head(h, -1) + tail(h, -1)) * 0.5) * dx
  area
}

# Vectorized wrapper to compute d for vectors of (a,b)
effect_vec <- function(a, b) {
  vapply(seq_along(a), function(i) effect_size(a[i], b[i], grid_n), numeric(1))
}

# Compute effect sizes
d1 <- effect_vec(a1, b1)   # for Step 1
d2 <- effect_vec(a2, b2)   # for Step 2
dc <- effect_vec(ac, bc)   # for Chain

# Categorize per your request
lt_both        <- sum(dc < pmin(d1, d2) - eps)
eq_zero        <- sum(dc <= eps)
between_excl   <- sum((dc > pmin(d1, d2) + eps) & (dc < pmax(d1, d2) - eps))
gt_both        <- sum(dc > pmax(d1, d2) + eps)

d1_lt_dc_lt_d2 <- sum((d1 + eps < dc) & (dc + eps < d2))
d2_lt_dc_lt_d1 <- sum((d2 + eps < dc) & (dc + eps < d1))

# Summaries
summary_tbl <- data.frame(
  Category = c("d_chain < both",
               "d_chain = 0",
               "min(d1,d2) < d_chain < max(d1,d2)",
               "d_chain > both",
               "d1 < d_chain < d2",
               "d2 < d_chain < d1"),
  Count = c(lt_both, eq_zero, between_excl, gt_both, d1_lt_dc_lt_d2, d2_lt_dc_lt_d1)
)
summary_tbl$Proportion <- round(summary_tbl$Count / N, 4)

means_tbl <- data.frame(
  Metric = c("mean d1", "mean d2", "mean d_chain"),
  Value  = round(c(mean(d1), mean(d2), mean(dc)), 4)
)

print(summary_tbl, row.names = FALSE)
print(means_tbl,    row.names = FALSE)

#Optional: quick visuals (uncomment if you want plots)
# library(ggplot2)
# df_plot <- data.frame(d1 = d1, d2 = d2, dc = dc, d_max = pmax(d1,d2))
# ggplot(df_plot, aes(x = d_max, y = dc)) +
#   geom_point(alpha = 0.2, size = 0.5) +
#   geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
#   labs(title = "Chain effect size vs max(d1, d2)",
#        x = "max(d1, d2)", y = "d_chain") +
#   theme_minimal()
# ggplot(data.frame(diff = dc - pmax(d1, d2)), aes(x = diff)) +
#   geom_histogram(bins = 60) +
#   geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
#   labs(title = "Distribution of d_chain - max(d1, d2)",
#        x = "difference", y = "count") +
#   theme_minimal()

```

#### Numeric examples {#numericexamples}

When the chain consists of two links and assuming ceiling lines above the diagonal, the intercept and slope of the chain necessity function of Equations \@ref(eq:effectsizeachain) and \@ref(eq:effectsizebchain), reduce to:

\begin{equation} 
(\#eq:effectsizebchaintwolinks)
a_{\text{chain}} = a_1b_2 + a_2
\end{equation}

\begin{equation} 
(\#eq:effectsizeachaintwolinks)
b_{\text{chain}} = b_1b_2
\end{equation}

If all variables are min-max normalized to \([0,1]\), the effect size of each link is, according to Equation \@ref(eq:effectsize1): 

\begin{equation} 
(\#eq:effectsizechain)
d_j = \frac{(1 - a_j)^2}{2b_j}
\end{equation}

The equation for effect size $d_{chain}$ of the chain necessity of $X_1$ for $Y$ is:

\begin{equation} 
(\#eq:ch5)
d_{\text{chain}} = 
\begin{cases}
\displaystyle \frac{(1 - a_{\text{chain}})^2}{2b_{\text{chain}}}, & \text{if } a_{\text{chain}} < 1 \\
0, & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
\end{equation} 

Two numeric examples of a chain consisting of two links (with ceiling lines above the diagonal) illustrate the behavior that the chain is as strong as its weakest link. In the first example, the two ceiling functions are:

\begin{align*}
f_1(X_1) = 0.4 + 2.0 \cdot X_1 \\
f_2(X_2) = 0.2 + 1.0 \cdot X_2
\end{align*}

According to Equations \@ref(eq:effectsizeachaintwolinks) and \@ref(eq:effectsizebchaintwolinks) the intercept and slope for the chain ceiling line are:

\begin{align*}
a_{chain} = 0.6  \\
b_{chain} = 2.0
\end{align*}

Following Equation \@ref(eq:effectsizechain), the two effect sizes $d_1$ and $d_2$ are:

\begin{align*}
d_1 = \frac{(1 - 0.4)^2}{2 \cdot 2.0} = \frac{0.36}{4.0} = 0.09  \\
d_2 = \frac{(1 - 0.2)^2}{2 \cdot 1.0} = \frac{0.64}{2.0} = 0.32
\end{align*}

According to Equation \@ref(eq:ch5) the chain effect size is:

\begin{equation}
(\#eq:effectsize1)
d_{chain} = \frac{(1 - 0.6)^2}{2 \cdot 2.0} = \frac{0.16}{4} = 0.04
\end{equation} 

The three $XY$-plots are shown in Figure \@ref(fig:chaineffect1).

```{r chaineffect1, echo=FALSE, fig.show="hold", message=FALSE, out.width = "30%", fig.align = 'center', fig.cap= "Example of a necessity chain. The effect sizes of the links are: $X_1 \\rightarrow X_2$ = 0.09, $X_2 \\rightarrow Y$ = 0.32, $X_1 \\rightarrow Y$ = 0.04."}
par(mfrow = c(1,3))
knitr::include_graphics(c("chaineffect1A.png", "chaineffect1B.png", "chaineffect1C.png"))
```


```{r chaineffect1code, echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE,  eval=FALSE, include=FALSE, fig.width=14, fig.height=4}
library(ggplot2)
#library(dplyr)
#library(tidyr)
#library(patchwork)

a1 = 0.4; b1 = 2.0 #X1-X2
a2 = 0.2; b2 = 1.0 #X2-Y
ac = 0.6; bc = 2.0 #X1-Y via chain

plot_ceiling <- function(a, b, xlab, ylab, title) {
  x_vals <- seq(0, 1, length.out = 100)
  y_vals <- a + b * x_vals
  y_vals <- pmin(1, pmax(0, y_vals)) # clip to [0, 1]

# Value of y when x = 0
  y0 <- pmin(1, pmax(0, a))  # ensures it stays in [0,1]  
  
  df <- data.frame(x = x_vals, y = y_vals)
  
  ggplot(df, aes(x = x)) +
    geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = y, ymax = 1), fill = "white", alpha = 0.6) +
    geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = 0, ymax = y), fill = "grey", alpha = 0.6) +
    geom_line(aes(y = y), color = "black", linewidth = 1) +
    # vertical segment at x = 0 from (0,0) to (0,y0)
    geom_segment(aes(x = 0, xend = 0, y = 0, yend = y0),
                 linewidth = 1, color = "black") +
    coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0,1), ylim = c(0,1)) +
    labs(x = xlab, y = ylab, title = title) +
    theme_minimal()
}

# Plot 1: X1 → X2
p1 <- plot_ceiling(a = a1, b = b1, xlab = "X1", ylab = "X2", title = "X1 → X2")

# Plot 2: X2 → Y
p2 <- plot_ceiling(a = a2, b = b2, xlab = "X2", ylab = "Y",  title = "X2 → Y")

# Plot 3: X1 → Y (chain)
p3 <- plot_ceiling(a = ac, b = bc, xlab = "X1", ylab = "Y",  title = "X1 → Y via chain")

# # Save the plot as a PDF
# ggsave("chaineffect1A.pdf", plot = p1, width = 5, height = 5, dpi = 600)
# ggsave("chaineffect1B.pdf", plot = p2, width = 5, height = 5, dpi = 600)
# ggsave("chaineffect1C.pdf", plot = p3, width = 5, height = 5, dpi = 600)

# Combine
#print(p1 + p2 + p3)

```
The second example is an example where chain necessity vanishes. Two ceiling functions are:
\begin{align*}
f_1(X_1) = 0.4 + 0.7 \cdot X_1 \\  
f_2(X_2) = 0.7 + 0.8 \cdot X_2
\end{align*}

According to Equations \@ref(eq:effectsizeachaintwolinks) and \@ref(eq:effectsizebchaintwolinks), the intercept and slope for the composite ceiling lines are:
\begin{align*}
a_{chain} = 1.02  \\
b_{chain} = 0.56
\end{align*}

According to Equation \@ref(eq:effectsizechain), the two effect sizes $d_1$ and $d_2$ are:
\begin{align*}
d_1 = \frac{(1 - 0.4)^2}{2 \cdot 0.7} = \frac{0.36}{1.4} \approx 0.26 \\
d_2 = \frac{(1 - 0.7)^2}{2 \cdot 0.8} = \frac{0.09}{1.6} \approx 0.06
\end{align*}

Referring to Equation \@ref(eq:ch5), the chain effect size is:
\begin{equation}
d_{chain} = 0
\end{equation}

The three $XY$-plots are shown in Figure \@ref(fig:chaineffect2).

```{r chaineffect2, echo=FALSE, fig.show="hold", message=FALSE, out.width = "30%", fig.align = 'center', fig.cap= "Example of a necessity chain. The effect sizes of the links are: $X_1 \\rightarrow X_2$ = 0.26, $X_2 \\rightarrow Y$ = 0.06, $X_1 \\rightarrow Y$ = 0."}
knitr::include_graphics('chaineffect2A.png')
knitr::include_graphics('chaineffect2B.png')
knitr::include_graphics('chaineffect2C.png')
```

```{r chaineffect2code, , echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE,  eval=FALSE, include=FALSE, fig.width=14, fig.height=4}
library(ggplot2)

a1 = 0.4; b1 = 0.7
a2 = 0.7; b2 = 0.8
ac = 1.02; bc = 0.56

plot_ceiling <- function(a, b, xlab, ylab, title) {
  x_vals <- seq(0, 1, length.out = 100)
  y_vals <- a + b * x_vals
  y_vals <- pmin(1, pmax(0, y_vals)) # clip to [0, 1]
  
  
# Value of y when x = 0
  y0 <- pmin(1, pmax(0, a))  # ensures it stays in [0,1]  
  
  df <- data.frame(x = x_vals, y = y_vals)
  
  ggplot(df, aes(x = x)) +
    geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = y, ymax = 1), fill = "white", alpha = 0.6) +
    geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = 0, ymax = y), fill = "grey", alpha = 0.6) +
    geom_line(aes(y = y), color = "black", linewidth = 1) +
    # vertical segment at x = 0 from (0,0) to (0,y0)
    geom_segment(aes(x = 0, xend = 0, y = 0, yend = y0),
                 linewidth = 1, color = "black") +
    coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0,1), ylim = c(0,1)) +
    labs(x = xlab, y = ylab, title = title) +
    theme_minimal()
}

# Plot 1: X1 → X2
p1 <- plot_ceiling(a = a1, b = b1, xlab = "X1", ylab = "X2", title = "X1 → X2")

# Plot 2: X2 → Y
p2 <- plot_ceiling(a = a2, b = b2, xlab = "X2", ylab = "Y",  title = "X2 → Y")

# Plot 3: X1 → Y (chain)
p3 <- plot_ceiling(a = ac, b = bc, xlab = "X1", ylab = "Y",  title = "X1 → Y")

# Save the plot as a PDF
 # ggsave("chaineffect2A.pdf", plot = p1, width = 5, height = 5, dpi = 600)
 # ggsave("chaineffect2B.pdf", plot = p2, width = 5, height = 5, dpi = 600)
 # ggsave("chaineffect2C.pdf", plot = p3, width = 5, height = 5, dpi = 600)

# Combine
# print(p1 + p2 + p3)

```

The examples illustrate that the chain effect size is smaller than the link effect sizes, and that the chain effect size can be zero while those of the links are not.

<!-- ### Necessity chain rules {#necessitychainrules} -->

<!-- In the previous section it was shown that a necessity chain is broken if one link is not necessary. Therefore the first rule for a necessity chain reads: -->

<!-- > **Rule 1. A necessity chain is broken if a link is broken.** -->

<!-- It may be that the link is sufficient (but not necessary) or that it contributes on average (but is not necessary). -->

<!-- > **Rule 2. The position of conditions in the necessity chain matters.** -->

<!-- A condition early in the chain gets its contribution dampened through all later links, while a condition late in the chain affects the ceiling almost directly.  -->



<!-- This is the reason that in the direct link, the necessary condition is most pronounced compared when it is part of a necessity chain.   -->
<!-- When there are $J+1$ variables (multiple necessary conditions and one outcome) then there are $J - 1$ intermediate conditions. When the first condition and the outcome are fixed while the intermediates have an arbitrary order, there are the following number of permutations of intermediates (and number of chains):  -->

<!-- \begin{equation}  -->
<!-- (\#eq:ch10) -->
<!-- \text{chains} \;=\; \sum_{Q=0}^{\,J-1} \binom{\,J-1\,}{Q}\; Q!  -->
<!-- \end{equation}  -->


<!-- where $Q$ is the number of selected intermediates in the chain. -->

<!-- According to this rule one of many potential necessity chains could exist for a specific $XY$-relationship. For example, according to Equation \@ref(eq:ch10), with four conditions and one outcome (J+1 = 5), there are 16 possible chains for the necessity of a given $X$ (here $X_1$) for $Y$: -->

<!-- *Direct:* -->
<!-- 1. $X_1 \rightarrow Y$ -->

<!-- *Single-intermediate:*   -->
<!-- 2. $X_1 \rightarrow X_2 \rightarrow Y$   -->
<!-- 3. $X_1 \rightarrow X_3 \rightarrow Y$   -->
<!-- 4. $X_1 \rightarrow X_4 \rightarrow Y$   -->

<!-- *Two intermediates:*   -->
<!-- 5.  $X_1 \rightarrow X_2 \rightarrow X_3 \rightarrow Y$   -->
<!-- 6.  $X_1 \rightarrow X_3 \rightarrow X_2 \rightarrow Y$   -->
<!-- 7.  $X_1 \rightarrow X_2 \rightarrow X_4 \rightarrow Y$   -->
<!-- 8.  $X_1 \rightarrow X_4 \rightarrow X_2 \rightarrow Y$   -->
<!-- 9.  $X_1 \rightarrow X_3 \rightarrow X_4 \rightarrow Y$   -->
<!-- 10. $X_1 \rightarrow X_4 \rightarrow X_3 \rightarrow Y$   -->

<!-- *All three intermediates:*   -->
<!-- 11. $X_1 \rightarrow X_2 \rightarrow X_3 \rightarrow X_4 \rightarrow Y$   -->
<!-- 12. $X_1 \rightarrow X_2 \rightarrow X_4 \rightarrow X_3 \rightarrow Y$   -->
<!-- 13. $X_1 \rightarrow X_3 \rightarrow X_2 \rightarrow X_4 \rightarrow Y$   -->
<!-- 14. $X_1 \rightarrow X_3 \rightarrow X_4 \rightarrow X_2 \rightarrow Y$   -->
<!-- 15. $X_1 \rightarrow X_4 \rightarrow X_2 \rightarrow X_3 \rightarrow Y$   -->
<!-- 16. $X_1 \rightarrow X_4 \rightarrow X_3 \rightarrow X_2 \rightarrow Y$ -->

<!-- A similar set of chains are possible when $X_2$ is the first (fixed condition) -->

<!-- According to Chain Rule 1, only one of the chain options can be necessary (if necessity holds). Normally, necessary conditions are hypothesized (see Chapter \@ref(hypothesisformulation)) and analysed (see Chapter \@ref(dataanalysis) as the direct link between $X$ and $Y$. Often there is no theoretical rationale for all chains, but i may be that a the necessity between one or more conditions is theoretically justified. -->

<!-- > **Rule 3. The necessity between $X$ and $Y$ via a chain is only plausible if there is theoretical support for all necessity links in the chain.** -->

<!-- This is a consequence of Rule 1, which states that without necessity in a link, chain necessity does not exist.  -->

<!-- > **Rule 4. A theoretically supported  necessity chain is only plausible if the effect size of the chain is considerably larger than the effect size of the direct link.** -->

<!-- Empirical results often show that the direct effect size is larger than the chain effect, see Rule 3. However, if such theoretically and empirically supported chain exists, Rule 1 implies that observed direct effects (and other chain effects) are spurious.  -->


<!-- For example, with two necessary conditions the possible chains could be: -->

<!-- $X_1 \rightarrow X_2 \rightarrow Y$  -->

<!-- $X_2 \rightarrow X_1 \rightarrow Y$  -->

<!-- When data are available, the chain effect sizes $d_{X_1Y}$ and $d_{X_1Y}$ -->


<!-- Then it cn -->



<!-- It is possible that $X_1$ has not only a *chain effect* (*indirect effect*) on $Y$ , but also a *direct effect* or an effect via *another necessity chain* on $Y$. -->
<!-- For example, consider a chain $X_1 \rightarrow X_2 \rightarrow Y$ consisting and a direct effect $X_1 \rightarrow Y$.  -->
<!-- b -->

<!-- \begin{equation} -->
<!-- \begin{cases} -->
<!-- \displaystyle X_1 \rightarrow Y, & \text{direct effect} \\ -->
<!-- X_1 \rightarrow X_2 \rightarrow Y, & \text{chain effect (indirect effect)} -->
<!-- \end{cases} -->
<!-- \end{equation}  -->

<!-- Suppose all necessity effects are observed: $X_1 \rightarrow Y$ (direct), $X_2 \rightarrow Y$, $X_1 \rightarrow X_2$. -->

<!-- When comparing the chain (indirect) effect of $X_1$ on $Y$ with its direct effect, the three possible outcomes are: -->

<!-- 1. The direct effect is considerably larger than the chain effect. This suggests that the direct necessity effect dominates. The indirect necessity effect may still exists but is weaker or redundant. -->
<!-- 2. The direct effect is about the same as the chain effect. This suggests that the necessity effect could be explained either way.      -->
<!-- 3. The direct effect is considerably smaller than the chain effect effect. This suggests the observed direct effect come originates from the necessity chain.   -->


<!-- ### Testing spuriousness -->
<!-- To guard against spuriousness necessity relations, NCA starts with theorizing causal necessity relationships (see Chapter \@ref(hypothesisformulation)). Without a necessity theory, analysing pointal data bear the risk that one or more observed $XY$ necessity patterns may be spurious. When two relations $X_1 \rightarrow Y$  and $X_2 \rightarrow Y$  suggest support for necessity because the $X_1Y$, $X_2Y$ plots have empty spaces in the upper-left corner, the true necessity may come from a chain of necessity between the variables. To empirically test if an observed necessity relationship may be (partly) compatible with spuriousness, the necessity effect sizes of a direct relation is compared with the necessity effect sizes of a chain.  -->
<!-- Large direct effect size relative to chain effect size support for the direct effect; small direct effect size relative to chain effect could give support for the chain effect. -->
<!-- size could be a byproduct of the chain such that spuriousness in possible. Therefore the following additional data analyses can be done to evaluate potential spuriousness (it is assumed that all variables are normalized using the same bounding box). -->


<!-- ### Comparing global and chain necessity {#testingnecessitychains} -->
<!-- When a necessity chain is theoretical plausible the necessity effect size of the chain necessity may be compared with the global necessity of the condition. -->

<!-- When the ceiling lines of all condition of a chain are straight and above the diagonal, and the variables are normalized with the same bounding box, the estimated ceiling intercepts and slopes of the conditions can be used as input to Equations \@ref(eq:effectsizeachain) and \@ref(eq:effectsizebchain) to obtain the chain effect size. -->

<!-- The test is illustrated with an example of two necessary conditions and one outcome ($n = 3$). It is assumed  that all variables are normalized using the same bounding box. It is also assumed that all possible chains are theoretical supported (which is normally unlikely).  -->

<!-- For analyzing the necessity effect of the first condition ($X_1$), according to Equation \@ref(eq:ch10) the following chains can theoretically exist: -->


<!-- 1. Specify potential direct and indirect relations -->
<!--     - Direct $X_1Y$ : $X_1 \rightarrow Y$ -->
<!--     - Indirect $X_1Y$: $X_1 \rightarrow X_2 \rightarrow Y$ -->

<!-- 2. Estimate the necessity effect sizes (observed) -->
<!--     - $d_{X_1Y}$ -->
<!--     - $d_{X_2Y}$ -->

<!-- 3. Estimate  necessity effect size of relations between the necessary conditions (observed) -->
<!--     - $d_{X_1X_2}$ -->

<!-- 4. Calculate chain necessity effect size -->
<!--     - $d_{X_1X_2Y}$ -->

<!-- 5. Compare effect sizes: -->
<!--     - if  $d_{X_1Y}$ >> $d_{X_1X_2Y}$ the direct effect is empirically plausible -->
<!--     - if  $d_{X_1Y}$ << $d_{X_1X_2Y}$ the chain effect is empirically plausible -->

<!-- ```{r, echo = FALSE, eval = FALSE} -->
<!-- library(NCA) -->
<!-- data(nca.example) -->
<!-- data <- nca.example -->
<!-- dataW <- data[-c(14,22,26),] -->

<!-- #normalize -->

<!--     stop("Scale must be a numeric vector with two elements (e.g., c(0, 100)).") -->
<!--   } -->
<!--   if (!is.numeric(theoretical_min) || !is.numeric(theoretical_max)) { -->
<!--     stop("Theoretical min and max must be numeric vectors.") -->
<!--   } -->

<!--   normalized_data <- data -->

<!--         normalized_data[[i]] <- NA_real_ -->

<!--         norm_values <- ((data[[i]] - min_val) / (max_val - min_val)) * -->

<!--         norm_values[abs(norm_values - new_max) < tol] <- new_max -->
<!--         norm_values <- pmax(new_min, pmin(new_max, norm_values)) -->
<!--         normalized_data[[i]] <- norm_values -->

<!--   normalized_data -->
