**➔ Q12. Is it explained why there is no substitute for the absence of $X$?**
Next, it is considered if, when a digital platform is absent, other technologies for example, email or satellite communication could serve as substitutes. Email is an asynchronous communication tool that may support collaboration in a broader sense, but does not enable the real-time interaction required for a public online meeting. Satellite communication may offer connectivity for specific groups or in remote areas, but does not facilitate general public online meetings. If no substitute exists in the theoretical domain, with the absence of an Internet connection the outcome cannot be achieved, confirming that $X$ is irreplaceable in this context $\rightarrow$ yes.

**➔ Q13. Are temporal aspects considered?**
An online meeting cannot begin without a working Internet connection already in place. Thus, the Internet connection must exist before or at least at the onset of the meeting. The Internet connection is necessary both for the onset of the online meeting (to initiate the call or session), and the continuation of the meeting (to maintain the live connection throughout). The necessity of an Internet connection for an online meeting is time-dependent in a technological sense: Before Internet-based communication tools existed (e.g., before the 1990s), an Internet connection was not necessary — online meetings didn’t exist as a concept, so only after 1990 the hypothesis is non-trivial. In the future, alternative technologies might make traditional Internet connections obsolete and become substitutes $\rightarrow$ yes.

If so, the hypothesis is now a formal necessity hypothesis, and is ready to be tested with empirical data.



<!-- XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX -->




<!-- examples -->


<!-- 3. Can I find cases where the absence of the condition is substituted ( compensated by another factor to allow the outcome)? -->

<!-- For the first question, cases with the outcome present are evaluated: -->

<!-- - Select imaginary cases (i.e., instances of the focal unit from the theoretical domain) where the outcome occurs. -->

<!-- - Check whether the condition is also present. -->

<!-- - Try to find exceptions where the condition is absent. -->

<!-- For the second question, cases without the condition are evaluated: -->
<!-- - Select imaginary cases (i.e., instances of the focal unit from the theoretical domain) without the condition. -->
<!-- - Check whether the outcome is absent. -->
<!-- - Try to find exceptions where the outcome is present. -->

<!-- For the third question, cases without the condition and with the outcome are evaluated: -->
<!-- - Select imaginary cases (i.e., instances of the focal unit from the theoretical domaain) without the condition and with the outcome. -->
<!-- - Identify substitutes for the absence of the condition. -->

<!-- For answering the first question for the Internet example, any person in the world who attends an online meeting can be selected. Most likely, the selected person has an internet connection. However, exceptions can be imagined: A person attending an online meeting may have an meeting with someone in the same organization an their computers may be connected by a LAN network (Local Area Network). Such local network does not utilizes the public Internet. This falsifying case can be considered an exception. A second type of exception is that a person without Internet can physically join the workplace of someone who is in the meeting.   -->

<!-- For answering the second question for the Internet example, any person in the world who does not have an Internet connection can be selected. Most likely, the selected person is not attending an online meeting. Again the two types of exceptions bould be identified this way as well. -->

<!-- For answering the third question of the Internet example, any person in the world who does not have an internet connection, but is attending an online meeting  can be selected. This is possible when the person has one of the above mentioned substitutes for an internet connection: a LAN network (if the meeting is with other persons connected with the same network) or access to the workplace of another person with a Internet connection.  -->



<!-- ### Handling exceptions and substitutes  -->
<!-- If exceptions and substitutes exist four different approaches can be used: -->

<!-- Eliminate the exceptions -->
<!-- 1. Redefine concepts to include/exclude exceptions   -->
<!-- The exception 'LAN network' can be included by redefining the condition 'internet connection' as 'network connection' defined as *the link or communication pathway between a devices such as a computer or smartphone and the global or local network known as Internet or LAN*. This means that the exception does not exist anymore as all people that have an online meeting have a network connection.   -->

<!-- The exception 'LAN network' can be excluded  by redefining the condition 'internet connection' as *the link or communication pathway between a devices such as a computer or smartphone and the global network known a Internet or LAN*.  This means that the exception does not exist anymore as all people that do not have an global Internet connection meeting have a network connection.   -->

<!-- and the global public network of interconnected computers known as the Internet* or to exclude LAN networks as:  -->


<!-- Which choice is made depends on the goal. depending on the intentions.  -->

<!-- . It can be excluded by redefining the   redefine X from internet to to mean *public* internet access. -->

<!-- 1. Reject the hypothesis following the deterministic necessity perspective. -->


<!-- 2. Accept the hypotheses with a few exceptions using the typicality necessity perspective. -->

<!-- 3. Redefine concepts to include/exclude exceptions   -->
<!-- redefine X from internet to to mean *public* internet access. -->


<!-- 4. Redefine the theoretical domain to include/exclude. -->


<!-- For example  -->

<!-- explore whether the condition was poorly defined or if the domain was too broad. -->


<!-- ### Handling substitutes -->
<!-- Some exceptions may reveal that the condition or outcome was defined too narrowly or broadly. For example, a person without a public internet connection attending a meeting via a private LAN might necessitate refining “internet connection” to mean *public* internet access. -->

<!-- Experimenting with Plausibility -->

<!-- - **Remove the condition**: Would the outcome still happen? (e.g., Can someone attend a meeting without internet?) -->
<!-- - **Add a substitute**: Could another factor compensate for the condition’s absence? (If not, the condition is likely necessary.) -->

<!-- ### Revise, Reject, or Confirm -->
<!-- Use the thought experiment to: -->
<!-- - **Reject** the hypothesis if counterexamples are convincing. -->
<!-- - Typicality: few exeption allow -->
<!-- - **Adapt** the hypothesis by refining the concepts or limiting the domain. -->
<!-- - **Confirm** the hypothesis if no credible exceptions exist. -->

<!-- ### Describe mechanism and Specify the temporal order -->

<!-- ## Step 3: Final necessity theory and hypothesis -->

<!-- Hypothesis is formulated  -->
<!-- repeated for a theory with multiple necessary conditions (see Chapter \@ref(theory)). -->

<!-- ################################# -->



<!-- testing two: improve the quality of the hypothesis. not well embedded  -->

<!-- complete interm of the  -->

<!-- why: -->
<!-- 1. theoretical support is requirement -->
<!-- 2. spe -->

<!-- - precise definition of concepts: valid measurement of related variables, part of porposition.  -->
<!-- - precise definition of focal unit: unit of analysis,  case examples of the focal unit, selection , measurement  -->
<!-- - precise definition of theoretical domain: samples from it -->
<!-- - precise specification of proposition: direction, empty corner -->

<!-- add mechanism -->

<!-- result:  -->
<!-- H1: X is necessary for Y  -->
<!-- X = ..  -->
<!-- Y = .. -->
<!-- focal unit = -->
<!-- theoretical domain =  -->


<!-- As discussed in part I of the book, NCA adopts a unique causal perspective that emphasizes what level of the condition must be present for a certain target level of the outcome to occur.  -->
<!-- Unlike probabilistic theories that focus on average effects (i.e., “X increases Y”), necessity theories ask whether *the absence of a certain level of X* guarantees *the absence of a certain level of Y*.  -->
<!-- A necessity theory (Chapter \@ref(theory)) describes the relationship between $X$ and $Y$ using necessity logic. A necessary theory can be an academic theory or a 'theory in use' that is or used in practice.  A necessity theory includes the following four core elements: -->

<!-- - **Focal Unit**: The entity being studied (e.g., person, team, organization). -->
<!-- - **Concepts**: Attributes or characteristics of the focal unit (e.g., intelligence, creativity of a person). -->
<!-- - **Proposition**: Theoretical statements that specify a necessity relationship between concepts. -->
<!-- - **Theoretical Domain**: The universe of cases (the context) in which the theory is assumed to hold. -->

<!-- The development of a necessity theory implies the specification of these core elements and consists of three steps. In the first step a *preliminary necessity theory* is formulated by specifying the focal unit, the concepts (condition $X$ and outcome $Y$), the proposition and the theoretical domain. The second step *challenges and refines* the preliminary theory using a thought experiment. The third step is the specification of the *final necessity theory* and formulates  the necessity hypothesis to be tested with empirical data. -->


<!-- 1. Specify the focal unit -->

<!-- 2. Specify the target Y -->

<!-- 3. Specify the X or X's -->

<!-- 4. Specify the preliminary proposition -->

<!-- 5. Specify the preliminary theoretical domain -->

<!-- 6. Challenge and refine -->
<!-- - conduct though experiment -->
<!-- - describe the mechanism -->
<!-- - specify the temporal order -->
<!-- - handling exceptions -->

<!-- 7. Specify the final theory -->

<!-- ### Step 1 Specify concepts and focal unit -->

<!-- In a necessity theory the concepts are the condition $X$ and and the outcome $Y$ which both are characteristics of the focal unit. A common starting point for formulating a necessity theory is to specify the target outcome $Y$. The outcome is often the main characteristic of interest. It can vary and is explained by necessity logic. The target $Y$ can be a desirable, undesirable, or neutral characteristic. Desirable outcomes include, for example, health, well-being, performance, innovation, and success. Undesirable outcomes may be sickness, risk, or failure. Neutral outcomes are those that are neither inherently good nor bad, such as body height, age, weight, temperature, or population size.  -->

<!-- After the outcome is specified, the potential necessary condition $X$. for this outcome needs to be formulated. -->



<!-- Next to the concept itself, it may be needed also to specify the target level of the outcome, for example if the interest is in high outcome or low outcome. In the theory such level is only expressed qualitatively. For example, 'presence of $Y$', 'absence of $Y$', 'low $Y$', 'high $Y$', etc. and this can be visualized by XY  (see Figure \@ref(fig:direction2)). -->



<!-- In the two above examples the target $Y$'s are presence of *specific behavior* (of a person) and high *child body height* (of a parent-child pair).  -->

<!-- Since the target outcome is a specific characteristic of the focal unit, specifying $Y$ often immediately reveals what the focal unit is. For example, if a person's intelligence is the target outcome, then 'person' is the focal unit. Similarly, if a firm's innovation performance is the target outcome, then 'firm' is the focal unit. However, sometimes this is less clear, for example when the child height is the target $Y$ of interest,   -->

<!-- Since the target outcome is a certain characteristic of the focal unit, from specifying the $Y$ it the focal unit s immediately clear. For example, when a person's intelligence is the target outcome, 'person' is the focal unit, or when a firm's innovation performance is the target outcome 'firm' is the focal unit.          -->
<!-- Step 1.1 Specify the focal unit -->

<!-- In the first step the focal unit of the theory is specified. Sometimes this is simple, for example when the existing Theory of Planned Behavior [TPB, @ajzen1991theory] is reformulated as a necessity theory. TPB is a well-known theory about individuals, thus the focal unit is 'individual' or 'person'. Other times the focal unit may be not immediately clear, for example when theorizing the relationship between parent height an child height [@galton1886regression]. Here the focal unit is 'parent-child pair'. Note that the focal unit should be expressed as a singular, not a plural, noun.      -->

<!-- Step 1.2 Specify the target $Y$ -->

<!-- The target $Y$ represents the outcome concept of interest. The target $Y$ is a characteristic of the focal unit that can vary. A target level of $Y$ will be absent if a necessary condition for the presence of the target level is absent. The target $Y$ can be desirable or undesirable. Desirable outcomes are for example, health, well-being, performance, innovation, success, etc. Undesirable outcomes may be sickness, risk, failure, etc. Usually, levels of concepts of a theory are only expressed qualitatively. This means that the the target $Y$ is expressed for example as 'present/absent' or 'low/high'.  In the two above examples the target $Y$'s are presence of *specific behavior* (of a person) and high *child body height* (of a parent-child combination).  -->

<!-- Step 1.3 Specify the $X$ or $X$'s -->
<!-- The $X$ or $X$'s represent the condition(s) that must be present to have the target $Y$. Similar to the target $Y$, a condition $X$ is another characteristic of the focal unit that can vary. Also the levels of condition concepts of a theory are usually expressed qualitatively. In the two above examples, a possible $X$ is presence of *intention for a specific behavior* (of a person) and a high *parent body height* (of a parent-child combination).  -->

<!-- ### Step 2 Specify the preliminary proposition -->
<!-- Since the $X$ (Step 2) and $Y$ (Step 3) are now formulated as characteristic of the same focal unit (Step 1), the preliminary proposition can be formulated as "X is necessary for Y" assuming presence/high $X$ is necessary for the presence/high $Y$ (\mbox{\textbf{+ nc +}} relationship). Other 'directions' of the necessity relationship are possible as well (see Section \@ref(direction)): absence/low $X$ is necessary for presence/high $Y$ (\mbox{\textbf{- nc +}}), presence/high $X$ is necessary for absence/low $Y$ (\mbox{\textbf{+ nc -}}), absence/low $X$ is necessary for presence/high $Y$ (\mbox{\textbf{- nc +}}), absence/low $X$ is necessary absence/low $Y$ (\mbox{\textbf{- nc -}}). For the two examples, the preliminary propositions are " the presence of *intention for a specific behavior* is necessary for the presence of *the specific behavior*"  and "a high *parent body height* is necessary for a high *child body height*". -->



<!-- Step 1.5 Specify the preliminary theoretical domain -->
<!-- The theoretical domain specifies the universe of units (cases) of the focal unit to which the proposition is intended to apply. This universe can be defined in various ways, such as temporally (e.g., a specific time period or era), geographically (e.g., a region, country, or global context), demographically (e.g., age group, profession, or population segment), or conceptually (e.g., type of organization or system). For the two examples the theoretical domains are for example 'all adults in the world', and 'all adult parent-child combinations in the world' (adult parents and children after reaching adulthood), respectively. -->




<!-- ## 2. What Is a Hypothesis and Why Do We Need It? -->

<!-- A hypothesis is a testable expectation about the causal relationship between variables. In the context of necessity theory, a **necessity hypothesis** articulates that one variable (the condition) is necessary for another (the outcome).  -->

<!-- Formulating such hypotheses is essential because it allows researchers to operationalize abstract theoretical propositions and test them empirically. The process of hypothesis development, refinement, and testing not only challenges theoretical claims but also builds scientific confidence in their validity. -->

<!-- ## 3. What Is a Necessity Hypothesis? -->

<!-- A necessity hypothesis expresses the belief that the absence of a certain condition prevents the outcome from occurring. The standard form is: -->

<!-- > **X is necessary for Y** -->

<!-- Alternative phrasings might include: -->
<!-- - "Low X is necessary for high Y" -->
<!-- - "The absence of X prevents Y" -->
<!-- - "Without X, Y cannot occur" -->

<!-- Unlike typical causal statements like "X increases Y," necessity hypotheses avoid verbs like *increase* or *influence*, and instead use terms referring to presence or absence (e.g., low, high, none, some). The implication is clear: the absence of the condition X *creates a boundary* beyond which Y cannot occur. This is visually represented as an **empty corner** in a scatter plot of X and Y — a signature pattern in Necessary Condition Analysis (NCA). -->

<!-- ## 4. How to Formulate a Necessity Hypothesis -->

<!-- ### Step 1: Preliminary Proposition -->
<!-- Start with an initial proposition based on the academic literature and expert insights. This proposition assumes that one concept is necessary for another within a defined context. -->
<!-- Where Do Necessity Hypotheses Come From? -->


<!-- ### Step 2: Thought Experiment -->
<!-- Mentally test the proposition by imagining real-world or hypothetical cases. Ask: Can I think of any cases where Y is present but X is absent? If yes, the proposition might need revision. If not, it holds as a potential necessity. -->

<!-- Evaluating Hypotheses Using Thought Experiments -->

<!-- A **thought experiment** is a mental simulation used to test the plausibility of a necessity hypothesis. This involves two stages: -->

<!-- Justifying the Necessity Proposition -->

<!-- To strengthen your necessity hypothesis, answer these three questions: -->

<!-- 1. **Why does Y not occur without X?** -->
<!-- 2. **Why is X always present when Y is present?** -->
<!-- 3. **Why can’t any other variable compensate for the absence of X?** -->

<!-- This causal reasoning helps explain the mechanism behind the necessity and builds theoretical credibility. -->

<!-- A. Evaluating Cases with the Outcome Present -->

<!-- - Select cases where the outcome occurs. -->
<!-- - Check whether the condition is also present. -->
<!-- - Try to find edge cases or exceptions. -->
<!-- - If exceptions exist, they may help redefine the concepts or theoretical scope. -->

<!-- B. Evaluating Cases with the Condition Absent -->

<!-- - Select cases without the condition. -->
<!-- - Check whether the outcome is absent. -->
<!-- - If exceptions exist, explore whether the condition was poorly defined or if the domain was too broad. -->

<!-- Handling Exceptions -->

<!-- Some exceptions may reveal that the condition or outcome was defined too narrowly or broadly. For example, a person without a public internet connection attending a meeting via a private LAN might necessitate refining “internet connection” to mean *public* internet access. -->

<!-- Experimenting with Plausibility -->

<!-- - **Remove the condition**: Would the outcome still happen? (e.g., Can someone attend a meeting without internet?) -->
<!-- - **Add a substitute**: Could another factor compensate for the condition’s absence? (If not, the condition is likely necessary.) -->






<!-- ### Step 3: Revise, Reject, or Confirm -->
<!-- Use the thought experiment to: -->
<!-- - **Reject** the hypothesis if counterexamples are convincing. -->
<!-- - **Adapt** the hypothesis by refining the concepts or limiting the domain. -->
<!-- - **Confirm** the hypothesis if no credible exceptions exist. -->

<!-- ### Step 4: Completing the Necessity Theory -->

<!-- To formalize a necessity theory, clearly define: -->
<!-- - **Focal Unit**: Who or what is being studied? -->
<!-- - **Condition and Outcome**: Which concepts are being related? -->
<!-- - **Theoretical Domain**: In which context(s) does the relationship hold? -->

<!-- If the hypothesis survives this scrutiny, it is ready for empirical testing. -->




<!-- **Example**:   -->
<!-- > *“A connection to the internet is necessary for attending an online meeting.”*   -->
<!-- - Focal unit: Person   -->
<!-- - Condition: Internet connection   -->
<!-- - Outcome: Online meeting attendance   -->
<!-- - Theoretical domain: All individuals attempting to join virtual meetings across the globe -->

<!-- This proposition implies that no person without an internet connection attends an online meeting — creating an *empty upper-left corner* in the X–Y data space. -->


<!-- ############################## -->
<!-- ## Summary of this chapter -->


<!-- In Chapter \@ref(theory) of Part I of this book, a necessity theory was defined as a theory that employs a necessity causal perspective for describing relationships between concepts (propositions). Four fundamental elements of a necessity theory were identified: (1) focal unit (e.g., person, organization, country), (2) concepts (variable characteristic of focal unit), (3) propositions (causal relations between concepts), and (4) theoretical domain (generalization to where the theory holds).  -->

<!-- Testing a causal theory starts with operationalizing the theory such that theoretical *concepts* become measurable *variables* (that can be qualitatively or quantitatively scored), and theoretical *propositions* become testable *hypotheses*.  -->

<!-- In this chapter a necessity hypothesis is defined as a statement that expresses a causal relationship between the variables $X$ (the condition) and $Y$ (the outcome). It is a claim made by the researcher or data analyst  which needs to be challenged and tested with empirical data to build confidence. Just like a theory, the hypothesis has a focal unit and a theoretical domain where it is supposed to hold. -->

<!-- A necessity hypothesis can be developed in three steps.  -->

<!-- 1. A preliminary necessity proposition is formulated based on existing knowledge from academic or practical literature and expert opinion -->

<!-- 2. The hypothesis is mentally evaluated using a thought experiment.  -->

<!-- 3. The results of the thought experiment are used to either: -->

<!--     - Reject the preliminary necessity hypothesis. -->

<!--     - Adapt the preliminary necessity hypothesis (by refinement of variable definitions and/or theoretical domain). -->

<!--     - Keep the preliminary hypothesis.  -->

<!-- If a (revised) hypothesis survives after the thought experiment, it is ready for empirical testing.  -->



<!-- ##	What is a hypothesis and why do we need it? -->

<!-- A hypothesis is an expectation about a causal relationship between variables.  -->

<!-- ##	What is a necessity hypothesis? -->

<!-- ##  How to formulate a necessity hypothesis? -->

<!-- ##  How to test a necessity hypothesis? -->



<!-- 1. Strategic goals -->
<!-- 2. Target Y's -->
<!-- 3. Target Y levels -->
<!-- 4. X's for target Y -->



<!-- hypothesis including mechanism, theoretical justification -->
<!-- Three goals: -->
<!-- 1. theoretically possible (not just random result)  -->
<!-- 2. temporal order (causal interpretability) -->
<!-- 3. direct the attention to the right expected empty corner -->



<!-- In a specific empirical study theoretical propositions are operationalized into hypotheses that describe the expected causal relationships that is studies. A hypothesis is usually a qualitative statement about this expected relationship. NCA starts with formulating a hypothesis that a condition is a necessary cause for the outcome. The basic format is: -->

<!-- \begin{equation}  -->
<!-- X is necessary for Y   -->
<!-- \end{equation} -->

<!-- Alternative wording can be used to express the same thing, for example:  -->

<!-- -  -->


<!-- In this formulation a 'positive' direction is assumed:  -->

<!-- If the direction is not specified, the **+ +** direction is implied.  -->

<!-- Also posible: negative -->

<!-- Note that in NCA the hypothesis is not described by a verb like in '$X$ *increases* $Y$' or '$X$ *has* a positive effect on $Y$', which are common formulations to express average effect relationships. In NCA a noun or adverb is used to  describe levels of $X$ and $Y$ as for example in '*the absence* of $X$ is necessary for *the presence* of $Y$' or '*low* $X$ is necessary for *high* $Y$'. Note also that the two 'ceiling lines' in the lower part of the plot can be considered as 'floor lines' suggesting the *sufficiency* of a condition $X$ for for a 'not so high' $Y$. An empty space in the lower-left corner represents that a high value of $X$ is necessary for a low value of $Y$. This is logically equivalent to the statement that a not-high level of $X$ is *sufficient* for a not-low level of $Y$. The floor line in this corner suggests therefore that a low level of $X$ is *sufficient* for a not so high level of $Y$. Similarly, an empty space in the lower-right corner suggests that a high level of $X$ is *sufficient* for a not so high level of $Y$. In situations that the empty space is in a lower corner, the sufficiency formulation of the necessary condition and the floor line may be helpful for theorizing about the relationship between $X$ and $Y$ [@goertz2013ceilings].       -->



<!-- where does the hypothesis comes from? new or existing  -->

<!-- In some fields the existence of a causal necessity relationship is widely known and accepted. An example is the psychological theory that intelligence is necessary for creativity. This hypothesis was proposed in the 1960's [@guilford1967nature] and is still widely around. Until recently, this theory could not be tested with a specific necessity method. When NCA became available it was possible to ensure theory-method fit by testing the hypothesis with NCA, as was done by @karwowski2016intellligence.  -->

<!-- In other fields, theories that are broadly accepted include systematic formulations of necessity. Yet, this specific causality is not widely recognized and when these theories are being tested generic causality is (implicitly) assumed. An example is the theory about micro-foundations of firm performance [@teece2007explicating]. NCA gives the opportunity to test such theories while ensuring theory-method fit. -->

<!-- In most research fields necessity formulations of the relationships of interest are scattered around in the literature. Although necessity causality exists in virtually all research areas, and necessity statements are commonly made, the necessity statements are usually not part of the theories. NCA gives the researcher an opportunity to formalize the theoretical necessity statements and test them with an appropriate method. -->

<!-- Three complementary approaches can be used for formulating necessary condition hypotheses. First, existing *necessity statements in the literature* can be identified. Although several necessity statements may use the word 'necessity' rather loosely as synonym for 'important',  many necessity statements refer to necessity logic and causality.  -->

<!-- Second, when *'important' contributing causal factors* for a outcome of interest have been identified with conventional methods, several of them could also be necessary conditions. For example, when a researcher formulates an additive theory with generic causes (to be analysed with regression analysis) arguments are provided why a concept is an important contributor to an outcome. As a standard, the arguments imply sufficiency logic ('$X$ produces $Y$'), but as an alternative, the relationship could also be described with necessity logic: an important factor that cannot be compensated for. Not only independent concepts but also mediator and moderator concepts in additive theories could be candidate necessary conditions. In other words, when current additive theories state that concepts are 'important' contributing concepts they may or may not (also) be necessary. But also contributing concepts that are considered 'unimportant' according to additive theories could be necessary. -->

<!-- Third, *'critical' and 'bottleneck' factors in practice* could be candidate necessary conditions to be included in the theory. Practitioners commonly act upon 'critical success factors' or 'must have' factors (versus 'nice to have' factors). When such factor does not have the required level it can become a bottleneck on which the practitioner must act to ensure a desired outcome. Practitioners often have a 'theory in use' [@dul2008case] in which they argue why a factor is related to an outcome. Often the arguments imply necessity logic. -->





<!-- ## Theoretical contribution {#theoreticalcontribution} -->

<!-- According to @campbell2022rejected scholars are expected to contribute the a 'conversation' about a certain topic in the academic literature. Based on @lange2017editors,  -->
<!-- suggestions can be made for formulating a contribution with a necessity theory. The conversation starts with a 'common ground'. The primary literature with the ongoing conversation about the problem/topic, and to which new insights with NCA will contribute, is identified (i.e., what do we know?).  Next, an interesting 'complication' in the literature is highlighted. This can be a missing element or failure in the literature, something that is missing in the conversation, or how the conversation falling short (i.e., what we don’t know?). Afterwards, it should be explained why this complication is a 'concern' (i.e., so what?). Next, in the 	'course of action' it is explained how the complication is addressed with NCA. Finally, the 'contribution' is described showing how and why the new insights are now part of the ongoing conversation. -->

<!-- I give three examples of possible formulations of a contribution with NCA: -->

<!-- * A contribution to a conversation on causal relationships of a phenomenon [e.g., @knoldissertation]. -->
<!-- * A contribution to a conversation on whether factors are complements or substitutes [e.g., @van2016contracts]. -->
<!-- * A contribution to conversation on a necessity relationship of a phenomenon [e.g., @karwowski2016intellligence]. -->

<!-- Tables \@ref(tab:contr1x), \@ref(tab:contr2x) and \@ref(tab:contr3x) provide details about how these contributions can be formulated. -->
<!-- <br/> -->
<!-- <br/> -->

<!-- library("kableExtra") -->
<!-- g2 <- read.csv("Theoretical contribution2a.csv")  -->
<!-- kbl(g2,  -->
<!--     caption = "Theoretical contribution with NCA: are factors complements or substitutes?", -->
<!--     booktabs = TRUE, -->
<!--     longtable = TRUE, -->
<!--     align = c("l")) %>% -->
<!--   kableExtra::kable_styling(latex_options = "HOLD_position") %>% -->
<!--   column_spec(1, width = "30em") %>% -->

<!--   #kable_paper("striped", full_width = F) %>% -->

<!--   pack_rows("Complication (What don't we know?)", 2, 2) %>% -->

<!--   pack_rows("Course of action", 4, 4) %>% -->
<!--   pack_rows("Contribution", 5, 5) -->
<!-- ``` -->


<!-- ## Developing a necessity theory {#developingnecessitytheory} -->

<!-- A necessity theory can be developed in three steps. First, a preliminary necessity proposition is formulated based on the literature or on the researcher's own ideas. Second, the proposition is mentally evaluated using a thought experiment. Third, the results of the thought experiment are used to (1) reject the preliminary necessity proposition, (2) adapts the preliminary necessity proposition (by refinement of concept definitions and/or theoretical domain) or (3) keeps the preliminary proposition. If a (revised) proposition survives after the thought experiment, it is ready for empirical testing with NCA. Testing a causal theory starts with operationalizing the theory such that theoretical concepts become measurable variables, and theoretical propositions become testable hypotheses (see chapter \@ref(hypothesisformulation)).  -->

<!-- Propositions are the core elements of a theory. They describe the expected causal relationships between the concepts. Whereas most theories use a probabilistic perspective on causality (if $X$ then probably $Y$), NCA uses a typicality perspective on causality (if $X$ then almost always $Y$). This section explains how the necessity proposition can be developed by first formulating the necessity proposition based the literature or the researcher's own ideas, and subsequently testing and refining these propositions using a thought experiment. -->

<!-- This section begins by exploring the process of formulating the core of a necessity theory, which are the necessary condition propositions. These propositions can be derived through an examination of existing literature or by innovative insights of researchers. To complete the theory, it is essential to define the focal unit, the concepts, and the theoretical domain within which the theory operates. Additionally, the theory can be refined and justified through the application of a thought experiment. -->

<!-- Necessity theory could also be developed in a specific theory building empirical study. In such exploratory stydy, data are collected from one or more cases (or a sample of cases) and data pattern are observed and analysed to formulate propositions to be tested in subsequent empirical research. An approach for theory building/exploratory study  for building necessity theory has been developed [e.g., @dul2008case; @hak2009theory; @dul2010necessary]. However, as empirical research is often expensive and time consuming, and since necessary condition propositions can often be formulated solely based on existing literature and the researcher's expertise, this book focuses on formulating necessary condition theory with literature and researcher's expertise, while using empirical data for testing rather than building theory^[Note that the results of a theory building/explorative empirical study also requires a post hoc theoretical justification of identified necessary propositions, using literature and the researcher's expertise, see chapter \@ref(empiricalresearch)].     -->


<!-- kwalitatieve formulering? -->

<!-- ### Literature review -->

<!-- The academic literature provides a wealth of information about potential necessary conditions in a certain field. Four approaches for identifying them can be distinguished: -->

<!--  - Necessity theories. -->

<!--  - Necessity statements. -->

<!--  - Causal models. -->

<!--  - Causal descriptions. -->

<!-- First, in some fields explicit *necessity theories* may already exist, but most likely have not been empirically tested extensively with an appropriate method. An example is the psychological theory that intelligence is necessary for creativity. This proposition was suggested in the 1960's [@guilford1967nature] and is still widely around. Until recently, this theory was only tested with regression-based methods, in particular segmented regression. However, regression-based methods inherently assume a probabilistic relationship rather than a necessity relationship, which results in theory-method misfit when testing necessity. After NCA became available it was possible to ensure theory-method fit by testing the necessity proposition with NCA, as was done for example by @karwowski2016intellligence and @shi2017relationship. Explicit necessary theories are still relatively uncommon. -->

<!-- Second, while explicit necessity theories are not prevalent in the literature, one can find numerous *necessity statements* throughout various fields. Although several statements may use the word 'necessity' loosely as synonym for 'important', many necessity statements are theoretical statements and refer to necessity logic and causality. For example, in the field of supply change management, @bokrantz2022building systematically reviewed necessity statements in the major journals and identified 157 statements that can be considered theoretical necessity claims. Most of these statements were not empirically tested, and if tested, all but four were tested with an improper (regression-based) method. It is highly probable that the situation is similar in other fields. Existing necessity statements in the literature can be reviewed, and selected statements can be elevated as propositions for a necessity theory, which can be subsequently tested with NCA. -->

<!-- Third, there is a plethora of studies with *causal models* that are tested with a regression-based method. Similar to formal theories such as the Technology Acceptance model (TAM) or the Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB), also causal models in specific studies assume probabilistic sufficiency causality. Therefore, these models could be revisited with a necessity perspective. Revisiting a causal model with a new causal lens is not a replication or robustness test of the existing model. It is a proposal for a new model or theory for obtaining additional insights. For formulating a necessity theory it is possible to revisit the entire model (see the TAM and TPB examples in section \@ref(theorytypes)), select only specific relations from the model, or select concepts that were not related in the model, but could have a necessity relationship. Hence, the original role of the concepts (e.g., independent, dependent, mediator, moderator, control) is not relevant for formulating a new causal model with these concepts. It is not uncommon that the article where the study is published suggests in the discussion section or conclusions to apply NCA for future research, and thus investigate the phenomena from a necessity perspective [e.g., @schmuck2022turning; @de2021european; @ogundipe2022interfirm]. Likewise, literature reviews of studies with regression-based methods and thus probabilistic sufficiency perspectives often propose exploring the phenomenon with NCA, hence from the necessity perspective as well [e.g., @delios2023reconsidering; @linder2022necessary; @aguinis2020methodological; @boon2019systematic; @dabic202140; @frazier2017psychological; @hauff2021analytical; @klimas2023attributes; @gajda2017creativity; @crede2021should; @zahoor2023international; @richter2022use]. -->

<!-- Fourth, in the literature, there are numerous *causal descriptions*, such as those found in theory and discussion sections of journal articles. In these descriptions, the type of causality is not explicitly stated, but various words are employed to underscore the importance of certain causes ($X$), potentially indicating necessity causality (only $Y$ if $X$). Examples of words for important (necessary?) causes are vital, crucial, significant, essential, key, fundamental, critical, indispensable, pivotal, imperative, required, etc.  The causal description may also include statements about the negative impact when important (necessary?) causes are absent (no $Y$ without $X$). The absence of the important cause may hinder, limit, reduce, block the desired outcome, such that the important cause becomes a bottleneck, barrier, constraint, hurdle for the desired effect. Concepts that are labeled this way may be necessary causes for a necessity theory. -->


<!-- ### Researcher's expertise. -->
<!-- The *researcher's expertise* can be an important source for formulating a necessity theory. It is assumed that the researcher is knowledgeable about the literature on the phenomenon of interest, and may also have practical knowledge or access to practitioners who have knowledge about the phenomenon.  Drawing upon the researcher's comprehensive understanding of the literature and possibly practical experience in a specific field, important factors that could be deemed necessary may be recognized. Frequently, based on this expertise, researchers can readily formulate necessity propositions. Practitioners can be an important source of information. They often have a 'theory in use' [@dul2008case] in which they argue why a factor is related to an outcome. Often the arguments imply necessity logic. Practitioners commonly act upon 'critical success factors' or 'must have' factors (versus 'nice to have' factors). The goal is to avoid failure when the factor does not have the required level and thus becomes a bottleneck on which the practitioner must act to ensure a desired outcome.  -->

<!-- ### Completing the theory -->
<!-- After using one or more of the above or alternative approaches to formulate a necessary condition proposition, the necessity theory can be made complete by explicitly defining its focal unit, its concepts (condition(s) and outcome(s)), and its theoretical domain.  -->
<!-- The next step is to evaluate and improve the necessity theory by using a thought experiment.  -->

<!-- ### Thought experiment {#thoughtexperiment} -->
<!-- In thought experiments researchers "manipulate independent variables in imagined scenarios" [@aguinis2023thought, p. 546]. The thought experiment for a necessity theory is meant to challenge, refine and justify the formulated the theory.  -->
<!-- The thought experiment can lead to various outcomes, such as providing support for the theory, redefining its concepts, propositions, or theoretical scope, getting more insights into why the relationship exists, or ultimately refuting the theory.  -->

<!-- The thought experiment for a necessity consists of several steps and is illustrated with the internet example.  -->

<!-- Describe and challenge -->

<!-- Challenging the necessity theory:  -->
<!-- Evaluating cases with outcome present: -->
<!-- Step 1. Select several cases (i.e., instances of the focal unit) from the theoretical domain that have the outcome. In the internet example the focal unit is 'person', the theoretical domain is 'all persons in the world' and presence of the outcome is 'attending an online meeting'. Thus, you can select any person in the world that attends an online meeting.  -->

<!-- Step 2. For each selected case with the outcome, evaluate whether or not the necessary condition in place. In the internet example it is evaluated whether all selected persons are connected with internet.  If yes continue with 3; if no, continue with Handling exceptions. -->

<!-- Step 3. Challenge the proposition by expanding the number of selected cases with the outcome, by selecting extreme cases or by selecting potential exceptions. Evaluate again whether or not the necessary condition is in place for these selected cases. If yes, continue with Evaluating cases with conditions absent; if no, continue with Handling exceptions. -->


<!-- Evaluating cases with condition absent: -->
<!-- Step 4 Select several cases from the theoretical domain that do not have the condition. In the internet example can select any person in the world that is not connected with internet.  -->

<!-- Step 5 For each selected case without the condition, evaluate whether or not the outcome is in place. In the internet example it is evaluated whether all selected persons that are not connected with internet also do not attend an online meeting.  If yes continue with 6; if no, continue with Handling exceptions. -->

<!-- Step 6. Challenge the proposition by expanding the number of selected cases without the condition, by selecting extreme cases or by selecting potential exceptions. Evaluate again whether or not the outcome is in place for these selected cases. If yes, continue with Experimentation and justification; if no, continue with Handling exceptions. -->



<!-- Refining the necessity theory -->
<!-- In Steps 2,3, 5 or 6 cases in the expected empty corner were identified, this potentially rejecting the necessity theory. -->
<!-- These cases can help to refine the necessity theory by re-defining the concepts (condition or outcome), or re-defining the theoretical scope. For example a person may have been identified who is in an online meeting with a colleague in the same organization both using computers that are connected by a LAN network. Such local network is local (e.g., within an organization, and does not utiflizes the public internet. Such falsifying case can be used toe redefine the condition sich that it is excluded from the theory meking the theoretical claim more precise. For example, the concept Connection to internet is defined as Connection to the public internet, this not to a private local network).      -->


<!-- meeting of people on different locations including different countires.  -->

<!-- within a limited geographical area, such as an office building, school campus, or home. It is typically used for local communication and sharing resources among devices within the same physical area. In a LAN-based meeting, participants must be physically connected to the same local network to communicate with each other. -->




<!-- Justifying the necessity theory -->
<!-- Now all imaginary cases with the outcome have the necessary condition in place, and all imaginary cases without the condition do not have the outcome. This supports the emptiness of the expected empty corner and thus the necessary condition proposition. -->

<!-- Experiment cases with outcome present: -->
<!-- Step 7. Select cases with the condition present and with the outcome present (upper right corner), for example the cases that were selected in 1 and 3.  -->
<!-- Step 8. Imagine to remove the condition, and explain why the outcome disappears.  -->
<!-- in the internect example) -->


<!-- Experiment cases with condition absent: -->
<!-- Step 9. Select cases with the condition absent abd the outcome absent (upper-left corner), for example the cases that were selected in 2 and 4.  -->
<!-- Step 10.Imagine to add potential compensating factors that could produce the outcome (e.g.. factors that have an average effect on the outcome), but fail to do so produce the outcome when the condition is absent. Thus explain why the absence of a necessary condition cannot be compensated by another factor. -->

<!-- Conclusion: When 8 and 10 are convincing stories the necessity theory is justified.  -->





<!-- yes select other cases, also extreme cases, from the theoretical domain that have the outcome   -->
<!-- one case is you, the reader. Any other cases could be selected as well because the theory is . all cases with the outcome  -->


<!-- Challenging -->
<!-- Select cases: -->



<!-- The proposition predicts that nearly all cases with the outcome also have the condition, and that nearly all cases without the condition do not have the outcome. This is shown in the contingency table of Table xx where all cells except the upper-left cell can have cases when (necessity applies.  -->
<!-- The though experiment challenges the emptiness of the upper-left cell.  -->

<!-- 1. define the theoretical domain where  -->

<!-- 1. Select cases from the theoretic that have the outcome (Y =1) -->
<!-- Do all cases have the condition? YES/NO -->
<!-- - if YES: go to 2  -->
<!-- - if NO: go to 2. -->
<!-- 2.Imagine removing the condition -->
<!-- Does the outcome disappear in all cases? -->
<!-- - if YES: for to 3  -->
<!-- - if NO: go to 4 -->

<!-- 3. Try to explain why the cases disappear.  -->

<!-- Scope: -->
<!-- 3.Try to find exceptions where  -->
<!-- What would happen if the condition   -->
<!-- 5. is this an exceptional case.  -->

<!-- nt . When necessity applies, the upper-left cell must be empty. cannot have   the t -->

<!-- having access to an electronic device (laptop, smartphone, table, etc.) -->
<!-- able to communicate -->
<!-- Technical skills to operate to electronic device. s -->

<!-- with a screen display (e.g. computer, tablet, smartphone)  -->
<!-- include generative AI ChatGPT -->

<!-- When a potential necessary condition is selected in one of the above scenarios, the researcher can do a *'thought-experiment'* to test if necessity is possible. She can ask the question "Can I imagine cases _with_ outcome $Y$, but _without_ condition $X$?". When such cases are relatively common, the conclusion is that the condition is _not_ necessary. When cases with the outcome $Y$ and without the condition $X$ are impossible or uncommon, the hypothesis may generally hold. The rare cases can be used to define the context where the hypothesis holds and where not, and thus to define the _theoretical domain_ of the theory. Defining the theoretical domain --where the theory is supposed to hold-- is important for all theories, because theories do rarely hold universally. -->

<!-- To illustrate the thought-experiment and the process of defining the theoretical domain, assume that a researcher wants to test the hypothesis 'understanding English is necessary for reading this book'. This statement is generally correct, but it is possible to imagine cases (persons) _with_ the outcome (reading the book) but _without_ the condition (not mastering English). Such exception is possible when someone translates the book in the language other than English, such that a reader who does not master English can read the translated book. This exception helps to specify the theoretical domain of the hypothesis: 'persons reading the original version of book'. Further exploration of exceptions can help to specify the theoretical domain. This process continues until the theoretical domain is precisely specified where the hypotheses is expected to hold virtually always. For testing this hypothesis, cases are selected from this specified theoretical domain. This can help to avoid sampling error (see section \@ref(outliers)). -->

<!-- It is also possible that the necessary condition is rejected in the thought experiment because the condition is formulated too narrow. For example, 'green apple' is not necessary for an apple pie, and also red apple is not necessary for an apple pie. However, the higher-order construct (apple) is necessary. Then it would make sense to formulate the necessary condition hypothesis with the higher order concept. In general, attractive theories are supposed to be valid in a broad set of contexts, not only in very specific situations.     -->

<!-- A hypothesis is usually only generally and semantically described as a theoretical statement. In NCA the common hypothesis is a qualitative theoretical statement like "X is necessary for Y" (in kind). A hypothesis usually is not described as a quantitative statement like "a specific level of X is necessary for a specific level of Y" (in degree).  -->

<!-- Experimenting with Plausibility -->

<!-- - **Remove the condition**: Would the outcome still happen? (e.g., Can someone attend a meeting without internet?) -->
<!-- - **Add a substitute**: Could another factor compensate for the condition’s absence? (If not, the condition is likely necessary.) -->

<!-- To strengthen your necessity hypothesis, answer these three questions: -->

<!-- 1. **Why does Y not occur without X?** -->
<!-- 2. **Why is X always present when Y is present?** -->
<!-- 3. **Why can’t any other variable compensate for the absence of X?** -->



<!-- ### Justification of the necessity proposition -->
<!-- A theory is more than a set of hypotheses. It is also important to describe the mechanism *why* $X$ is necessary for $Y$.  -->
<!-- A first requirement for claiming causality is that condition occurs before the outcome: first the condition, then the outcome. Furthermore, the necessity relationship can be argued by answering three why questions: -->

<!-- * Why if $X$ is absent also $Y$ is absent? This can be done by imagining a case without $X$ and without $Y$ and describing why without $X$, $Y$ cannot be produced.  -->
<!-- * Why if $Y$ is present also $X$ is present? This can be done by imagining a case with $X$ and with $Y$ and describing why $Y$ would disappear when $X$ disappears, thus why $Y$ could not have happened without $X$? -->
<!-- * Why the absence of $X$ cannot be compensated by another concept? This can be done by imagining a case without $X$ and with $Y$. If this case can exist, what is the consequence for the theory’s domain (smaller?) or for the concept $X$ (higher level concept?). -->

<!-- ```{r contingency0, echo=FALSE, out.width="100%", out.height="100%",fig.align ='center', results='hide', message=FALSE, warning=FALSE} -->

<!-- #make empty 2x2 contingency table -->
<!-- X<- c(0.25, 0.75) #X-values center of cell -->
<!-- Y<- c(0.25, 0.25) #Y-values center of cell -->

<!-- # make plot window square and set size of the axes and labels -->
<!-- par(pty = "s", cex.axis = 0.8, cex.lab = 0.8) -->

<!-- #give names to axes -->
<!-- xname = "X" -->
<!-- yname = "Y" -->

<!-- #give names to axis labels -->
<!-- xlabel = rep(c("","0", "1", "")) -->
<!-- ylabel = rep(c("","0", "1", "")) -->
<!-- # xlabel = rep(c("","Absent", "Present", "")) -->
<!-- # ylabel = rep(c("","Absent", "Present", "")) -->

<!-- # Create the plot with square aspect ratio -->
<!-- plot (X,Y, xlab=xname, ylab=yname, xlim = c(0,1), ylim=c(0,1), pch=19, cex=2, axes = F, yaxs="i", xaxs="i", type="n")  -->
<!-- axis(1, at = c(0, 0.25, 0.75, 1.25), pos=0, tck=0, labels=xlabel) #X has two values -->
<!-- axis(2, at = c(0, 0.25, 0.75, 1.25), pos=0, tck=0, labels=ylabel) #Y has two values -->
<!-- abline(v=c(0.5,1), h=c(0.5,1)) -->

<!-- #fill cells -->
<!-- rect(0, 0.5, 0.5, 1,  col = "grey") -->
<!-- rect(0, 0, 0.5, 0.5,  col = "grey") -->
<!-- rect(0.5, 0, 1, 0.5,  col = "grey") -->
<!-- rect(0.5, 0.5, 1, 1,  col = "grey") -->

<!-- #add points in cells -->
<!-- #points(X,Y, pch=19, cex=2) -->

<!-- #add text in cells -->
<!-- text(0.25,0.25,"6", cex=1.2) -->
<!-- text(0.25,0.75,"8", cex=1.2) -->
<!-- text(0.75,0.25,"3", cex=1.2) -->
<!-- text(0.75,0.75,"2", cex=1.2) -->

<!-- #add ceiling line -->
<!-- # segments(0,0.5,0.5,0.5, lty=1, col="black", lwd = 3) #horizontal part of ceiling line -->
<!-- # segments(0.5,0.5,0.5,1, lty=1, col="black", lwd = 3) #vertical part of ceiling line -->
<!-- ``` -->


<!-- ```{r theory0, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE, out.width = "100%", fig.cap= "Questions to justify a necessity hypothesis."} -->
<!-- knitr::include_graphics('theory-formulation.png') -->
<!-- ```  -->

<!-- Figure \@ref(fig:theory) shows that these questions relate to three cells of the 2x2 contingency table that are compatible with the necessary condition hypothesis: where cases are possible or impossible when the necessary condition 'the presence of $X$ is necessary for the presence of $Y$' holds. Note that the lower right cell is not informative about necessity.   -->

<!-- Describing the causal mechanism is often difficult. It is a creative process inspired by the three questions. The answers can be based on exploring and combining knowledge from the literature, from practice and from the researcher's own experience. In the literature, researchers may partly use necessity arguments when describing the causal mechanism why an 'important' factor produces an outcome. Practitioners may tell stories why a 'must have' factor is 'critical' or a 'bottleneck' for the outcome and why he acts upon them for getting the outcome.  -->

<!-- ## Theoretical contribution {#theoreticalcontribution} -->

<!-- According to @campbell2022rejected scholars are expected to contribute the a 'conversation' about a certain topic in the academic literature. Based on @lange2017editors,  -->
<!-- suggestions can be made for formulating a contribution with a necessity theory. The conversation starts with a 'common ground'. The primary literature with the ongoing conversation about the problem/topic, and to which new insights with NCA will contribute, is identified (i.e., what do we know?).  Next, an interesting 'complication' in the literature is highlighted. This can be a missing element or failure in the literature, something that is missing in the conversation, or how the conversation falling short (i.e., what we don’t know?). Afterwards, it should be explained why this complication is a 'concern' (i.e., so what?). Next, in the 	'course of action' it is explained how the complication is addressed with NCA. Finally, the 'contribution' is described showing how and why the new insights are now part of the ongoing conversation. -->

<!-- I give three examples of possible formulations of a contribution with NCA: -->

<!-- * A contribution to a conversation on causal relationships of a phenomenon [e.g., @knoldissertation]. -->
<!-- * A contribution to a conversation on whether factors are complements or substitutes [e.g., @van2016contracts]. -->
<!-- * A contribution to conversation on a necessity relationship of a phenomenon [e.g., @karwowski2016intellligence]. -->

<!-- Tables \@ref(tab:contr1), \@ref(tab:contr2) and \@ref(tab:contr3) provide details about how these contributions can be formulated. -->
<!-- <br/> -->
<!-- <br/> -->



<!-- ```{r contr1, echo = FALSE, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE, include=TRUE, cache=TRUE} -->

<!--     longtable = TRUE, -->
<!--     align = c("l")) %>% -->

<!--   column_spec(1, width = "30em") %>% -->
<!--   #kable_paper("striped", full_width = F) %>% -->
<!--   pack_rows("Common Ground (What do we know?)", 1,1) %>% -->
<!--   pack_rows("Complication (What don't we know?)", 2, 2) %>% -->
<!--   pack_rows("Concern (So what?)", 3, 3) %>% -->
<!--   pack_rows("Course of action", 4, 4) %>% -->

<!-- ``` -->
<!-- <br/> -->

<!-- library("kableExtra") -->
<!-- g3 <- read.csv("Theoretical contribution3a.csv") -->
<!-- kbl(g3, -->
<!--     caption = "Theoretical contribution with NCA: Add to the conversation on a necessity relationship of a phenomenon.", -->
<!--     booktabs = TRUE, -->
<!--     longtable = TRUE, -->
<!--     align = c("l")) %>% -->
<!--   kableExtra::kable_styling(latex_options = "HOLD_position") %>% -->

<!--   #kable_paper("striped", full_width = F) %>% -->

<!--   pack_rows("Complication (What don't we know?)", 2, 2) %>% -->

<!-- ## Necessity in degree {#necessityindegree} -->

<!-- Upto know in kind -->
<!-- theories in the social science are verbal, qualitative  -->
<!-- same for propositions  -->

<!-- next chapter (in degree) -->

<!-- zzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzz--- -->

<!-- A. Theory -->

<!-- 1. Are all elements of the theory (focal unit, condition, outcome, and theoretical domain) defined? -->

<!-- - Yes → go to B. triviality -->
<!-- - No → Undefined theory: define and return to A. Theory -->

<!-- B. triviality -->

<!-- 2. Do cases exist with the outcome? -->

<!-- - Yes → go to 3   -->
<!-- - No → Theory is trivial: Stop (reverse theory) -->

<!-- 3. Do cases exist without the condition? -->

<!-- - Yes → go to C. Challenge 1   -->
<!-- - No → Theory is trivial: Stop (reverse theory) -->

<!-- C. Challenge 1 -->

<!-- 4. Select cases with the outcome. Do all cases have the condition? -->

<!-- - Yes → Supportive cases: go to 5 -->
<!-- - No → Counterexamples: go to D. Counterexamples -->

<!-- 5. Is it possible to find counterexamples with the outcome and without the condition? -->

<!-- - Yes → Counterexamples: go to D. Counterexamples   -->
<!-- - No → Theory not rejected: go to E. Challenge 2 -->

<!-- D. Counterexamples -->

<!-- 6. Are the counterexamples rare? -->

<!-- - Yes → Exceptions: go to F. Exceptions   -->
<!-- - No → Theory rejected: make new theory and return to A. Theory -->

<!-- E. Challenge 2 -->

<!-- 7. Select cases without the condition. Do all cases lack the outcome? -->

<!-- - Yes → Supportive cases: go to 8 -->
<!-- - No → Counterexamples: go to D. Counterexamples -->

<!-- 8. Is it possible to find counterexamples without the condition and with the outcome? -->

<!-- - Yes → Counterexamples: go to D. Counterexamples   -->
<!-- - No → Theory not rejected: go to G. Finalize Theory -->

<!-- F. Exceptions -->

<!-- 9. Is it possible to exclude exceptions by redefining the concepts and theoretical domain? -->

<!-- - Yes → Refine theory: return to B. triviality   -->
<!-- - No → go to 10 -->

<!-- 10. Do you wish to adopt the typicality perspective on necessity? -->

<!-- - Yes → Theory not rejected: go to G. Finalize Theory   -->
<!-- - No → Theory rejected: make new theory and return to A. Theory -->

<!-- G. Finalize Theory -->

<!-- 11. Is it explained: -->

<!--   - Why in cases with the outcome (typically) the condition is present; -->
<!--   - Why in cases without the condition (typically) the outcome is absent; -->
<!--   - Why in cases without the condition (typically) the absence of the condition cannot be compensated? -->

<!-- - Yes → Theory is testable: End   -->
<!-- - No → Explain the theoretical necessity mechanism and return to G. Finalize Theory -->


<!--chapter:end:070-hypothesis-formulationJO.Rmd-->

# Data {#datacollection}

## Summary of this chapter
This chapter describes the steps between the hypothesis and data analysis that are needed to obtain empirical data for testing a formal necessity hypothesis. The hypothesis discussed in the previous chapter (Chapter \@ref(hypothesisformulation)) guides which data should be collected. The hypothesis has precise definitions of (1) the focal unit, (2) the concepts $X$ and $Y$, (3) the direction of necessity and a causal explanation, and (4) the theoretical domain where the hypothesis is supposed to hold. Empirical data refer to observed $x$- and $y$-scores (values or levels) of variables that represent the concepts $X$ and $Y$. These scores are measured on cases selected from the theoretical domain. To collect high-quality empirical data for testing the necessity hypothesis, three key decisions must be made: (1) selection of study design,  which is the type of study (observational study, longitudinal study, case study, or experimental study discussed in Section \@ref(researchdesign)), (2) sampling or selection of cases from the theoretical domain: random sampling and  purposive case selection (Section \@ref(samplingandselection)), and (3) conducting the measurement to obtain valid and reliable $x$ and $y$ scores (e.g., qualitative or quantitative data; archival or new data) for each case (Section \@ref(measurement)). Section \@ref(dataset) discusses the format of a useful dataset.  \
While NCA does not impose new requirements on data collection and treats empirical data as input to NCA rather than as part of NCA, some choices must be tailored to fit the logic of necessity. This applies, for example, to the study design when a necessity experiment is done (Section \@ref(necessityexperiment)), or to the use of purposive case selection in a small-n qualitative study (Section \@ref(smallncaseselection)).


<!-- Empirical research {#empiricalresearch} -->


<!-- ## AANPASSINGEN DOEN -->

<!-- 2. data/ -->
<!--   sample: zie focal unit en theoretical domain  -->
<!--   concepts - variabelen die worden gemeten -->
<!-- 3.  -->

<!-- expected empty corner volgt uit hypothese: directions: -->

<!-- , where the empty space is in different corners. -->

<!--  ```{r cornersxxxx, echo=FALSE, fig.show="hold", out.width='50%', fig.cap="'Direction' of necessary conditions. upper-left: a high value of $X$ is necessary for a high value of $Y$ (**+ +**). Upper-right: a low value of $X$ is necessary for a high value of $Y$ (**- +**). Lower-left: a high value of $X$ is necessary for a low value of $Y$ (**+ -**). Lower-right: a low value of $X$ is necessary for a low value of $Y$ (**- -**)."} -->
<!--  #par(mar = c(4, 4, .1, .1)) -->
<!--  par(mfrow = c(2,2)) -->
<!--  knitr::include_graphics("corner1.png") -->
<!--  knitr::include_graphics("corner2.png") -->
<!--  knitr::include_graphics("corner3.png") -->
<!--  knitr::include_graphics("corner4.png") -->
<!--  ``` -->


<!-- Note also that the two 'ceiling lines' in the lower part of the plot can be considered as 'floor lines' suggesting the *sufficiency* of a condition $X$ for for a 'not so high' $Y$. An empty space in the lower-left corner represents that a high value of $X$ is necessary for a low value of $Y$. This is logically equivalent to the statement that a not-high level of $X$ is *sufficient* for a not-low level of $Y$. The floor line in this corner suggests therefore that a low level of $X$ is *sufficient* for a not so high level of $Y$. Similarly, an empty space in the lower-right corner suggests that a high level of $X$ is *sufficient* for a not so high level of $Y$. In situations that the empty space is in a lower corner, the sufficiency formulation of the necessary condition and the floor line may be helpful for theorizing about the relationship between $X$ and $Y$ [@goertz2013ceilings]. -->




<!-- ## Two empty corners {#morecorners}  -->
<!-- It is possible that a researcher formulates a theory that a single factor has two necessity relations with the outcome at the same time, implying that more than one corner is empty. -->

<!-- First, a researcher may theorize that an *optimum* (not low, not high) level of $X$ is necessary for a *high* level of $Y$. Then the upper-left *and* the upper-right corners are expected to be empty (Figure \@ref(fig:corners1234), left). Similarly, a researcher may theorize that an *optimum* (not low, not high) level of $X$ is necessary for a *low* level of $Y$. Then the lower-left and the lower-right corners are expected to be empty (Figure \@ref(fig:corners1234), right). -->

<!--  ```{r corners1234xxx, echo=FALSE, fig.show="hold", out.width='50%', fig.cap="Left: The necessity of an *optimum* level of $X_{c1} < X < X_{c2}$ for a *high* level of $Y = Y_c$. Right: The necessity of an *optimum* level of $X_{c1} < X < X_{c2}$ for a *low* level of $Y = Y_c$."} -->
<!--  par(mfrow = c(1,2)) -->
<!--  knitr::include_graphics("corners12.png") -->
<!--  knitr::include_graphics("corners34.png") -->
<!--  ``` -->

<!-- It is also possible that a researcher theorizes that a *high* level of $X$ is necessary for an *extreme* (low or high) level of $Y$. Then the upper-left and the lower-left corners are expected to be empty (Figure \@ref(fig:corners1324), left). Similarly, a researcher may theorize that a *low* level of $X$ is necessary for an *extreme* (low or high) level of $Y$. Then the upper-right and the lower-right corners are expected to be empty (Figure \@ref(fig:corners1324), right). -->


<!--  ```{r corners1324xxx, echo=FALSE, fig.show="hold", out.width='50%', fig.cap="Left: The necessity of a *high* level of $X > X_c$ for an *extreme* (low or high) level of $Y = Y_{c1}$ or $Y = Y_{c2}$. Right: The necessity of a *low* level of $X < X_c$ for an *extreme* (low or high) level of $Y = Y_{c1}$ or $Y = Y_{c2}$."} -->
<!--  par(mfrow = c(1,2)) -->
<!--  knitr::include_graphics("corners13.png") -->
<!--  knitr::include_graphics("corners24.png") -->
<!--  ``` -->

<!-- The final possible situation with two empty corners is that a researcher theorizes that a *high* level of $X$ is necessary for a *high* level of $Y$ and a *low* level of $X$ is necessary for a *low* level of $Y$ (Figure \@ref(fig:corners1423), left). Similarly, a researcher may theorize that a *low* level of $X$ is necessary for a *high* level of $Y$ and that a *high* level of $X$ is necessary for a *low* level of $Y$. Then two opposite corners are empty (Figure \@ref(fig:corners1423), right). -->

<!--  ```{r corners1423xxx, echo=FALSE, fig.show="hold", out.width='50%', fig.cap="Left: The necessity of a *high* level of $X > X_{c1}$ for a *high* level of $Y = Y_{c1}$, and the necessity of *low* level of $X < X_{c2}$ for a *low* level of $Y = Y_{c2}$. Right: The necessity of *low* level of $X < X_{c1}$ for a *high* level of $Y = Y_{c1}$, and the necessity of *high* level of $X > X_{c2}$ for a *low* level of $Y = Y_{c2}$."} -->
<!--  par(mfrow = c(1,2)) -->
<!--  knitr::include_graphics("corners14.png") -->
<!--  knitr::include_graphics("corners23.png") -->
<!--  ``` -->
<!-- In the ```NCA``` software, it is possible to do a combined statistical  test of the two necessity relations of a single factor by using the argument ```effect_aggregation``` in the ```nca_analysis``` function. The software estimates the aggregate effect size, which is the sum of the effect sizes of the separate empty spaces, and its $p$-value. This can be illustrated with the example that a country's high level of Risk taking is necessary for a high level and sufficient for a low level of a country's Innovation performance. -->

<!-- ```{r, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE,  eval=FALSE} -->
<!-- library(NCA) -->
<!-- data(nca.example) -->

<!--                        corner = 1, -->
<!--                        ceilings = "ce_fdh", -->
<!--                        test.rep = 10000) -->
<!-- model1 -->
<!-- nca_output(model1, plots = T, summaries = F) -->

<!-- #High Risk taking is sufficient for low Innovation performance -->

<!--                        ceilings = "ce_fdh", -->
<!--                        test.rep = 10000) -->
<!-- model2 -->
<!-- nca_output(model2, plots = T, summaries = F) -->

<!-- #High Risk taking is necessary for high Innovation performance and sufficient for low Innovation performance -->
<!-- model3 <- nca_analysis(nca.example, -->

<!--                        test.rep = 10000) -->
<!-- model3 -->
<!-- ``` -->

<!-- Model 1 tests the emptiness of the upper-left corner; model 2 of the lower-right corner and model 3 is the aggregate test. The results of the latter test shows that the combined necessity/sufficiency relationship of Risk taking for Innovation performance is not rejected. -->


<!-- ## The meaning of 'necessary but not sufficient' {#necessarybutnotsufficient} -->
<!-- Figure \@ref(fig:corners) shows a single factor with a one necessity relationship, and Figures \@ref(fig:corners1234), \@ref(fig:corners1324), and \@ref(fig:corners1423) show a single factor with two necessity relationships. In all figures, consistent with necessity, there are no observations in the empty space and observations in the grey area. If the necessity relationship holds, and no observations were in the grey area, all observations would be exactly on the border line. Then, the necessary condition is also a sufficient conditions. However this is not realistic as single necessary conditions are seldom also sufficient. This implies that the words 'but not sufficient' can normally be added to all necessity statements. -->

<!-- For example, upper-left Figure \@ref(fig:corners), shows a single factor with one necessary condition: only the upper-left corner is empty. This corresponds to the statement "a high level of $X$ is necessary for a high level of $Y$". If all points were on the border line (ceiling line) the condition is also sufficient resulting in the statement: "a high level of $X$ is necessary *and sufficient* for a high level of $Y$". In practice, observations will appear below the ceiling line. This justifies the statement that "a high level of $X$ is necessary *but not sufficient* for a high level of $Y$". -->

<!-- Figure \@ref(fig:corners1234), left is an example of a factor with two necessity relations, "a high level of $X$ is necessary for a high level of $Y$" and "a low level of $X$ is necessary for a high level of $Y$" (thus an optimum level of $X$ is necessary for a high level of $Y$). If all observations were on the two ceiling lines, the words 'and sufficient' could be added to the word 'necessary'. Again, in practice observations show up below the border lines such that the words "but not sufficient" can be added to the word 'necessary'. The same holds for any other necessity relationship shown in Figures \@ref(fig:corners), \@ref(fig:corners1234), \@ref(fig:corners1324), and \@ref(fig:corners1423). -->

<!-- Any necessity statement can be rephrased as a sufficiency statement by formulating its logical complement. For example, the upper-left corner of Figure \@ref(fig:corners1234) left (ignoring the upper-right corner) indicates that a high level of $X$ is necessary but not sufficient for a high value of $Y$. This can be reformulated as 'the absence of a high level of $X$ is sufficient but not necessary for the absence of a high value of $Y$'. This means that a level of $X < X_{c1}$ guarantees (is sufficient for) $Y < Y_c$. -->

<!-- Similarly, the lower-right corner of Figure \@ref(fig:corners1234) right (ignoring the lower-left corner) indicates that a low level of $X$ is necessary but not sufficient for a low value of $Y$. This can be reformulated as: 'the absence of a low level of $X$ is sufficient but not necessary for the absence of a high level of $Y$'. A value of $X > X_{c2}$ guarantees that $Y > Y_c$.  -->

<!-- When a single factor has two necessary relationships, it is possible to use the necessity formulation for one necessary condition, and the sufficiency formulation for the other. For example, in Figure \@ref(fig:corners1423), left the double necessity formulation is: 'a high level of $X > X_{1c}$ is necessary but not sufficient for a high level of $Y = Y_{c1}$', and 'the absence or low level of $X < X_{c2}$ is necessary but not sufficient for the absence or low level of $Y = Y_{2c}$'. When the necessity formulation is used for the upper-left necessity relation, and the sufficiency formulation for the lower-right necessity relation, the new equivalent statement is: 'a high level of $X > X_{1c}$ is necessary but not sufficient for a high level of $Y = Y_{c1}$', and 'a high level of $X < X_{c2}$ is sufficient but not necessary for a high level of $Y = Y_{2c}$'. -->

<!-- Note that dual statements cannot be combined into a single statement, because the two border lines have a different location. This means that for a given $Y$ there are different levels of $X$ (see Figures \@ref(fig:corners1234), and \@ref(fig:corners1423)) and for a given $X$ there different levels of $Y$ (see Figures \@ref(fig:corners1324), and \@ref(fig:corners1423)). -->

<!-- Sometimes, the situation in Figure \@ref(fig:corners1423), left is formulated 'in kind' as '$X$ is necessary and sufficient for $Y$', which implies that the upper-left necessity relationship is formulated in terms of necessity, and the lower-right in terms of sufficiency. However, this formulation is problematic when the actual necessity relationships are 'in degree'. The 'in degree' formulation of the situation is *not*: 'a high level of $X$ is necessary and sufficient for a high level of $Y$', but instead: '$X > X_{c1}$ is necessary for $Y = Y_{c1}$ and $X > X_{c2}$ is sufficient for $Y = Y_{c2}$'. There is no common $X$ that is necessary and sufficient for a common $Y$ and no common $Y$ for which a common $X$ is necessary and sufficient.  -->

<!-- Only when the two borderlines overlap, the 'in kind' statement also holds in an 'in degree' situation: 'a certain level of $X$ is necessary and sufficient for a certain level of $Y$'. At best, the 'in kind' formulation could be re-formulated as 'a high level of $X$ is necessary for a high level of $Y$ and sufficient for a not so high level of $Y$', or similar. The theoretical meaning is that a high level of $X$ is necessary for (enables) a high level of $Y$ (border line = ceiling line) and is sufficient for (ensures) a low level of $Y$ (border line = floor line).    -->



<!-- For example, a theory that assumes probabilistic sufficiency causal relationship between certain concepts, can additionally assume a typicality necessity relationship between theses or other concepts of the theory. -->

<!-- on a for some of the relationship   perspective,  some of the relations when NCA is combined with a regression-based analysis, some relations are only combines with  dbetween concepts are also considered but also this way, wherease other are condiealso other types of relationships       -->


<!-- s between all or a selection of the concepts of a theory [@bokrantz2022building]. Necessity *theorizing* is formulating necessity theory using necessity logic when describing causal relationships between concepts.   -->


<!-- One  theory about the necessary conditions for success of start-up firms was developed based on literature and common beliefs, but this necessity theory was rejected after empirical testing with NCA [@arenius2017no].  -->

<!-- ^[A moderator in a probabilistic theory is a concept that influences the average *relationship* between other concepts. A moderator  does not exist in a necessity theory, as the necessity relationship can only exist between concepts] -->






<!-- One of the goals of empirical research is to built and test theories. One of the main advantage of a good theory is that prediction can be made beyond the situation where the theory is tested.  -->

<!-- Theory building and theory testing empirical research is done to obtain a good theory. Theory testing empirical research starts with formulating the theoretical expectation (hypothesis) drawn from a theory, and subsequently test the hypothesis data from reality. In a empirical single study, the hypothesis is tested once. After several replications (repeated single studies), confidence may be gained (or not) that the hypothesis is plausible, giving support to the theory. A single study cannot be conclusive about a hypothesis or a theory.  -->

<!-- In this book I focus on theory testing research in which a hypothesis is formulated and tested with data. A general research approach consists of the following part: -->

<!-- 1. Formulate theoretical expectation (hypothesis)  -->

<!-- 2. Collect data -->

<!--   * select research design -->

<!--   * select cases (sampling) -->

<!--   * measure concepts -->

<!-- 5. analyse data -->

<!-- 6. report results -->


 

<!-- ## Theory testing and exploration -->

<!-- ## Stepwise approach -->
<!-- Experiment is done for determining the temporal direction not for controlling the confounders/  -->

<!-- ## Research design {#researchdesign} -->


<!-- ## Data collection {#datacollection} -->

<!-- ## Observational Study -->

<!-- ###  -->
<!-- ### Endogeneity -->

<!-- #### Confounders/omitted variable bias/ control variables -->
<!-- Confounders are variables that can influence both the independent variable (condition) and the dependent variable (outcome) in a study. In a regression-based analysis they can create a biased regression coefficient between the condition and the outcome when they are omitted from the regression equation (omitted variable bias), leading to incorrect conclusions about causality.  -->
