  rename(to_shape = shape)

#multiplying diamond offsets
arrows <- arrows %>%
  mutate(
    offset_start_x = ifelse(from_shape == "diamond", 
                            start_x + (offset_start_x - start_x) * diamond_multiplier, 
                            offset_start_x),
    offset_start_y = ifelse(from_shape == "diamond", 
                            start_y + (offset_start_y - start_y) * diamond_multiplier, 
                            offset_start_y),
    offset_end_x = ifelse(to_shape == "diamond", 
                          end_x + (offset_end_x - end_x) * diamond_multiplier, 
                          offset_end_x),
    offset_end_y = ifelse(to_shape == "diamond", 
                          end_y + (offset_end_y - end_y) * diamond_multiplier, 
                          offset_end_y)
  )

#Positioning the arrow labels

arrows <- arrows %>%
  mutate(
    label_x = (offset_start_x + offset_end_x) / 2 + coalesce(label_dx, 0),
    label_y = (offset_start_y + offset_end_y) / 2 + coalesce(label_dy, 0)
  )


#rounded rectangles - creating entire roudned dataframe to later only select the rounded boxes
get_rounded_rect_coords <- function(x, y, width, height, n_points = 100) {
  radius <- height
  left_angles <- seq(pi / 2, 3 * pi / 2, length.out = n_points / 2)
  right_angles <- seq(3 * pi / 2, pi / 2 + 2 * pi, length.out = n_points / 2)
  left_center_x <- x - width + radius
  right_center_x <- x + width - radius
  left_x <- left_center_x + radius * cos(left_angles)
  left_y <- y + radius * sin(left_angles)
  right_x <- right_center_x + radius * cos(right_angles)
  right_y <- y + radius * sin(right_angles)
  data.frame(x = c(left_x, right_x), y = c(left_y, right_y))
}
#retrieves rounded coordinates and turns it into a new dataframe with all nodes as roudned rectanges
rounded_coords <- do.call(rbind, lapply(1:nrow(nodes), function(i) {
  coords <- get_rounded_rect_coords(nodes$x[i], nodes$y[i], box_width, box_height)
  coords$id <- nodes$id[i]
  coords$group <- paste0("rounded_", nodes$id[i])
  coords
}))
#Add "shape" values to new rounded rectangle df - to be used to index these shapes later
rounded_coords <- left_join(rounded_coords, nodes[c("id", "shape")], by = "id", keep = F)



#plot
p <-  ggplot() +
  #rectangles
  geom_rect(data = filter(nodes, shape == "rect"),
            aes(
              xmin = x - box_width,
              xmax = x + box_width,
              ymin = y - box_height,
              ymax = y + box_height 
            ),
            fill = "lightgray",
            color = "black",
            linewidth = 0.6
  ) +
  
  #diamonds
  geom_polygon(
    data = filter(nodes, shape == "diamond") %>%
      rowwise() %>%
      mutate(
        coords = list(data.frame(
          x_d = x + c(0, diamond_width , 0, -diamond_width),
          y_d = y + c(diamond_height, 0, -diamond_height, 0)
        ))
      ) %>%
      tidyr::unnest(coords),
    aes(x = x_d, y = y_d, group = id),
    fill = "lightgray", color = "black") +
  
  #Rounded rectangle
  geom_polygon(data = filter(rounded_coords, shape == "rounded"),
               aes(x = x, y = y, group = group),
               fill = "lightgray", color = "black") +
  
  #lines
  geom_segment(data = arrows,
               aes(x = offset_start_x, y = offset_start_y, xend = offset_end_x , yend = offset_end_y),
               arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.2, "cm"), type = "closed"),
               linewidth = 0.4) +
  
  
  
  geom_text(
    data = filter(arrows, !is.na(label)),
    aes(x = label_x, y = label_y, label = label),
    size = 2.5,
    color = "black"
  ) + 
  
  #labels
  geom_text(data = nodes,
            aes(x = x, y = y, label = label),
            size = text_size,
            lineheight = 0.9
  ) +
  
  ##INSERTING FINAL DETAILS
  #halfway intersecting arrow from correct to measurement-apply arrow
  geom_segment(aes(x = 4 * spacing_x, y = 4 * spacing_y, xend = 4 * spacing_x, yend = 8 * spacing_y),
               arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.2, "cm"), type = "closed"),
               linewidth = 0.4) +
  
  geom_segment(aes(x = 3 * spacing_x + box_width, y = 4 * spacing_y, xend = 4 * spacing_x, yend = 4 * spacing_y),
               linewidth = 0.4) +
  #right-hand 90-degree arrow
  geom_segment(aes(x = 7 * spacing_x, y = 6 * spacing_y, xend = 7 * spacing_x, yend = 8 * spacing_y - box_height),
               arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.2, "cm"), type = "closed"),
               linewidth = 0.4) +
  geom_segment(aes(x = 5 * spacing_x + diamond_width, y = 6 * spacing_y, xend = 7 * spacing_x, yend = 6 * spacing_y),
               linewidth = 0.4) +
  
  #style
  coord_equal() +
  theme_void() +
  theme(
    plot.margin = margin(20, 20, 20, 20)
  )


# print(p)
# 
# ggsave("outlier2_1GJ.png", p, width = 10, height = 15, dpi = 600)
# ggsave("outlier2_1GJ.pdf", p, width = 10, height = 15, dpi = 600, device = cairo_pdf)
 
```

```{r outlier2_2Gcode, echo = FALSE, eval = FALSE}
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(ggplotify)
library(tidyr)
library(grid)

# === Layout parameters ===
box_width <- 1
box_height <- 0.5
text_size <- 3
spacing_y <- 0.9
spacing_x <- 1.6

diamond_multiplier <- 1.3
diamond_height <- box_height * diamond_multiplier
diamond_width  <- box_width  * diamond_multiplier

# === Node positions (REMOVED: "decide") ===
nodes <- data.frame(
  id = c("keep", "deterministic", "typicality", "with", "without", "part", "exception", "report"),
  shape = c("rect", "diamond", "diamond", "rect", "rect", "rect", "rect", "rounded"),
  label = c(
    "Keep outlier \ncase",
    "Deterministic \nperspective?",
    "Typicality \nperspective?",
    "Conduct NCA \nwith outlier",
    "Conduct NCA \nwithout outlier",
    "Consider outlier \npart of the \nphenomenon",
    "Consider outlier\nan exception\nDo robustness check\nwith/without outlier.",
    "Report results"
  ),
  x = spacing_x * c(1, 1, 1, 3, 3, 5, 5, 7),
  y = spacing_y * c(10, 8, 6, 8, 6, 8, 6, 7),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)

# === Arrow definitions ===
# NOTE: typicality -> with ("No") will be drawn as a custom multi-segment path later.
arrows <- data.frame(
  from =      c("keep",          "deterministic", "typicality", "deterministic", "typicality", "with",   "without",   "part",   "exception"),
  to   =      c("deterministic", "typicality",    "with",       "with",          "without",    "part",   "exception",  "report", "report"),
  label =     c( NA,             "No",            "No",         "Yes",           "Yes",        NA,       NA,           NA,       NA),
  label_dx =  c( NA,              0.2,             0.2,         -0.1,            -0.1,         NA,       0,            NA,       0),
  label_dy =  c( NA,              0.1,             0.1,          0.2,             0.2,         NA,       0.2,          NA,       0.2),
  start_dir = c("b",             "b",             "b",          "r",             "r",          "r",      "r",          "r",      "r"),
  end_dir   = c("t",             "t",             "l",          "l",             "l",          "l",      "l",          "l",      "l"),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)

# === Retrieve basis for X and Y start/end points ===
arrows <- arrows %>%
  left_join(nodes %>% select(id, x, y), by = c("from" = "id")) %>%
  rename(start_x = x, start_y = y) %>%
  left_join(nodes %>% select(id, x, y), by = c("to" = "id")) %>%
  rename(end_x = x, end_y = y)

# === Offsetting the arrows ===
get_start_x <- function(start_x, start_dir, box_width) {
  start_x + ifelse(start_dir == "r", box_width,
                   ifelse(start_dir == "l", -box_width, 0))
}
get_start_y <- function(start_y, start_dir, box_height) {
  start_y + ifelse(start_dir == "t", box_height,
                   ifelse(start_dir == "b", -box_height, 0))
}
get_end_x <- function(end_x, end_dir, box_width) {
  end_x + ifelse(end_dir == "r", box_width,
                 ifelse(end_dir == "l", -box_width, 0))
}
get_end_y <- function(end_y, end_dir, box_height) {
  end_y + ifelse(end_dir == "t", box_height,
                 ifelse(end_dir == "b", -box_height, 0))
}

arrows$offset_start_x <- with(arrows, get_start_x(start_x, start_dir, box_width))
arrows$offset_start_y <- with(arrows, get_start_y(start_y, start_dir, box_height))
arrows$offset_end_x   <- with(arrows, get_end_x(end_x, end_dir, box_width))
arrows$offset_end_y   <- with(arrows, get_end_y(end_y, end_dir, box_height))

# === Adjusting offset data for diamonds ===
arrows <- arrows %>%
  left_join(nodes %>% select(id, shape), by = c("from" = "id")) %>%
  rename(from_shape = shape) %>%
  left_join(nodes %>% select(id, shape), by = c("to" = "id")) %>%
  rename(to_shape = shape) %>%
  mutate(
    offset_start_x = ifelse(from_shape == "diamond",
                            start_x + (offset_start_x - start_x) * diamond_multiplier,
                            offset_start_x),
    offset_start_y = ifelse(from_shape == "diamond",
                            start_y + (offset_start_y - start_y) * diamond_multiplier,
                            offset_start_y),
    offset_end_x = ifelse(to_shape == "diamond",
                          end_x + (offset_end_x - end_x) * diamond_multiplier,
                          offset_end_x),
    offset_end_y = ifelse(to_shape == "diamond",
                          end_y + (offset_end_y - end_y) * diamond_multiplier,
                          offset_end_y)
  )

# === Positioning arrow labels (default) ===
arrows <- arrows %>%
  mutate(
    label_x = (offset_start_x + offset_end_x) / 2 + coalesce(label_dx, 0),
    label_y = (offset_start_y + offset_end_y) / 2 + coalesce(label_dy, 0)
  )

# === Custom “No” polyline: typicality -> with
# Shape requested: down -> horizontal -> up (stop before other arrow) -> right into box
no_row <- arrows %>% filter(from == "typicality", to == "with", label == "No") %>% slice(1)

drop <- 0.5         # how far down first
gap_y <- 0.35        # stay below the other arrow into the "with" box
gap_x <- 0.35        # stop just before entering, then final short step right

sx <- no_row$offset_start_x
sy <- no_row$offset_start_y
ex <- no_row$offset_end_x
ey <- no_row$offset_end_y

target_y <- ey - gap_y
pre_end_x <- ex - gap_x

# pick a horizontal “corridor” x (to the left of the other incoming arrow)
mid_x <- pre_end_x

no_path <- data.frame(
  x = c(sx, sx, mid_x, mid_x, pre_end_x, ex),
  y = c(sy, sy - drop, sy - drop, target_y, target_y, target_y),
  group = "no_path"
)

# Put the "No" label on the horizontal segment (down+horizontal)
no_label <- data.frame(
  x = (sx + mid_x) / 2 + 0.1,
  y = (sy - drop) + 0.15,
  label = "No"
)

# Remove the default straight segment for this arrow (we draw it ourselves)
arrows_straight <- arrows %>% filter(!(from == "typicality" & to == "with" & label == "No"))

# === Rounded rectangles helper ===
get_rounded_rect_coords <- function(x, y, width, height, n_points = 100) {
  radius <- height
  left_angles  <- seq(pi / 2, 3 * pi / 2, length.out = n_points / 2)
  right_angles <- seq(3 * pi / 2, pi / 2 + 2 * pi, length.out = n_points / 2)
  
  left_center_x  <- x - width + radius
  right_center_x <- x + width - radius
  
  left_x  <- left_center_x  + radius * cos(left_angles)
  left_y  <- y + radius * sin(left_angles)
  right_x <- right_center_x + radius * cos(right_angles)
  right_y <- y + radius * sin(right_angles)
  
  data.frame(x = c(left_x, right_x), y = c(left_y, right_y))
}

rounded_coords <- do.call(rbind, lapply(1:nrow(nodes), function(i) {
  coords <- get_rounded_rect_coords(nodes$x[i], nodes$y[i], box_width, box_height)
  coords$id <- nodes$id[i]
  coords$group <- paste0("rounded_", nodes$id[i])
  coords
}))
rounded_coords <- left_join(rounded_coords, nodes[c("id", "shape")], by = "id", keep = FALSE)

# === Plot ===
p <- ggplot() +
  # rectangles
  geom_rect(
    data = filter(nodes, shape == "rect"),
    aes(
      xmin = x - box_width, xmax = x + box_width,
      ymin = y - box_height, ymax = y + box_height
    ),
    fill = "lightgray", color = "black", linewidth = 0.6
  ) +
  # diamonds
  geom_polygon(
    data = filter(nodes, shape == "diamond") %>%
      rowwise() %>%
      mutate(coords = list(data.frame(
        x_d = x + c(0, diamond_width, 0, -diamond_width),
        y_d = y + c(diamond_height, 0, -diamond_height, 0)
      ))) %>%
      tidyr::unnest(coords),
    aes(x = x_d, y = y_d, group = id),
    fill = "lightgray", color = "black"
  ) +
  # rounded rectangles
  geom_polygon(
    data = filter(rounded_coords, shape == "rounded"),
    aes(x = x, y = y, group = group),
    fill = "lightgray", color = "black"
  ) +
  
  # straight arrows (all except the custom “No”)
  geom_segment(
    data = arrows_straight,
    aes(x = offset_start_x, y = offset_start_y, xend = offset_end_x, yend = offset_end_y),
    arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.2, "cm"), type = "closed"),
    linewidth = 0.4
  ) +
  
  # custom “No” polyline arrow
  geom_path(
    data = no_path,
    aes(x = x, y = y, group = group),
    linewidth = 0.4,
    lineend = "butt",
    arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.2, "cm"), type = "closed")
  ) +
  
  # arrow labels (all except the custom “No”)
  geom_text(
    data = filter(arrows_straight, !is.na(label)),
    aes(x = label_x, y = label_y, label = label),
    size = 2.5,
    color = "black"
  ) +
  # custom “No” label
  geom_text(
    data = no_label,
    aes(x = x, y = y, label = label),
    size = 2.5,
    color = "black"
  ) +
  
  # node labels
  geom_text(
    data = nodes,
    aes(x = x, y = y, label = label),
    size = text_size,
    lineheight = 0.9
  ) +
  
  coord_equal() +
  theme_void() +
  theme(plot.margin = margin(20, 20, 20, 20))

print(p)

# Save (optional)
# ggsave("outlier2_2GJ.png", p, width = 10, height = 15, dpi = 600)
# ggsave("outlier2_2GJ.pdf", p, width = 10, height = 15, device = cairo_pdf)
 

```  

In the first step of NCA's outlier decision approach, the potential outlier case is selected. Next, it is evaluated is the case is sampling or case selection error. This refers to a case not being part of the theoretical domain of the *formal* necessity hypothesis. For example, the case may be a large company, whereas the necessity theory applies to small companies only. An outlier case due to sampling or case selection error should be removed from further analysis. If the case is not an outlier because of sampling or selection error, it should be evaluated if the case may have measurement error. The condition or the outcome may have been incorrectly scored, which could happen for a variety of reasons. If the case has measurement error and this error can be corrected, the outlier case becomes a regular case and is kept for the analyses. 

If the measurement error cannot be corrected, two situations may apply. If the measurement error occurs in the outcome score, the case should be removed from further analysis of all necessary conditions (if there are multiple necessary conditions). If the measurement error occurs in the condition score, the case should be removed from further analysis of that condition, but the analyst must decide to keep it or the remove it for the analysis of other conditions. This depends on the analyst's judgement if the case is generally problematic, or only regarding the specific condition where it was identified as an outlier case (the case may not be an outlier case for other conditions). 

If the outlier is not caused by sampling/selection error or measurement error or no information about sampling error or measurement error is available, the outlier may be considered an 'outlier for unknown reasons'. The analyst may then decide to apply an outlier rule that is justifiable and convincing. A mechanistic outlier rule (e.g., based on percentages) to decide whether the case should be kept or removed is usually not desirable. Such rules are arbitrary but their consequences on the results are usually large. Therefore, applying a mechanistic outlier rule is generally not advised. If it is used anyway and outliers are removed mechanistically, it should be reported and justified, and a robustness check should be done comparing the results with and without applying the outlier rule (Section \@ref(robustnesschecks)).  
<!-- For example, a case may be considered an outlier if it is three standard deviations away from the mean, but that may affect the scope and thus the effect size considerably. For bivariate ceiling outliers no statistically and generally accepted outlier rule exists. Such a rule may therefore be stated by the analyst and be based on, for example, the magnitude of the absolute or relative effect size changes when the case is removed (Section \@ref(outlieridentification)).  -->
<!-- However, mechanistic outlier rules should be specified and justified. If an outlier rule is applied, a robustness check with and without using the outlier rule is recommended. -->
Note that concluding that necessity is rejected because of presence of outliers may be an important result. The condition may on average influence the outcome, but apparently it is not essential as cases exist that can compensate the absence or a low value of the condition. The outliers that reject necessity may be best or innovative cases that are able to achieve a desired outcome without or with a low level of the condition. If the outcome is undesirable, outlier cases may need different action to reduce the outcome than standard actions focused on the condition. In other words, outliers may be informative rather than being errors. *Rejecting* necessity due to outliers and discussing outliers may be a more valuable result than *accepting* necessity after removal of outliers. If no outlier rule is applied (the preferred approach), the potential outlier case is kept, and the next step of NCA's outlier decision approach begins.
  
```{r outlierdecision2, echo=FALSE, fig.align ='center', fig.cap='Outlier decision tree for Step 2 of the outlier decision approach.', out.width='99%', fig.align='center'}
knitr::include_graphics("outlier2_2GJ.png")
```

The decision tree for the second step is illustrated in Figure \@ref(fig:outlierdecision2). This picks up from the decision to keep the outlier case because the case has no known error, and no outlier rule is applied.
To continue the analysis two perspectives are distinguished for necessity causality: the *deterministic perspective*, and the *typicality perspective* [Section \@ref(typicalitynecessity) and @dul2024different]. The  deterministic perspective, in principle, does not allow points above the ceiling. However, some noise may be tolerated, namely when the case is close to the ceiling in the medium purity ceiling zone (Section \@ref(purity)). If this perspective is adopted the analysis continues with the outlier. 

The typicality perspective, in principle, allows outlier cases that are further away from the ceiling, but only if they are rare. These outliers are called *exceptions*. Accepting the typicality perspective means accepting that the outlier is not an error but behaves differently than other cases with another (unknown) explanation than necessity. 
Thus, the typicality perspective can only be adopted if the case:

- is a rare exception.

- is (presumably) not an error.

- is isolated and far from the ceiling (not noise).

- behaves different than other cases for unknown reasons. 

If the typicality perspective is adopted, the analysis continues without the outlier. because it does not represent the necessity phenomenon that is studied. It can however be studied separately because of the unusual characteristic. Since a case without an error is removed from the analysis, a robustness check should be done with and without the exception to change how it affects the results.

If the typicality perspective is not adopted, the analysis continues with the outlier.^[Note that the typicality perspective can only be used when *rare* exceptions exist. Having many points far above the ceiling or having points that are not isolated from the rest of the points, do not qualify for adopting the typicality perspective. The typicality perspective is only meant for a very limited number of isolated points that represent another (unknown) explanation than necessity. Typicality is about "rare exceptions" [@goldstein2012typicality] and applied to NCA's deterministic causal logic (Chapter \@ref(causality)),  deterministic necessity  becomes "deterministic with exceptions" [@dul2024different], not probabilistic necessity. Also see Section \@ref(counterexamplesexceptions) on how to deal with exceptions during the development of a formal necessity hypothesis.] 

In general, the handling of outliers (keep or remove) relies on a series of decisions made by the analyst. It is advised to perform robustness checks to evaluate the sensitivity of the results to specific outlier decisions and to report the results (Section \@ref(robustnesschecks)).

<!-- and is far from the ceiling. If such exception is identified, the analyst may adopt the typicality perspective on necessity, see Section \@ref(typicalitynecessity). Sections \@ref(purity) and \@ref(purity3) shows how exceptions can be identified using the model fit measures is *exceptions*. -->

<!-- If the outlier is far from the ceiling and rare, the typicality perspective may be adopted.  -->

<!-- ) that ar not allow points above the ceiling. However, some noise may be tolerated, namely when the case is close to the ceiling in the medium purity ceiling zone (Section \@ref(purity)).  -->


<!-- If this perspective is adopted the analysis continues with the outlier.  -->

<!-- above the ceiling  are near above the ceiling.The model fit metric noise  but above the ceiling. the points are close to the ceiling (in the medithis is considered not to violated outliers above  -->

<!-- exceptions. An outlier for unknown reasons is then considered to be part of the phenomenon of interest, and the analysis includes the outlier. This is the default approach. Some outliers close to the ceiling may reflect noise. Other outliers may be real  -->


<!-- The outlier decision tree of Figure \@ref(fig:outlierdecision1) reflects this perspective. -->

<!-- The *typicality perspective* can be formulated as 'if $X$ then typically $Y$'. This perspective does allow rare exceptions. An outlier for unknown reasons is then considered to be an *exception*. The analysis itself is done without the exception, but it is shown in the $XY$-plot, and exceptions are discussed separately in the report of the study. -->

<!-- If such exception is identified, the analyst may adopt the typicality perspective on necessity, see Section \@ref(typicalitynecessity). Sections \@ref(purity) and \@ref(purity3) shows how exceptions can be identified using the model fit measures is *exceptions*.^[Note that the typicality perspective can only be used when *rare* exceptions exist. Having many points above the ceiling (e.g., because of noisy data) or having points that are not isolated from the rest of the points, do not qualify for adopting the typicality perspective. The typicality perspective is only meant for a very limited number of isolated points that represent another (unknown) explanation than necessity. Typicality is about "rare exceptions" [@goldstein2012typicality] and applied to NCA's deterministic causal logic (Chapter \@ref(causality)),  deterministic necessity  becomes "deterministic with exceptions" [@dul2024different], not probabilistic necessity. Also see Section \@ref(counterexamplesexceptions) on how to deal with exceptions in NCA during the development of a formal necessity hypothesis.] -->

<!-- The outlier decision tree of Figure \@ref(fig:outlierdecision1) reflects this perspective. -->

In the illustrative example, the outlier approach is applied as follows. The case Japan is the only potential outlier to be considered. The first step of the outlier decision approach (Figure \@ref(fig:outlierdecision1)) is the evaluation of whether Japan could be an outlier case because of sampling/selection error. The theoretical domain of the hypothesis was defined as "all countries in the world" (Section \@ref(illustrativeexample)). Japan is one of these countries, so sampling/selection error does not apply. 

Next, the question is whether Japan's scores for $X$ and/or $Y$ could have measurement error. This seems unlikely as these scores are obtained from a reputable archival dataset that has been widely used elsewhere, and discussions of measurement error specifically for Japan have not emerged before. The next consideration is to apply an outlier rule for Japan. 

Since a mechanistic outlier rule is not preferred, and Japan is a ceiling outlier for which no generally accepted outlier rules are available, the result of the first step of the outlier decision approach is to keep Japan as outlier case for further consideration.

In the second step of the outlier approach (Figure \@ref(fig:outlierdecision2)) it is decided to consider Japan as an exception for unknown reasons. One speculation may be that Japan's high level of innovation is focused more on incremental innovation of systems than on disruptive start-up style of innovation based on high-risk and high failure entrepreneurship of individuals. Despite the relatively small number of cases in the dataset (28) the illustrative example considers Japan a rare exception. This means that the full analysis must be re-done to obtain an estimate of the main necessity effect without Japan, and that Japan is considered an exception, to be discussed separately in the report. Additionally, a robustness check in which the results with and without the exception should be conducted and reported.

<!-- the which could be speculated ), heeping it in the phenomenin  -->
<!-- ### Outlier decision approach {#outlierdecisionmaking2} -->
<!-- After potential outliers are identified, the next question is what to do with them: remove or keep. Because removing outliers has a large effect on the outcome (see the definition of outlier), removing a case from the data set  without further judgment is not recommended. In general, the recommendation is to keep a potential outlier, unless there is a good reason to remove it (thus "keep unless", instead of "remove unless"). When an outlier is removed it is important to report that it was removed and why.  -->

<!-- Figure \@ref(fig:outlierdecision) shows a decision tree to assist analysts in deciding about keeping or removing a potential outlier. -->

<!-- ```{r outlierdecision, echo=FALSE, fig.cap='Outlier decision tree.', out.width='100%', fig.align='center'} -->
<!-- knitr::include_graphics("outlier2.png") -->
<!-- ``` -->

<!-- First, the potential outlier case is selected. It is then evaluated for sampling error. Sampling error refers a case that does not represent the theoretical domain of the theory that is tested or to which the analyst wants to generalize the results. For example, the case may be a large company, whereas the theory applies to small companies only. This is a reason to remove the outlier case.  -->

<!-- If there is no sampling error, the potential outlier case may have measurement error. The condition or the outcome may have been incorrectly scored, which could happen for a variety of reasons. If there is measurement error, and the error can be corrected then the outlier case may become a regular case and can stay in the dataset. If the measurement error cannot be corrected the case can be removed from the dataset and this reason is reported.  -->

<!-- If there is no information about sampling error or measurement error, the analyst can decide to apply an outlier rule.  If the analyst applies an outlier rule, for example based on the magnitude of the absolute or relative effect size changes when the case is removed (see section \@ref(outlieridentification)). Such rule should be specified and justified. The outlier rule decides whether the case is kept or removed. If no outlier rule is applied the potential outlier case remains in the data set.  -->

<!-- When a potential outlier case has a large influence the necessity effect size if removed, but stays in the dataset, a *sensitivity analysis* can be done. In this analysis the analyst explores the influence of the potential outlier case not only on the effect size (see above), but also the $p$-value of the effect size to judge if (lack of) evidence of necessity in the data remains approximately the same or not.  -->

<!-- Furthermore, when a potential outlier case has a large influence the necessity effect size if removed, but stays in the dataset, the analyst might consider taking a 'probabilistic view' on necessary. In this view necessity is considered to be present, even if outlier cases do not support it. The necessity analysis is done with the data set without the outliers (e.g., NCA is done with the incomplete dataset) and the outliers are added afterwards again. The analyst should be reluctant to use this approach, in particularly for ceiling outliers, because ignoring such outlier usually results in an increase of the effect size.  -->

In the following analysis, Japan is removed from the `nca.example` dataset, followed by re-doing the data analysis: 

```{r echo = TRUE, eval = TRUE, cache=TRUE, message=TRUE, warnings= FALSE}
library(NCA)
data(nca.example)

nca.exampleN <- nca.example[-14,] #exclude Japan
set.seed(123) # for replicability of estimated  p-value
modelN <- nca_analysis(nca.exampleN, 1,3, 
                       ceilings = 'cr_fdh', 
                       test.rep = 10000)
modelN
```

The result show that the effect size $d$ = 0.44 and the $p$-value = 0.023. Both values meet the selected threshold levels of $d$ = 0.10 and $\alpha$ = 0.05 such that the necessity hypothesis is not rejected (see however Section \@ref(modelfit2) about model fit and Section \@ref(robustnesschecks) about robustness checks). 

To compare the results with and without the exceptions, it is possible to extract the $XY$-plot of this analysis that is done without the exception, and then to add the exception back to show its original position, and to include the original ceiling line as well using the following script.

```{r ncaexamplenojapanshow, echo = TRUE, eval = FALSE, cache=TRUE, message=TRUE, warnings= FALSE}
library(NCA)
library(ggplot2)
data(nca.example)
# Coordinates of the exception
x <- nca.example["Japan", "Individualism"]
y <- nca.example["Japan", "Innovation performance"]

# Intercept and slope of original ceiling line 
model <- nca_analysis(nca.example, 1, 3, ceilings = "cr_fdh")
model
a <- nca_extract(model, x = "Individualism", 
                 ceiling = "cr_fdh", 
                 param = "Intercept")
b <- nca_extract(model, x = "Individualism", 
                 ceiling = "cr_fdh", 
                 param = "Slope")

#2 NCA without exception
modelN <- nca_analysis(
  nca.example[row.names(nca.example) != "Japan", ], 1, 3, 
  ceilings = "cr_fdh", custom = c(a,b) )
modelN

# XY plot for comparison
out <- nca_output(modelN, plots = TRUE, summaries = FALSE)
p <- out$plots[["Individualism"]] +
  geom_point(aes(x = x, y = y), shape = 1, colour = "blue") +
  geom_point(aes(x = x, y = y), shape = 4, colour = "red",
             size = 2, stroke = 1.2)
print(p)
```

```{r ncaexamplenojapanrun, echo = FALSE, eval = FALSE, cache=TRUE, message=TRUE, warnings= FALSE}
library(NCA)
library(ggplot2)
data(nca.example)
# Coordinates of the exception
x <- nca.example["Japan", "Individualism"]
y <- nca.example["Japan", "Innovation performance"]

# Intercept and slope of original ceiling line 
model <- nca_analysis(nca.example, 1, 3, ceilings = "cr_fdh")
model
a <- nca_extract(model, x = "Individualism", ceiling = "cr_fdh", param = "Intercept")
b <- nca_extract(model, x = "Individualism", ceiling = "cr_fdh", param = "Slope")

#2 NCA without exception
modelN <- nca_analysis(
  nca.example[row.names(nca.example) != "Japan", ], 1, 3, ceilings = "cr_fdh", custom = c(a,b) )
modelN

# XY plot for comparison
out <- nca_output(modelN, plots = TRUE, summaries = FALSE)
p <- out$plots[["Individualism"]] +
  geom_point(aes(x = x, y = y), shape = 1, colour = "blue") +
  geom_point(aes(x = x, y = y), shape = 4, colour = "red", size = 2, stroke = 1.2)
print(p)
ggsave("Individualism_Japan.pdf", plot = p, width = 15, height = 15, units = "cm")

```{r ncaexample4, echo=FALSE, fig.cap='$XY$-plot of illustrative example with one exception (crossed out). Solid line: ceiling line after removal of the exception. Dashed line: ceiling line before removal of the exception.', out.width='75%', fig.align='center', cache=TRUE}
#knitr::include_graphics("noJapan.png")
#knitr::include_graphics("noJapan BWcool.png")
knitr::include_graphics("Individualism_Japan.png")
#knitr::include_graphics("Individualism_Japan BWcool.png")
```

Figure \@ref(fig:ncaexample4) shows the $XY$-plot with the outlier case, the original ceiling line and the new ceiling line.
The above procedures are for one outlier at a time. The judgment becomes more complex when the analyst considers removing more than one outlier one by one or at the same time. For example, after removing the first outlier, a case that was originally not part of the first set of single potential outliers, may be identified as a new potential outlier, etc. Generally, removing outliers is not advised unless the analyst has very good reasons for it.    

## Model fit {#modelfit2}
In Chapter \@ref(mathematics), specific model fit metrics for NCA were introduced to evaluate how well the chosen necessity model (bounding box and ceiling line) represents the necessity relationship suggested by the data. Since NCA's model is mathematical and not statistical (no error term), common statistical model fit metrics such as R$^2$ or likelihood-based fit metrics are not applicable. To illustrate the use of NCA's model  fit metrics, they are applied to the illustrative example. In the ```NCA``` software for ```R```, the values of the model fit metrics can be obtained using ```summaries = TRUE``` in the ```nca_output``` function as follows:
  
```{r echo = TRUE, eval= TRUE, cache=TRUE, warnings= FALSE}
library(NCA)
data(nca.example)

nca.exampleN <- nca.example[-14,] #exclude Japan
set.seed(123) # for replicability of estimated  p-value
modelN <- nca_analysis(nca.exampleN, 1,3, 
                       ceilings = 'cr_fdh', 
                       test.rep = 10000)
nca_output(modelN, plots = FALSE, summaries = TRUE)
```

<!-- ```{r , echo=FALSE, out.width='99%', fig.align='left', cache=TRUE} -->
<!-- #knitr::include_graphics("outputmodelfit.png") -->
<!-- #knitr::include_graphics("Output summary with model fit3.png") -->
<!-- knitr::include_graphics("Output summary with model fit4CR.png") -->
<!-- ``` -->

```{r puritysoliditycode, echo=FALSE, eval= FALSE}
# ceiling: iso solidity/veracity -- 06-02-2025 - Main
#Revised 24 September 2025
#revised 5 January 2026 (sharpness 1 is different now)
#combined soveV44JD.R and soveHlpV44JD.R

####################### START HELPER FUNCTIONS ##################### (from soveV44JD.R) 

markInputData <- function(theD, caseIdNo = NULL, caseDesc = NULL){
  # theD <- test; caseDesc <- "caseDescriptor"
  
  caseDescriptor <- "caseDescriptor"
  if (is.null(caseDesc)){ caseDesc <- "caseDescriptor"}  # go for default
  cn <- colnames(theD)
  if (is.element(caseDesc, cn)) { # variable caseDesc exists already
    if (!is_tibble(theD)) {
      theD <- as_tibble(rownames_to_column(theD, var = "rowDesciptor"))
    } 
  } else { # we work with a tibble
    if (!is_tibble(theD)) { # create from column
      theD <- as_tibble(rownames_to_column(theD, var = caseDesc))
    } else { # is already a tibble, so create it
      theD %<>% mutate("{caseDesc}" = paste0(caseDesc, "_", row_number() ))
    } 
  }
  # if not NULL add a case number, overwriting any existing variable of the same name
  if (!is.null(caseIdNo)) theD %<>% mutate(!!caseIdNo := row_number(), .before = 1)
  return(theD)
} # markInputData 

addColRow2xyPoints <- function(xy) {# xy: points
  
  crRank <- function(x) { # x: numericals to be ranked in col/row
    d <- x %>% sort() %>% diff()
    d <- case_when(d == 0 ~ 0, .default = 1) # 0 stays 0, non-zeros become 1
    cd <- cumsum(d)   # my rank: cumulative sum
    r <- c(1, 1 + cd) # compensate for the one value lost due to differing
  }
  
  xy %>% arrange(x, y) # possibly changes order of rows
  
  if (nrow(xy) > 0) {
    xy %<>% arrange(x) %>% mutate(col = crRank(x)) # add column data
    xy %<>% arrange(y) %>% mutate(row = crRank(y)) # add row data
  } else {
    xy <- NULL
  }
  
  return(xy) # Warning: the order of rows possibly changed
} # addColRow2xyPoints

reduceX2Ceil <- function(xceil, br) { # go from extended ceiling to ceiling
  xceil %<>% arrange(x, y)
  ceil_south <- filter(xceil, y == br$y.low)
  ceil_west <- filter(xceil, x == br$x.low)
  if ((nrow(ceil_south) == 2) | (nrow(ceil_west) == 2)) {#delete south-west (1st_pnt)
    ceil <- xceil[-1, ]
  } else {
    ceil <- xceil
  }
  ceil_north <- filter(ceil, y == br$y.high)
  ceil_east <- filter(ceil, x == br$x.high)
  if ((nrow(ceil_north) == 2) | (nrow(ceil_east) == 2)) {#delete north-east
    ceil %<>% head(-1)
  }
  if (nrow(ceil) == 1) {
    if ((ceil$x == br$x.low) & (ceil$y == br$y.high)) {
      ceil <- NULL # there is no ceiling (a very degenerate case)
    }
  }
  return(ceil)
} #  reduceX2Ceil

closing2BR <- function(xyP,  br) {
  # Extend the points in xyP with tips/ends towards sw and ne of bounding rectangle
  # (while keeping the feasible space (space in br below/right of the points in xyP) 
  # to a minimum).
  
  # xyP <- hn
  
  br_xyP <- xyP %>% select(x, y) %>% 
    summarize(across(everything(), 
                     .fns = list(low = min, high = max), 
                     .names = "{.col}.{.fn}"))
  ceil <- xceil <- xyP
  # projections
  #if ((br_xyP$y.low > br$y.low) & (br_xyP$y.low > br$y.low)){ 
  if (br_xyP$y.low > br$y.low) {
    # xyP does not meet the bottom (south side) of br
    xceil %<>% add_row(x = br_xyP$x.low, y = br$y.low, .before = .1) 
    if (br_xyP$x.low > br$x.low) {
      ceil %<>% add_row(x = br_xyP$x.low, y = br$y.low, .before = .1) 
    }
  }
  if (br_xyP$x.high < br$x.high) { # xyP does not meet the east side of bb
    xceil %<>% add_row(x = br$x.high, y = br_xyP$y.high) 
    if (br_xyP$y.high < br$y.high) {
      ceil %<>% add_row(x = br$x.high, y = br_xyP$y.high)
    } 
  }
  # add sw and ne corners of br (if not already there):
  if (nrow(filter(xceil, x == br$x.low, y == br$y.low)) == 0) { # no sw so add
    xceil %<>% add_row(x = br$x.low, y = br$y.low, .before = 1)
  }
  if (nrow(filter(xceil, x == br$x.high, y == br$y.high)) == 0) { # no ne so add
    xceil %<>% add_row(x = br$x.high, y = br$y.high)
  }
  return(list(ceil = ceil, xceil = xceil))
} # closing2BR

cornerCalcs <- function(D) { # D = data (tibble) with xy variables
  # adds north-west corners to the tibble
  # D <- theData
  
  D %<>% 
    dplyr::mutate(igo = row_number()) %>% # igo: initially given order in D  
    dplyr::arrange(x, y)%>% # order data, first on x-value, then on y-value
    dplyr::mutate(xysi = row_number(), .before = 1) # xysi = xy-sorted index 
  
  nData <-  nrow(D)  # number of data points 
  corners <- NULL    # nwCorners[j] is the xysi-index for the j-th NW-corner
  i <- 1             # dummy index for current data point (an xysi-index)
  
  while (i <= nData) { # i is potential NW-corner as y-value has just risen
    ymax <- D$y[i]     # maximum y-value encountered (thus far)
    while ((D$x[i] == D$x[i + 1]) & (i < nData)) { # vertical data: keep going up
      i <- i + 1
      ymax <- max(ymax, D$y[i])
    }
    corners <- c(corners, i) # expand identified corners
    while ((i <= nData) & (D$y[i] <= ymax)) {i <- i + 1} # go2 next y-jump   
  }  
  
  icolStart <- rep(NA, length(corners))
  for (j in seq_along(corners)) { 
    sel <- filter(D, x == x[corners[j]]) # vertical: points with same x
    icolStart[j] <- min(sel$xysi)        # first xysi that is dominated by j-th corner
  }
  xcolStart <- c(icolStart, nData + 1) # new column after end of data (for convenience)
  
  pr <- rep(NA, nData)      # will, for each case, identify (corner) peers (by xysi)
  colSize <- rep(NA, nData) # number in columns (for corner points)
  for (k in seq_along(icolStart)) { # k <- 1
    pr[xcolStart[k]: (xcolStart[k+1]-1)] <- corners[k]
    colSize[corners[k]] <- length(xcolStart[k]: (xcolStart[k+1]-1))
  }
  
  # look up peer indices in initially given order (in stead of xysi):
  prigo <- pr # will hold initially given indices for the peers
  for (u in unique(pr)){# remember that D has been sorted by (unique) xysi
    prigo[pr == u] <- slice(D, u)$igo # u (xysi) --> ..$igo (original index) 
  }
  
  nw <- tibble(igo = D$igo, corner = D$xysi %in% corners, 
               start = D$xysi %in% icolStart,
               peer = prigo,
               size = colSize) # the [NW or SE] corner points (peers)
  # Restore original order of rows:
  nw %<>% arrange(igo) %>% select(-c("igo")) # sort by igo and then loose igo
  
  return(nw) 
} # cornerCalcs 

# Constructions ----
# calculations
new_xyCorners <- function(xyD,  sides = c("nw_corners", "se_corners", "both_corners")) {
  # xyD: a frame with x and y variables
  xyD %<>% select(x, y)
  sides <- match.arg(sides, c("nw_corners", "se_corners", "both_corners"))
  
  if (sides == "nw_corners" | sides == "both_corners"){
    nwCorn <- cornerCalcs(xyD) %>% rename_with(~ paste0("nw_", .))
  } else {nwCorn <- NULL}
  if (sides == "se_corners" | sides == "both_corners"){
    seD <- xyD %>% mutate(x = -x, y = -y)
    # Results (used) will remain the same upon constant shifts of seD. 
    # So, offsetx - x, offsety - y would work for any choice of 
    # fixed values offsetx, and offsety. Here we take both values equal zero.
    seCorn <- cornerCalcs(seD) %>% rename_with(~ paste0("se_", .))
  } else {seCorn <- NULL}
  xyD <- bind_cols(xyD, nwCorn, seCorn)
  # as a subclass
  class(xyD) <- c("xyCorners", class(xyD)) # not really used
  attr(xyD, "sides") <- sides
  
  return(xyD)
} # new_xyCorners

addNooks <- function(xys) {
  xys %<>% select(x, y) %>% arrange(x, y)
  if (nrow(xys) > 1) { # add nooks via (inefficient) add_row
    for (i in (1: (nrow(xys) - 1))) { 
      xys %<>% add_row(x = xys$x[i + 1], y = xys$y[i]) }
  }
  xys %<>% distinct()
  return(xys)
}

makeStp <- function(xyH, br) {
  #' Construct step ceiling from hooks (= nw corners) and bounding rectangle (br) such that 
  #' feasible space is minimum (and empty space is maximum).
  #' xyH (hooks): variables x and y with x increasing => y increasing
  
  # xyH <- filter(prongs$xyO, nw_corner); br <- prongs$br
  
  hn <- xyH %>% select(x, y) %>% arrange(x, y) 
  
  if (nrow(hn) == 0){ # trivial cases
    if (is.null(br)) {
      return(NULL)
    } else {
      ceilings <- lists(ceil = NULL,
                        xceil = tibble(x = c(br$x.low, br$x.low, br$x.high),
                                       y = c(br$y.low, br$y.high, br$y.high)))
      return(ceilings)
    }
  } # trivial cases
  
  hn <- addNooks(hn)
  
  ceilings <- closing2BR(hn, br) # extending to the south/east to touch the BR
  
  # adding column and row information (by ranking): 
  
  ceil <- addColRow2xyPoints(ceilings$ceil) %>% arrange(x, y)
  ceil <- reduceX2Ceil(ceil, br)
  xceil <-  addColRow2xyPoints(ceilings$xceil) %>% arrange(x, y)
  
  # Note. The curve traced out by the (x, y) points in the final hn 
  # consists of alternating horizontal (h) - vertical (v) line segments 
  # from the sw-point of the rectangle br to the ne-point of the rectangle bb. 
  # There are a priori two patterns depending on whether departure from 
  # the se-point is horizontal or vertical. So we can have, a priori,
  # sw - h - v - h - v - ... - ne  or  sw - v - h - v - h ... - ne.
  # Note that hn in this way cements the information on 
  # the feasible space: (inner) ceiling + box.
  # As a consequence, hn has precisely two points with 
  # the same value for x or with the same value for y, unless for tips.
  
  return(list(ceil = ceil, xceil = xceil))
} # makeStp

makeVrs <- function(xyD, br){
  # xyD <- nwCorners; br <- prongs$br
  
  findExtremeNW <- function(xyData, br){ # variable return to scale (concave hull) + support lines
    # uses package `rstackdeque'
    
    xyData %<>% 
      arrange(x, y) %>% mutate(xysi = row_number(), .before = 1) # xysi = xy-sorted
    xysi <- pull(xyData, xysi)
    NW <- as.matrix(select(xyData, c(x, y)))
    
    outCross <- function(p1, p2, p3){ # z-component of outer product (p2 - p1) x (p3 - p1)
      (p2[1]-p1[1]) * (p3[2] - p1[2]) - (p2[2] - p1[2]) * (p3[1] - p1[1])
    }
    
    if (nrow(NW) > 2) {
      U <- rstack()
      U <- insert_top(U, 1)
      U <- insert_top(U, 2)
      for (k in (3:nrow(NW))) {# k <- 3
        p3 <- NW[k, ]
        p2 <- NW[peek_top(U), ]
        V <- without_top(U)
        p1 <- NW[peek_top(V), ]
        onLeft <- outCross(p1, p2, p3) > 0 # p3 on the left of line p1 --> p2 
        while (onLeft & ((length(U) > 1))) {
          U <- V
          if (length(U) > 1) {
            p2 <- NW[peek_top(U), ]
            V <- without_top(U)
            p1 <- NW[peek_top(V), ]
            onLeft <- outCross(p1, p2, p3) > 0
          }
        }
        U <- insert_top(U, k)
      }
      idx <- xysi[unlist(as.list(U))]
    } else { # fewer than three points in xyData
      idx <- xysi
    }
    
    xyData <- mutate(xyData, ext = xysi %in% idx)
    extremes <- filter(xyData, ext)
    
    supporting_lines <- NULL
    if (nrow(extremes) > 1) {
      for (k in (1 : (nrow(extremes) - 1))) { # k <- 1; k <- k + 1
        p1 <- extremes[k, ]
        p2 <- extremes[k+1, ]
        b <- (p1$y - p2$y)/(p1$x - p2$x)
        a <- p2$y - p2$x * b
        tmp <- tibble(xysi1 = p1$xysi, xysi2 = p2$xysi, 
                      a = a, b = b, 
                      x1 = p1$x, y1 = p1$y, x2 = p2$x, y2 = p2$y)
        supporting_lines <- bind_rows(supporting_lines, tmp)
      }
    } else { # only single point in vrs (concave hull)
      supporting_lines <- tibble(xysi1 = as.numeric(NA), xysi2 =  as.numeric(NA), 
                                 a = 1, b = 0, 
                                 # x1 = extCeil[1,]$x, y1 = extCeil[1,]$y, ##########################
                                 # x2 = extCeil[1,]$x, y2 = extCeil[1,]$y) ########################
      x1 = xyData[1,]$x, y1 = xyData[1,]$y, #######################
      x2 = xyData[1,]$x, y2 = xyData[1,]$y) ######################
      
    }
    
    return(list(extremes = extremes, supporting_lines = supporting_lines))
  } # findExtremeNW
  
  if (is.null(br)) { # go for empirical box:
    br <- xyD %>% select(x,y) %>% 
      summarize(across(everything(), .fns = list(low = min, high = max), 
                       .names = "{.col}.{.fn}")) 
  } else { # use the given br as selector for the data
    xyD %<>% filter(between(x, br$x.low, br$x.high) & between(y, br$y.low, br$y.high))
  }
  
  extData <- findExtremeNW(xyD, br) # Note. Supporting lines will remain unused here. ##############added ,br
  # Now close the line formed by extData$extremes towards the br (in south-east directions):
  ceilings <- closing2BR(extData$extremes, br) # no hooks allowed
  ceil <- addColRow2xyPoints(ceilings$ceil)
  ceil <- reduceX2Ceil(ceil, br)
  xceil <- addColRow2xyPoints(ceilings$xceil)
  
  return(list(ceil = ceil, xceil = xceil))
} # makeVrs

findProngs <- function(tb, sides = c("nw_corners", "se_corners", "both_corners"),
                       monikers = list(xn = "x", yn = "y"), 
                       br = NULL, naremove = TRUE){ 
  # Finding the north-west disposal hull (having free disposal to the south-east)
  
  # tb <- ncaX1; sides <- c("nw_corners", "se_corners", "both_corners")
  # monikers <- list(xn = "Individualism", yn = "Innovation performance")
  # br <- NULL; naremove <- TRUE
  
  # preparations
  # oriD <- markInputData(tb, caseIdNo = "caNo") # add "caNo" and caseDescription
  oriD <- tb
  sides <- match.arg(sides, c("nw_corners", "se_corners", "both_corners"))
  
  xyD <- rename(oriD, !!!list(x = monikers$xn, y = monikers$yn)) # working with x and y
  if (naremove) xyD %<>% filter(if_all(c(x, y), complete.cases))
  
  xyObj <- new_xyCorners(xyD,  sides = sides)
  xyNO <- xyD %>% select(!c(x, y))
  xyD <- bind_cols(xyObj, xyNO)
  
  if (is.null(br)) { # go for empirical box:
    br <- xyD %>% select(x,y) %>% 
      summarize(across(everything(), .fns = list(low = min, high = max), 
                       .names = "{.col}.{.fn}")) 
  } else { # use the given br as selector for the data
    xyD %<>% filter(between(x, br$x.low, br$x.high) & between(y, br$y.low, br$y.high))
  }
  
  
  
  

  
  
  
  
  res <- list(xyObj = xyObj, xyD = xyD, oriD = oriD, monikers = monikers, br = br)
  return(res)
} # findProngs

abCeil4Data <- function(xyP, ilk = "RG") {
  
  performLP <- function(xyP, weights = NULL, hallObjective = FALSE) {
    # xyP <- nwCorners ; weights <- NULL; hallObjective <- FALSE
    # note: a >= 0; so (possibly) need to shift data (for using lpSolve)
    # Weights = objective weights, possibly: weights <- NULL or weights <- "col"
    
    mini <- xyP %>% # minimums of inputs
      summarize(across(c(x,y), .fns = list(low = min), .names = "{.col}.{.fn}"))
    xyP %<>% mutate(x = x - mini$x.low, y = y - mini$y.low) # shifts
    numRows <- nrow(xyP)
    sway <- rep(1, numRows) # leads to equal weights
    if (!(is.null(weights))) { # so we are weighing the points
      sway <- xyP[, weights]   # the weights = data in column/mast
    }
    
    if (hallObjective){
      obj <- c(1, 1/2) # the Hall objective
    } else {
      obj <- c(sum(sway), sum(sway * xyP$x))   # "ordinary" objective coefficients 
    }
    leftside <- cbind(rep(1, numRows), xyP$x)  # lhs of constraints (maybe weighted)
    inEq <- rep(">=", numRows)                 # direction of constraint(s)
    rightside  <- xyP$y                        # rhs of constraints 
    
    LP_ <- lp("min", obj, leftside, inEq, rightside) # LP with shifted data inputs
    a_ <- LP_$solution[1];  b_ <- LP_$solution[2]    # coefficients wrt shifted data
    a <- a_ - b_ * mini$x.low + mini$y.low           # nb: a gets measured at x = 0!
    
    return(c(a = a, b = b_)) # line: y = a + b x, so raw ab
  } # performLP
  
  performRG <- function(xyP) { # do regression of y on x
    
    ab <- c(a = 0, b = 1) 
    if (nrow(xyP) > 1)  {
      LM <- lm(y ~ x, data = xyP)
      ab <- c(a = unname(LM$coefficients[1]), b = unname(LM$coefficients[2]))
    } else {print(glue_col("{red Single-point regression!? Diagonal: a = 0 and b = 1.}"))}
    
    return(ab)
  } # performRG
  
  if (ilk == "RG") {ab <- performRG(xyP)} else {ab <- performLP(xyP)}
  if (ab["b"] <= 0) { ab["b"] <- 10^{-12}} # no horizontal or downward slope
  
  return(ab) # raw ab
} # abCeil4Data

intersectLineBox <- function(ab, br = unitBox){ # crossings of box with line and es
  
  dx <- br$x.high - br$x.low; dy <- br$y.high - br$y.low
  a <- ab[1]; b <- ab[2]
  
  if (dx * dy <= 0) {
    print("Not a true box! Surface <= 0.")
    return(NULL)}
  if (b <= 0) {
    print("Need positive slope!")
    return(NULL)}
  
  miss <- FALSE # does the ab-line miss the box?
  # intersection y = a + b x with x = bb$x.low:
  wi <- c(br$x.low, a + b * br$x.low) # west intersection
  # intersection y = a + b x with y = bb$y.low:
  si <- c((br$y.low - a)/b, br$y.low) # south intersection
  if (wi[1] < si[1]) {swi <- si} else {swi <- wi} # pick most eastern point
  if (swi[1] > br$x.high) {miss <- TRUE; es <- 1} # box to north-west of line
  if (swi[2] > br$y.high) {miss <- TRUE; es <- 0} # box to south-east of line
  
  # intersection y = a + b x with x = bb$x.high: 
  ei <- c(br$x.high, a + b * br$x.high) # east intersection
  # intersection y = a + b x with y = bb$y.high:
  ni <- c((br$y.high - a)/b, br$y.high) # north intersection
  # pick most western point
  if (br$x.high > (br$y.high - a)/b) {nei <- ni} else {nei <- ei}
  if (nei[1] < br$x.low) {miss <- TRUE; es <- 0} # box to south-east of line
  if (nei[2] < br$y.low) {miss <- TRUE; es <- 1} # box (unreasonably) to north-west of line 
  
  if (!miss) {# regular case: no miss and two intersections swi and nei with box's boundary
    es <- (swi[1] - br$x.low) * dy
    es <- es + (1/2) * (nei[1] - swi[1]) * (2 * br$y.high - swi[2] - nei[2])
  }
  
  blis <- list(wi = wi, si = si, ei = ei, ni = ni) # "box-line" intersections
  
  return(list(es = es/(dx * dy), swi = swi, nei = nei, blis = blis, 
              inline = c(a = a, b = b), inbox = br))
}# intersectLineBox

doLinCeil <- function(xyP, br, ilk = "RG"){ 
  # determine line and bounding rectangle from xy points in xyP
  
  # xyP <- nwCorners; br <- prongs$br; ilk <- "RG"
  
  ab <- abCeil4Data(xyP, ilk = ilk) # we now have the raw values for a and b
  # BR <- summarize(xyP, across(c(x,y), .fns = list(low = min, high = max), 
  #                            .names = "{.col}.{.fn}"))
  iLB <- intersectLineBox(ab, br = br) # non-inflated
  # intersections: south-west (swi) and north-east (nei) intersections
  swi <- unname(iLB$swi) # on boundary of BB as both ab and BR derive from the same data
  nei <- unname(iLB$nei) # on boundary of BB as both ab and BR derive from the same data
  xyP <- tibble(x = c(swi[1], nei[1]), y = c(swi[2], nei[2]))
  xyP %<>% distinct()
  ceilings <- closing2BR(xyP, br)
  # Note. For received linear cases the extended ceiling gets traced out by 4 points.
  
  # add col/row information on the points in the (x)ceiling:
  ceil <- addColRow2xyPoints(ceilings$ceil) 
  ceil <- reduceX2Ceil(ceil, br)
  xceil <- addColRow2xyPoints(ceilings$xceil)
  
  deltay <- br$y.high - br$y.low
  a1 <- (ab["a"] - br$y.low + ab["b"] * br$x.low) / deltay
  b1 <- (br$x.high - br$x.low) * ab["b"] / deltay
  nrm <- c(a = a1, b = b1)
  
  return(ceilings = list(ceil = ceil, xceil = xceil, ab = list(raw = ab, nrm = nrm)))
} # doLinCeil

make2SidedWeb <- function(ceilings, enlargement = 0){ 
  #' compute on meshes formed by the extended ceiling's break-points
  #' Meshes (maze) form a partition of the bounding rectangle based on the breakpoints 
  #' in the extended ceiling.
  #' 
  #' ceilings <- stpceil; xceil <- ceilings$xceil; enlargement <- 10^-10
  #' ceilings <- myStpCeils
  
  do2SidedRaster <- function(xyCR) {# raster from xy-column-rows (later the meshes)
    # xyCR <- enlceil
    
    Mcol <- pull(xyCR, col) %>% max # note that minimum is 1
    Mrow <- pull(xyCR, row) %>% max # note that minimum is 1
    colx <- select(xyCR, col, x, strain) %>% rename(strainx = strain)
    distinct_col <- distinct(colx, col, .keep_all = TRUE)
    distinct_col %<>% mutate(cardDirEW = case_when(col == Mcol ~ "east",
                                                   col == 1 ~ "west",
                                                   .default = "inner"))
    rowy <- select(xyCR, row, y, strain) %>% rename(strainy = strain)
    distinct_row <- distinct(rowy, row, .keep_all = TRUE)
    distinct_row %<>% mutate(cardDirNS = case_when(row == Mrow ~ "north",
                                                   row == 1 ~ "south",
                                                   .default = "inner"))
    pnts <- expand_grid(distinct_col, distinct_row)
    
    mincol <- pnts %>% filter(col == 1) %>% rename_with(~paste0("m", .x))
    maxcol <- pnts %>% filter(col == max(col)) %>% rename_with(~paste0("M", .x))
    horiz <- left_join(mincol, maxcol, by = join_by(mrow == Mrow))
    
    minrow <- pnts %>% filter(row == 1) %>% rename_with(~paste0("m", .x))
    maxrow <- pnts %>% filter(row == Mrow) %>% rename_with(~paste0("M", .x))
    verti <- left_join(minrow, maxrow, by = join_by(mcol == Mcol))
    # Note. horiz and verti will only be used for producing a grid in plots.
    
    
    # Note. When applied to (x)ceiling the "..rangeby.." yield two consecutive points max.
    colrangebyrow <- xyCR %>% select(col, x, row) %>% group_by(row) %>% 
      summarize(across(everything(), .fns = list(low = min, high = max), 
                       .names = "{.col}.{.fn}")) 
    rowrangebycol <- xyCR %>% select(col, y, row) %>% group_by(col) %>% 
      summarize(across(everything(), .fns = list(low = min, high = max), 
                       .names = "{.col}.{.fn}"))
    
    pnts %<>% # Note. We could alternatively work with "rowrangebycolumn".
      mutate(hpos = case_when(between(col, colrangebyrow$col.low[row], 
                                      colrangebyrow$col.high[row]) ~ "on",
                              col < colrangebyrow$col.low[row] ~ "nw",
                              .default = "se"))
    # Note. hpos gives position of points with respect to ceiling (xyCR)
    
    return(list(pnts = pnts, 
                colrangebyrow = colrangebyrow, 
                rowrangebycol = rowrangebycol,
                horiz = horiz,
                verti = verti,
                Mcol = Mcol,
                Mrow = Mrow))
  } # do2SidedRaster
  
  #cat("Info (of make2SidedWeb): ", red("Enlargement"), " = ", enlargement, "\n") ###################################
  # ceil <- ceilings$ceil (our example case)
  xceil <- ceilings$xceil %>% mutate(strain = "regular") # pre-enlargement
  obr <- xceil %>%  # original, un-inflated ("pre-enlargement") bounding rectangle
    summarize(across(c(x, y), .fns = list(low = min, high = max) , 
                     .names = "{.col}.{.fn}")) 
  
  enlceil <- xceil  # start with pre-enlargement
  # if enlargement then add points to xceil to enlarge it and obtain enlceil
  enlarged <- FALSE
  if (enlargement > 0) {
    enlarged <- TRUE
    dx <- obr$x.high - obr$x.low
    dy <- obr$y.high - obr$y.low
    enlceil %<>% 
      add_row(x = obr$x.low - enlargement * dx / 2, 
              y = obr$y.low - enlargement * dy / 2, strain = "enlarged", .before = 1)
    enlceil %<>% 
      add_row(x = obr$x.high + enlargement * dx / 2, y = obr$y.high + enlargement * dy / 2,
              strain = "enlarged")
    # and redo column and row numbering:
    enlceil %<>% addColRow2xyPoints()
  }
  
  ceilings$enlceil <- enlceil
  ras <- do2SidedRaster(enlceil) # xy (and col-row) raster with info 
  rp <- ras$pnts # raster points
  
  rp %<>% arrange(col, row) 
  sw <- rp %>% slice(1)        # first row identifies the south-west of br
  ne <- rp %>% tail(1)         # last row identifies the north-east of br
  # bounding rectangle coordinates from xyCR (= xceil):
  br <- tibble(x.low = sw$x, y.low = sw$y, x.high = ne$x, y.high = ne$y) # enlarged!
  
  BRs <- list(obr = obr, br = br)
  
  # Out of the raster points we build the mesh (= partition by mazes = rectangles)
  # Meshes: raster points (x, y) are considered as south-east points in a mesh
  # below procedure may give at first some incomplete/degenerate/non-existing 
  # nw points/meshes which subsequently get removed
  rp %<>% rename(se_x = x, se_y = y, se_pos = hpos) 
  # Now compute the meshes with current points in rp considered as se-points.
  r1 <- rp %>% # A copy of rp used to find (potential) nw points of meshes.
    mutate(row_ = row - 1, col_ = col + 1) %>% 
    rename(nw_x = se_x, nw_y = se_y, nw_pos = se_pos)
  # Note. nw_pos 'inherits' thru se_pos which 'inherits' thru hpos
  mesh <- 
    dplyr::left_join(rp, r1, join_by(row == row_, col == col_), keep = NULL)
  
  # Remove "invalid" meshes - having corners or row-col with NA-values:
  mesh %<>% drop_na(c("row.y", "col.y", "col", "row", "nw_x")) 
  
  # fixing up remaining, valid, meshes, such as
  # using only the 4 ordinal directions (e, w, s, n) for the mesh: 
  maze <- rename(mesh, w_x = nw_x, w_col = col.y, e_x = se_x, e_col = col, 
                 s_y = se_y, s_row = row, n_y = nw_y, n_row = row.y)
  
  maze %<>% rowwise %>%
    mutate(onEdge = is.element("enlarged", c(strainx.x, strainx.y, strainy.x, strainy.y)))
  
  maze %<>% mutate(strainx.x = NULL, strainx.y = NULL, strainy.x = NULL, strainy.y = NULL)
  
  # Note. Points (e_x, s_y) are south and east lines in their maze (cell) 
  # and (w_x, n_y) are the north and west lines in that maze, and so on.
  # Note that we only track positions on the nw and se intersection points. 
  # Such is sufficient (for our purposes) as the ceiling runs sw-ne.
  # w_x ~ x.low; e_x ~ x.high; s_y ~y.low; n_y ~ y.high
  
  # some additional info on mazes for easy identification:
  maze %<>% ungroup %>%
    arrange(e_x, s_y) %>% 
    mutate(mazeID = paste0("maze_", row_number()), .before = 1)
  
  # position (stance), south-east or north-west, or bisected, of meshes wrt ceiling:
  maze %<>% 
    mutate(stance = case_when((nw_pos == "nw" & se_pos == "se") ~ "bisect",
                              (nw_pos == "on" | nw_pos == "se") ~ "se",
                              .default = "nw" ) )
  # Areas of meshes (with bi-sect counting for half):
  maze %<>% mutate(area = ifelse(stance == "bisect", 
                                 (e_x - w_x) * (n_y - s_y) / 2,
                                 (e_x - w_x) * (n_y - s_y)) )
  
  # Do away with irrelevant 0-area meshes:
  maze %<>% filter(area > 0) # probably redundant, but ... safety first
  
  # Unconditional areas to north-west (for each of the 4 corners of a mesh)
  # Note. The same point, when a corner in 4 meshes, gets calculated 4 times
  maze %<>% 
    mutate(se_NW_den = (e_x - br$x.low) * (br$y.high - s_y), 
           nw_NW_den = (w_x - br$x.low) * (br$y.high - n_y),
           sw_NW_den = (w_x - br$x.low) * (br$y.high - s_y),
           ne_NW_den = (e_x - br$x.low) * (br$y.high - n_y))
  # and total (unconditional) area to the south-east of each point in a mesh:
  maze %<>% 
    mutate(se_SE_den = (br$x.high - e_x) * (s_y - br$y.low), 
           nw_SE_den = (br$x.high - w_x) * (n_y - br$y.low),
           sw_SE_den = (br$x.high - w_x) * (s_y - br$y.low),
           ne_SE_den = (br$x.high - e_x) * (n_y - br$y.low))
  
  # Now for in-solidity numerators (num) for each of the 4 corners of a mesh:
  maze %<>% 
    mutate(se_NW_num = NA, nw_NW_num = NA, sw_NW_num = NA, ne_NW_num = NA)
  # Deviance areas to north-west (for each of the 4 corners of a mesh)
  # Deviance = from what is expected for a point on the ceiling 
  # (here the feasible area is to the NW of the point under consideration) 
  # Note: inefficiency of a point may get (re-)calculated up to 4 times
  for (i in (1: nrow(maze))) { # stance in "se, bisect": 100%/50% in feas. space
    # i <- 1
    cl <- maze$e_col[i]; rw <- maze$s_row[i] 
    maze$se_NW_num[i] <- 
      filter(maze, e_col <= cl, s_row >= rw, stance %in% c("bisect", "se")) %>%
      summarize(across(.cols = area, sum)) %>% .$area
    cl <- maze$w_col[i]; rw <- maze$n_row[i] 
    maze$nw_NW_num[i] <- 
      filter(maze, e_col <= cl, s_row >= rw, stance %in% c("bisect", "se")) %>%
      summarize(across(.cols = area, sum)) %>% .$area
    rw <- maze$s_row[i]; cl <- maze$w_col[i]
    maze$sw_NW_num[i] <- 
      filter(maze, e_col <= cl, s_row >= rw, stance %in% c("bisect", "se")) %>%
      summarize(across(.cols = area, sum)) %>% .$area
    rw <- maze$n_row[i]; cl <- maze$e_col[i]
    maze$ne_NW_num[i] <- 
      filter(maze, e_col <= cl, s_row >= rw, stance %in% c("bisect", "se")) %>%
      summarize(across(.cols = area, sum)) %>% .$area
  }
  
  # Now then for in-veracity numerators for each of the 4 corners of a maze:
  maze %<>% 
    mutate(se_SE_num = NA, nw_SE_num = NA, sw_SE_num = NA, ne_SE_num = NA)
  # Deviance areas to south-east (for each of the 4 corners of a maze)
  # Deviance from what is expected for a point on the ceiling 
  # (here the feasible area is to the SE) 
  # inefficient calculation: a point/value may get (re-)calculated up to 4 times
  for (i in (1: nrow(maze))) { # i <- 2
    rw <- maze$s_row[i]; cl <- maze$e_col[i]
    maze$se_SE_num[i] <- 
      filter(maze, w_col >= cl, n_row <= rw, stance %in% c("bisect", "nw")) %>%
      summarize(across(.cols = area, sum)) %>% .$area
    rw <- maze$n_row[i]; cl <- maze$w_col[i]
    maze$nw_SE_num[i] <-  
      filter(maze, w_col >= cl, n_row <= rw, stance %in% c("bisect", "nw")) %>%
      summarize(across(.cols = area, sum)) %>% .$area
    rw <- maze$s_row[i]; cl <- maze$w_col[i]
    maze$sw_SE_num[i] <- 
      filter(maze, w_col >= cl, n_row <= rw, stance %in% c("bisect", "nw")) %>%
      summarize(across(.cols = area, sum)) %>% .$area
    rw <- maze$n_row[i]; cl <- maze$e_col[i]
    # alternative: rw<- maze$ne_row[i]; cl <- maze$ne_col[i]
    maze$ne_SE_num[i] <- 
      filter(maze, w_col >= cl, n_row <= rw, stance %in% c("bisect", "nw")) %>%
      summarize(across(.cols = area, sum)) %>% .$area
  }
  
  # Finished with areas, now for the deviance calculations.
  # In-solidity and in-veracity ratios: circumvent division by zero.
  # As an in-solidity involves the north&west of the br, special care 
  # (to avoid cancellation/division by zero) is needed for meshes 
  # touching (osculating) the west- or north-side of the br
  maze %<>% # do meshes touch the bounding box in the north or west?
    mutate(nw_loc = 
             ifelse((w_x == br$x.low) & (n_y == br$y.high), "north-west",
                    case_when(w_x == br$x.low ~ "west",
                              n_y == br$y.high ~ "north",
                              .default = "other")))
  maze %<>% # do meshes touch the bounding box in the south or east?
    mutate(se_loc = 
             ifelse((e_x == br$x.high) & (s_y == br$y.low), "south-east",
                    case_when(e_x == br$x.high ~ "east",
                              s_y == br$y.low ~ "south",
                              .default = "other")))
  
  # vx: west-east x-value of up-going (going north(-east) ~ vertical) ceiling 
  # per mesh, id-ed thru south (se-sw) row: vx = x where the ceiling crosses to ne. 
  lookup_vx <- ras$colrangebyrow %>% 
    select(row, x.high, col.high) %>% rename(vx = x.high, vc = col.high)
  maze <- left_join(maze, lookup_vx, by = join_by(s_row == row), keep = NULL)
  # the point (s_row,vc) is where the ceiling crosses the line determined
  # by the south of the mesh towards the line determined by the north of the mesh,
  # in particular (se_row,vc) is on the ceiling.
  
  # we need the slope for the ceiling running from the south-line to the north-line of mesh
  # if the point (se_row,vc) is on the east of the bb the slope, vs, is infinite,
  # meaning the slope is vertical, otherwise (se_row,vc) is the sw-point of a mesh in which
  # the ceiling can be assumed to run for the south-line to the north-line.
  # Search for the mesh with sw-corner (vc, se_row = sw_row):
  lookup_maze <- 
    maze %>% select(w_col, w_x, s_row, s_y, e_col, e_x, n_row, n_y, stance) 
  
  xmaze <- 
    dplyr::left_join(maze, lookup_maze, suffix = c("", ".v"),
                     by = join_by(vc == w_col, s_row == s_row))
  
  xmaze %<>% 
    mutate(vslope = 
             case_when(stance.v == "bisect" ~ (n_y.v - s_y.v) / (e_x.v - w_x.v),
                       is.na(stance.v) ~ Inf,
                       .default = Inf))
  # cleaning up superfluous values (for efficiency):
  xmaze %<>% select(!ends_with(".v"))
  
  # Now, the analogous computation in the y-direction:
  # horizontal ceiling in south-north (hy) of maze:
  # Note. We will use south-west [] as (join_by) reference point.
  lookup_hy <- ras$rowrangebycol %>% 
    select(col, y.high, row.high) %>% rename(hy = y.high, hr = row.high)
  xmaze <- left_join(xmaze, lookup_hy, by = join_by(w_col == col), keep = NULL)
  
  xymaze <- dplyr::left_join(xmaze, lookup_maze, suffix = c("",".h"),
                             by = join_by(hr == s_row, w_col == w_col))
  xymaze %<>% 
    mutate(hslope = 
             case_when(stance.h == "bisect" ~ (n_y.h - s_y.h) / (e_x.h - w_x.h), 
                       is.na(stance.h) ~ 0,
                       .default = 0))
  # cleaning up superfluous values (for efficiency):
  xymaze %<>% select(!ends_with(".h"))
  
  # Note. Computing (in-)solidity: looking for feasible space to nw.
  # So, of special concern are meshes touching the bb on the north and/or west.
  # Note: when !(stance %in% c("bisect", "nw") it has to be "se".
  # So, fall-through cases stance %in% c("bisect", "nw") are "se" cases.
  xymaze %<>% # [special: mesh touching west or north of bb] cases of in-solidity: 
    mutate(sw_insol = 
             case_when(stance %in% c("bisect", "nw") ~ 0,
                       nw_loc == "west" ~ (hy - s_y)/(br$y.high - s_y),
                       .default = sw_NW_num/sw_NW_den),
           ne_insol = 
             case_when(stance %in% c("bisect", "nw") ~ 0,
                       nw_loc == "north" ~ (e_x - w_x)/(e_x - br$x.low),
                       .default = ne_NW_num/ne_NW_den),
           nw_insol = 
             case_when(((stance %in% c("bisect", "nw")) | (nw_NW_den == 0)) ~ 0,
                       .default = nw_NW_num/nw_NW_den),
           se_insol = se_NW_num/se_NW_den)
  # Note. Computing (in-)veracity: looking for empty space to se.
  # Falling thru stance %in% c("bisect", "se") one arrives at "nw"
  # Note there is at most 1 stance = "nw" mesh with mh = "south" or "east"
  xymaze %<>% # [special: mesh touching east or south of bb] cases of in-veracity:
    mutate(sw_inver = 
             case_when(stance %in% c("bisect", "se") ~ 0,
                       se_loc == "south" ~ (e_x - w_x)/(br$x.high - w_x),
                       .default = sw_SE_num/sw_SE_den),
           ne_inver = 
             case_when(stance %in% c("bisect", "se") ~ 0,
                       se_loc == "east" ~ (n_y - s_y)/(n_y - br$y.low),
                       .default = ne_SE_num/ne_SE_den),
           nw_inver = nw_SE_num/nw_SE_den,
           se_inver = 
             case_when(((stance %in% c("bisect", "se")) | (se_SE_den == 0)) ~ 0,
                       .default = se_SE_num/se_SE_den))
  
  xymaze %<>% rowwise() %>% 
    mutate(se_inacc = max(se_insol, se_inver), 
           sw_inacc = max(sw_insol, sw_inver),
           nw_inacc = max(nw_insol, nw_inver),
           ne_inacc = max(ne_insol, ne_inver)) %>% ungroup()
  
  doInsolInverLookUp <- function(rsp, maz) {# rsp <- ras$pnts; maz <- xymaze
    
    rsp %<>% rowwise %>%
      mutate(insolver 
             = case_when(
               ((cardDirEW != "east")  & (cardDirNS != "north")) ~
                 list(filter(maz, w_x == x, s_y == y) %>% select(sw_insol, sw_inver)),
               ((cardDirEW != "east") & cardDirNS == "north") ~
                 list(filter(maz, w_x == x, n_y == y) %>% select(nw_insol, nw_inver)),
               ((cardDirEW == "east") & (cardDirNS != "south")) ~
                 list(filter(maz, e_x == x, n_y == y) %>% select(ne_insol, ne_inver)),
               ((cardDirEW == "east") & (cardDirNS == "south")) ~ 
                 list(filter(maz, e_x == x, s_y == y) %>% select(se_insol, se_inver)),
               .default = list(c(NA,NA))
             ))
    
    rsp %<>%  # split insolver into insol and inver:
      mutate(insol = insolver[[1]], inver = insolver[[2]]) %>% # loose insolver
      mutate(insolver = NULL)
    
    return(rsp)
  } # doInsolInverLookUp
  
  ras$pnts %<>% doInsolInverLookUp(xymaze)  
  
  # effect size:
  se_maze <- filter(xymaze, s_row == min(s_row), e_col == max(e_col))
  nces <- 1 -  se_maze$se_insol
  
  dossier <- list(message = "", portrayal = "")
  
  if (nrow(xymaze) == 1) { 
    if (xymaze$stance != "bisect") { # ceiling fully in bb boundary
      if (xymaze$stance == "nw") {
        TM1 <- "\n Single mesh: Empty space goes to south-east of bounding rectangle! \n"
        TM2 <- "There is no feasible space: in-veracity equals 1 (except on ceiling). \n"
        TM3 <- "Any iso-(in-)veracity-line will be meaningless! \n \n"
        dossier$message <- red(paste0(TM1, TM2, TM3))
        dossier$portrayal <- "no_feasible_space"
      } else { # xymaze$stance == "se"
        TM1 <- "\n Single mesh: Feasible point at the north-west of bounding rectangle! \n"
        TM2 <- "There is no empty space and in-solidity equals 1 (except on ceiling). \n" 
        TM3 <- "Any iso-(in-)solidity-line will be meaningless! \n \n"
        dossier$message <- red(paste0(TM1, TM2, TM3))
        dossier$portrayal <- "no_empty_space"
      }
    }
  }
  
  # extra detail: filter mazes to se of ceiling (for solidity calculations)
  # Do we have a full (in contrast to bisected)  sw and/or  ne squares?
  xymaze %<>% mutate(nesw_sqr = case_when(
    (w_x == br$x.low) &  (s_y == br$y.low) & (!stance == "bisect") ~ "sw_sqr",
    (e_x == br$x.high) & (n_y == br$y.high) & (!stance == "bisect") ~  "ne_sqr",
    .default = "other"
  ))
  
  return(list(ceilings = ceilings, raster = ras, BRs = BRs, enlarged = enlarged,
              maze = xymaze, nces = nces, dossier = dossier))
} # make2SidedWeb

# helper function: conversion from standard (net) to raw inaccuracy.
nciNorm2Raw <- function(niacc, d, solidity = TRUE) { 
  # niacc = normalized in-accuracy (in-solidity or in-veracity) measure
  # d = effect size; solidity = TRUE: solidity transformation
  
  nacc <- 1 - niacc # is nacc is some (solidity/veracity) normalized measure
  if (solidity) {
    raw <- tibble(rsol = (1 - d) * nacc + d, # raw solidity
                  risol = 1 - rsol )         # raw in-solidity = (1-d)*(1-nacc)
  } else {
    raw = tibble(rver = d * nacc + (1 - d), # raw veracity
                 river = 1 - rver  )        # raw in-veracity
  }
  return(raw)
} # nciNorm2Raw 

nciRaw2Norm <- function(riacc, d, solidity = TRUE) { 
  # riacc = raw in-accuracy (in-solidity or in-veracity) measure
  # d = effect size; solidity = TRUE: solidity transformation
  
  racc <- 1 - riacc # racc is some (solidity/veracity) raw measure
  if (solidity) {
    nrm <- tibble(nsol = (racc - d)/(1 - d), # nrm solidity 
                  nisol = 1 - nsol )         # nrm in-solidity = (1-racc)/(1-d)
  } else {
    nrm = tibble(nver = (racc - (1 - d))/d, # nrm veracity
                 niver = 1 - nver  )        # nrm in-veracity
  }
  return(nrm)
} # nciRaw2Norm 


# ---- Spread Index (helper) ----
spread_index <- function(x, xmin, xmax) {
  stopifnot(is.numeric(x))
  if (!is.finite(xmin) || !is.finite(xmax) || xmax <= xmin) {
    stop("xmax must be > xmin, both finite.")
  }
  x <- x[is.finite(x)]
  if (length(x) == 0L) return(NA_real_)
  z <- sort((x - xmin) / (xmax - xmin))
  z <- pmin(pmax(z, 0), 1)
  gaps <- diff(c(0, z, 1))
  mean_gap <- mean(gaps)
  if (mean_gap == 0) return(0)
  cv <- stats::sd(gaps) / mean_gap
  1 / (1 + cv)
}#spread






makeIsoCurve <- function(wb, rina, nsam = 100, facet = "se") {
  # rina is a net (normalized) value! ; rina = .2; nsam = 100; facet = "se"
  
  # helper functions: roots and samples (from conic sections)
  iBegFin <- function(x, y, vx, hy, sv, sh, t0, s0, JJ, xx, yy, ex, ny, 
                      bf = "beg", low = TRUE) {
    
    cs_solve4x <- function(a, n, b, h, k, c, valy){ # conic section: x given (val)y
      # Ayoub B. Ayoub. The Central Conic Sections Revisited.
      # ax^2 + (2ny + 2h)x + by^2 + 2ky + c = 0
      # valy = (given) y value ; valy <- 0; valy <- height
      
      solve2 <- function(p, q, r) {
        # p x^2 + q x + r = 0; Note. only after real soloutions
        # solving for x: only applied in cases with 1-2 real solution(s)
        roots <- c(x1 = NA, x2 = NA)
        D <- q^2 - 4 * p * r
        if (p != 0) {
          if (D >= 0) {
            roots <- c(x1 = (- q - sqrt(D))/ (2 * p), 
                       x2 = (- q + sqrt(D))/ (2 * p) )
          }
        } else { # p == 0
          if (q != 0) {roots <- c(x1 = -r/q, x2 = -r/q)} 
        }
        return(roots)
      } # solve2
      
      # p x^2 + q x + r = 0
      p <- a
      q <- 2 * (n * valy + h)
      r <- b * valy^2 + 2 * k * valy + c
      roots <- solve2(p, q, r)
      return(roots)
    } # cs_solve4x: solve conic section for x given valy
    
    # Warning. Use only when knowing that the box is being tapped, so that
    # there is a swi-crossing and a nei-crossing solution.
    # Note. We are not interested in the y-coordinates of crossings.
    delx <- x - vx; Delx <- x - xx; dely <- hy - y; Dely <- yy - y
    a = sh/2
    n = - (1-JJ)/2
    b = (1/sv) / 2 # JJ <- 0.05
    h = (dely - JJ * Dely)/2 
    k = -(delx - JJ * Delx)/2
    c = t0 - JJ * s0 
    width <- ex - x    # width of this (current) box/mesh
    height <- ny - y
    
    # solve for south-west crossing
    if (bf == "beg") {
      rt <- cs_solve4x(a, n, b, h, k, c, 0) # valy = 0
      if (is.na(rt["x1"])) {
        dx <- 0
      } else { 
        rx <- ifelse(low, min(rt["x1"], rt["x2"]), max(rt["x1"], rt["x2"]))
        dx <- ifelse(between(rx, 0, width), rx, NA) # in our application, NA never happens
      }
      crossing <- c(dx = dx, dy = NA)
    } else { # bf = "final"
      # solve for north-east crossing
      rt <- cs_solve4x(a, n, b, h, k, c, height) # height = ny-y is the height of the box
      if (is.na(rt["x1"])) {
        dx <- ex - x
      } else {
        rx <- ifelse(low, min(rt["x1"], rt["x2"]), max(rt["x1"], rt["x2"]))
        dx <- ifelse(between(rx, 0, width), rx, ex - x)
      }
      crossing <- c(dx = dx, dy = NA)
    } 
    return(crossing)
  } # iBegFin
  
  ibfNESW_sol <- function(x, y, vx, hy, sv, sh, JJ, xx, yy, ex, ny, bx, bf) {
    # Find intersections swi and nei for ne/sw boxes/meshes under solidity.
    
    Delx = x - xx ; Dely = yy - y ;dely = hy - y; delx = x - vx;  
    if (bf == "beg") { # xing = swi
      if (bx == "ne_sqr") {# north: dy = hy - y
        # Fun: dy =  2 * s *( 1- JJ) * dx + Dely - 2 * s * JJ * Delx
        # dx = (dy - Dely) / (2 * s (1-JJ)) + (JJ * Delx)/ (1-JJ)
        s <- sv # limit case: s -> infty
        if (is.infinite(s)) {
          xing <- c(dx = max(0, JJ * Delx / (1-JJ)), dy = 0)
        } else {
          xing <- c(dx = max(0, - Dely / (2 * s * (1-JJ)) + JJ * Delx / (1-JJ)),
                    dy = max(0, Dely - 2 * s * JJ * Delx))
        }
      } else { # bx = "sw_sqr"
        # Fun: dy =   (s * dx/2 + (dely - JJ * Dely)) / (1- JJ)
        # dx = 2 * ((1 - JJ) * dy - (dely - JJ * Dely))) / s
        s <- sh # limit case s -> 0
        if (s == 0) {
          xing <- c(dx = 0, dy = max(0, (dely - JJ * Dely)/ (1-JJ)))
        } else {
          xing <- c(dx = max(0, 2 * (JJ * Dely - dely) / s),
