<!-- - which scores to used (unnormalized) -->



<!-- ADAPT THIS TEXT" -->
<!-- PLS SEM Guidelines for Reviewers -->
<!-- In this review I have focused on the application of NCA within the PLS-SEM framework. I like the paper’s topic and it use of classic theories for explaining behavior, in particular because classic theories are often formulated (or can be interpreted) as necessity theories. Necessity has been sidelined by complex regression- based models, made feasible by increasing computational power, which allows handling complexity—possibly at the expense of insight. NCA results can add clarity, simplicity and interpretability, if properly applied.  -->
<!-- I have the following main remarks and suggestions (I  will leave minor remarks for a possible next round of review).   -->
<!-- 1. Explain the type of sufficiency causality used in PLS-SEM as “probabilistic sufficiency” -->
<!-- The paper uses the term sufficiency for the standard PLS-SEM analysis and the term necessity for the analysis with NCA. However, the correct term for the PLS-SEM analysis is “probabilistic sufficiency for the following reasons. -->
<!-- When PLS-SEM is combined with NCA, two different types of causality are used, and this should be carefully noted. In PLS-SEM and other regression-based methods, the common causal interpretation (of regression or path coefficients) is ‘if X then probably Y ‘. Here a single X is considered a probabilistic cause that produces an “average effect” on Y, ceteris paribus. This type of causality can be  called ‘probabilistic sufficiency”, but not just ‘sufficiency’.  A ‘sufficient cause’ (if X then (always) Y’) is a deterministic cause that (nearly) always produces the outcome. The term ‘sufficiency’ is commonly used for this type of causality in for example mathematics, philosophy and for methods like Qualitative Comparative Analysis, QCA (that considers a combination of Xs as being sufficient for Y ). The deterministic term ‘sufficiency’  relates directly to ‘necessity’, which is another deterministic type of causality (if not X than (nearly) always not Y). Common phrasing in the conditional logic and causality literature such as  “necessary but not sufficient, or “necessary and sufficient” (also used in this paper) refer to the deterministic meaning of the two, and such phrasings become confusing at best when one is deterministic and the other one is probabilistic.   -->
<!-- Therefore, when the paper talks about ‘sufficiency’ of the structural relationships in the PLS-SEM model, this should be phrased as “probabilistic sufficiency”, to differentiate it from the common interpretation of ‘sufficiency’ as deterministic sufficiency. When the structural relationships of the PLS-SEM model are analyzed from the perspective of deterministic necessity (with NCA), the name ‘necessity’ is fine because the common interpretation of ‘necessity’ is deterministic necessity, and that is what the paper does and intends to do. Details about the different types of causality can be found in: Dul, J. (2024). A different causal perspective with Necessary Condition Analysis. Journal of Business Research, 114618. -->

<!-- 2. Formulate and justify the necessity hypothesis -->
<!-- It is common practice to formulate hypotheses for the structural part of the PLS-SEM model by formulating hypotheses for each structural relationship. Usually, In this paper the empirical literature is used to justify the hypotheses of the structural model. This literature (implicitly) employs the ‘probabilistic sufficiency perspective (e.g., when regression-based analyses are employed). This means that the “probabilistic sufficiency” hypothesis of the structural relations can be properly justified by referring to this literature.  However, for justifying the necessity relationships of the relationships in the structural model this literature has serious limitations. The presence of a probabilistic relationship or average effect is not conclusive or informative about necessity. It is possible that a necessity relationship exists when the literature claims and justifies that there is a probabilistic sufficiency relationship, or that no necessity relationship exists when the literature claims and justifies that there is no probabilistic sufficiency relationship. It is possible that no necessity relationship exists when the literature claims and justifies a probabilistic sufficiency relationship, and that a necessity relationship exists when the literature does not claim and justify a probabilistic sufficiency relationship. Probabilistic sufficiency and necessity are different causal lenses that may act independently (see also comment 1). This means that it is not enough to say: “Since we know that there is a probabilistic sufficiency relationship (an average effect), we now claim and test the necessity of the relationship”, or similar  […]).  -->
<!-- This means that for a single structural relation in a PLS-SEM model two separate hypotheses need to be formulated: the standard probabilistic sufficiency hypothesis and a necessity hypothesis.  Furthermore, each hypotheses of the pair must be justified differently. Since each of the 10 relationships in the structural model of Figure 3 is analyzed both from the PLS-SEM and the NCA perspective, there are actually 20 hypotheses that are tested. Each hypothesis must be formulated and justified separately. -->
<!-- A necessity hypothesis and justification can be obtained by a combination of two approaches. Forst it may be useful to do thought experiments and logical reasoning to evaluate WHY (a certain level o)f X must be present to have (a certain level of) the outcome, and/or WHY the absence of that X is a bottleneck that will always produce the absence of Y, and/or WHY the absence of X cannot be compensated by the presence of another determinant. Particularly it could be evaluated mentally whether (1) cases exist with the outcome but without the condition, (2) cases exist without the condition but with the outcome, and (3) cases without the condition can have the outcome with a substitute condition. If these cases exist, the relationship may not be necessary, or only necessary in a specific theoretical domain (boundary condition of the hypothesis). Second, it may be useful to review causal claims in the literature (that are usually done for justifying a probabilistic sufficiency relationship) whether explicit or implicit necessity claims are part of it. These causal claims can be found for example in theory development or discussion sections of journal articles. Not seldom explicit necessity claims are made there, even if the causal theory and related methods are based on probabilistic sufficiency. Necessity claims come with different wordings. Words like needed, required, critical, essential, indispensable, etc. refer to the need for the presence of the necessary condition for having the outcome (there must be X to have Y), and words like bottleneck, constraint, block, etc. refer to absence of the outcome when the necessary condition is absent (no Y without X). It may be useful to go back to the writings of Rogers, Ajzen and Cialdini and of later scholars who interpreted their work to see if they use implicit necessity logic when describing relations.  Recently NCA has been applied or suggested as to accommodate the necessity interpretation of the TPB model (see for example Frommeyer, B., Wagner, E., Hossiep, C. R., and Schewe, G. (2022). The utility of intention as a proxy for sustainable buying behavior–a necessary condition analysis. Journal of Business Research, 143:201–213 or Rozenkowska, K. (2023). Theory of planned behavior in consumer behavior research: A systematic literature review. International Journal of Consumer Studies). These publications also may help to give a  theoretical justifications for a necessity relationship. Note that NCA does not accept a condition as being necessary if such theoretical rationale for necessity is missing, both in theory testing research and in explorative research. -->

<!-- 3. Conduct and report NCA according to the guidelines for good practice. -->
<!-- The paper claims to have followed guidelines for NCA but it is still far from following the recent guidelines of good NCA practice as proposed in: Dul, J., Hauff S., & Bouncken, R.B. (2023). Necessary condition analysis (NCA): Review of topics in recent research and suggestions for good practice. Review of Managerial Science, 17, 683–714.  -->
<!-- Apart from theoretical justification (see comment 2) three other main guidelines are: -->
<!-- -	Show the scatter plot of all conditions that are evaluated for necessity, thus not just the for the conditions where necessity was supported, but also for conditions were necessity was not supported.  One reason is that this allows the reader for a visual inspection about what is the best ceiling line and about the possible presence of outliers that could explain the (non)results.  -->
<!-- -	Select the ceiling(s) based on the number of levels of X and Y and the expected or visually observed (non-)linearity of the border.  Only show the selected ceiling lines in the scatter plot. Do not show the two (default) ceiling lines if these are not selected for the analysis. Often, as in this article, the CE-FDH ceiling line is selected in PLS-SEM studies without justification, possibly because this line was selected (for good reasons) in the first study that applied NCA with PLS-SEM, but this reason may not apply to the study at hand. -->
<!-- -	Present the bottleneck table for the non-rejected necessary conditions.  Decide how to present the bottleneck table (e.g., percentage of range, actual values, percentiles). Often the default (percentage of range) is given but the used of actual values or percentiles is more informative. For example, the use of percentiles helps to set priorities at group level for formatting practical recommendations.  -->
<!-- I suggest to implement these NCA guidelines carefully, or give explicit reasons in the paper when a guideline is not followed. -->

<!-- 4. Conduct a robustness check for NCA -->
<!-- The robustness of the NCA results are unclear. Any analyst must make several choices for conducting NCA. Two important choices are the selection of the ceiling line and the inclusion or exclusion of potential outliers (you could evaluate potential outliers with the R package for NCA). NCA’s robustness check verifies if different choices will result in important differences in results and the conclusion about the hypothesis. If not, the results are robust, if yes, the results are fragile. Both outcomes are acceptable as long as there is transparency and conclusions based on fragile outcomes are presented reluctantly.  Details about robustness checks for NCA can be found in: Dul, J. (2021). Advances in Necessary Condition Analysis. online book. -->

<!-- 5. Discuss the practical consequences of NCA findings -->
<!-- The discussion section on the practical contribution is rather limited and could be extended. -->
<!-- One of the advantages of combining PLS-SEM with NCA is that NCA results are directly practically relevant. Whereas PLS-SEM results can inform practice about average effects for a group of cases (if X is implemented in the group, the group average Y will change, see the IPMA approach), and NCA results when applied to groups can also direct the attention to the group with the largest number of cases that do not have the necessary condition in place (see the recent cIPMA approach), NCA results can also inform practice that the necessary condition(s) must be present at the right level in each single case, to make a desired outcome possible, thus direction attention to individual cases that did not satisfy the necessary condition.  For making the latent construct scores better interpretable (the values are directly connected to the anchor points of the (Likert) scales), unstandardized latent construct are preferred for the NCA analysis of the structural relationships.  To make the PLS-SEM results comparable to the NCA results this could also apply to the PLS-SEM analysis. -->
<!-- I hope that you consider these comments as useful. I realize that in particular handling the theoretical justification of the necessity hypotheses may be challenging, but this is needed for a proper theoretical contribution with NCA in a high ranked journal. I wish you good luck with improving the paper.  -->



<!-- I am generally satisfied with the paper’s changes but still see some important issues. -->
<!-- I appreciate that the theoretical underpinning of the necessity relationship is improved. It is good news (and I was not surprised about it) that the founders of the original theories used in this paper indeed refer implicitly and explicitly to necessity causal logic, which gives the current paper a stronger rationale and theoretical justification for formulating necessity hypotheses and for conducting NCA. -->
<!-- However, I am not so satisfied with the NCA robustness check, which I suggested to do in my original comment 4. The robustness check is a check of the conclusion that the authors derive, in this case whether a condition is necessary or not. The check challenges the conclusion that a condition is supported AND challenges the conclusion that a conclusion is not supported in several ways, and I suggested to do at least: -->
<!-- 1.	using another choice of a (also possibly relevant!) ceiling line (in this case use CR-FDH if the main analysis to derive to the conclusion was done with CE-FDH). -->
<!-- 2.	by doing an outlier analysis and making a choice about them.  -->
<!-- The first problem is that the paper makes the wrong interpretation about the goal if the robustness check. The authors let the rustiness check drive the conclusion, rather than being a check of the conclusion. The conclusion about necessity is made by using three criteria (theoretical support, effect size and $p$-value). AFTER this conclusion is made a robustness check should be done to verify if the conclusion is robust or fragile. So, a conclusion that a necessary condition is supported can be robust or fragile, and a conclusion that a necessary condition is not supported can be robust or fragile. The authors seem to mis-interpreted a “fragile result” as a results where the condition is not supported, and similarly a “robust result” as a result where the condition is supported.   -->
<!-- The second problem is that the paper does not do an outlier analysis, which is part of NCA robustness check (see the reference in my original comment 4). It seems that the results are (partly) driven by outliers. The outlier analysis can be done with the function nca_outliers of the NCA R package. The absence of this function in the SmartPLS package is not a reason not to do an outlier analysis. -->
<!-- Related to this issue, the scatter plots of all conditions that are evaluated for necessity must be presented as I suggested in the original comment 3. Thus, not just the for the conditions where necessity was supported, but also for conditions where necessity was not supported.  One reason is that this allows the reader for a visual inspection about what is the best (alternative) ceiling line and about the possible presence of outliers that could explain the (non)results.  -->


## NCA with QCA {#ncaqca}
<!-- see: [NCA website](https://www.erim.eur.nl/necessary-condition-analysis/about-nca/faq/nca-and-other-data-analysis-methods/nca-and-qca/). -->

Qualitative Comparative Analysis (QCA) has its roots in political science and sociology, and
was developed by Charles Ragin [@ragin1987comparative;@ragin2000fuzzy;@ragin2009redesigning]. QCA has steadily evolved over the years, and currently many types of QCA approaches exist. A common interpretation of QCA as described by @schneider2012set and @mello2021qualitative is followed in this book.

### Principles of QCA
Set theory is at the core of QCA. Instead of studying relations between variables, set theory focuses on relations between sets. Cases can be either part of a set or not part of a set. The Netherlands, for example, is a case that is ‘in the set’ of rich countries, and Ethiopia is a case that is ‘out of the set’ of rich countries. Set membership scores (rather than variable scores) are linked to a case. Regarding the set of rich countries, the Netherlands has a set membership score of 1 and Ethiopia of 0. In the original version of QCA the set membership scores could only be 0 or 1. This version of QCA is called crisp-set QCA (csQCA). Later also fuzzy-set QCA (fsQCA) was developed. Here the membership scores can also have values between 0 and 1. For example, Croatia could be allocated a set membership score of 0.7, indicating that it is ‘more in the set’ than ‘out of the set’ of rich countries.

In QCA, relations between sets are studied. Suppose that one set is the set of rich countries ($X$), and another set is the set of countries with happy people (‘happy countries’, $Y$). QCA uses Boolean (binary) algebra and expresses the relationship between condition $X$ and outcome $Y$: the presence or absence of $X$ is related to the presence or absence of $Y$. More specifically, the relations are expressed in terms of sufficiency and necessity. For example, the presence of $X$ (being a country that is part of the set of rich countries) could be theoretically stated as *sufficient* for the presence of $Y$ (being a country that is part of the set of happy countries). In this case, the following claims would apply: All rich countries are happy countries; the set of rich countries is a subset of the set of happy countries; no rich country is not a happy country; set $X$ is a subset of set $Y$. Alternatively, another theory could state that the presence of $X$ (being country that is part of the set of rich countries) is *necessary* for the presence of $Y$ (being a country that is part of the set of happy countries). All happy countries are rich countries; the set of rich countries is a superset of the set of happy countries; no happy country is not a rich country; set $X$ is a superset of set $Y$.

QCA’s main causal interest is sufficiency. QCA assumes that a configuration of single conditions produces the outcome. For example, the condition of being in the set of rich countries ($X_1$) AND the condition of being in the set of democratic countries ($X_2$) is sufficient for the outcome of being in the set of happy countries ($Y$). QCA’s Boolean logic statements for this sufficiency relationship is expressed as follows:

\begin{equation} 
(\#eq:qca1)
X_1*X_2 → Y
\end{equation}

where the symbol ‘$*$’ means the logical ‘AND’, and the symbol ‘$→$’ means ‘is sufficient for’.

Furthermore, QCA assumes that several alternative configurations may exist that can produce the outcome, known as ‘equifinality’. This is expressed in the following example:

\begin{equation} 
(\#eq:qca2)
X_1*X_2 + X_2*X_3*X_4 → Y
\end{equation}

where the symbol ‘$+$’ means the logical ‘OR’. 

It is also possible that the absence of a condition is part of a configuration. This is shown in the following example:

\begin{equation} 
(\#eq:qca3)
X_1*X_2 + X_2*{\neg}X_3*X_4 → Y
\end{equation}

where the symbol ‘$\neg$’ means ‘absence of’. 

Single conditions that are necessary/non-redundant in a configuration that is sufficient for the outcome are called INUS conditions [@mackie1965causes]. An INUS condition is an ‘Insufficient but Non-redundant (i.e., Necessary) part of an Unnecessary but Sufficient condition.’ In this expression, the words ‘part’ and ‘condition’ are somewhat confusing because ‘part’ refers to the single condition and ‘condition’ refers to the configuration that consists of single conditions (parts). Insufficient refers to the fact that a part (single condition) is not itself sufficient for the outcome. Non-redundant refers to the necessity of the part (single condition) for the configurations being sufficient for the outcome. Unnecessary refers to the possibility that other configurations can also be sufficient for the outcome. Sufficient refers to the fact that the configuration is sufficient for the outcome. 

Although a single condition may be locally necessary for the configuration to be sufficient to produce the outcome, it is not globally necessary for the outcome because the single condition may be absent in another sufficient configuration. INUS conditions are thus usually not necessary conditions for the outcome (the latter are the conditions that NCA considers). Hence, in above generic logical statements about relations between sets, see Equations \@ref(eq:qca1), \@ref(eq:qca2), \@ref(eq:qca3), $X$ and $Y$ can only be absent or present (Boolean algebra), even though the individual members of the sets can have fuzzy scores. Both csQCA and fsQCA use only binary levels (absent or present) of the condition and the outcome when formulating the solution. 

The starting point of QCA's data analysis is to transform variable scores (if available) into set membership scores. Variable scores are called "raw" scores and the transformation process is called ‘calibration’. Calibration can be based on the distribution of the data, the measurement scale, or expert knowledge. The goal of calibration is to get scores of 0 or 1 (csQCA) or between 0 and 1 (fsQCA) to represent the extent to which the case belongs to the set (set membership score). In fsQCA, cases with membership score below 0.5 do not count as having the condition present and contribute to the assessment of absence of the condition. Similarly, cases with membership score above 0.5 qualify as having the condition present. 

As a qualitative method, QCA's calibration process is preferably guided by knowledge about the studied cases. However, particularly in large-n fsQCA studies a 'mechanistic' (data driven) transformation is often unavoidable due to lack of access to the cases. For this transformation the non-linear logistic function is usually selected. This selection is somewhat arbitrary (but is built into popular QCA software) and moves the variable scores to the extremes (0 and 1) in comparison to just (linear) transformation (e.g., min-max normalization) of the data. With the standard logistic transformation, low values move towards 0 and high values towards 1. When no substantive reason exists for the logistic transformation, and mechanistic transformation is unavoidable, linear transformation from raw scores to set membership scores (normalization) may be preferred to avoid changing the distribution of the data and obscure possible necessary conditions [@dul2016orm]. Moving the $X$- and $Y$-scores to the extremes implies that cases in the $XY$-plot with low to middle values of $X$ move to the left and cases with middle to high values of $Y$ move upwards. As a result, the upper-left corner is filled with more cases. Consequently, potential meaningful empty spaces in the original data (indicating necessity) may not be identifiable. With the normalized transformation, the cases stay where they are; an empty space in a corner of the $XY$-plot with the original data stays empty. The normalized transformation is an alternative to an arbitrary transformation: it just changes variable score into set membership scores, without affecting the distribution of the data. A calibration evaluation tool to check the effect of calibration on the necessity effect size is available at https://r.erim.eur.nl/r-apps/qca/. 

QCA performs two separate analyses with calibrated data: a necessity analysis for identifying necessary conditions^[In QCA both potential single necessary condition and potential substitutable necessary condition (OR combinations of conditions) can be identified, although it is often difficult to theoretically justify an OR-combination, see Section \@ref(comparisonncaqca).], and a sufficiency analysis (‘truth table’ analysis) for identifying sufficient configurations. Although the focus in QCA is on sufficiency, a necessity analysis precedes the sufficiency analysis because if a necessary condition is not part of the sufficient configuration, the outcome will not be produced: 

> “If a causal condition passes the analyst’s test of necessity, then this condition should be made a component of every causal expression that the analyst examines subsequently in the analysis of sufficiency." [@ragin2000fuzzy, p. 254]

In csQCA, $X$ and $Y$ are expressed as dichotomous set membership scores: present (in the set) or absent (not in the set). In the $XY$-table, a necessary condition in kind is identified when the upper-left cell is (almost) empty  (Figure \@ref(fig:qcanca1)). Necessity consistency is the number of cases in the upper-right cell divided by the number of cases in the upper-left plus upper-right cell.  


```{r qcanca1, echo=FALSE, out.width='45%', fig.align = "center", fig.cap="Necessity analysis by dichotomous NCA and crisp set QCA.", cache=TRUE}
knitr::include_graphics('Figure 1.png')
```


In fsQCA, $X$ and $Y$ are expressed as discrete or continuous set membership scores indicating the extent to which the condition or outcome is in the set. In the $XY$-plot, a diagonal is drawn and a  necessary condition in kind is identified when the area above the diagonal is (almost) empty  (Figure \@ref(fig:qcanca2)-left). Necessity **in**consistency is the sum of vertical distances of cases above the diagonal, divided by the sum of vertical distances of all cases. Necessity consistency is the  complement of this value (1 - inconsistency).


<!-- (ref:caption3) Comparison of necessity analysis with fsQCA’s (Left) and with NCA (Right). The ceiling in QCA is predefined (diagonal) and in NCA estimated (with CE-FDH). Data from @rohlfing2013improving; see also @vis2018analyzing.  -->
```{r qcanca2, echo=FALSE, out.width='100%', fig.align = "center", fig.cap="Comparison of necessity analysis with fsQCA’s (Left) and with NCA (Right). The ceiling in QCA is predefined (diagonal) and in NCA estimated (with CE-FDH). Data from @rohlfing2013improving; see also @vis2018analyzing. ", cache=TRUE}
knitr::include_graphics('Figure 2AB.png')
#knitr::include_graphics('Figure 2AB BWcool.png')
```


The main analysis of csQCA and fsQCA is the sufficiency analysis. Whereas QCA's necessity analysis uses linear algebra to identify necessity, QCA's sufficiency analysis uses Boolean algebra and truth tables. The truth table is a logical summary of all ($2^k$) possible combinations of $k$ conditions ($X$'s) that are selected for the analysis. A combination of conditions is referred to as a configuration. In configurations, conditions can only be considered present or absent (with the 0.5 membership score threshold point used in fsQCA; absent: < 0.5; present: > 0.5). The purpose of QCA is to identify which of the possible configurations in the truth table are actually observed in the data, and to establish whether the outcome is present or absent in the observed configurations. This leads to QCA’s *solution table* consisting of configurations that are considered to be able to produce the outcome ($Y$ = 1). A second solution table consists of configurations that are considered to not produce the outcome ($Y$ = 0).

Over the years, different approaches have been suggested to integrate an observed necessary condition from QCA's necessity analysis (for $Y$ = 1 and $Y$ = 0, respectively) into the sufficiency solution. For example, Ragin has suggested to *add* the necessary condition if it is absent in an observed configuration, to *remove* the necessary condition from all configurations, and to *ignore* necessity.^[Ragin 2000, (p. 254) "If several conditions pass the test of necessity, they are all *inserted* into the causal expressions that are subsequently tested for sufficiency.” Ragin (2009, p. 110): “It is often useful to check for necessary conditions before conducting the fuzzy truth table procedure. Any condition that passes the test and that “makes sense” as a necessary condition can be *dropped* from the truth table procedure, which, after all, is essentially an analysis of sufficiency." [@MendelKorjani2010, p. 83.] quote Ragin’s view on this approach as follows: “That being said (*ignore* necessity), there is still a lot of interest in necessary conditions in the social sciences." (emphasis added).] It appears that the necessity analysis of QCA is often ignored and if this analysis is done, different approaches are used  for integration of the necessity analysis with the sufficiency analysis.

### Comparison NCA and QCA {#comparisonncaqca}

NCA and QCA share several characteristics. For a causal interpretation of data, both NCA and QCA rely on conditional logic (necessity and sufficiency), and theoretical argumentation for causality. Although NCA primarily uses  quantitative variable-based approaches, it can also be applied (like QCA) with calibrated set membership scores. Both methods can be used in large-n or small-n settings. In a large-n setting, both methods preferably use probability sampling and statistical generalization, although this is not common in QCA [@braumoeller2015guarding]. 

Despite these similarities there are also major differences [@dul2016jbr; @vis2018analyzing]. NCA focuses on necessity, whereas QCA focuses on sufficiency. NCA is mainly a deductive approach requiring a formal necessity hypothesis whereas QCA is mainly an inductive approach. Although QCA carefully selects conditions, a hypothesis how these are related to the outcome is usually not provided. The difference is evident when analyzing OR-combinations of necessity. An OR-combination ($X_1$ or $X_2$ is necessary for $Y$) is often an indication that a higher order construct is necessary for the outcome. A green apple is not necessary for an apple pie, nor is a red apple, but apple is necessary for an apple pie. In NCA's procedures for formulating a formal necessary condition hypothesis (Chapter \@ref(hypothesisformulation)), a relevant higher order construct, rather than the related lower order construct, would likely be recognized and become part of the analysis. An inductive QCA approach may not consider OR-combinations before the analysis, but may identify them afterwards. However, many identified necessity OR-combinations have no substantive meaning as by logic, any necessary condition that is combined with another condition is a necessary OR-combination, even if the combination does not have a substantive meaning.
<!-- If a single condition ($X_1$) is already necessary, then any OR-combination that includes $X_1$ (like $X_1$ OR $X_2$, $X_1$ OR $X_3$, etc.) will also be necessary by definition simply because $X_1$ is part of them. These combinations, however, do not have any new or meaningful interpretation.  -->
If apple is necessary for an apple cake, then the combination [apple OR blue sky] is also necessary by logic, but this does not mean that blue sky is necessary or relevant. The combination is logically true but substantively meaningless.

<!-- When a analyst uses NCA or OLS, these common fundamental limitations should be acknowledged. When NCA and OLS are used in combination the fundamental differences between the methods should be acknowledged. It is important to stress that one method is not better than the other. NCA and OLS are different and address different research questions. To ensure theory-method fit, OLS is the preferred method when the analyst is interested in an average effect of $X$ on $Y$, and NCA is the preferred method when the analyst is interested in the necessity effect of $X$ on $Y$.  -->

Since NCA is an approach for analyzing necessity, it can be compared with QCA's necessity analysis only. When NCA is applied with dichotomous set membership scores as input for NCA's necessity analysis,  the conclusions are usually the same as with csQCA's necessity analysis. Without cases in the upper-left cell, QCA's consistency = 1, which supports necessity. In this situation NCA's effect size is 1, and assuming theoretical support and statistical significance, NCA supports necessity as well. However, NCA differs completely from fsQCA's necessity analysis. Whereas in fsQCA the 'ceiling line' is predefined as the diagonal in the $XY$-plot (Figure \@ref(fig:qcanca2)-left), in NCA the ceiling line is usually estimated from data^[It is also possible to predefine a ceiling line, see Section \@ref(ceilinglinespecification).], which is usually above the diagonal. When fsQCA rejects necessity when too many cases are above the diagonal (using necessity consistency as model fit measure), NCA's necessity analysis may conclude that necessity does exist, though not for all levels of $X$ and $Y$. Whether or not necessity-in-kind should be rejected depends in NCA on the effect size, the availability of theoretical support for necessity and the $p$-value (Section \@ref(necessityinkind)), but not directly on its model fit measures (Section \@ref(modelfit)). Additionally, when necessity-in-kind is observed, NCA can analyze necessity-in-degree to evaluate which level of $X$ is necessary for which level of $Y$ (Section \@ref(bottleneck)).  

When fsQCA supports necessity, some cases may be in the ‘empty’ zone above the diagonal as *deviant cases*. According to NCA the same cases may be close to the ceiling line and are considered *best cases*: cases that are able to achieve a high outcome with a low level of the conditions (assuming that $X$ is an effort and $Y$ is a desired outcome). In NCA, cases above the ceiling are considered not feasible and classified as noise (close above to the ceiling) or outliers/exceptions (further away from the ceiling) as discussed in Section \@ref(modelfit). 

<!-- ```{r, echo = FALSE, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE, include=TRUE} -->
<!-- library("kableExtra") -->
<!-- g <- read.csv("DifferencesNCAQCA.csv")  -->
<!-- kbl(g, caption = "Basic guidelines for good NCA practice", longtable = TRUE, align = c("l", "l", "l")) %>% -->
<!--   kable_styling() -->
<!--     # kable_paper("striped", full_width = F) %>% -->
<!--   # pack_rows("Theoretical justification", 1,3) %>% -->
<!--   # pack_rows("Meaningful data", 4, 4) %>% -->
<!--   # pack_rows("Scatter plot", 5, 6) %>% -->
<!--   # pack_rows("Ceiling line", 7, 7) %>% -->
<!--   # pack_rows("Effect size", 8, 9) %>% -->
<!--   # pack_rows("Statistical test", 10, 11) %>% -->
<!--   # pack_rows("Bottleneck analysis (necessity-in-degree)", 12, 17) %>% -->
<!--   # pack_rows("Descriptions of NCA", 18, 26)  -->
<!-- ``` -->


<!-- ```{r, table1ncaqca, echo=FALSE, out.width='100%', fig.align = "center", fig.cap="Comparison of NCA and QCA.", cache=TRUE} -->
<!-- knitr::include_graphics('Table1ncaqcav2.png') -->
<!-- ``` -->

<!-- NCA and QCA are only the same in a very specific situation of ‘in kind’ necessity: A single $X$ is -->
<!-- necessary for $Y$, and $X$ and $Y$ are dichotomous set membership scores (0 and 1). Then the -->
<!-- analyses of NCA and QCA are exactly the same. However, NCA normally uses variable scores, -->
<!-- but can also set membership scores when NCA is applied in combination with QCA (see -->
<!-- below). In addition to the ‘in kind’ necessity that both methods share, NCA also formulates -->
<!-- ‘in degree’ necessity. QCA also formulates ‘in kind’ necessity of ‘OR’ combinations of -->
<!-- conditions, as well as ‘in kind’ sufficiency of configurations of conditions. -->
<!-- The main interest of NCA is the necessity ‘in degree’ of single factors that enable the outcome, -->
<!-- whereas the main interest of QCA is the sufficiency ‘in kind’ of (alternative) configurations of -->
<!-- conditions. -->

<!-- Regarding research strategy most NCA studies are observational studies (both small N and large-n), although also experiments are possible. Most QCA studies are small N observational studies, although increasingly also large-n studies are employed with QCA, in particular in the business and management area. The experimental research strategy is rare (if not absent) in QCA. Regarding case selection/sampling purposive sampling is the main case selection strategy in QCA. It is also possible in small N NCA studies. For large-n studies sampling strategies such as those used in regression analysis (preferably probability sampling) are used both in NCA and QCA. Regarding measurement, NCA uses valid and reliable variable scores unless it is used in combination with QCA, in which case NCA uses calibrated set membership scores. QCA uses calibrated set membership scores and cannot use variable scores. In QCA data with variable scores may be used as input for the ‘calibration’ process to transform variable scores into set membership scores. Regarding data analysis in fsQCA a necessary condition is assumed to exist if the area above the diagonal reference line in an $XY$ scatter plot is virtually empty (see Figure \@ref(fig:qcanca2) A). In contrast, NCA uses the ceiling line as the reference line (see Figure \@ref(fig:qcanca2) B) for evaluating the necessity of $X$ for $Y$ (with possibly some cases above the ceiling line; accuracy below 100%). In situations where fsQCA observes ‘deviant cases’, NCA includes these cases in the analysis by ‘moving’ the reference line from the diagonal position to the boundary between the zone with cases and the zone without cases. [Note that moving the QCA's diagonal parallel upward to address inaccuracy has been suggested by @ragin2000fuzzy (p. 225, Figure 8.4), but this was not followed up by other QCA analysts]. NCA considers cases around the ceiling line (and usually above the diagonal) as ‘best practice’ cases rather than ‘deviant’ cases. These cases are able to reach a high level of outcome (e.g., an output that is desired) for a relatively low level of condition (e.g., an input that requires effort). For deciding about necessity, NCA evaluates the size of the ‘empty’ zone as a fraction of the total zone (empty plus full zone), which ratio is called the necessity effect size. If the effect size is greater than zero (an empty zone is present), and if according to NCA’s statistical test this is unlikely a random result of unrelated $X$ and $Y$, NCA identifies a necessary condition ‘in kind’ that can be formulated as: ‘$X$ is necessary for $Y$’, indicating that for at least a part of the range of $X$ and the range of $Y$ a certain level of $X$ is necessary for a certain level of Y. Additionally, NCA can quantitatively formulate necessary condition ‘in degree’ by using the ceiling line: ‘level $X_c$ of $X$ is necessary for level $Y_c$ of $Y$’. The ceiling line represents all combinations $X$ and $Y$ where $X$ is necessary for $Y$. Although also fsQCA’s diagonal reference line allows for making quantitative necessary conditions statements, e.g. $X$ > 0.3 is necessary for $Y$ = 0.3, fsQCA does not make such statements. -->
<!-- When the ceiling line coincides with the diagonal (corresponding to the situation that fsQCA considers) the statement ‘$X$ is necessary for $Y$’ applies to all $X$-levels [0,1] and all $Y$-levels [0,1] -->
<!-- and the results of the qualitative necessity analysis of fsQCA and NCA are the same. When the ceiling line is positioned above the diagonal ‘$X$ is necessary for $Y$’ only applies to a specific range of $X$ and a specific range of $Y$. Outside these ranges $X$ is not necessary for $Y$ (‘necessity inefficiency’). Then the results of the qualitative necessity analysis of fsQCA and NCA can be different. -->
<!-- Normally, NCA identifies more necessary conditions than fsQCA, mostly because the diagonal is used as reference line. In the example of Figure \@ref(fig:qcanca2), NCA identifies that $X$ is necessary for $Y$ because there is an empty zone above the ceiling line. However, fsQCA would conclude that $X$ is not necessary for $Y$, because the necessity consistency level is below 0.9. FsQCA’s necessity analysis can be considered as a special case of NCA: an NCA analysis with discrete or continuous fuzzy set membership scores for $X$ and $Y$, a ceiling line that is diagonal, an allowance of a specific number of cases in the empty zone given by the necessity consistency threshold, and the formulation of a qualitative ‘in kind’ necessity statement. -->

### Combining NCA and QCA

There are two ways to combine NCA and QCA in a multimethod study. First, since both NCA and QCA employ a necessity analysis based on necessity causal logic, these two approaches can be used jointly in a *method-triangulation multimethod study*. Second, since QCA also employs a sufficiency analysis based on configurational sufficiency causal logic, NCA can be combined with QCA's sufficiency analysis in a *causal-pluralism multimethod study*. Both types of multimethod studies are discussed below in more detail. 

## Combining NCA and QCA in a multimethod study {#combiningncaandqcainamultimethodstudy}

### Steps for conducting NCA with QCA's necessity analysis  {#necessityanalysisncaqca}
Figure \@ref(fig:qcaflowchart-methodtriangulation) shows a flowchart for conducting a necessity analysis with NCA and QCA in a method-triangulation multimethod study.

```{r qcaflowchart-methodtriangulation, echo=FALSE, out.width='60%', fig.align = "center", fig.cap="Flowchart for conducting a method-triangulation multimethod study with NCA and QCA's necessity analysis.", cache=TRUE}
knitr::include_graphics('Flowchart_QCA_methodtriangulation.png')
```


```{r qcaflowchart-methodtriangulation-code, echo = FALSE, eval=FALSE}
# QCA flowchart with straight-line arrows and three identical vertical flows (Left, Middle, Right)

library(DiagrammeR)
library(DiagrammeRsvg)
library(rsvg)

flowchart <- grViz("
digraph flowchart {

  # --- GRAPH SETTINGS ---
  graph [rankdir = TB, splines = ortho]  # <- makes edges straight
  node [shape = rectangle, style = filled, fillcolor = lightgrey, fontname = Helvetica, fontsize = 12]
  edge [arrowhead = vee, color = black]

  # --- LEFT FLOW ---
  D1 [label = '4a. Conduct NCA\n(necessity)']
  D2 [label = '5a. NCA results']

  # --- MIDDLE FLOW ---
  A2 [label = '1. Theorize necessity relationship(s)']
  B2 [label = '2. Collect raw data']
  E2 [label = '6. Interpret results']

  # --- RIGHT FLOW ---
  C3 [label = '3. Calibrate data']
  D3 [label = '4b. Conduct QCA\n(necessity)']
  D4 [label = '5b. QCA results']

  # --- CONNECTIONS ---
  A2 -> B2
  B2 -> C3
  B2 -> D1
  C3 -> D1
  C3 -> D3
  D1 -> D2
  D2 -> E2
  D3 -> D4
  D4 -> E2

  # --- ALIGNMENT ---
  {rank = same; A2}
  {rank = same; B2}
  {rank = same; C3}
  {rank = same; D1; D3}
  {rank = same; E2}

}
")

flowchart

# Export the flowchart to PDF
# 1. Convert the graph to an SVG
svg_code <- export_svg(flowchart)

# 2. Convert the SVG to a PDF
#rsvg_pdf(charToRaw(svg_code), file = "SEMflowchart_QCA_methodtriangulation.pdf")

cat("PDF successfully created")
```



The study starts with theorizing the necessity relationship(s). In NCA this means the formulation of a formal necessity hypothesis (Chapter \@ref(hypothesisformulation)) in which not only $X$ and $Y$ are selected, but also the causal relationship is theoretically justified. In QCA the relationship between the conditions and outcome is usually not discussed extensively before the analysis is done and usually no necessity hypothesis is formulated. This reflects that QCA is a more inductive approach. 

The next step is collecting (raw) data. In QCA the data need to be calibrated to transform the data into meaningful set membership scores. Qualitative data may be calibrated based on case knowledge of the user and expert judgment (the direct method), and quantitative data can be calibrated by mechanistically applying a transformation function (the indirect method). NCA accepts both calibrated data and raw data (when the raw data are valid and reliable), but QCA accepts only calibrated data. Therefore, two types of NCA-QCA method-triangulation for analyzing necessity can be done:

1. NCA with raw data and QCA with calibrated data.

2. NCA and QCA both with calibrated data.

After the NCA and QCA analyses are conducted, in the last step the results are interpreted in combination.

### Example NCA-QCA method-triangulation multimethod study {#examplencaqcamethodtriangulationmultimethodstudy}
This section presents an example of integrating NCA in QCA according to a method-triangulation multimethod necessity study. The example is based on a study by @lipset1959some, discussed in @ragin2009fsqca. The outcome variable is SURV: survival of democracy during the inter-war period. Assuming that a theoretical rationale for necessity exists, five conditions were analyzed for necessity using both QCA and NCA: 

- DEV: Level of development: expressed as GDP per capita (USD) in the raw data.

- URB	Level of urbanization: percent of the population in towns with 20,000 or more
inhabitants in the raw data.

- LIT	Level of literacy: percent of the literate population (raw data).

- IND	Level of industrialization: percent of the industrial labor force (raw data).

- STB	Government stability: “political-institutional (in)stability” expressed in the raw data by the number of cabinets which governed in the period under study.

The raw data were calibrated into fuzzy set membership scores (the calibration process is not discussed here). The analyses can be done as follows:  \

``` {r , echo = FALSE, eval = TRUE, warning=FALSE, message= FALSE}
library(QCA)
library(NCA)
``` 


``` {r , echo = TRUE, eval = TRUE, warning=FALSE, message= TRUE}
library(QCA)
library(NCA)

# Data
conditions <- c("DEV", "URB", "LIT", "IND", "STB") #names of the conditions"
outcome <- "SURV" #name of the outcome
data(LR) #Lipset raw data
data(LF) #Lipset calibrated data

#QCA-calibrated (fuzzy)
pofind(LF, outcome = "SURV") #single necessary conditions

#NCA-calibrated (fuzzy)
set.seed(123) # for reproducible results
model_calibrated<- nca_analysis(LF,
                                1:5,6,
                                ceilings = "ce_fdh",
                                test.rep = 10000)
model_calibrated

# NCA-raw data
set.seed(123) # for reproducible results
model_raw <- nca_analysis(LR,
                          1:5,6,
                          ceilings = "ce_fdh",
                          corner = c(1,1,1,1,2),
                          test.rep = 10000)
model_raw
```

<!-- ``` {r Lipset-hide , echo = TRUE, eval = TRUE, warning=FALSE, message= FALSE, result = FALSE} -->
<!-- library(QCA) -->
<!-- library(NCA) -->

<!-- # Data -->
<!-- conditions <- c("DEV", "URB", "LIT", "IND", "STB") #names of the conditions" -->
<!-- outcome <- "SURV" #name of the outcome -->
<!-- data(LR) #Lipset raw data -->
<!-- data(LF) #Lipset calibrated data -->

<!-- #QCA-calibrated (fuzzy) -->
<!-- pofind(LF, outcome = "SURV") #single necessary conditions -->

<!-- #NCA-calibrated (fuzzy) -->
<!-- model_calibrated<- nca_analysis(LF,  -->
<!--                                 1:5,6,  -->
<!--                                 ceilings = "ce_fdh",  -->
<!--                                 test.rep = 10000)  -->
<!-- model_calibrated -->

<!-- # NCA-raw data -->
<!-- model_raw <- nca_analysis(LR,  -->
<!--                           1:5,6,  -->
<!--                           ceilings = "ce_fdh",  -->
<!--                           corner = c(1,1,1,1,2), #raw STB data are inverse -->
<!--                           test.rep = 10000) -->
<!-- model_raw -->
<!-- ``` -->


<!-- ``` {r Lipset-show1 , echo = FALSE, eval = TRUE, warning=FALSE, message= FALSE, result = TRUE} -->

<!-- pofind(LF, outcome = "SURV") #single necessary conditions -->
